topics.title: Solar Thermal Collectors - Flat Plate Collectors - Solar Concentrators (Parabolic Trough, Parabolic Dish, Central Tower Collector) - Solar Air Heaters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Collectors - Flat Plate Collectors - Solar Concentrators (Parabolic Trough, Parabolic Dish, Central Tower Collector) - Solar Air Heaters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Solar Thermal Collector?

A **Solar Thermal Collector** is a device designed to absorb incident solar radiation, convert it into thermal energy (heat), and transfer this heat to a working fluid (water, air, oil, or molten salt). Unlike photovoltaic (PV) systems that convert sunlight directly into electricity, thermal collectors harness the *heating effect* of photons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A solar thermal collector is a heat exchanger that transforms solar radiant energy into internal (thermal) energy of a transport medium. The classification is based on the method of solar energy concentration and the operating temperature range.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Solar Thermal Collectors

| Class | Type | Concentration Ratio (C) | Temperature Range |
|-------|------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Non-Concentrating** | Flat Plate Collector (FPC) | C = 1 | 30°C – 80°C |
| **Non-Concentrating** | Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC) | C = 1 | 50°C – 150°C |
| **Concentrating (Line Focus)** | Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC) | C = 10 – 50 | 100°C – 400°C |
| **Concentrating (Point Focus)** | Parabolic Dish (PD) | C = 500 – 3000 | 250°C – 1500°C |
| **Concentrating (Point Focus)** | Central Tower Receiver (CRS) | C = 300 – 1500 | 500°C – 2000°C |

---

### 1.3 The Flat Plate Collector (FPC) — The "Workhorse"

A **Flat Plate Collector** is a rectangular, box-like device that captures both *beam* and *diffuse* solar radiation on a flat absorbing surface, without the use of any optical concentration.

**Components of a Standard FPC:**
1. **Transparent Cover (Glazing):** Usually 1–3 layers of low-iron tempered glass. Reduces top heat losses (convection & radiation).
2. **Absorber Plate:** A metal sheet (copper, aluminium) coated with a **selective surface** (e.g., black chrome, nickel-pigmented alumina) having high absorptivity (α ≥ 0.95) and low emissivity (ε ≤ 0.10).
3. **Tubes / Risers:** Bonded to the absorber plate. The heat-transfer fluid flows through these.
4. **Header Pipes:** Distribute the fluid to the risers.
5. **Insulation:** Fibrous glass or rock wool on the back and sides to minimize bottom and edge losses.
6. **Casing:** Aluminium or galvanized iron enclosure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a flat plate collector as a "greenhouse in reverse engineering." Just as a greenhouse traps heat to grow plants, the FPC traps solar heat — but with one important addition: a selectively-coated black plate that *loves* absorbing sunlight and *hates* re-emitting it as infrared radiation.

**Useful Energy Equation (Hottel–Whillier–Bliss):**

$$Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

Where:
- $Q_u$ = Useful heat gained (W)
- $A_c$ = Collector aperture area (m²)
- $F_R$ = Heat removal factor
- $S$ = Absorbed solar radiation per unit area (W/m²)
- $U_L$ = Overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_i$ = Fluid inlet temperature (°C or K)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C or K)

The standard solar constant is **$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$** and the Air Mass 1.5 spectrum is the KTU reference benchmark.

---

### 1.4 The Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC) — "The Field of Mirrors"

A **Parabolic Trough Collector** is a line-focusing concentrating collector. The reflector has a parabolic cross-section that focuses parallel sun rays onto a linear receiver tube located at the focal axis.

**Equation of the Parabola:**

$$y^2 = 4 f x$$

Where $f$ is the focal length. The receiver tube is a metal pipe coated with a selective surface, enclosed in a glass envelope to reduce convective losses (evacuated between them).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine holding a curved mirror under sunlight. The mirror focuses light onto a single bright line. A parabolic trough is the industrial, large-scale version — the "bright line" is the receiver tube, and the fluid inside it gets superheated, just like how a child can burn paper using a magnifying glass.

**Applications:** Solar thermal power plants (SEGS in California, Noor in Morocco), industrial process heat, solar cooling.

---

### 1.5 The Parabolic Dish (Dish-Stirling System) — "The Sun Magnifier"

A **Parabolic Dish** is a point-focus concentrator shaped like a satellite dish. It tracks the sun in two axes, focusing all rays onto a single focal point where the receiver (often coupled to a Stirling engine) is mounted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A parabolic dish is the "largest magnifying glass on Earth." It's like the reflector behind a flashlight — but instead of producing a small LED beam, it produces a focused dot of solar energy so intense it can melt steel.

**Highest efficiency of all:** The dish-Stirling system can achieve ~31% peak solar-to-electric conversion efficiency.

---

### 1.6 The Central Tower Collector (CRS / Solar Power Tower) — "The Forest of Heliostats"

A **Central Receiver System (CRS)** consists of a field of sun-tracking mirrors (**heliostats**) that reflect sunlight onto a single receiver mounted atop a tall tower. The working fluid (molten salt, water/steam, or air) is heated to extremely high temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a crowd of people with hand mirrors at a stadium, all aiming their reflections at a single point on the stage. The "stage" is the receiver at the top of the tower. By 2024, modern CRS systems use **molten salt** (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) capable of storing heat for up to **15 hours** — solving the day/night solar energy problem.

---

### 1.7 The Solar Air Heater — "The Dryer"

A **Solar Air Heater (SAH)** is a variant of the flat plate collector where **air** is the working fluid instead of water. Since air has low density and heat capacity, special designs are used to enhance heat transfer:
- **V-corrugated absorber**
- **Perforated plate**
- **Matrix absorber** (packed bed)
- **Transverse wire matrix**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A solar air heater is the "hairdryer of renewable energy." Instead of heating water for showers, it heats air for drying crops, lumber, fish, and textiles. It is a critical component in **agriculture and food processing industries** in India and Africa.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

**For a Flat Plate Collector (FPC):**

1. **Solar radiation** $I_t$ (beam + diffuse) strikes the glass cover.
2. A fraction $(\tau \alpha)$ is transmitted through the glass and absorbed by the selective coating.
3. The absorbed energy is conducted through the absorber plate to the **bonded riser tubes**.
4. The fluid (water) flowing in the tubes picks up the heat by convection.
5. Heat losses occur via the **top** (convection + radiation through glass), **bottom** (conduction through insulation), and **edges** (conduction through frame).
6. The net energy balance gives the **useful heat gain** $Q_u$.

**Energy Balance at Steady State:**

$$Q_A = Q_u + Q_{\text{top}} + Q_{\text{bottom}} + Q_{\text{edge}}$$

Expanding:

$$A_c \cdot S = \dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i) + A_c U_t (T_p - T_a) + A_c U_b (T_p - T_a) + A_c U_e (T_p - T_a)$$

Where:
- $S$ = Absorbed radiation (W/m²)
- $\dot{m}$ = Mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $U_t, U_b, U_e$ = Top, bottom, edge loss coefficients (W/m²·K)
- $T_p$ = Mean plate temperature (K)

**Overall Loss Coefficient:**

$$U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$$

For single-glazed collectors, $U_L \approx 5.0$ W/m²·K. For double-glazed, $U_L \approx 2.5$ W/m²·K.

---

### 2.2 The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation (HWBE) — Derivation Logic

The HWBE is the **single most important equation** for any KTU solar exam.

**Step 1:** The useful heat gain is the fluid enthalpy rise:

$$Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i)$$

**Step 2:** Define a **Heat Removal Factor** $F_R$ which accounts for the fact that fluid temperature rises non-uniformly across the risers:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3:** The collector efficiency factor $F'$ relates the *actual* useful heat to the *ideal* case where the entire absorber is at fluid inlet temperature:

$$F' = \frac{1/U_L}{W \left[ \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{\pi D h_i} \right] + \frac{1}{F h_i}}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Tube spacing (m)
- $C$ = Bond conductance (W/m·K)
- $D$ = Tube inner diameter (m)
- $h_i$ = Internal fluid heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $F$ = Fin efficiency

**Step 4:** The final HWBE:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]}$$

---

### 2.3 Optical Efficiency of Concentrators

For a concentrating collector, optical losses are critical:

$$\eta_o = (\tau \alpha) \rho_r \gamma (1 - c_f)$$

Where:
- $\tau \alpha$ = Transmissivity–Absorptivity product of the receiver
- $\rho_r$ = Reflectivity of the mirror (typically 0.85–0.95 for silvered glass)
- $\gamma$ = Geometric intercept factor
- $c_f$ = Fraction of radiation lost by cloud cover/mirror gap

For a parabolic trough: $\eta_o \approx 0.65$ to $0.75$.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_i - T_a)]$ | Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation | W |
| 2 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot I_t}$ | Instantaneous thermal efficiency | – |
| 3 | $\eta_o = (\tau \alpha) \rho_r \gamma (1 - c_f)$ | Optical efficiency | – |
| 4 | $C = A_a / A_r$ | Geometric concentration ratio | – |
| 5 | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ | Overall heat loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 6 | $F = \dfrac{\tanh(mW)}{mW}$ | Fin efficiency of absorber | – |
| 7 | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 h_{w}}{k_{plate} \delta}}$ | Fin parameter | 1/m |
| 8 | $F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m}c_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - e^{-A_c U_L F' / \dot{m}c_p}\right]$ | Heat removal factor | – |
| 9 | $S = I_t (\tau \alpha)_e$ | Absorbed radiation per unit area | W/m² |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{loss,top}} = h_{w,c} (T_p - T_a) + \epsilon \sigma (T_p^4 - T_a^4)$ | Top heat loss (convection + radiation) | W/m² |
| 11 | $h_{w,c} = 1.78 (T_p - T_a)^{1/3}$ (Stine–Geyer) | Wind convection coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{Stirling}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{absorbed}}}$ | Parabolic dish efficiency | – |
| 13 | $T_{\text{max}} = T_a + \dfrac{S}{U_L}$ | Stagnation temperature | K |
| 14 | $Q_{\text{tower}} = N_h \cdot A_h \cdot I_b \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{\text{shadow}} \cdot \eta_{\text{interf}}$ | Central tower collected power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write $U_L$ in W/m²·K, $A_c$ in m², and $T$ in **Kelvin (K)** for consistency in the HWBE. Many students lose marks due to unit mismatches between °C and K.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

- **FPC**: Domestic water heating, swimming pool heating, building-integrated solar thermal (BIST).
- **PTC**: Utility-scale solar thermal electricity (e.g., 250 MW Noor-Ouarzazate, Morocco).
- **Parabolic Dish**: Distributed generation, off-grid rural electrification (especially in defence and remote areas).
- **Central Tower**: Grid-scale baseload solar with thermal storage (Crescent Dunes, USA; DEWA, UAE).
- **Solar Air Heater**: Industrial drying, desalination, space heating, agricultural product drying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation (Full Form)

**Starting Energy Balance (per unit collector area):**

We consider a *single riser tube* with the absorber plate of width $W$ (tube pitch) on either side.

Let:
- $T_p$ = Average absorber plate temperature (K)
- $T_{fi}$ = Local fluid temperature at position $x$ along the tube (K)
- $S$ = Solar radiation absorbed by the plate (W/m²)
- $U_L$ = Combined top, bottom, edge heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $h_i$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient inside tube (W/m²·K)
- $D$ = Tube inner diameter (m)
- $W$ = Tube spacing (m)

**Step 1: Plate-to-Fluid Heat Transfer**

The useful heat flux $q_u$ from the plate to the fluid across the riser:

$$q_u = F' \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

The factor $F'$ is the **collector efficiency factor**, accounting for temperature drop from plate to fluid.

**Step 2: Differential Energy Balance on a Fluid Element**

Consider a fluid element of length $dx$ in the riser tube:

$$\dot{m} c_p \, dT_{fi} = F' W \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right] dx$$

This is a first-order linear ODE in $T_{fi}(x)$.

**Step 3: Integration Across the Tube Length $L$**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT_{fi}}{S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)} = \frac{F' W}{\dot{m} c_p} \, dx$$

Let $\theta = T_{fi} - T_a - S/U_L$ (a substitution to remove the constant term):

$$\frac{d\theta}{S - U_L \theta} = -\frac{F' W}{\dot{m} c_p} dx$$

Integrating from inlet ($x=0$, $T_{fi} = T_i$) to outlet ($x=L$, $T_{fi} = T_o$):

$$\int_{T_i}^{T_o} \frac{dT_{fi}}{S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)} = \frac{F' W L}{\dot{m} c_p}$$

Solving:

$$\left. \frac{-1}{U_L} \ln\left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right] \right|_{T_i}^{T_o} = \frac{F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p}$$

Where $A_c = W \cdot L$ is the gross collector area.

**Step 4: Applying the Limits**

$$\frac{-1}{U_L} \left\{ \ln\left[S - U_L(T_o - T_a)\right] - \ln\left[S - U_L(T_i - T_a)\right] \right\} = \frac{F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_L} \ln \left[ \frac{S - U_L(T_i - T_a)}{S - U_L(T_o - T_a)} \right] = \frac{F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p}$$

**Step 5: Solving for $T_o$**

Exponentiating both sides:

$$S - U_L(T_o - T_a) = \left[ S - U_L(T_i - T_a) \right] \exp\left[ -\frac{U_L F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p} \right]$$

Multiplying through by $\dot{m} c_p$:

$$\dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i) = \dot{m} c_p \left( \frac{S}{U_L} + T_a - T_i \right) \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\frac{U_L F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p} \right) \right]$$

**Step 6: Introducing the Heat Removal Factor $F_R$**

By definition, the useful heat gain is:

$$Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i)$$

Comparing with the standard form, we define:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{U_L A_c} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\frac{U_L F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p} \right) \right]$$

**Step 7: Final Result — The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation**

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = F_R \left[ (\tau \alpha)_e - \frac{U_L (T_i - T_a)}{I_t} \right]}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Collector Efficiency Factor $F'$

The factor $F'$ relates the *actual* useful heat to the *ideal* useful heat (where the absorber plate is everywhere at $T_i$).

**Step 1: Energy Balance on a Fin Element (Plate Strip)**

The plate strip of width $W/2$ on each side of a tube acts as a fin. The differential equation governing temperature in the fin is:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - m^2 (T - T_a) = -\frac{h_w S}{k \delta}$$

Where:
- $m^2 = 2 h_w / (k \delta)$
- $h_w$ = Combined convection + radiation coefficient
- $k$ = Plate thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\delta$ = Plate thickness (m)

**Step 2: Solving with Boundary Conditions**

Boundary conditions:
- At tube wall ($x=0$): $T = T_b$ (bond temperature)
- At fin tip ($x = W/2$): $dT/dx = 0$ (insulated edge)

The solution gives the **fin efficiency**:

$$F = \frac{\tanh(mW/2)}{mW/2} \quad \text{or in tube spacing form:} \quad F = \frac{\tanh(mW)}{mW}$$

**Step 3: Heat Flow at the Bond**

Useful heat flux from one tube pitch:

$$q_u = \frac{T_b - T_a}{\frac{W}{C} + \frac{1}{\pi D h_i F}}$$

Where $C$ is the bond conductance (W/m·K) between tube and plate.

**Step 4: Combining with Fin and Plate Losses**

Using $F$ and including the top + bottom + edge losses, the final form of $F'$ is:

$$\boxed{F' = \frac{1/U_L}{\dfrac{W}{C} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{F \, t \, U_L}{\pi D h_i} \right] + \dfrac{1}{h_i \pi D}}}$$

Or in the commonly used textbook form (Sukhatme & Nayak):

$$F' = \frac{1}{U_L} \left[ \frac{1}{\frac{W}{(F \cdot t + W) \left(\frac{1}{U_t [(\tau \alpha)/0.97] - T_a]} \right)} \right]$$

For board exams, the simplified form is:

$$F' = \frac{1/U_L}{\frac{W}{C \cdot (1 + F \cdot t / W)} \cdot \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{W}{k \delta (F \cdot t + W)}}$$

**KTU students should remember the qualitative meaning:** $F'$ ranges from 0 to 1, where $F' \to 1$ means a perfect collector (no plate-to-fluid temperature drop), and $F' \to 0$ means poor heat extraction.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A flat plate collector has $A_c = 2.0$ m², $F_R = 0.85$, $U_L = 5.5$ W/m²·K, $(\tau \alpha) = 0.85$, $I_t = 800$ W/m², $T_i = 30$°C, $T_a = 25$°C, $\dot{m} = 0.02$ kg/s, $c_p = 4180$ J/kg·K.

**Find:** (a) Useful heat gain $Q_u$, (b) Outlet temperature $T_o$, (c) Efficiency $\eta_c$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Compute absorbed radiation $S$:

$$S = I_t (\tau \alpha) = 800 \times 0.85 = 680 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Apply the HWBE:

$$Q_u = 2.0 \times 0.85 \times \left[ 680 - 5.5 \times (30 - 25) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 1.7 \times \left[ 680 - 27.5 \right] = 1.7 \times 652.5 = 1109.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step (b):** Apply fluid energy balance:

$$T_o = T_i + \frac{Q_u}{\dot{m} c_p} = 30 + \frac{1109.25}{0.02 \times 4180}$$

$$T_o = 30 + \frac{1109.25}{83.6} = 30 + 13.27 = 43.27 \text{°C}$$

**Step (c):** Collector efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \frac{1109.25}{2.0 \times 800} = \frac{1109.25}{1600} = 0.6933 = 69.33\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The efficiency is within the realistic range of 40–80% for FPC systems. The outlet temperature rise (≈ 13.27°C) is also realistic.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: FPC vs PTC vs Dish vs Tower

| Parameter | FPC | PTC | Dish | Tower |
|-----------|-----|-----|------|-------|
| **Concentration ratio C** | 1 | 10–50 | 500–3000 | 300–1500 |
| **Tracking** | Fixed | 1-axis (N-S) | 2-axis | Field heliostats |
| **Working fluid** | Water | Oil/Molten salt | Air/Hydrogen | Molten salt/Steam |
| **Outlet temperature** | 30–80°C | 150–400°C | 500–1500°C | 500–1000°C |
| **Efficiency (peak)** | 60–75% | 60–75% (thermal) | 29–31% (electric) | 35–45% (electric) |
| **Storage capability** | Low | Moderate | None | Excellent (15 h) |
| **Cost per m²** (2024) | ₹3,500 | ₹8,000 | ₹15,000 | ₹20,000 |
| **Best application** | Water heating | Industrial process | Distributed power | Grid baseload |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Flat Plate Collector (FPC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SUN] --> B1[Broadband Solar Radiation I_t]
    B1 --> C1[Transparent Glass Cover 1-3 layers]
    C1 -->|tau = transmittance| D1[Absorber Plate with Selective Coating alpha = 0.95 epsilon = 0.10]
    D1 -->|Conduction through plate| E1[Tube Risers bonded to plate]
    E1 -->|Convection hi| F1[Working Fluid Water or Glycol]
    F1 -->|Heat absorbed| G1[Outlet Fluid Hot to storage]
    D1 -->|Top loss Ut| C1
    D1 -->|Bottom loss Ub| H1[Insulation Glass wool / Rockwool]
    D1 -->|Edge loss Ue| I1[Side Insulation Frame]
    G1 --> J1[Useful Heat Gain Qu]
    H1 --> K1[Heat Loss Q_loss]
    I1 --> K1
    J1 --> L1[Storage Tank / Load]
    K1 --> M1[Ambient]
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Parabolic Trough System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[SUN] --> B2[Beam Solar Radiation I_b]
    B2 --> C2[Parabolic Reflector Mirror]
    C2 -->|rho_r = 0.92| D2[Receiver Tube at Focal Line]
    D2 -->|tau alpha = 0.96| E2[Selective Coating on tube]
    E2 -->|Conduction through tube| F2[Heat Transfer Fluid HTF Therminol VP-1 or Molten Salt]
    F2 --> G2[Hot HTF Outlet 350-400 C]
    G2 --> H2[Heat Exchanger Steam Generator]
    H2 --> I2[Steam Turbine Rankine Cycle]
    I2 --> J2[Electrical Generator]
    J2 --> K2[Grid or Load]
    D2 -->|Glass envelope evacuated| L2[Convective Loss Reduction]
    L2 --> M2[Cold HTF Return]
    M2 --> F2
    C2 -->|Tracking 1 axis N-S| N2[Sun Tracking Controller]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of a Central Tower Receiver (CRS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A3[SUN] --> B3[Beam Solar Radiation I_b]
    B3 --> C3[Heliostat Field 10000 mirrors]
    C3 -->|rho and eta_intercept| D3[Receiver at Tower Top 100-200 m]
    D3 --> E3[Aperture Cavity or External Receiver]
    E3 --> F3[Working Fluid Molten Salt or Steam]
    F3 -->|Hot salt 565 C| G3[Hot Storage Tank]
    G3 --> H3[Steam Generator]
    H3 --> I3[Superheated Steam 540 C]
    I3 --> J3[Ranking Steam Turbine]
    J3 --> K3[Generator]
    K3 --> L3[Power to Grid]
    G3 --> M3[Cold Salt Return 290 C]
    M3 --> E3
    F3 --> N3[Thermal Storage 15 hours]
    N3 --> O3[Power Generation Day and Night]
    C3 --> P3[Master Control and Tracking]
    P3 --> C3
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of a Solar Air Heater (SAH)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Sun] --> B4[Solar Radiation I_t]
    B4 --> C4[Glazing Low Iron Glass]
    C4 -->|tau| D4[Absorber Plate Single Pass / V Corrugated]
    D4 -->|Conduction| E4[Air Channel below plate]
    E4 -->|Convection h_air| F4[Air Stream Heated 30-80 C]
    F4 --> G4[Outlet to Drying Chamber]
    D4 -->|Convection + Radiation| H4[Top Loss U_t]
    H4 --> C4
    D4 -->|Bottom loss U_b| I4[Insulation]
    I4 --> J4[Ambient]
    G4 --> K4[Use Cases Crop Drying Timber Textile Space Heating]
    F4 --> L4[Blower or Natural Draft]
    L4 --> E4
```

---

### 4.5 Concentration Ratio vs Operating Temperature Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A5[Concentration Ratio C] --> B5[C = 1]
    A5 --> C5[C = 10 to 50]
    A5 --> D5[C = 500 to 3000]
    A5 --> E5[C = 300 to 1500]
    B5 --> F5[FPC 30 to 80 C]
    C5 --> G5[PTC 100 to 400 C]
    D5 --> H5[Dish Stirling 500 to 1500 C]
    E5 --> I5[Central Tower 500 to 1000 C]
    F5 --> J5[Domestic Heating]
    G5 --> K5[Industrial Process Heat]
    H5 --> L5[High Temp Power]
    I5 --> M5[Grid Power with Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams above provide a **Functional Architecture View** rather than physical drawings. Each block represents a subsystem, and the arrows indicate energy flow, mass flow, or control signal flow. This is the KTU-recommended approach for engineering drawings in board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Heat Removal Factor" ($F_R$) in a flat plate solar collector. State its range and physical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Heat Removal Factor** $F_R$ is the ratio of the actual useful heat gain of a collector to the useful heat gain that would be obtained if the absorber plate were uniformly at the fluid inlet temperature $T_i$.

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p} \right) \right]$$

**Range:** $0 < F_R < 1$ (typically 0.7 – 0.9 for well-designed FPCs).

**Physical significance:** It accounts for the rise in fluid temperature above $T_i$ as it flows through the collector, which reduces the local temperature difference $(T_p - T_i)$ available for heat transfer, thereby reducing useful heat gain compared to the ideal case.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a flat plate collector and a concentrating solar collector. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Flat Plate Collector | Concentrating Collector |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Concentration ratio | C = 1 | C > 1 |
| Tracking required | No (fixed) | Yes (1-axis or 2-axis) |
| Absorbs diffuse radiation | Yes | No (only beam) |
| Operating temperature | 30°C – 80°C | > 100°C |
| Optical concentration | None | Yes (lens or mirror) |

**Examples:**
- **FPC:** Domestic water heater, swimming pool heater.
- **Concentrating:** Parabolic trough (SEGS plant), parabolic dish (Stirling), central tower (Crescent Dunes).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation for a flat plate solar collector. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A flat plate solar collector with an aperture area of 3.0 m² is used to heat water from 28°C to 65°C. The absorbed solar radiation is 720 W/m², the overall heat loss coefficient is 5.8 W/m²·K, the heat removal factor is 0.82, and the ambient temperature is 28°C. Determine:
  (i) The useful heat gain of the collector.
  (ii) The instantaneous thermal efficiency.
  (iii) The mass flow rate of water required. (7 marks)

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat flow.
2. Absorber plate is isothermal along the direction perpendicular to flow.
3. Bond between tube and plate has finite thermal conductance.
4. Glass cover properties are uniform.
5. Edge losses are lumped into $U_L$.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**

Starting from the differential energy balance on a fluid element:

$$\dot{m} c_p \, dT_{fi} = F' W [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)] dx$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT_{fi}}{S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)} = \frac{F' W}{\dot{m} c_p} dx$$

Integrating from $T_i$ to $T_o$ across length $L$:

$$\frac{1}{U_L} \ln \left[ \frac{S - U_L(T_i - T_a)}{S - U_L(T_o - T_a)} \right] = \frac{F' A_c}{\dot{m} c_p}$$

Defining the heat removal factor $F_R$ and solving for $Q_u$:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_i - T_a)]}$$

This is the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation. (Full marks awarded for complete derivation with stated assumptions.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A_c = 3.0$ m², $T_i = 28$°C, $T_a = 28$°C, $S = 720$ W/m², $U_L = 5.8$ W/m²·K, $F_R = 0.82$.

**(i) Useful heat gain (3 marks):**

$$Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_i - T_a)]$$

Since $T_i - T_a = 0$:

$$Q_u = 3.0 \times 0.82 \times [720 - 0] = 3.0 \times 0.82 \times 720$$

$$Q_u = 1.770 \text{ kW} = 1770 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Instantaneous thermal efficiency (2 marks):**

We need the incident solar radiation $I_t$ to find $\eta_c$. Using $S = I_t (\tau \alpha)$, and assuming $(\tau \alpha) = 0.85$:

$$I_t = \frac{S}{(\tau \alpha)} = \frac{720}{0.85} = 847 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \frac{1770}{3.0 \times 847} = \frac{1770}{2541} = 0.6966 = 69.66\%$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate (2 marks):**

$$Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i) = \dot{m} \times 4180 \times (65 - 28)$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{1770}{4180 \times 37} = \frac{1770}{154660} = 0.01145 \text{ kg/s} \approx 41.2 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part A):**
> 1. **Forgetting the assumption list (–1 mark):** State all five assumptions explicitly.
> 2. **Unit mismatch between °C and K in HWBE (–1 mark):** Since the equation uses a *difference* $T_i - T_a$, either °C or K is fine, but **be consistent**.
> 3. **Missing the final form of $F_R$ (–2 marks):** Many students derive correctly but forget to define $F_R$ explicitly.
> 4. **Mass flow rate calculation:** Common error is forgetting to subtract $T_i$ from $T_o$ — gives wrong answer.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Central Tower Solar Power Plant**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the parabolic trough, parabolic dish, and central tower collectors in terms of:
  (i) Concentration ratio
  (ii) Tracking mechanism
  (iii) Working fluid
  (iv) Application
Provide a justification for why central tower systems are preferred for grid-scale solar with storage. (7 marks)

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**Part (a) — Central Tower System (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
1. **Heliostat field:** Thousands of sun-tracking flat mirrors, each independently controlled to track the sun in two axes.
2. **Tower:** A tall structure (100–250 m) with the receiver at its top.
3. **Receiver:** A cavity-type or external-type heat exchanger where the working fluid is heated to 500–1000°C.
4. **Working fluid:** Molten salt (60% $\text{NaNO}_3$ + 40% $\text{KNO}_3$), water/steam, or air.
5. **Storage tank system:** Two-tank molten salt storage (cold and hot) for 24-hour dispatch.
6. **Power block:** Steam turbine, generator, condenser, cooling tower.

**Working principle:**
- Heliostats track the sun and reflect beam radiation onto the receiver.
- Receiver absorbs the concentrated flux and heats the working fluid.
- Hot fluid is stored in the hot storage tank.
- When power is needed, the hot fluid passes through a heat exchanger → generates steam → drives turbine → generator → grid.

**Functional block diagram** (as shown in Section 4.3).

**Advantages:**
- High operating temperature → high cycle efficiency (Carnot).
- Excellent thermal storage (up to 15 hours) → dispatchable power.
- Modular heliostat field → easy scaling.
- Centralized maintenance.

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost.
- High water/land requirement.
- Optical losses from heliostat field (~30%).
- Requires direct normal irradiance (DNI > 1800 kWh/m²/yr).

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Central Tower |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Concentration ratio** | 10 – 50 | 500 – 3000 | 300 – 1500 |
| **Tracking** | 1-axis (N-S) | 2-axis | Heliostat field (2-axis) |
| **Working fluid** | Therminol oil / Molten salt | Hydrogen / Helium (Stirling) | Molten salt / Water-Steam |
| **Receiver type** | Linear tube | Point focus (cavity) | External or cavity |
| **Outlet temperature** | 350 – 400°C | 700 – 1500°C | 500 – 1000°C |
| **Storage compatible** | Moderate | Poor | Excellent |
| **Application** | Industrial heat, grid | Distributed power | Grid baseload with storage |
| **Capacity range** | 10 – 250 MW | 5 – 50 kW | 10 – 400 MW |
| **Land area (per MW)** | ~5 acres | ~2 acres | ~4 acres |

**Justification for central tower with storage (4 marks):**
The central tower system is the **most preferred** for grid-scale solar with storage because:
1. **High temperature** enables use of a supercritical Rankine cycle with high conversion efficiency.
2. **Molten salt** is a low-cost, non-toxic, high-energy-density storage medium, allowing **15–18 hours** of full-load storage.
3. The **heliostat field is modular** — capacity can be expanded by adding more heliostats.
4. It can deliver **firm, dispatchable power** day and night, addressing the intermittency problem of solar PV.
5. Successful commercial deployments: **Gemasolar (Spain)**, **Crescent Dunes (USA)**, **DEWA CSP Tower (UAE)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> 1. **Sketch requirement (–2 marks):** Even if a Mermaid diagram is provided in the notes, in the exam paper you must draw a *neat block diagram* with arrows and labels. Unlabelled boxes lose marks.
> 2. **Comparison table should be tabulated, not in paragraphs (–1 mark):** Always present comparisons in a markdown or neatly drawn table.
> 3. **Justification must include the "why" of thermal storage (–1 mark):** Don't just state facts — connect them to the *solar intermittency problem*.
> 4. **Heliostat definition (–1 mark):** Don't confuse heliostats with PV panels.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — HIGH-FREQUENCY REVISION POINTS FOR BOARD EXAM:**

**Definitions to Memorize:**
- ✅ Flat plate collector, selective surface, absorptivity, emissivity
- ✅ Heat removal factor $F_R$, collector efficiency factor $F'$
- ✅ Concentration ratio $C = A_a / A_r$
- ✅ Optical efficiency $\eta_o = (\tau \alpha) \rho_r \gamma (1 - c_f)$
- ✅ Overall heat loss coefficient $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$

**Key Equations (must memorize the form):**
- ✅ Hottel–Whillier–Bliss: $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_i - T_a)]$
- ✅ Efficiency: $\eta_c = F_R[(\tau \alpha) - U_L(T_i - T_a)/I_t]$
- ✅ Stagnation temperature: $T_{st} = T_a + S/U_L$
- ✅ Fin efficiency: $F = \tanh(mW)/(mW)$

**Numerical Values to Remember:**
- ✅ Solar constant: $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²
- ✅ AM 1.5 spectrum: $I_{AM1.5} = 1000$ W/m²
- ✅ Selective coating: $\alpha = 0.95$, $\varepsilon = 0.10$
- ✅ Mirror reflectivity (silvered glass): $\rho_r = 0.92$
- ✅ U_L for FPC: 5.0 W/m²·K (single glazed), 2.5 W/m²·K (double glazed)
- ✅ Molten salt composition: 60% $\text{NaNO}_3$ + 40% $\text{KNO}_3$

**Concentrator Classification — Memory Trick:**
> **"L T D T" → Line Trough, Dish = 2-axis, Tower = Top-mounted.**
> Trough: Line focus, 1-axis.
> Dish: Point focus, 2-axis, highest CR.
> Tower: Point focus via field, best storage.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ❌ Using °C in HWBE when ambient is in K (do not mix; use either both in °C or both in K).
- ❌ Forgetting that FPC absorbs *diffuse* radiation, but concentrators absorb *only beam*.
- ❌ Confusing "aperture area" $A_a$ with "absorber area" $A_r$.
- ❌ Stating that $F_R$ depends on $T_i$ (it depends only on $\dot{m}$, $A_c$, $U_L$, $F'$).
- ❌ Confusing solar *thermal* with solar *PV*.

**Real-World Examples (for 2-mark sub-questions):**
- ✅ India's first CSP plant: **National Solar Thermal Power Facility, Jodhpur** (1 MW).
- ✅ India's largest CSP: **NTPC-Reliance, Rajasthan** (Phase-I 100 MW).
- ✅ World's largest CSP complex: **Noor-Ouarzazate, Morocco** (510 MW).
- ✅ World's largest solar tower: **DEWA CSP Tower, Dubai** (100 MW, 15 h storage).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> - For 3-mark questions: Definition + Equation + Significance (3 components).
> - For 7-mark sub-questions: Always include a *labeled block diagram* and *mention real-world applications*.
> - For 14-mark questions: State assumptions → derive → solve numerically → discuss results. Never skip a step in derivations.
> - Always end a numerical with **units and a physical interpretation** ("This 70% efficiency indicates a well-designed FPC suitable for domestic water heating").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation** (also called *Concentrated Solar Power, CSP*) is the process of converting solar radiation into electrical energy by first converting it into thermal (heat) energy and then using that heat to drive a conventional thermodynamic power cycle (Rankine, Brayton, or Stirling). Unlike photovoltaic (PV) systems that exploit the *photovoltaic effect* in semiconductors, CSP systems rely on optical concentration of sunlight onto a small receiver area to achieve the high working fluid temperatures (400 °C – 1500 °C) required for efficient conversion via a heat engine governed by the **Carnot principle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The student must be able to distinguish between *solar thermal* (heat-based) and *solar photovoltaic* (electron-based) conversion technologies, identify the four major CSP architectures — Parabolic Trough, Parabolic Dish, Solar Power Tower, and Linear Fresnel — and compute collector/gross efficiencies using the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Solar Constant (Iₛc) = 1367 W/m²** (Mean Earth–Sun distance, AM 1.5)
> - **Albedo of Earth's Surface ≈ 0.2 – 0.3**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann Constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
> - **Extraterrestrial Normal Irradiance on a clear day ≈ 1.0 kW/m² at noon**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine trying to light a campfire with a single match in bright sunlight — it fails because the *intensity per unit area* is too low. Now imagine using a **magnifying glass** to focus the entire sunbeam onto a single dry leaf — it instantly ignites. *That is the entire logic of solar thermal power.*

A regular flat surface collects sunlight at its natural density (~1000 W/m²). That temperature cap is too low (~100 °C) to drive a steam turbine efficiently. But when we use **mirrors or lenses** to concentrate that same sunlight by a factor of 30× to 2000×, the *flux density* on a small receiver shoots up to tens or hundreds of *kilowatts per square meter*, producing temperatures high enough to boil water, heat molten salt, or even heat air to glow.

So the "trick" of solar thermal electricity is:

> **Sun (broad) → Mirror/Lens (focus) → Hot Receiver (small & hot) → Heat Engine → Generator → Electricity**

The heat engine is a familiar, mature technology (the same turbines used in coal, gas, or nuclear plants) — only the *source of heat* is solar instead of fossil.

### 1.3 Why Solar Thermal Power?

| Attribute | Solar Thermal (CSP) | Solar PV |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Heat → Mechanical → Electrical | Photon → Electron → Electrical |
| Typical efficiency | 30–40 % (with storage) | 15–22 % |
| Thermal storage | Inherent (molten salt) | Requires batteries |
| Land use | Moderate–High | Low–Moderate |
| Capacity factor (with storage) | 40–70 % | 15–25 % (no storage) |
| Best suited for | Utility-scale dispatchable power | Distributed & rooftop |

> [!NOTE]
> The **dispatchability** of CSP — its ability to deliver power even when the sun is not shining, by using stored thermal energy — is the single most important engineering advantage that distinguishes it from solar PV in the renewable energy mix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Solar Geometry and Available Radiation

#### 2.1.1 Solar Declination (δ)
The angle between the line joining the centers of the Sun and Earth and the equatorial plane. It varies seasonally between **±23.45°**.

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)$$

where $n$ = day of the year (1 = Jan 1).

#### 2.1.2 Hour Angle (ω)
The angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation (15° per hour).

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

#### 2.1.3 Angle of Incidence (θᵢ)
The angle between the incoming sunbeam and the normal to the tilted collector surface.

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega$$

For a south-facing surface in the northern hemisphere ($\gamma = 0$):

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$$

where $\phi$ = latitude, $\beta$ = collector tilt angle.

#### 2.1.4 Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI)
The component of solar radiation received per unit area on a surface held *perpendicular to the Sun's rays*. This is the **only** component usable by concentrating collectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clearness Index (kₜ):**
> $$k_t = \frac{H}{H_0}$$
> where $H$ = monthly average daily global radiation on a horizontal surface, $H_0$ = extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface. A high $k_t$ (> 0.65) and a high **direct fraction** (> 0.7) are prerequisites for economic CSP plants.

### 2.2 Flat-Plate Collectors (Non-Concentrating, for Heating)

The **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** gives the *useful heat gain* per unit area of a flat plate collector:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) I_T - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_u$ | Useful heat gain (W) | — |
| $A_c$ | Collector aperture area (m²) | — |
| $F_R$ | Heat removal factor | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| $(\tau\alpha)$ | Effective transmittance–absorptance product | 0.7 – 0.85 |
| $I_T$ | Total solar irradiance on tilted surface (W/m²) | 0 – 1100 W/m² |
| $U_L$ | Overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K) | 3 – 8 W/m²·K |
| $T_{in}$ | Fluid inlet temperature (K) | — |
| $T_a$ | Ambient temperature (K) | — |

The **instantaneous efficiency** is:

$$\eta = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

This is a straight line when $\eta$ is plotted against $\dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{I_T}$ — its slope gives $F_R U_L$ and its intercept gives $F_R(\tau\alpha)$.

### 2.3 Concentrating Collectors (CSP Architectures)

| Type | Concentration Ratio (C) | Temp Range (°C) | Tracking |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parabolic Trough | 30 – 100 | 300 – 500 | Single-axis |
| Linear Fresnel | 25 – 100 | 250 – 400 | Single-axis |
| Parabolic Dish | 1000 – 2000 | 500 – 1500 | Two-axis |
| Solar Power Tower (Heliostat field) | 300 – 1500 | 500 – 1500 | Two-axis (mirrors) |

#### 2.3.1 Concentration Ratio (Geometric)
$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

The **maximum theoretical temperature** achievable for a perfect blackbody receiver is:

$$T_{\max} = T_a \cdot C^{1/4} \quad \text{(with losses included)}$$

A simpler practical cap is the **Stine–Geyer limit**:

$$T_{\max} \approx 0.85 \cdot \sqrt[4]{C} \cdot T_{\text{Sun,opt}}$$

For a dish with $C = 2000$, this yields receiver temperatures above **1500 K**, ideal for Stirling or Brayton engines.

#### 2.3.2 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)
- Reflector: parabolic cylinder, aluminum or silvered glass.
- Receiver: glass-enclosed, selectively-coated absorber tube (HCE — Heat Collection Element).
- Working fluid: synthetic oil (Therminol VP-1) up to **400 °C**, or molten salt (60% NaNO₃ / 40% KNO₃) up to **565 °C**.
- Optical efficiency: **η_opt = K·(τα)·(1 − bf)**, where $K$ = incidence angle modifier, $bf$ = blocking-and-shadowing factor.

#### 2.3.3 Solar Power Tower (Central Receiver)
- Field of thousands of **heliostats** (each a 2-axis tracked flat mirror) focuses sunlight on a single tower-mounted receiver.
- Receiver transports heat via molten salt, water/steam, or air.
- Highest storage capability; commercial plants: Ivanpah (USA), Noor III (Morocco, 150 MW, 7.5 h storage).

#### 2.3.4 Parabolic Dish
- Paraboloidal mirror concentrates sun on a focal-point receiver.
- Pairs naturally with a **Stirling engine** (high efficiency, 31.25 % record).
- Modular, no water cooling needed in dry regions.

#### 2.3.5 Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)
- Multiple long, flat, slightly-curved mirror strips focus sunlight onto a fixed overhead receiver tube.
- Cheaper than troughs but lower optical efficiency due to higher incidence angles.

### 2.4 Thermal Energy Storage (TES) — The CSP Differentiator

Three families of storage:

| Type | Medium | Storage Duration | Round-trip η |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible (two-tank molten salt) | 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃ | 4 – 15 h | ~ 93 % |
| Latent (PCM) | Salt hydrates, metals | 2 – 6 h | ~ 85 % |
| Thermochemical | Reversible reactions (e.g., CaO ⇌ Ca(OH)₂) | Days | ~ 80 % (lab) |

The **sensible heat storage** equation:

$$Q_{\text{stored}} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$$

For a 100 MWh plant with molten salt ($c_p$ ≈ 1.55 kJ/kg·K, $\Delta T$ ≈ 200 K):

$$m = \frac{Q}{c_p \Delta T} = \frac{100 \times 3.6 \times 10^6}{1550 \times 200} \approx 116{,}000 \text{ kg of salt}$$

### 2.5 The Power Conversion Cycle

For a **Rankine cycle** (used in trough and tower plants):

$$\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{condenser}}}{T_{\text{boiler}}}$$

Turbine shaft work feeds a synchronous generator:

$$P_{\text{electric}} = \eta_{\text{turb}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{to cycle}}$$

**Overall plant efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{plant}} = \eta_{\text{optical}} \times \eta_{\text{receiver}} \times \eta_{\text{cycle}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}}$$

A typical trough plant: $0.7 \times 0.85 \times 0.38 \times 0.96 \approx 22\,\%$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-Ready Formula Box (Save to Memory for KTU 2024 ESE):**

| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = 23.45\sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | Solar declination |
| 2 | $\omega = 15(t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$ | Hour angle (degrees) |
| 3 | $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ | Angle of incidence (south-facing) |
| 4 | $I_T = I_{b} \cos\theta_i + I_d\left(\frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}\right) + I\rho_g\left(\frac{1-\cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | Total radiation on tilted surface |
| 5 | $Q_u = A_c F_R\!\left[(\tau\alpha)I_T - U_L(T_{in} - T_a)\right]$ | Hottel-Whillier-Bliss |
| 6 | $\eta = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{I_T}$ | Flat-plate efficiency |
| 7 | $C = A_{\text{aperture}}/A_{\text{receiver}}$ | Geometric concentration ratio |
| 8 | $T_{\max} = 0.85\, C^{1/4}\, T_{\text{Sun}}$ | Max theoretical receiver temp |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{opt}} = K(\tau\alpha)(1-bf)$ | Parabolic-trough optical efficiency |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{stored}} = m c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat storage |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 1 - T_c/T_h$ | Carnot limit (Rankine) |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{plant}} = \eta_{\text{opt}}\eta_{\text{rec}}\eta_{\text{cycle}}\eta_{\text{gen}}$ | Overall efficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** All temperatures in the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation **must be in Kelvin (K)** when computing losses, but you can use °C only if the $(T_{in} - T_a)$ difference is taken in K (since a temperature *difference* is identical in K and °C). Mixing units is the most common KTU mark-loss error.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spain (Andasol 1, 2, 3)**: 50 MW parabolic-trough with **7.5 h** of molten-salt storage, supplying 350 GWh/year.
- **Morocco (Noor-Ouarzazate Complex)**: World's largest CSP plant cluster — 580 MW combined (trough + tower + PV hybrid).
- **India (JNNSM Phase-II Batch-IV)**: 2 GW CSP tender; 6 × 100 MW solar power towers with 4 h storage under development in Rajasthan.
- **USA (Ivanpah, 392 MW)**: Three solar power towers in the Mojave Desert, California.

> [!NOTE]
> CSP is best suited to **high-DNI regions** (> 2000 kWh/m²/year): Rajasthan, Thar, Sahara, Mojave, Atacama, Pilbara, and the Iberian plateau. Kerala has moderate DNI — suitable for *hybrid* systems with biomass back-up.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Temperature in a Solar Concentrator

**Starting principle (Energy balance on receiver):**

A blackbody cavity receiver of aperture $A_r$ absorbs solar flux and loses by radiation only. In steady state:

$$\text{Concentrated absorbed flux} = \text{Re-radiated thermal flux}$$

$$C \cdot I_{sc} \cdot A_r = \sigma \, A_r \, T_{\max}^4$$

where $C = A_{\text{aperture}}/A_{\text{receiver}}$ and the blackbody is assumed perfect.

**Step 1 — Simplify by dividing both sides by $A_r$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C \cdot I_{sc} &= \sigma \, T_{\max}^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $T_{\max}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\max}^4 &= \frac{C \cdot I_{sc}}{\sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Take the fourth root:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\max} &= \left(\frac{C \cdot I_{sc}}{\sigma}\right)^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute standard values** ($I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m², $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴):

For $C = 1$ (no concentration):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\max} &= \left(\frac{1 \times 1367}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}\right)^{1/4} = (2.41 \times 10^{10})^{1/4} \approx 394\,\text{K} \approx 121\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

For $C = 100$ (trough):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\max} &= \left(\frac{100 \times 1367}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}\right)^{1/4} = 100^{0.25} \times 394 \approx 3.16 \times 394 \approx 1245\,\text{K} \approx 972\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

For $C = 2000$ (dish):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\max} &= (2000)^{0.25} \times 394 \approx 6.69 \times 394 \approx 2636\,\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering reality check:**
Real receivers lose by *convection* as well, and selective coatings give $\alpha \approx 0.95$ but $\epsilon \approx 0.10$ at receiver temperature. A more realistic form:

$$T_{\max} = \left(\frac{C \cdot I_{sc} \cdot \alpha}{\sigma \cdot \epsilon + h_{\text{conv}}}\right)^{1/4}$$

where $h_{\text{conv}}$ is the convective loss coefficient in W/m²·K.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Useful Heat from a Flat-Plate Collector (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss)

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the absorber plate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gain} - \text{Losses} &= \text{Useful heat} \\
A_c (\tau\alpha) I_T - A_c U_L (T_p - T_a) &= \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})
\end{aligned}$$

where $T_p$ is the *plate* temperature, $U_L$ the *overall loss coefficient* (top + bottom + edge).

**Step 2 — Introduce the **collector efficiency factor** $F'$:**

The absorber plate is not isothermal — fins and tubes spread the heat unevenly. Define:

$$F' = \frac{\dfrac{1}{U_L}}{\dfrac{1}{U_L} + \dfrac{(W-D)}{(W \cdot h_f)}}$$

where $W$ = tube spacing, $D$ = tube outer diameter, $h_f$ = fin-to-tube heat-transfer coefficient.

This allows the plate temperature to be replaced by the *fluid* temperature:

$$T_p = T_f + \frac{Q_u/A_c}{F' U_L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T_p$ into the energy balance and solve:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= A_c F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) I_T - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

where $F_R$ is the **heat removal factor** that combines $F'$ and the fluid-side heat-transfer coefficient $h_{fi}$:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit $\dot{m} \to \infty$ (quasi-static test):**

$F_R \to F'$ — the standard ASHRAE test condition.

**Step 5 — Define instantaneous efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_i &= \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} \\
       &= F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \cdot \frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **linear efficiency curve** plotted on the $X$-axis as $\frac{T_{in} - T_a}{I_T}$ — exactly the form every KTU numerical problem expects.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A flat-plate solar collector has aperture area $5\,\text{m}^2$, $F_R(\tau\alpha) = 0.72$, and $F_R U_L = 5.5\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. The ambient temperature is $30\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the water inlet temperature is $60\,^\circ\text{C}$. Solar irradiance on the tilted surface is $850\,\text{W/m}^2$. Find:
(a) The useful heat gain $Q_u$
(b) The instantaneous collection efficiency $\eta_i$
(c) The mass flow rate of water if outlet temperature must be $80\,^\circ\text{C}$

**Solution (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= A_c F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) I_T - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right] \\
    &= 5 \times 0.72 \left[\frac{0.72}{0.72 \times ????}\text{...stop}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

*Correction — use the clean form:*

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= A_c \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_T - F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right] \\
    &= 5 \left[ 0.72 \times 850 - 5.5 \times (60 - 30) \right] \\
    &= 5 \left[ 612 - 165 \right] \\
    &= 5 \times 447 \\
    &= 2235\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_i &= \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} = \frac{2235}{5 \times 850} = \frac{2235}{4250} = 0.526 = 52.6\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution (c):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) &= Q_u \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{Q_u}{c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})} = \frac{2235}{4186 \times (80 - 60)} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{2235}{4186 \times 20} = \frac{2235}{83720} = 0.0267\,\text{kg/s} \approx 96\,\text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of CSP Technologies

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Solar Tower | Parabolic Dish | Linear Fresnel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Concentration ratio | 30 – 100 | 300 – 1500 | 1000 – 2000 | 25 – 100 |
| Operating temp (°C) | 300 – 500 | 500 – 1500 | 500 – 1500 | 250 – 400 |
| Annual solar-to-electric η | 14 – 18 % | 18 – 25 % | 25 – 31 % | 9 – 13 % |
| Storage compatible | Yes (molten salt) | Yes (best) | Limited (battery) | Limited |
| Tracking | 1-axis | 2-axis (heliostat) | 2-axis | 1-axis |
| Land use (m²/MWh/yr) | 6 – 8 | 4 – 6 | 8 – 12 | 5 – 7 |
| Commercial scale | 50 – 250 MW | 10 – 200 MW | 5 – 50 kW modules | 30 – 150 MW |
| Record efficiency | 21 % (Solar Genix) | 25 % (Gemasolar) | 31.25 % (SES Dish) | 13 % (AREVA) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Solar Power Tower** dominates when *storage* and *capacity factor* are priorities. The **Parabolic Dish** dominates when *peak efficiency* is the goal. The **Trough** dominates when *commercial maturity* and *risk-mitigation* are required. These trade-offs appear routinely in KTU 14-mark design questions.

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic CSP Power Plant Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun((Sun)) -- DNI --> Field[Solar Field: Mirrors / Heliostats]
    Field -- Concentrated Beam --> Receiver[Central Receiver / HCE]
    Receiver -- Hot Fluid --> HotStorage[Hot Storage Tank]
    HotStorage -- Hot Salt or Steam --> HX[Heat Exchanger / Steam Generator]
    Receiver -- Direct Path --> HX
    HX -- High-P Steam --> Turbine[Steam Turbine]
    HotStorage -- Bypass Flow --> Turbine
    Turbine -- Shaft Work --> Generator[Synchronous Generator]
    Generator -- Electrical Output --> Grid((HV Grid Step-Up))
    Cond[Condenser] -- LP Steam --> Turbine
    Pump[Feedwater Pump] -- Liquid Water --> HX
    ColdStorage[Cold Salt Storage Tank] -- Salt Return --> HotStorage
    Receiver -. Heat Loss .-> Atmos[Ambient Sky]
    Cond -. Heat Rejection .-> Cooling[Cooling Tower / Air-Cooled Condenser]

    style Grid fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> The **energy cascade** above shows that of every 100 units of solar energy reaching the heliostat field, only **20–25 units** are converted to grid-grade electricity. The biggest losses are *optical* (5–8 %) and *receiver* (10–15 %). Improving these is the focus of next-generation CSP research (e.g., supercritical CO₂ cycles, particle receivers, gas-phase receivers at 700 °C+).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define solar constant. What is its standard value and what assumptions are used in its definition?*

**Model Answer:**
The **solar constant** is defined as the rate at which solar radiant energy is received per unit area by a surface placed **perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere**, at the **mean Earth–Sun distance** of $1\,\text{AU} = 1.496 \times 10^{11}\,\text{m}$, with the Sun at its mean activity.
**Standard value = 1367 W/m²** (ISO 9845-1, AM 1.5 spectrum).
**Assumptions:**
1. Sun emits as a **blackbody at 5778 K**.
2. No atmospheric attenuation.
3. The receiving surface is **normal to the line of sight** to the Sun.
4. The Earth–Sun distance is fixed at the mean of $1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*Differentiate between flat-plate collectors and concentrating collectors. List two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Flat-Plate | Concentrating |
|---|---|---|
| Tracking | Fixed (or seasonal tilt) | 1-axis or 2-axis |
| Concentration ratio | 1 (no concentration) | 25 – 2000 |
| Operating temperature | Up to ~ 100 °C | 300 – 1500 °C |
| Absorbs diffuse radiation | Yes | No (uses DNI only) |
| Applications | Water heating, space heating | Electricity generation |
| Examples | Standard liquid & air heater | Parabolic Trough, Dish, Fresnel, Solar Tower |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** for the useful heat gain of a flat-plate solar collector. Clearly define the **heat removal factor $F_R$** and the **overall heat loss coefficient $U_L$**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A flat-plate solar water heater has the following specifications:
- Aperture area = 3 m²
- $F_R (\tau\alpha) = 0.74$
- $F_R U_L = 6.0\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- Solar irradiance on the tilted surface = 920 W/m²
- Ambient temperature = 28 °C
- Water inlet temperature = 55 °C

Calculate:
1. The useful heat gain $Q_u$ (W)
2. The instantaneous collection efficiency
3. The exit water temperature if mass flow rate is 0.012 kg/s ($c_p$ of water = 4186 J/kg·K) (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:**

**Step 1** — Energy balance on the absorber plate (in steady state):

$$A_c (\tau\alpha) I_T = A_c U_L (T_p - T_a) + Q_u$$

where $T_p$ is the *plate* (absorber) temperature and $Q_u$ is the useful heat.

**Step 2** — Recognize that the plate is *not* uniform in temperature. Use the **fin-and-tube** analysis to relate $T_p$ to the fluid bulk temperature $T_f$:

$$Q_u = A_c F' \left[ U_L (T_p - T_a) \right] - \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Rearranging:

$$T_p = T_f + \frac{Q_u / A_c}{F' U_L}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute $T_p$ into Step 1 and solve for $Q_u$:

$$A_c (\tau\alpha) I_T = A_c U_L \left( T_f + \frac{Q_u / A_c}{F' U_L} - T_a \right) + Q_u$$

$$A_c (\tau\alpha) I_T - A_c U_L (T_f - T_a) = Q_u \left( 1 + \frac{1}{F'} \right)$$

Multiplying both sides by $F' / (1 + F')$ and using $F_R = F' / (1 + F' - F' U_L/\dot{m} c_p)$:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) I_T - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]}$$

This is the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation**.

- $F_R$ = heat removal factor (ratio of actual useful heat to that which would be obtained if the absorber were at the fluid inlet temperature).
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient from plate to ambient (top + bottom + edge), in $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**1) Useful heat gain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= A_c \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_T - F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right] \\
    &= 3 \left[ 0.74 \times 920 - 6.0 \times (55 - 28) \right] \\
    &= 3 \left[ 680.8 - 162 \right] \\
    &= 3 \times 518.8 = 1556.4\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**2) Instantaneous efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_i &= \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} = \frac{1556.4}{3 \times 920} = \frac{1556.4}{2760} = 0.564 = 56.4\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**3) Exit temperature:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) \\
1556.4 &= 0.012 \times 4186 \times (T_{out} - 55) \\
1556.4 &= 50.232 \times (T_{out} - 55) \\
T_{out} - 55 &= 30.99 \\
T_{out} &= 85.99\,^\circ\text{C} \approx 86\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, adapted]`

**(a)** With neat sketches (functional block diagrams), explain the working principle of **Solar Power Tower** and **Parabolic Trough** systems. Compare their concentration ratios, working temperatures, and storage compatibility. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 10 MW solar power plant uses a **parabolic trough** field with:
- Optical efficiency = 75 %
- Receiver thermal efficiency = 82 %
- Rankine-cycle efficiency = 36 %
- Generator efficiency = 96 %

Find:
1. The plant's overall efficiency.
2. The heliostat field area required if mean DNI = 800 W/m² and the plant runs for 6 hours of equivalent full-load operation.
3. Comment on the choice of a solar **tower** vs **trough** for adding 4 hours of storage to this plant. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Working Principles (see diagrams in Section 4 for reference):**

**Solar Power Tower:**
- An array of 2-axis tracked flat mirrors (**heliostats**) reflects sunlight onto a receiver mounted on top of a tall tower.
- Working fluid (molten salt, water, or air) is heated to 500–1500 °C.
- Heat is exchanged to a steam cycle or used directly to drive a turbine.
- High concentration ratio (300–1500) due to the geometric field layout.

**Parabolic Trough:**
- A cylindrical parabolic mirror reflects and focuses parallel sun rays onto a linear absorber tube running along the focal line.
- The tube carries Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF) — typically synthetic oil or molten salt.
- Single-axis tracking (north–south axis).
- Concentration ratio: 30 – 100.

| Feature | Solar Tower | Parabolic Trough |
|---|---|---|
| Concentration ratio | 300 – 1500 | 30 – 100 |
| Working temperature | 500 – 1500 °C | 300 – 500 °C |
| Storage compatibility | Excellent (2-tank molten salt) | Good (sensible/PCM) |
| Tracking | 2-axis (each heliostat) | 1-axis (whole field) |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**1) Plant overall efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{plant}} &= \eta_{\text{opt}} \times \eta_{\text{rec}} \times \eta_{\text{cycle}} \times \eta_{\text{gen}} \\
                    &= 0.75 \times 0.82 \times 0.36 \times 0.96 \\
                    &= 0.212 = 21.2\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**2) Required heliostat / mirror area:**

The energy required for 6 full-load hours at 10 MW:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= P \times t = 10 \times 10^6 \times 6 \times 3600 = 2.16 \times 10^{11}\,\text{J} = 60{,}000\,\text{kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

The incident solar energy needed:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{incident}} &= \frac{E}{\eta_{\text{plant}}} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{11}}{0.212} = 1.019 \times 10^{12}\,\text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

With mean DNI = 800 W/m², the equivalent hours of full-sun needed:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{sun}} &= \frac{E_{\text{incident}}}{\text{DNI} \times A} = \frac{E}{(\text{DNI}) \times A \times \eta_{\text{plant}}}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $A$ directly using annual approach:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{P \times 6\,\text{h (effective)}}{(\text{DNI}) \times \eta_{\text{plant}} \times 6\,\text{h (effective) per day}} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{800 \times 0.212} \\
A &= \frac{10 \times 10^6}{169.6} = 58{,}962\,\text{m}^2 \approx 5.9\,\text{ha}
\end{aligned}$$

**3) Tower vs Trough for 4-hour storage:**

**Recommendation: Solar Power Tower.**

Justification:
- Tower systems natively use **molten salt** as both HTF and storage medium (single-fluid two-tank concept), giving **up to 15 h** of storage.
- Trough systems need an **HTF-to-salt heat exchanger** for storage, which adds parasitic loss and capital cost.
- The 565 °C salt temperatures achievable in a tower translate to a **higher Rankine efficiency** (closer to the Carnot limit) and a **smaller** thermal storage tank for the same MWh.
- For 4 hours of 10 MW storage (40 MWh thermal), the tower's higher operating temperature reduces tank volume by a factor of $\sim (565/400) = 1.4$ compared to a trough with synthetic-oil HTF.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Triggers (Module 1 Solar Thermal):**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing °C and K in $F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a)$. Always use a *temperature difference* (numerically equal in K and °C), but state the unit as K for the coefficient.
> 2. **Wrong angle formula:** Using the general $\cos\theta_i$ for a horizontal surface. For a horizontal surface, $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ (the zenith angle formula), not the tilt-angle formula.
> 3. **Forgetting the incidence angle modifier $K$** when the sun is not normal to the aperture — it can reduce $\tau\alpha$ by 5–15 %.
> 4. **Carnot vs Rankine:** Do not write $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 1 - T_c/T_h$ and call it Rankine efficiency. That's the Carnot limit. The *actual* Rankine cycle efficiency is lower due to turbine isentropic losses, pump work, and condensation temperature.
> 5. **Trough vs Tower confusion:** Students often interchange HTF (synthetic oil used in troughs) and TES fluid (molten salt used in towers). A trough plant can use salt as TES via a heat exchanger; a tower can use salt as both HTF and TES.
> 6. **DNI vs GHI:** CSP uses **DNI** (Direct Normal Irradiance). PV uses **GHI** (Global Horizontal Irradiance). Always state which one your $I$ refers to.
> 7. **Concentration ratio — geometric vs optical:** $C_{\text{optical}} = C_{\text{geom}} \times \eta_{\text{opt}}$. If asked for "the maximum temperature" use $C_{\text{geom}}$; if asked for "the effective flux on receiver" use $C_{\text{optical}}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Solar constant** $= 1367\,\text{W/m}^2$ at 1 AU; varies $\pm 3.3\%$ with $1/r^2$ over the year.
- **Declination** $\delta$ ranges $\pm 23.45°$ (zero at equinoxes).
- **Hour angle** $\omega = 15° \times$ (solar hours from noon).
- **Cosine of angle of incidence** depends on $\phi$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, $\delta$, $\omega$.
- **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss** is the *master equation* for flat-plate collector useful heat.
- **$F_R(\tau\alpha)$** is the y-intercept of the efficiency curve; **$-F_R U_L$** is the slope.
- **Concentration ratio** is the aperture area ÷ receiver area.
- **Max temperature** in a concentrator $\propto C^{1/4}$ (4th-root law of radiation).
- **Parabolic Trough:** single-axis, 30–100×, 300–500 °C, mature commercial.
- **Solar Power Tower:** 2-axis heliostats, 300–1500×, 500–1500 °C, best for storage.
- **Parabolic Dish:** 2-axis, 1000–2000×, 500–1500 °C, pairs with Stirling engine (record 31.25 %).
- **Linear Fresnel:** cheap, single-axis, 25–100×, 250–400 °C.
- **Thermal Energy Storage** types: Sensible (molten salt, $Q = mc_p \Delta T$), Latent (PCM, $Q = mL$), Thermochemical (reversible reactions).
- **Plant efficiency** = product of optical × receiver × cycle × generator efficiencies.
- **Carnot efficiency** sets the upper bound; real Rankine cycle is ~ 35–40 % of the Carnot value.
- **DNI** (Direct Normal) drives CSP; **GHI** drives PV.
- **Capacity factor** of a CSP plant with storage can reach **40–70 %**, comparable to coal.
- **India targets:** 100 GW solar by 2030, with significant CSP under the National Solar Mission (JNNSM).
- **Greenest CSP plants:** Noor III (Morocco) — 24-hour solar electricity achieved using salt storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Sunlight → Mirror → Receiver → Hot Fluid → Turbine → Generator → Grid.* Memorize this chain — it is the single most common 3-mark definition and 14-mark question template across all CSP architectures.
