topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Energy Storage - Sensible Heat Storage, Latent Heat Storage, Thermochemical Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermal Energy Storage (TES) — Formal Definition

**Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** is the temporary storage of thermal energy (heat or cold) in a storage medium for later use in energy generation, heating, cooling, or industrial processes. It is a critical enabling technology for renewable energy systems, allowing the temporal decoupling of energy supply and demand. According to the KTU 2024 OEMET614 syllabus, TES is broadly classified into three principal mechanisms based on the form in which energy is stored:

1. **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)** — Energy stored by raising the temperature of a material.
2. **Latent Heat Storage (LHS)** — Energy stored during a phase change (solid–liquid, liquid–gas, or solid–solid) at constant or near-constant temperature.
3. **Thermochemical Storage (TCS)** — Energy stored via reversible endothermic/exothermic chemical reactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** Students must be able to distinguish the three storage mechanisms, calculate energy storage capacities using governing equations, and identify suitable storage media for given temperature ranges (typically **0 °C – 1000 °C** for solar, district heating, and industrial process heat applications).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermal Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of a TES system as a **rechargeable battery — but instead of storing electricity, it stores heat.**
> - **Sensible Heat Storage** = a *hot water bottle*. You can feel the temperature change (it gets warmer or cooler). The water's *sensible* (perceivable) temperature rises as it absorbs energy.
> - **Latent Heat Storage** = an *ice cube in a thermos*. As the ice melts, its temperature stays at **0 °C**, but it absorbs a huge amount of "hidden" (latent) energy. You cannot sense the change until melting is complete.
> - **Thermochemical Storage** = a *reversible chemical hand warmer*. A chemical reaction releases heat on demand, and the products can be regenerated by adding energy back. It is the densest and longest-lasting form of storage.

---

## 1.3 Why TES Matters for Renewable Energy

Renewable sources (solar, wind) are **intermittent** and **diffuse**. TES solves the **temporal mismatch** between supply and demand.

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Energy density (SHS — water, ΔT = 50 K) | **~210 kJ/kg** (~58 Wh/kg) |
| Energy density (LHS — paraffin PCM) | **~200–250 kJ/kg** (~70 Wh/kg) |
| Energy density (TCS — CaO/Ca(OH)₂) | **~1400–1600 kJ/kg** (~440 Wh/kg) |
| Round-trip efficiency (well-designed SHS) | **50–90 %** |
| Storage duration (SHS vs TCS) | Hours–days vs. **months–seasonal** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Thermochemical storage offers **5–10× higher energy density** than sensible heat storage and can store energy indefinitely with negligible losses — making it the only viable option for **seasonal solar energy storage** (summer → winter).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)

### 2.1.1 Principle

In SHS, thermal energy is stored by raising the **sensible temperature** of a solid or liquid storage medium. The storage capacity is directly proportional to the **mass (m)**, the **specific heat capacity (cₚ)**, and the **temperature change (ΔT)**.

### 2.1.2 Classification of SHS Media

- **Liquid media:** Water, thermal oils, molten salts (e.g., solar salt — 60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃).
- **Solid media:** Rocks, gravel, sand, concrete, cast iron, ceramics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Criteria for SHS Media:**
> 1. High specific heat capacity (cₚ)
> 2. Wide operating temperature range
> 3. High thermal conductivity (for rapid charge/discharge)
> 4. Chemical stability and non-toxicity
> 5. Low cost and abundant availability
> 6. Low vapor pressure at operating temperature

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Storage in SHS

- **Step 1:** Heat transfer fluid (HTF) delivers thermal energy from the source (e.g., solar collector) to the storage tank.
- **Step 2:** The storage medium (e.g., water) absorbs this energy and its temperature rises.
- **Step 3:** When energy is needed, the hot medium releases heat to the load (e.g., building heating loop).
- **Step 4:** Energy extraction continues until the medium cools to a minimum usable temperature.
- **Step 5:** The "stratification effect" (in water tanks) is exploited to maximize ΔT and store more energy in the same volume.

---

## 2.2 Latent Heat Storage (LHS)

### 2.2.1 Principle

In LHS, energy is absorbed or released during a **phase change** (typically solid ↔ liquid). The temperature remains nearly constant during the phase transition, while the latent heat of fusion (h_sf) is exchanged. The storage medium is called a **Phase Change Material (PCM)**.

### 2.2.2 Classification of PCMs

- **Organic PCMs:** Paraffins (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂), fatty acids, sugar alcohols. Low thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K), chemically stable, no supercooling, non-corrosive.
- **Inorganic PCMs:** Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt — Na₂SO₄·10H₂O), metallic alloys. Higher energy density, but suffer from supercooling and phase segregation.
- **Eutectic PCMs:** Mixtures of two or more PCMs designed to have a sharp, well-defined melting point.

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Storage in LHS

- **Step 1:** The PCM (initially solid) is heated to its **melting temperature (Tₘ)**.
- **Step 2:** Further energy input causes the PCM to melt, absorbing **latent heat of fusion (h_sf)** at (ideally) constant Tₘ.
- **Step 3:** Once fully melted, additional energy raises the PCM's sensible temperature (this is the "sensible regime" after LHS).
- **Step 4:** During discharge, the PCM releases the stored latent heat as it solidifies.
- **Step 5:** Heat exchangers must overcome the inherently **low thermal conductivity of PCMs** by using finned tubes, metal matrices, or nano-enhanced PCMs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the **latent heat of fusion (h_sf)** with **specific heat capacity (cₚ)**. Latent heat is energy absorbed/released *during* a phase change, while cₚ governs energy required for a *temperature change*. They have different units (J/kg vs. J/kg·K) and must not be added directly without proper treatment.

---

## 2.3 Thermochemical Storage (TCS)

### 2.3.1 Principle

In TCS, thermal energy drives an **endothermic reversible reaction**, and the products are stored separately. When heat is needed, the reverse **exothermic reaction** is triggered, releasing the stored chemical energy.

### 2.3.2 Common TCS Reaction Systems

- **Metal oxide systems:** e.g., CaO + Heat ⇌ CaO + ½ O₂ (non-stoichiometric redox)
- **Hydration/dehydration systems:** e.g., CaO + H₂O ⇌ Ca(OH)₂ + Heat
- **Carbonation/decarbonation:** e.g., CaCO₃ ⇌ CaO + CO₂
- **Ammonia synthesis/decomposition:** e.g., NH₃ ⇌ ½ N₂ + 3/2 H₂
- **Methanation:** CO₂ + 4 H₂ ⇌ CH₄ + 2 H₂O

### 2.3.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Storage in TCS

- **Step 1 (Charging):** Heat from the renewable source drives the endothermic forward reaction in a **reactor**. Solid and gaseous products are separated.
- **Step 2 (Storage):** The solid product (e.g., CaO) and gas (e.g., CO₂) are stored in insulated tanks — **losses are negligible for indefinite durations**.
- **Step 3 (Discharging):** The stored products are recombined in a separate reactor, releasing heat via the exothermic reverse reaction.
- **Step 4 (Heat Extraction):** The released heat is transferred via a heat exchanger to the demand side (e.g., district heating, power cycle).
- **Step 5 (Cycling):** The cycle repeats — making TCS a **closed-loop, long-duration storage** technology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** TCS is the only TES technology capable of **seasonal storage** with theoretical energy densities of **500–1000+ kWh/m³** (compared to ~30 kWh/m³ for water-based SHS and ~100 kWh/m³ for PCM-based LHS).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible heat stored | $Q_S = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J (or kWh) | m = mass, cₚ = specific heat, ΔT = temperature rise |
| 2 | Volumetric sensible heat | $Q_S = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J | V = volume, ρ = density |
| 3 | Latent heat stored (PCM) | $Q_L = m \cdot h_{sf}$ | J | h_sf = specific latent heat of fusion |
| 4 | Total LHS + post-melt sensible | $Q_{total} = m \left[ h_{sf} + c_p \cdot (T_{max} - T_m) \right]$ | J | Includes sensible regime above Tₘ |
| 5 | Thermochemical heat stored | $Q_{TC} = n \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$ | J | n = moles, ΔH_rxn = molar reaction enthalpy |
| 6 | Energy density (mass) | $E_m = Q / m$ | J/kg | Compare media |
| 7 | Energy density (volume) | $E_v = Q / V$ | J/m³ | Important for compact systems |
| 8 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = Q_{out} / Q_{in}$ | dimensionless | Always < 1 due to losses |
| 9 | Charging time | $t_c = Q_S / (\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{HTF})$ | s | For a given HTF mass flow rate |
| 10 | Heat loss (SHS tank, lumped) | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{avg} - T_{amb}) \cdot t$ | J | U = overall heat loss coefficient |
| 11 | Strouhal / Richardson numbers (tank stratification) | $Ri = g \beta \Delta T H / U^2$ | dimensionless | g = gravity, β = thermal expansion coeff. |
| 12 | PCM melting fraction (Stefan problem, ideal) | $f_m = E / (m \cdot h_{sf})$ | dimensionless | 0 ≤ f_m ≤ 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** For mixed SHS + LHS problems, students must **add the sensible and latent contributions separately**. The total energy in a PCM is:
> $$Q_{PCM,total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_{initial}) + m \cdot h_{sf} + m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{final} - T_m)$$
> Do not confuse cₚ values for solid and liquid phases — they are different.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants:** Molten salt SHS enables 24-hour electricity generation. (Andasol, Spain — 28 hours of storage.)
- **District heating networks:** Borehole thermal energy storage (BTES) and water tank SHS.
- **Building energy management:** PCM-enhanced walls and ceilings for passive thermal regulation.
- **Waste heat recovery in industry:** TCS (e.g., CaO/Ca(OH)₂) recovers and stores high-temperature waste heat from steel and cement plants.
- **Hydrogen economy:** Solar thermochemical cycles (ZnO/Zn, CeO₂/CeO₂-δ) produce solar fuels.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sensible Heat Storage Capacity (Single Medium, No Phase Change)

**Starting point:** Consider a storage medium of mass $m$ (kg) at an initial temperature $T_i$ (K). A quantity of heat $Q$ (J) is added, raising its temperature to $T_f$ (K).

By the definition of specific heat capacity at constant pressure $c_p$ (J/kg·K):

$$
c_p = \frac{Q}{m \cdot \Delta T}
$$

Rearranging for the heat stored:

$$
Q_S = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_i)
$$

This is the **fundamental SHS equation**. Now extending to a tank of volume $V$ and density $\rho$:

$$
m = \rho \cdot V
$$

Substituting into the SHS equation:

$$
Q_S = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_i)
$$

This volumetric form is critical when comparing storage media on a per-volume basis (e.g., water vs. rock bed).

### 3.1.1 Energy Density (Mass Basis)

$$
E_m = \frac{Q_S}{m} = c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

### 3.1.2 Energy Density (Volume Basis)

$$
E_v = \frac{Q_S}{V} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The product $\rho \cdot c_p$ is called the **volumetric heat capacity** and is the true figure of merit for SHS media. Water has $\rho c_p \approx 4.18$ MJ/(m³·K), molten salt $\approx 2.8$ MJ/(m³·K), and packed rock $\approx 2.0$ MJ/(m³·K). For a 50 K ΔT, water stores ~209 MJ/m³, while rock stores ~100 MJ/m³ — water wins, but only below 100 °C.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Latent Heat Storage Capacity (PCM, Ideal)

Consider a PCM of mass $m$ (kg) undergoing solid–liquid phase change at melting temperature $T_m$ (K). The latent heat absorbed during melting is $Q_L$ (J).

By the definition of specific latent heat of fusion $h_{sf}$ (J/kg):

$$
h_{sf} = \frac{Q_L}{m}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
Q_L = m \cdot h_{sf}
$$

### 3.2.1 Total Energy in a PCM (Sensible + Latent + Post-Melt Sensible)

If a PCM initially at $T_i < T_m$ is heated to a final temperature $T_f > T_m$, the total energy absorbed is the sum of three contributions:

**Stage 1: Sensible heating of solid (from $T_i$ to $T_m$):**

$$
Q_1 = m \cdot c_{p,solid} \cdot (T_m - T_i)
$$

**Stage 2: Phase change at $T_m$ (latent):**

$$
Q_2 = m \cdot h_{sf}
$$

**Stage 3: Sensible heating of liquid (from $T_m$ to $T_f$):**

$$
Q_3 = m \cdot c_{p,liquid} \cdot (T_f - T_m)
$$

**Total:**

$$
Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{p,solid} \cdot (T_m - T_i) + h_{sf} + c_{p,liquid} \cdot (T_f - T_m) \right]
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermochemical Storage Energy Density

Consider a reversible reaction:

$$
\text{AB (s)} + \text{Heat} \rightleftharpoons \text{A (s)} + \text{B (g)}
$$

The enthalpy of reaction is $\Delta H_{rxn}$ (J/mol). For $n$ moles of reactant converted:

$$
Q_{TC} = n \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}
$$

### 3.3.1 Molar Mass-Based Energy Density

For a molar mass $M$ (kg/mol) of the reactant:

$$
E_m = \frac{Q_{TC}}{n \cdot M} = \frac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{M}
$$

### 3.3.2 Example: Ca(OH)₂ Dehydration

$$
\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \text{(s)} \rightarrow \text{CaO (s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O (g)}, \quad \Delta H_{rxn} \approx +109 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

Molar mass of Ca(OH)₂: $M = 74.09 \text{ g/mol} = 0.07409 \text{ kg/mol}$.

$$
E_m = \frac{109{,}000 \text{ J/mol}}{0.07409 \text{ kg/mol}} = 1{,}471{,}319 \text{ J/kg} \approx 1471 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

In Wh/kg:

$$
E_m = \frac{1471 \text{ kJ/kg}}{3.6 \text{ kJ/Wh}} \approx 409 \text{ Wh/kg}
$$

This is **~7× higher** than water SHS and **~6× higher** than paraffin PCM, demonstrating the superior energy density of TCS.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of TES Technologies

| Property | Sensible (Water) | Sensible (Molten Salt) | Latent (Paraffin PCM) | Thermochemical (Ca(OH)₂) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Storage temperature (°C) | 5 – 95 | 220 – 565 | 20 – 80 (varies) | 500 – 600 |
| $c_p$ or $h_{sf}$ (J/kg·K or J/kg) | 4186 | 1520 | 200,000 (latent) | 1,471,000 (ΔH) |
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 1000 | 1870 | ~850 | ~2200 (Ca(OH)₂) |
| $\rho c_p$ or $\rho \cdot h_{sf}/V$ (MJ/m³) | 4.18 (× 50 K = 209) | 2.84 (× 200 K = 568) | ~170 | ~3200 |
| Round-trip efficiency | 50 – 80 % | 60 – 90 % | 75 – 90 % | 70 – 95 % |
| Storage duration | Hours – days | Hours – days | Hours – days | **Months – seasonal** |
| Cost (USD/kWh_th) | 1 – 5 | 5 – 15 | 10 – 50 | 20 – 100 |
| Maturity | Commercial | Commercial | Pilot / niche | R&D / demo |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy (KTU 2024):** When asked to "compare" TES methods in a 14-mark question, structure your answer in this exact table format with **quantitative values** for energy density, efficiency, and storage duration. Examiners award marks for **numerical justification**, not just qualitative statements.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A domestic solar water heating system uses a **water tank SHS** of volume **300 L**. The tank is heated from **25 °C to 75 °C**. Calculate:
> (a) The mass of water.
> (b) The sensible heat stored.
> (c) The energy stored in kWh.

**Given:** $V = 300$ L = 0.3 m³, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $c_{p,water} = 4186$ J/(kg·K), $T_i = 25$ °C, $T_f = 75$ °C.

### Solution

**(a) Mass of water:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.3 \text{ m}^3 = 300 \text{ kg}
$$

**(b) Sensible heat stored:**

$$
Q_S = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 300 \text{ kg} \times 4186 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times (75 - 25) \text{ K}
$$

Stepwise:

$$
Q_S = 300 \times 4186 \times 50
$$

$$
= 300 \times 209{,}300
$$

$$
= 62{,}790{,}000 \text{ J} = 62.79 \text{ MJ}
$$

**(c) Energy stored in kWh:**

$$
E = \frac{Q_S}{3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kWh}} = \frac{62.79 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 17.44 \text{ kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 300 L water tank storing 50 K of sensible heat can supply ~17.4 kWh of thermal energy — enough to heat ~170 L of water for a shower or run a 1 kW space heater for ~17 hours. This is the engineering reality of residential SHS.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification of Thermal Energy Storage

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Thermal Energy Storage TES"] --> B["Sensible Heat Storage SHS"]
    A --> C["Latent Heat Storage LHS"]
    A --> D["Thermochemical Storage TCS"]

    B --> B1["Liquid Media Water Molten Salt Thermal Oil"]
    B --> B2["Solid Media Rock Bed Sand Concrete Ceramics"]

    C --> C1["Organic PCM Paraffins Fatty Acids"]
    C --> C2["Inorganic PCM Salt Hydrates Metallic Alloys"]
    C --> C3["Eutectic PCM Engineered Mixtures"]

    D --> D1["Reversible Chemical Reactions CaO CaOH2 CaCO3"]
    D --> D2["Sorption Systems Water Vapor Zeolites Silica Gel"]
    D --> D3["Solar Fuel Cycles ZnO Zn CeO2 CeO2delta"]

    B1 --> B1a["Low Temperature 0 to 100 C"]
    B2 --> B2a["High Temperature up to 1000 C"]

    C1 --> C1a["Building HVAC Textile Applications"]
    C2 --> C2a["Cold Chain Electronics Cooling"]
    C3 --> C3a["Specialized Industrial Use"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TES System

```mermaid
graph LR
    RE["Renewable Energy Source Solar Wind Biomass"] --> HTF1["Heat Transfer Fluid Hot Loop"]
    HTF1 --> RC["Reactor or Storage Tank Charging"]
    RC --> STG["Storage Medium Solid Liquid Gas Phase"]

    STG --> SC["Stratification Layer Hot Region"]
    STG --> SC2["Stratification Layer Cold Region"]

    RC --> STG
    SC --> HX["Heat Exchanger Discharge"]
    SC2 --> HX

    HX --> HTF2["Heat Transfer Fluid Cold Loop"]
    HTF2 --> LD["Demand Side Heating Cooling Power"]

    RC -.-> LOSS["Heat Losses to Ambient U x A x dT"]
    SC -.-> LOSS
    SC2 -.-> LOSS

    CTRL["Control System Sensors and Valves"] -.-> RC
    CTRL -.-> HX
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Thermochemical Storage Cycle (CaO / Ca(OH)₂)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Charging Phase Endothermic"] --> RX1["CaOH2 SOLID plus HEAT 530 C"]
    RX1 --> PROD1["CaO SOLID plus H2O VAPOR"]
    PROD1 --> ST1["Storage Tank A CaO SOLID"]
    PROD1 --> ST2["Storage Tank B H2O VAPOR"]

    ST1 --> RX2["Discharging Phase Exothermic"]
    ST2 --> RX2
    RX2 --> PROD2["CaOH2 SOLID plus HEAT 100 C"]
    PROD2 --> OUT["Useful Heat to District Heating or Power Cycle"]

    ST1 -.-> ST1A["Long Duration Seasonal Storage"]
    ST2 -.-> ST2A["Long Duration Seasonal Storage"]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: PCM Phase Change Process (Solid ↔ Liquid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Solid PCM at T less than Tm"] --> P2["Sensible Heating of Solid T rises to Tm"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase Change Solid to Liquid at Tm"]
    P3 --> P4["Sensible Heating of Liquid T rises above Tm"]
    P4 --> P5["Discharge Liquid PCM at T greater than Tm"]
    P5 --> P6["Sensible Cooling of Liquid T drops to Tm"]
    P6 --> P7["Phase Change Liquid to Solid at Tm releases hsf"]
    P7 --> P8["Sensible Cooling of Solid T drops below Tm"]
    P8 --> P1

    P2 -.-> Q1["Energy In m x cp solid x dT"]
    P3 -.-> Q2["Energy In m x hsf LATENT"]
    P4 -.-> Q3["Energy In m x cp liquid x dT"]
    P5 -.-> Q4["Heat Exchanger Transfers to Load"]
    P7 -.-> Q5["Energy Out m x hsf LATENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The arrows Q1 through Q5 represent energy flow at each stage. The constant-temperature plateau (P3 and P7) is the signature of LHS — the temperature does not rise despite energy being absorbed, because the energy goes into breaking intermolecular bonds rather than increasing kinetic energy.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between sensible heat storage and latent heat storage. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) | Latent Heat Storage (LHS) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy form | Stored as temperature rise | Stored during phase change |
| Temperature | Varies during charging/discharging | Nearly constant at phase change |
| Governing equation | $Q_S = m c_p \Delta T$ | $Q_L = m h_{sf}$ |
| Example | Water in a solar tank (1) | Paraffin wax PCM (1) |
| Energy density | Lower (water ~210 kJ/kg at 50 K) | Higher (paraffin ~200 kJ/kg in narrow T range, but ~6× per volume) |

(Each well-explained row: 1 mark. Example: 1 mark. Total = 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is thermochemical heat storage? Why is it preferred for seasonal energy storage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Thermochemical heat storage (TCS) is a method of storing thermal energy using the **reversible endothermic/exothermic chemical reactions**. During charging, heat drives an endothermic reaction (e.g., Ca(OH)₂ → CaO + H₂O), and the products are stored separately. During discharge, the products recombine exothermically to release heat.

**Why preferred for seasonal storage (1.5 marks):**
1. **Highest energy density** (~5–10× higher than SHS/LHS), enabling compact storage.
2. **Near-zero heat losses** during storage because the products are chemically stable and can be stored at ambient temperature.
3. **Indefinite storage duration** (months to years) without degradation — making seasonal summer-to-winter storage feasible.
4. Suitable for **high-temperature applications** (up to 1000 °C) relevant to solar fuels and process industries.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**A. (a)** Explain in detail the working principle of **sensible heat storage (SHS)** with neat sketches. Discuss the selection criteria for sensible heat storage media. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A **molten salt thermal storage tank** has a capacity of **15,000 tonnes** of solar salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃). The salt is heated from **250 °C to 565 °C**.
**Given:** $c_p = 1520$ J/(kg·K).
**(i)** Calculate the total sensible heat stored (in MJ and MWh).
**(ii)** If the plant discharges this energy at a constant rate of **150 MW (thermal)**, calculate the **discharge time** in hours.
**(iii)** Comment on the suitability of this storage capacity for a commercial CSP plant. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for A(a):**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
SHS works on the principle that when a substance is heated, its **sensible temperature rises** in proportion to the energy added. The energy stored is given by $Q_S = m c_p \Delta T$.

**System components:**
1. **Heat source** (solar collector, industrial waste heat, etc.)
2. **Heat transfer fluid (HTF)** — circulates between source and storage
3. **Storage tank** — contains the storage medium (water, molten salt, rock, sand)
4. **Heat exchanger** — extracts heat during discharge
5. **Insulation** — minimizes standby losses
6. **Control system** — regulates flow and temperature

**Charging cycle:** HTF delivers heat → storage medium temperature rises → energy stored as $Q_S$.

**Discharge cycle:** HTF extracts heat from storage medium → temperature falls → energy delivered to load.

**Selection criteria (3 marks):**
1. High **specific heat capacity** ($c_p$) for compact storage.
2. High **thermal conductivity** for rapid charge/discharge.
3. **Chemical stability** over the operating temperature range.
4. **Low vapor pressure** to avoid tank pressurization.
5. **Wide temperature range** of operation.
6. **Low cost**, **abundant**, and **non-toxic**.
7. Compatibility with container materials (no corrosion).

---

**Model Solution for A(b):**

**Given:** $m = 15{,}000$ tonnes = $15 \times 10^6$ kg, $T_i = 250$ °C, $T_f = 565$ °C, $c_p = 1520$ J/(kg·K), $\dot{Q} = 150$ MW_th.

**(i) Sensible heat stored (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta T = T_f - T_i = 565 - 250 = 315 \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q_S = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} \times 1520 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 315 \text{ K}
$$

Stepwise calculation:

$$
= 15 \times 10^6 \times 1520 = 2.28 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
\times 315 = 7.182 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 7182 \text{ GJ} = 7182 \times 10^3 \text{ MJ}
$$

Converting to MWh:

$$
Q_S = \frac{7.182 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}}{3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ J/MWh}} = 1995 \text{ MWh}
$$

**(ii) Discharge time (2 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{Q_S}{\dot{Q}} = \frac{1995 \text{ MWh}}{150 \text{ MW}} = 13.3 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Suitability comment (2 marks):**
A 13.3-hour storage capacity is **excellent for a commercial CSP plant**. It allows continuous power generation from sunset to the next morning (typical solar plant needs 12–15 hours of storage to provide 24-hour baseload power). The Andasol-3 plant in Spain uses 28,500 tonnes of molten salt for 7.5 hours of full-load storage, so this 15,000-tonne system is well-engineered for mid-size commercial CSP operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**B. (a)** With the help of a phase-change diagram, explain the operation of **latent heat storage (LHS) using PCMs**. Discuss the types of PCMs and their characteristic properties. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** A residential building uses a **paraffin PCM (n-eicosane, C₂₀H₄₂)** in its ceiling panels. The PCM has:
- Melting temperature: $T_m = 36$ °C
- Latent heat of fusion: $h_{sf} = 247$ kJ/kg
- $c_{p,solid} = 1900$ J/(kg·K)
- $c_{p,liquid} = 2100$ J/(kg·K)
- Mass of PCM: $m = 50$ kg
- Night-time ambient temperature: $T_{night} = 20$ °C
- Day-time ambient temperature: $T_{day} = 38$ °C (above $T_m$)

**Calculate:**
**(i)** The total energy stored by the PCM during the day when it heats from 20 °C to 38 °C.
**(ii)** The energy released during the night when it cools back to 20 °C (assume reversible, no supercooling).
**(iii)** If 1 kWh of electricity is required to produce the same heating effect, compare the PCM storage with an electrical storage battery (assume 85 % battery round-trip efficiency). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Phase-change diagram and operation (4 marks):**

The PCM operation involves three distinct stages (as shown in Diagram 4 of Section 4):
1. **Sensible heating of solid** from $T_i$ to $T_m$ — energy input = $m c_{p,solid} (T_m - T_i)$.
2. **Phase change at constant $T_m$** — energy input = $m h_{sf}$ (latent).
3. **Sensible heating of liquid** from $T_m$ to $T_f$ — energy input = $m c_{p,liquid} (T_f - T_m)$.

During discharge, the reverse occurs, releasing the stored energy at nearly constant temperature (highly desirable for thermal comfort).

**Types of PCMs (3 marks):**

| Type | Examples | Properties | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| Organic (paraffin) | n-eicosane, n-octadecane | Chemically stable, no supercooling, low conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K), non-corrosive | Building HVAC, textiles |
| Organic (non-paraffin) | Fatty acids, sugar alcohols | Higher energy density than paraffins, biodegradable | Green building materials |
| Inorganic (salt hydrates) | Glauber's salt (Na₂SO₄·10H₂O), CaCl₂·6H₂O | High latent heat, higher conductivity (~0.5–0.7 W/m·K), but suffer from supercooling and phase segregation | Cold storage, ice packs |
| Eutectic mixtures | Engineered salt + paraffin blends | Sharp, well-defined melting point; tunable $T_m$ | Specialized industrial and medical applications |

---

**Model Solution for B(b):**

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $T_m = 36$ °C, $T_i = 20$ °C, $T_f = 38$ °C, $h_{sf} = 247{,}000$ J/kg, $c_{p,solid} = 1900$ J/(kg·K), $c_{p,liquid} = 2100$ J/(kg·K).

**(i) Total energy stored during the day (3 marks):**

**Stage 1: Solid heating (20 °C → 36 °C):**

$$
Q_1 = m \cdot c_{p,solid} \cdot (T_m - T_i) = 50 \times 1900 \times (36 - 20)
$$

$$
= 50 \times 1900 \times 16 = 1{,}520{,}000 \text{ J} = 1.52 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Stage 2: Latent heat (at 36 °C):**

$$
Q_2 = m \cdot h_{sf} = 50 \times 247{,}000 = 12{,}350{,}000 \text{ J} = 12.35 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Stage 3: Liquid heating (36 °C → 38 °C):**

$$
Q_3 = m \cdot c_{p,liquid} \cdot (T_f - T_m) = 50 \times 2100 \times (38 - 36)
$$

$$
= 50 \times 2100 \times 2 = 210{,}000 \text{ J} = 0.21 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Total:**

$$
Q_{total} = 1.52 + 12.35 + 0.21 = 14.08 \text{ MJ} = 14.08 \times 10^6 \text{ J}
$$

In kWh:

$$
E = \frac{14.08 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 3.91 \text{ kWh}_{th}
$$

**(ii) Energy released during cooling (2 marks):**

Assuming ideal reversible behavior, the energy released is equal to the energy stored (minus negligible losses):

$$
Q_{release} \approx Q_{total} = 14.08 \text{ MJ} = 3.91 \text{ kWh}_{th}
$$

**(iii) Comparison with electrical battery (2 marks):**

A battery delivering 1 kWh of usable energy requires:

$$
E_{in} = \frac{1 \text{ kWh}}{0.85} = 1.176 \text{ kWh}_{electricity}
$$

In MJ:

$$
E_{in} = 1.176 \times 3.6 = 4.235 \text{ MJ}_{electricity}
$$

For equivalent thermal energy from PCM, the input thermal energy was 14.08 MJ (free solar gain in this case, so the comparison favours PCM if renewable source is available).

**Comparison summary:**
- **PCM storage** stores 3.91 kWh_th per 50 kg with no energy conversion losses (uses free solar/ambient heat).
- **Battery** requires 1.176 kWh_e input to deliver 1 kWh_e output, with 15 % loss to heat.
- For **thermal applications** (space heating, hot water), PCM is more efficient and economical than converting electricity to heat.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in TES Questions (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in final answers.** A value of "14.08" without "MJ" or "kWh" will lose **at least 1 mark**. Always annotate units.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $c_p$ (sensible) and $h_{sf}$ (latent).** Many students incorrectly add them or interchange them. The KTU examiner penalizes this with a flat **–2 marks** in 14-mark numerical problems.
>
> 3. **Using wrong ρ or cₚ values.** For water, always use $c_p = 4186$ J/(kg·K); for solar salt, $c_p = 1520$ J/(kg·K); for rock, $c_p \approx 800$–900 J/(kg·K). Hard-coded wrong values lose **2–3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the third stage of PCM (post-melt sensible).** In multi-stage problems, students often compute only $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ and miss $Q_3$ — losing **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg.** 1 tonne = 1000 kg. Students who write $Q = 15000 \times 1520 \times 315$ directly will be **off by a factor of 1000** and lose 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Writing formulas without explaining physics.** KTU 2024 OBE-based marking rewards **conceptual clarity** (Bloom's Level 2/3) as much as numerical accuracy. Always include 1–2 lines of explanation alongside every formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ TES = temporary storage of heat/cold; classified into **SHS, LHS, TCS**.
- ✅ **SHS equation:** $Q_S = m c_p \Delta T$ — energy proportional to mass, specific heat, and temperature change.
- ✅ **LHS equation:** $Q_L = m h_{sf}$ — energy stored during constant-temperature phase change.
- ✅ **TCS equation:** $Q_{TC} = n \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$ — energy stored in chemical bonds; highest density.
- ✅ **Total PCM energy** = sensible (solid) + latent + sensible (liquid) — **three stages, three equations**.
- ✅ **SHS media:** water (low T), molten salt (mid T), rock/sand (high T).
- ✅ **PCM types:** organic (paraffin, fatty acid), inorganic (salt hydrate), eutectic.
- ✅ **TCS examples:** CaO/Ca(OH)₂, CaCO₃/CaO+CO₂, NH₃ synthesis, methane reforming.
- ✅ **Energy density order:** TCS (≫ 1000 kJ/kg) > LHS (~200–300 kJ/kg) > SHS (water ~210 kJ/kg @ 50 K).
- ✅ **Storage duration order:** TCS (months–seasonal) > LHS (hours–days) > SHS (hours–days with losses).
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** is highest for SHS at moderate temperatures (water tanks) and decreases for high-T SHS and TCS.
- ✅ **Stratification** in water tanks maximizes ΔT and energy extraction.
- ✅ **Supercooling** and **phase segregation** are key challenges in inorganic PCMs.
- ✅ **Conversions to remember:** 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ; 1 Wh = 3600 J.
- ✅ **Key Mermaid diagrams** to memorize: (i) TES classification tree, (ii) system block flow, (iii) TCS cycle, (iv) PCM phase-change process.
- ✅ **Key exam phrases:** "volumetric heat capacity," "phase change material," "endothermic/exothermic," "round-trip efficiency," "seasonal storage."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photovoltaic System for Power Generation
Solar Pond
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Pond

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Pond** is a specially designed saline water body (artificial lake) that simultaneously acts as a **solar energy collector** and a **long-term thermal energy storage device**. It traps incident solar radiation, prevents convective heat losses by exploiting a deliberately engineered vertical salt-concentration (and hence density) gradient, and accumulates the absorbed heat in the deeper, denser brine layers at temperatures well above ambient (typically **60 °C – 90 °C**, occasionally exceeding **100 °C**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim intent):**
> "A solar pond is a salinity-gradient solar collector cum storage system in which the lower convective zone (LCZ) acts as a thermal reservoir, the non-convective zone (NCZ) acts as an optical and thermal insulator, and the upper convective zone (UCZ) acts as the interface to the atmosphere."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Natural Thermos Flask"

Imagine a **large outdoor swimming pool** painted black at the bottom. Now, instead of ordinary water, you fill it with:
- Fresh water at the **top** (like a thin transparent lid),
- Progressively **saltier and heavier water** towards the **bottom** (like a dense black soup).

When sunlight enters the top fresh-water layer, it travels down, gets absorbed by the dark bottom, and the water tries to **heat up and rise** (just like hot air rising). But — and this is the magic — the **salt below is so heavy** that the heated water **cannot rise through it**. So the heat is **trapped** at the bottom. The pond has effectively turned itself into a **self-insulating solar thermos bottle**, storing energy for weeks or even months.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The salt-gradient solar pond is one of the few renewable systems that **stores thermal energy by default**, eliminating the need for a separate battery or sensible-latent heat storage unit.

### 1.3 The Three Functional Zones (Zonal Architecture)

A working salt-gradient solar pond is divided into three vertical regions, each with a distinct engineering function:

| Zone | Abbreviation | Typical Depth | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Upper Convective Zone | **UCZ** | 0.1 – 0.3 m | Thin, low-salinity, mixes with atmosphere; minor losses only |
| Non-Convective Zone | **NCZ** | 1.0 – 1.5 m | Transparent salt gradient; acts as transparent insulator |
| Lower Convective Zone | **LCZ** (Storage Zone) | 1.0 – 2.0 m | Saturated brine, dark bottom, **stores thermal energy** |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

- **Solar constant (extraterrestrial):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$
- **Density of fresh water (4 °C):** $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Saturated brine density (NaCl, ~26 %):** $\rho_{brine} \approx 1200 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Specific heat of brine:** $c_p \approx 3300 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$
- **Solar transmittance of water:** $\tau \approx 0.85$ per metre (visible spectrum)
- **Critical Rayleigh number for stability:** $Ra_c \approx 1000$

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love to ask for a **labelled diagram of the three zones** — always draw the bottom dark, the LCZ in dark blue, the NCZ in gradient, and the UCZ in light blue, with arrows showing incident radiation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics of Suppressing Convection

A normal body of water exposed to solar radiation **cannot store heat** beyond a few degrees above ambient. This is because, as the bottom layer heats up, it becomes less dense, rises, and is replaced by cooler water — this is **free (natural) convection**. The energy keeps circulating and is lost to the atmosphere by evaporation and surface convection.

A solar pond **breaks this cycle** by ensuring the **density increase due to salt concentration exceeds the density decrease due to temperature rise**, at every horizontal layer.

Mathematically, the **local stability criterion** is:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z}\right)_{\text{total}} > 0
$$

Expanding using two independent contributions:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial C}\frac{dC}{dz}}_{\text{salt (stabilising)}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T}\frac{dT}{dz}}_{\text{temperature (destabilising)}}
$$

Since $\partial \rho / \partial T < 0$ (density falls with temperature), the thermal term is **destabilising**, and the salt term must be **larger in magnitude** to ensure overall stability.

### 2.2 Types of Solar Ponds (KTU Classification)

1. **Salt-Gradient (Non-Convective) Solar Pond** — the standard, most efficient type.
2. **Partitioned (Shallow) Solar Pond** — partitioned shallow cells, less efficient but no gradient maintenance.
3. **Saturated Salt Solar Pond** — uniform saturated brine, relies on suspended particles for convection suppression (obsolete).
4. **Membrane-Covered Solar Pond** — covered with transparent membrane to suppress evaporation.

### 2.3 Methods of Heat Extraction (KTU High-Yield)

| Method | Mechanism | Typical η | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External Heat Exchanger** | Hot brine pumped, passed through HX, returned | 15 – 25 % | Space heating, desalination |
| **In-Pond Heat Exchanger** | Coils immersed in LCZ | 20 – 30 % | Process heat, ORC plants |
| **Direct Flash Evaporation** | Brine flashed at low pressure | 10 – 18 % | Small-scale turbines |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the equations below are **must-know** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise symbols, units, and ranges.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{stored} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = V \, \rho \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored in LCZ | J or Wh |
| 2 | $I(t) = I_0 \, \tau^n$ | Beer-Lambert attenuation of solar radiation in water | W/m² |
| 3 | $\rho(z) = \rho_w \left[1 + \beta_s (C(z) - C_0)\right]$ | Density profile with salt concentration $C$ | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $\rho(z) = \rho_w \left[1 - \beta_T (T(z) - T_0)\right]$ | Density profile with temperature $T$ | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $Ra = \dfrac{g \, \beta_T \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu \, \alpha}$ | Rayleigh number of pond | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Ra \le Ra_c \approx 1000$ | Stability criterion | — |
| 7 | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Max. thermodynamic efficiency | — |
| 8 | $\eta_{collector} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{I \cdot A \cdot \Delta t}$ | Overall pond efficiency | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dC}{dz} \ge \dfrac{\beta_T}{\beta_s} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | Salt gradient design equation | %/m |
| 10 | $P_{turbine} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T_{HX} \, \eta_{ORC}$ | Power output via ORC | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical reminder:** Notice the use of `\beta_s` and `\beta_T` — the saline expansion coefficient and the thermal expansion coefficient of water, respectively. $\beta_s \approx 7 \times 10^{-4}$ per % NaCl; $\beta_T \approx 2 \times 10^{-4}$ per K.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solar ponds are deployed in:
- **Electricity generation** via Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) — e.g., Beit HaArava, Israel (5 MW).
- **Desalination** — Multi-Effect Distillation (MED) coupled to solar ponds.
- **Space heating and greenhouse cultivation** in rural Rajasthan, Iran, Tibet.
- **Process heat** for dairy, textile, and salt industries.
- **Aquaculture** — fish farming in the warm lower layers (collateral use).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, **always** structure the answer as: (i) construction, (ii) working, (iii) stability criterion with derivation, (iv) heat extraction, (v) applications, (vi) limitations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Local Stability Criterion of the Salt-Gradient Pond

**Statement to prove:** A vertical density gradient $\partial \rho / \partial z > 0$ suppresses convection in the NCZ.

**Step 1 — General density function:**
Consider water with two variable fields — temperature $T(z)$ and salt concentration $C(z)$:

$$
\rho = \rho(T, C)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply total differential along depth $z$:**

$$
\frac{d\rho}{dz} = \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T}\right)_C \frac{dT}{dz} + \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial C}\right)_T \frac{dC}{dz}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute linearised forms:**

For a salt-water solution, linearisation gives:

$$
\rho = \rho_w \left[ 1 - \beta_T (T - T_0) + \beta_s (C - C_0) \right]
$$

Differentiating with respect to $z$:

$$
\frac{d\rho}{dz} = \rho_w \left[ -\beta_T \frac{dT}{dz} + \beta_s \frac{dC}{dz} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply stability requirement $d\rho / dz > 0$:**

$$
\rho_w \left[ -\beta_T \frac{dT}{dz} + \beta_s \frac{dC}{dz} \right] > 0
$$

Dividing by the positive quantity $\rho_w \beta_T$:

$$
\frac{dC}{dz} > \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_s} \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Design Inequality:**

$$
\boxed{\ \frac{dC}{dz} \ge \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_s} \cdot \frac{dT}{dz} \ }
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The salt concentration must rise with depth **at least** as fast as the temperature-induced density drop would otherwise cause instability.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rayleigh Number and the Critical Gradient

**Statement:** Show that the salt-gradient pond is stable when $Ra \le 1000$.

**Step 1 — Define the Rayleigh number for a fluid layer of depth $L$:**

$$
Ra = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu \, \alpha}
$$

where $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$, $\beta$ = thermal expansion coefficient, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for water at 30 °C:**

$$
\nu \approx 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \alpha \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \beta_T \approx 3 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute for a typical LCZ ($\Delta T = 50$ K, $L = 1.5$ m):**

$$
Ra = \frac{9.81 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 50 \times (1.5)^3}{0.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
Ra = \frac{9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 3.375}{1.12 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
= \frac{4.967 \times 10^{-1}}{1.12 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
= 4.43 \times 10^{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Apparent paradox:** $Ra \gg 1000$, so the pond is **NOT stable**!

The resolution: this $Ra$ is for a **uniform fluid**; in a **non-convective gradient pond**, the effective $Ra$ is computed **locally** between adjacent thin layers (typically 1 cm). For a 1 cm thick micro-layer:

$$
Ra_{local} = \frac{9.81 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1 \times (0.01)^3}{0.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
= \frac{2.943 \times 10^{-10}}{1.12 \times 10^{-13}} = 2627
$$

Reducing temperature gradient further (NCZ), one achieves $Ra_{local} \le 1000$, satisfying stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination trick:** If you compute $Ra$ over the **entire pond depth**, you will always get unstable values. The KTU-correct method is to compute the **local gradient Rayleigh number** over 1–10 cm micro-layers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Storage Capacity of the LCZ

**Statement:** Determine the heat stored in a solar pond of area $A = 1000 \ \text{m}^2$ with $h_{LCZ} = 1.5$ m, $T_{avg} = 70$ °C, $T_{ambient} = 25$ °C.

**Step 1 — Mass of LCZ brine:**

$$
m = A \cdot h_{LCZ} \cdot \rho_{brine} = 1000 \times 1.5 \times 1200 = 1.8 \times 10^{6} \ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat stored relative to ambient:**

$$
Q = m \, c_p \, (T_{LCZ} - T_{amb}) = 1.8 \times 10^{6} \times 3300 \times (70 - 25)
$$

$$
= 1.8 \times 10^{6} \times 3300 \times 45
$$

$$
= 1.8 \times 10^{6} \times 1.485 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
= 2.673 \times 10^{11} \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh:**

$$
Q = \frac{2.673 \times 10^{11}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 7.425 \times 10^{4} \ \text{kWh} = 74,250 \ \text{kWh}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This explains why a 1000 m² pond can supply **heating for ~50 homes for one winter day** in Kerala, or run a small 10 kW ORC for 1000+ hours.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solar Pond Efficiency

The instantaneous collection efficiency of a solar pond is:

$$
\eta_{sp} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{extracted}}{I \cdot A_{pond}}
$$

For an external heat exchanger extracting $T_{in} = 80$ °C and returning at 50 °C:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{in} - T_{out}) = \dot{m} \times 3300 \times 30
$$

For an insolation of $I = 700 \ \text{W/m}^2$ and $A = 1000 \ \text{m}^2$:

$$
\eta_{sp} = \frac{\dot{m} \times 3300 \times 30}{700 \times 1000} = \frac{9.9 \times 10^{4} \cdot \dot{m}}{7 \times 10^{5}}
$$

A typical design mass flow $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s gives:

$$
\eta_{sp} = \frac{4.95 \times 10^{5}}{7 \times 10^{5}} \approx 0.707 \rightarrow 70.7 \%
$$

Such high efficiency is **misleading**: the **exergy (thermodynamic) efficiency** of a pond is rarely above 5–8 % because of the low temperature of the storage zone.

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners often compare **energy efficiency vs. exergy efficiency** of solar ponds. Always declare that **first-law efficiencies are high but second-law efficiencies are low** due to low-grade thermal storage.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pros and Cons (For 14-mark answers)

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Low cost, large-scale thermal storage | Large land area required |
| Long storage duration (months) | Salt-gradient maintenance is critical |
| No moving parts in collection zone | Surface evaporation losses |
| Simultaneous storage + collection | Lower temperatures (60–90 °C) limit turbine efficiency |
| Useful for rural electrification | Ground-water seepage / leakage |
| Multi-purpose (heating + aquaculture) | Susceptible to dust, algae, and turbidity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Zonal Architecture of a Salt-Gradient Solar Pond

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUN["Incident Solar Radiation I W per m2"] --> UCZ_TOP["UCZ Upper Convective Zone 0.1 to 0.3 m Fresh water T ambient"]
    UCZ_TOP --> NCZ_MID["NCZ Non Convective Zone 1.0 to 1.5 m Salt gradient Transparent insulator"]
    NCZ_MID --> LCZ_BOTTOM["LCZ Lower Convective Zone 1.0 to 2.0 m Saturated brine Dark bottom T 60 to 90 C"]
    LCZ_BOTTOM --> HX["External or In Pond Heat Exchanger"]
    HX --> APP["Application Space Heating ORC Power Desalination Aquaculture"]
    NCZ_MID -. blocks .-> CONV["Suppressed Convection Rayleigh less than 1000"]
    LCZ_BOTTOM -. stores .-> STORE["Thermal Energy Q equals m cp delta T"]
    style SUN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style UCZ_TOP fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
    style NCZ_MID fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style LCZ_BOTTOM fill=#191970,stroke=#333,color:#fff
    style HX fill=#FFA500,stroke=#333
    style APP fill=#90EE90,stroke=#333
    style CONV fill=#FFB6C1,stroke=#333
    style STORE fill=#FF69B4,stroke=#333,color=#fff
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Solar Pond + Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) Power Plant

```mermaid
graph LR
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define a salt-gradient solar pond. Mention the functions of the three zones.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A salt-gradient solar pond is a **saline water body that simultaneously collects and stores solar energy** by maintaining a vertical salt concentration gradient that suppresses natural convection.

- **UCZ (Upper Convective Zone):** Thin, fresh, mixed layer at the surface; mediates between atmosphere and storage; contributes to evaporative loss.
- **NCZ (Non-Convective Zone):** Salt-gradient middle layer; transparent; acts as an optical window and **thermal insulator** by suppressing convection.
- **LCZ (Lower Convective Zone):** Saturated, dark-bottomed layer; **stores thermal energy** at 60–90 °C; the actual "collector cum storage" zone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*State and explain the stability criterion of a salt-gradient solar pond.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a stable pond, the density must **increase with depth** at every horizontal layer:

$$
\frac{d\rho}{dz} = \rho_w \left[ -\beta_T \frac{dT}{dz} + \beta_s \frac{dC}{dz} \right] > 0
$$

This yields the design inequality:

$$
\frac{dC}{dz} \ge \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_s} \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}
$$

The salt-induced stabilising gradient must exceed the temperature-induced destabilising gradient. Numerically, this requires a salt concentration rise of about **20 % (by mass) from top to bottom** over 1–1.5 m of NCZ.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries a compulsory question of 14 marks. The two choices below are **completely independent alternatives** in the style of ESE Part B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — Salt Gradient & Stability Focus**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **salt-gradient solar pond**. List the functions of each zone. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **local stability criterion** for a salt-gradient solar pond. Show that the salt concentration gradient must exceed the temperature-induced destabilising gradient. Use a numerical example to demonstrate. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and working (7 marks):**

A salt-gradient solar pond is a shallow artificial basin (1–3 m deep, area 100 – 10,000 m²) lined with **black HDPE or PVC liner** (5 mm) over a compacted clay sub-base. The bottom is coated with **black paint or HDPE** to maximise solar absorption.

Filling sequence (from bottom to top):
1. **LCZ (bottom 1.0–2.0 m):** Saturated NaCl brine (~26 % by mass, density $\approx 1200 \ \text{kg/m}^3$), black bottom.
2. **NCZ (middle 1.0–1.5 m):** Salt concentration **linearly decreasing** from 26 % at the bottom of NCZ to ~3–5 % at the top of NCZ. This is achieved by **controlled diffusion or by layering-and-mixing** during commissioning.
3. **UCZ (top 0.1–0.3 m):** Fresh water, density $\approx 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, exposed to atmosphere.

**Working:** Solar radiation passes through UCZ and NCZ (both transparent), gets absorbed at the dark LCZ bottom, raising the LCZ temperature to 60–90 °C. The salt gradient prevents hot LCZ brine from rising, so heat is **trapped and stored**. Heat is extracted by circulating LCZ brine through an external heat exchanger (or by in-pond coils).

(Include a labelled diagram similar to Section 4.1 for full marks.)

**(b) Derivation of the stability criterion (7 marks):**

Start with the general density function of brine:

$$
\rho = \rho(T, C)
$$

Apply total differential along depth $z$:

$$
\frac{d\rho}{dz} = \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T}\right)_C \frac{dT}{dz} + \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial C}\right)_T \frac{dC}{dz}
$$

Substitute linearised forms:

$$
\rho = \rho_w \left[ 1 - \beta_T (T - T_0) + \beta_s (C - C_0) \right]
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{d\rho}{dz} = \rho_w \left[ -\beta_T \frac{dT}{dz} + \beta_s \frac{dC}{dz} \right]
$$

Stability condition: $d\rho / dz > 0$, so:

$$
\boxed{\ \frac{dC}{dz} \ge \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_s} \cdot \frac{dT}{dz} \ }
$$

**Numerical example:**
Take $\beta_T = 2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$, $\beta_s = 7 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{per \% NaCl}$, and a temperature gradient $dT/dz = -30 \ \text{K/m}$ (temperature falls upward).

Required salt gradient:

$$
\frac{dC}{dz} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{7 \times 10^{-4}} \times 30 = 8.57 \ \%/\text{m}
$$

So over a 1.5 m NCZ, the salt concentration must rise by about **12.8 %** from top to bottom. This matches the standard design of going from ~3 % at the top to ~26 % at the bottom of the NCZ.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — Heat Extraction & Applications Focus**

**(a)** Explain the various methods of **heat extraction** from a solar pond. Compare their efficiencies. (7 marks)

**(b)** A salt-gradient solar pond has a storage zone (LCZ) of area **1500 m²** and depth **1.8 m**. The brine has density **1180 kg/m³** and specific heat **3400 J/(kg·K)**. The LCZ temperature is **75 °C** and ambient is **25 °C**. Calculate: (i) the heat stored in the LCZ, (ii) the heat stored in kWh, and (iii) the average power if the heat is extracted uniformly over **8 hours**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Methods of heat extraction (7 marks):**

1. **External Heat Exchanger (most common):**
   Hot brine is pumped out of the LCZ, passed through a plate heat exchanger where it gives up heat (e.g., to ORC working fluid or process water), and the cooled brine is reinjected into the LCZ.
   - **Efficiency:** 15–25 % of incident solar energy.
   - **Advantage:** Easy maintenance, no fouling of pond internals.
   - **Disadvantage:** Pumping losses; brine contamination risk.

2. **In-Pond Heat Exchanger:**
   Coils of HDPE pipe (or titanium) immersed directly in the LCZ. A secondary fluid (water or thermal oil) circulates through the coils.
   - **Efficiency:** 20–30 % (better because no pumping of brine).
   - **Advantage:** No brine disturbance to LCZ stratification.
   - **Disadvantage:** Difficult to clean; corrosion in salt water.

3. **Direct Flash / Boiling in LCZ:**
   If LCZ temperature > 100 °C (rare, requires ~5–7 m depth with very high insolation), the brine can be partially flashed to drive a low-pressure turbine.
   - **Efficiency:** 10–18 %.

4. **Hybrid (HX + ORC):**
   Most modern systems use an external HX coupled to an Organic Rankine Cycle with R245fa or similar low-boiling-point fluid.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A = 1500 \ \text{m}^2$, $h = 1.8 \ \text{m}$, $\rho = 1180 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 3400 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$, $T_{LCZ} = 75 \ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{amb} = 25 \ ^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta t = 8 \ \text{h} = 28800 \ \text{s}$.

**(i) Heat stored:**

Mass of LCZ:
$$
m = A \cdot h \cdot \rho = 1500 \times 1.8 \times 1180
$$

$$
m = 3.186 \times 10^{6} \ \text{kg}
$$

Heat stored:
$$
Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = 3.186 \times 10^{6} \times 3400 \times (75 - 25)
$$

$$
Q = 3.186 \times 10^{6} \times 3400 \times 50
$$

$$
Q = 5.4162 \times 10^{11} \ \text{J}
$$

**(ii) Heat in kWh:**

$$
Q = \frac{5.4162 \times 10^{11}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 1.5045 \times 10^{5} \ \text{kWh} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{5} \ \text{kWh}
$$

**(iii) Average power over 8 hours:**

$$
P = \frac{Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{5.4162 \times 10^{11}}{28800} = 1.881 \times 10^{7} \ \text{W}
$$

$$
P \approx 18.8 \ \text{MW}
$$

**Final answers:**
- (i) $Q = 5.42 \times 10^{11} \ \text{J}$
- (ii) $Q = 1.50 \times 10^{5} \ \text{kWh}$
- (iii) $P = 18.81 \ \text{MW}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups** — students often write $Q$ in J without converting to kWh when the question asks for both. Always check the units asked.
> 2. **Density of brine vs. water** — using $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ instead of $\rho_{brine} = 1180 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$** — using $T_{hot}$ directly loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Stability criterion errors** — using a **single global** Rayleigh number instead of the **local** Rayleigh number is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this list as a **last-minute 5-minute revision** before the KTU exam.

- [x] **Definition:** Solar pond = solar collector + thermal storage in one body of salt water.
- [x] **Three zones:** UCZ, NCZ, LCZ — remember **U**pper, **N**on-convective, **L**ower.
- [x] **Stability criterion:** $\frac{dC}{dz} \ge \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_s} \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}$ — must hold **locally**.
- [x] **Critical Rayleigh number:** $Ra_c \approx 1000$ for local micro-layers, **not** for the whole pond.
- [x] **Salt concentration:** typically 0–5 % (UCZ) → 3–5 % (top of NCZ) → 26 % (bottom of NCZ/LCZ).
- [x] **Storage temperature:** 60–90 °C at the LCZ; 100 °C+ only in deep arid-region ponds.
- [x] **Heat storage equation:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T = V \rho c_p \Delta T$.
- [x] **Heat extraction methods:** External HX (15–25 %), In-pond HX (20–30 %), Direct flash (10–18 %).
- [x] **Efficiency:** First-law (energy) high (60–80 %); second-law (exergy) low (5–8 %).
- [x] **Applications:** Space heating, ORC power, desalination (MED), aquaculture, process heat.
- [x] **Real-world installations:** Beit HaArava (Israel, 5 MW), Bhuj (India, experimental), El Paso (USA).
- [x] **Limitations:** Large area, salt maintenance, evaporation, low-grade heat, algae/turbidity.
- [x] **Examination keyword:** "Salt-gradient" + "Rayleigh" + "Three zones" — together fetch 7+ marks.
- [x] **Numerical standard:** always assume $c_p = 3300 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for brine unless otherwise specified.
- [x] **Conversion:** $1 \ \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \ \text{J}$ — never forget this.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Solar Cell?

A **Solar Cell** (also called a **Photovoltaic Cell**, PV Cell) is a semiconductor device that converts the energy of sunlight directly into electricity through the **Photovoltaic Effect** — a physical and chemical phenomenon directly related to the photoelectric effect discovered by Edmond Becquerel in 1839 and operationally demonstrated in silicon by Bell Labs in 1954.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> A solar cell is the basic building block of a photovoltaic system. It is essentially a large-area p-n junction diode that generates a DC electrical current when its surface is exposed to solar radiation (photons). The cell is fabricated primarily from semiconductor materials such as crystalline silicon, thin films, or emerging perovskite/organic compounds.

The **fundamental output** of a single solar cell (typically producing ~0.5 V to 0.7 V open-circuit voltage) is a DC voltage, and multiple cells are interconnected in series and parallel to form a **PV module** (or **PV panel**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Electrical Symbol & Key Metrics**
> - **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$):** ~ **0.5 V to 0.7 V** per cell (for crystalline silicon)
> - **Short-Circuit Current Density ($J_{SC}$):** ~ **30 mA/cm² to 40 mA/cm²** at AM1.5
> - **Power Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$):** **15% – 25%** (commercial monocrystalline), **>40%** (lab multi-junction)
> - **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Cell temperature = **25 °C**, Air Mass = **AM1.5G**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Under Rainfall" Model

Imagine a wide, open field receiving a heavy rain shower.

- The **rain droplets** = **photons** (light energy packets streaming from the Sun).
- The **field** = the **semiconductor material** of the solar cell.
- Tiny **collection cups** placed strategically across the field = the **p-n junction electric field**, which scoops up the energy and channels it.
- The **water collected in buckets** = **electrons (electric current)**.
- The **pipes carrying the water away** = the **external circuit (load)**.
- The **amount of water captured** depends on:
  1. **Rain intensity** (irradiance, W/m²) ☁️🌧️
  2. **Size of the field** (cell area) 📐
  3. **Cup design efficiency** (cell material & technology) ☕
  4. **How cold the cups are** (operating temperature — colder is better!) ❄️

Just as not every raindrop is captured (some splash out, some miss the cup), not every photon generates usable electricity — some are reflected, some pass through, and some generate heat instead of charge carriers. This is why no solar cell is 100% efficient.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy matters for KTU exams:** Examiners love questions that ask you to "explain the principle of working of a solar cell in simple terms." A 3-mark answer combining this analogy with the formal p-n junction explanation scores very high.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Modern Relevance

- **1839:** Edmond Becquerel discovers the photovoltaic effect.
- **1954:** Bell Labs produces the first practical silicon solar cell (efficiency ~6%).
- **1970s:** Oil crisis triggers terrestrial PV research.
- **2024:** Global installed PV capacity exceeds **1.5 TW (Terawatts)**. India targets **500 GW** of non-fossil capacity by 2030.
- **Emerging frontiers:** Perovskite-silicon tandem cells, bifacial modules, floating PV, agrivoltaics.

---

## 1.4 Roadmap of This Topic (Module 1 Sub-divisions)

| Sub-section | What You Will Learn |
|-------------|---------------------|
| 1. Types of Solar Cells | 1st Gen, 2nd Gen, 3rd Gen, and emerging chemistries |
| 2. Principle of Working | Photovoltaic effect, p-n junction, charge generation & collection |
| 3. Performance Characteristics | I-V curve, P-V curve, Fill Factor, Efficiency, temperature effects |
| 4. Production Process | Block-level flow from raw silica to finished module |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solar Cells (Types of Solar Cells)

Solar cells are categorized into **three principal generations** based on the materials used, manufacturing cost, efficiency, and maturity of technology.

### A. First Generation (1G) — Crystalline Silicon (c-Si) Cells

These are the **workhorse** of today's commercial PV industry, accounting for **>95% of global production**.

| Sub-type | Construction | Efficiency (Commercial) | Key Feature |
|----------|--------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| **Monocrystalline (Mono-c-Si)** | Single-crystal Si ingot grown by Czochralski process | 20% – 25% | Uniform black color, highest efficiency |
| **Polycrystalline (Multi-c-Si)** | Cast Si ingot with multiple grains | 15% – 20% | Blue speckled color, cheaper |

**Advantages of 1G cells:**
- Abundant raw material (silicon is the 2nd most abundant element in Earth's crust).
- Proven, stable technology with **>30-year lifespan**.
- High, well-characterized efficiency.

**Disadvantages of 1G cells:**
- High energy payback time (~2 years).
- Rigid form factor.
- Energy-intensive production (high temperatures in ingot growth).

### B. Second Generation (2G) — Thin-Film Cells

Deposited as **thin layers (few µm)** of semiconductor on a substrate (glass, metal, plastic). Lower material cost; flexible in some cases.

| Sub-type | Active Material | Efficiency (Commercial) | Key Feature |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si)** | Hydrogenated Si (a-Si:H) | 6% – 9% | Used in calculators, flexible modules |
| **Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)** | CdTe | 18% – 22% | Cheapest per watt; leader — First Solar |
| **Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS)** | Cu(In,Ga)Se₂ | 15% – 22% | Highest thin-film lab efficiency |
| **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** | GaAs | 28% – 35% | Space applications, very high efficiency |

**Advantages of 2G cells:**
- Lower material consumption.
- Better performance under high temperature & low light.
- Flexible substrates possible (roll-to-roll manufacturing).

**Disadvantages of 2G cells:**
- Lower efficiency per unit area (more space needed).
- Some materials are toxic (Cd) or rare (In, Ga, Te).

### C. Third Generation (3G) — Emerging & Multi-junction Cells

Designed to surpass the **Shockley-Queisser limit (~33%)** of single-junction silicon cells.

| Sub-type | Construction | Lab Efficiency | Status |
|----------|--------------|----------------|--------|
| **Multi-junction (Tandem)** | III-V layers (GaInP/GaAs/Ge) | > 47% (6-junction) | Used in space, concentrator PV |
| **Dye-Sensitized (DSSC)** | TiO₂ + dye + electrolyte | 11% – 14% | "Grätzel cell", low-cost, transparent |
| **Perovskite Solar Cells (PSC)** | CH₃NH₃PbI₃ family | 26% (single), 33%+ (tandem) | Fastest-growing research area |
| **Organic PV (OPV)** | Conductive polymers | 10% – 18% | Ultra-thin, printable, flexible |
| **Quantum Dot Solar Cells** | PbS, CdSe nanocrystals | 18%+ | Tunable bandgap |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favorite Question Format:** "Compare First, Second, and Third Generation solar cells." (This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU Part B. Memorize the table above.)

### D. Comparison Summary — The "KTU Snapshot Table"

| Parameter | 1G (c-Si) | 2G (Thin Film) | 3G (Emerging) |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Material cost | High | Low | Variable |
| Efficiency | High (15–25%) | Moderate (8–22%) | Very high (15–47%) |
| Maturity | Fully commercial | Commercial | Lab / pilot scale |
| Temperature coefficient | -0.4 %/°C | -0.2 to -0.3 %/°C | Varies |
| Lifetime | 25–35 years | 15–25 years | < 10 years (degradation) |
| Cost/Watt | Lowest (~$0.25/W) | Lowest thin-film | Higher (declining) |

---

## 2.2 Principle of Working — The Photovoltaic Effect

A solar cell operates on the **Photovoltaic (PV) Effect**, defined as the generation of a potential difference (voltage) across a p-n junction when photons of suitable energy strike the semiconductor material.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Photon Incidence:** Sunlight (composed of photons of energy $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$) strikes the front surface of the cell. The anti-reflective coating (ARC, e.g., SiN) minimizes reflection losses.

**Step 2 — Photon Absorption:** A photon is absorbed only if its energy $\geq$ the semiconductor **bandgap energy ($E_g$)**:
$$E_{photon} = h\nu \geq E_g$$
- If $E_{photon} < E_g$ → photon passes through (transmission loss).
- If $E_{photon} = E_g$ → just absorbed, optimal.
- If $E_{photon} > E_g$ → absorbed, but excess energy is lost as **thermalization loss** (electron relaxes to band edge, releasing heat).

**Step 3 — Electron-Hole Pair (EHP) Generation:** The absorbed photon excites a valence-band electron to the conduction band, leaving behind a **hole** in the valence band. This is called an **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.

**Step 4 — Charge Separation by the Junction Field:** The built-in electric field at the **p-n junction depletion region** (formed due to the diffusion of majority carriers and creation of a space-charge region) **sweeps electrons to the n-side** and **holes to the p-side**. This separation prevents recombination and creates the photogenerated current.

**Step 5 — Current Extraction:** The separated charges flow through the external circuit via the front (n-side, with finger contacts) and back (p-side, with full back contact) metal electrodes, delivering DC electrical power to the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the P-N Junction:**
> Without a junction, the photo-generated EHPs would recombine within microseconds, and no net current would flow. The p-n junction creates an **asymmetric potential barrier (~0.6 V for Si)** that acts like a **one-way valve** — pushing electrons in one direction, holes in the other.

### Mathematical Form of the Photogenerated Current

Under illumination, the **total current** delivered by a solar cell is given by the **ideal diode equation with light generation term**:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- $I$ = net terminal current (A)
- $I_L$ = photogenerated (light) current (A) — proportional to irradiance
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (A) — depends on temperature
- $q$ = elementary charge = **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
- $V$ = terminal voltage (V)
- $k$ = Boltzmann's constant = **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

A more accurate **two-diode model** (accounting for recombination losses in the depletion region) is:

$$I = I_L - I_{01} \left( e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{kT}} - 1 \right) - I_{02} \left( e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{2kT}} - 1 \right) - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Where $R_s$ is the **series resistance** (ideally zero) and $R_{sh}$ is the **shunt resistance** (ideally infinite).

---

## 2.3 Performance Characteristics of a Solar Cell

The performance is graphically captured by the **I-V (Current-Voltage) characteristic curve** under STC:

### Key Performance Parameters

1. **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{SC}$):** The current flowing when terminals are shorted ($V = 0$). Essentially equals $I_L$ in the ideal model.
2. **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$):** The voltage when terminals are open ($I = 0$):

$$V_{OC} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right) \approx \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} \right)$$

3. **Maximum Power Point (MPP):** The single operating point $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ on the I-V curve where the product $P = V \times I$ is maximized.
4. **Fill Factor (FF):** A dimensionless measure of the "squareness" of the I-V curve:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$

   - Typical values: **0.7 – 0.85** for good crystalline cells.
   - Higher FF = better cell quality.

5. **Power Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of maximum electrical power output to incident solar power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G}$$

   Where:
   - $A$ = cell area (m²)
   - $G$ = incident irradiance (W/m²) — at STC, $G = 1000$ W/m²

6. **Temperature Coefficients:**
   - Voltage decreases with temperature: $\frac{dV_{OC}}{dT} \approx -2$ mV/°C per cell
   - Current increases slightly with temperature: $\frac{dI_{SC}}{dT} \approx +0.05$ %/°C
   - Net effect: **Efficiency drops ~0.4 %/°C** for c-Si

7. **Effect of Irradiance:** $I_{SC} \propto G$ (linear); $V_{OC} \propto \ln(G)$ (logarithmic).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-PRIORITY for KTU Numerical Questions. Memorize units and signs carefully.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J or eV | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 2 | Condition for absorption | $E_g \leq h\nu$ | eV | $E_g$(Si) = **1.12 eV**; $E_g$(GaAs) = **1.42 eV** |
| 3 | Ideal diode (with light) | $I = I_L - I_0\left(e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1\right)$ | A | Single-diode model |
| 4 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{OC} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | V | Logarithmic in $I_L$ |
| 5 | Short-circuit current | $I_{SC} \approx I_L$ | A | At $V = 0$ |
| 6 | Fill Factor | $FF = \frac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | dimensionless | 0.7 – 0.85 typical |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V_{OC} I_{SC} FF}{A \cdot G}$ | % or decimal | Divide by $A \cdot G$ (W) |
| 8 | MPP power | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = V_{OC} I_{SC} \cdot FF$ | W | Peak of P-V curve |
| 9 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$ | V | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 10 | Temperature effect on $\eta$ | $\Delta\eta \approx -0.4\,\%/\!\!^\circ\text{C}$ | — | For c-Si |
| 11 | Series resistance loss | $\Delta P = I^2 R_s$ | W | $R_s$ should be minimal |
| 12 | Shunt resistance loss | $\Delta P = \frac{V^2}{R_{sh}}$ | W | $R_{sh}$ should be maximal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Table-Breaking Mistake to Avoid:** Never use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside the markdown table (e.g., for $\frac{kT}{q} \ln(I_L / I_0)$). In LaTeX inside tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, or break the expression across rows.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar farms (utility-scale):** 1G mono-c-Si dominates due to highest $/Watt and 30-year warranties.
- **Rooftop residential:** 1G and 2G CIGS compete on aesthetics (black panels) and cost.
- **Space satellites:** 3G multi-junction GaAs cells provide >30% efficiency under concentrated sunlight.
- **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV):** 2G thin-film and 3G DSSC offer semi-transparent, colored panels.
- **Internet-of-Things (IoT):** 2G a-Si and 3G OPV provide ultra-low-power indoor harvesting.
