a)^2 \cdot 2a V_1 $$ $$ P_{\text{extracted}} = 2 \rho A V_1^3 a (1 - a)^2 $$ **Step 9 — Compute Available Upstream Power.** $$ P_{\text{wind}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3 $$ **Step 10 — Form the Power Coefficient $C_P$.** $$ C_P = \dfrac{P_{\text{extracted}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \dfrac{2 \rho A V_1^3 a (1 - a)^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = 4 a (1 - a)^2 $$ **Step 11 — Maximize $C_P$ with Respect to $a$.** Take derivative and set to zero: $$ \dfrac{dC_P}{da} = 4 \left[ (1 - a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1 - a)(-1) \right] = 4(1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0 $$ Either $a = 1$ (trivial, no power) or $\boxed{a = 1/3}$. **Step 12 — Evaluate $C_P$ at $a = 1/3$.** $$ C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 = 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4}{9} $$ $$ C_{P,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Betz's Law (Final Form):** > No ideal wind turbine can convert more than **59.26%** of the kinetic energy in wind into useful mechanical energy. The optimal induction factor is $a = 1/3$, which corresponds to the downstream wake velocity being exactly **2/3** of the upstream velocity. ### 3.3 Derivation: Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) The **tip speed ratio** $\lambda$ compares the linear speed of the blade tip to the incoming wind speed. For a rotor of radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) or rotational speed $n$ (rpm): **Step 1 — Linear velocity at the blade tip.** $$ v_{\text{tip}} = \omega R $$ **Step 2 — Express $\omega$ in terms of rpm.** $$ \omega = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60} = \dfrac{\pi n}{30} $$ **Step 3 — Form the TSR.** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{v_{\text{tip}}}{V} = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R n}{30 V} $$ > **Interpretation:** High-speed, multi-blade turbines (e.g., Savonius) operate at **low TSR (~0.5–1)**, while modern 3-blade utility HAWTs operate at **high TSR (~7–9)** for maximum $C_P$. ### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem Walkthrough) **Problem:** A wind turbine has rotor diameter $D = 80 \; \text{m}$, operates in air density $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, and experiences wind speed $V = 12 \; \text{m/s}$. Assuming Betz-optimal operation, compute (a) total available wind power, (b) maximum extractable power, and (c) rotor rpm if the tip speed ratio $\lambda = 8$. **Solution:** (a) Swept area: $$ A = \pi R^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 5026.55 \; \text{m}^2 $$ Total wind power: $$ P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot (12)^3 $$ $$ P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728 $$ $$ P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5.32 \times 10^6 \; \text{W} = 5.32 \; \text{MW} $$ (b) Maximum extractable (Betz-limited) power: $$ P_{\text{Betz}} = 0.5926 \times 5.32 \; \text{MW} \approx 3.15 \; \text{MW} $$ (c) Rotational speed from TSR: $$ n = \dfrac{30 \lambda V}{\pi R} = \dfrac{30 \cdot 8 \cdot 12}{\pi \cdot 40} = \dfrac{2880}{125.66} \approx 22.9 \; \text{rpm} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** Modern utility-scale turbines rotate at ~15–25 rpm — slow enough that the blade tips still travel at ~80 m/s, near the speed of sound. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Wind Turbine Anatomy — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Atmospheric Boundary Layer
Wind Resource] --> B[Wind Shear Profile
Velocity Increases with Height] B --> C[Rotor Hub
Diameter D] C --> D[Aerodynamic Blades
Airfoil Section NACA 44xx] D --> E[Rotor Disk
Swept Area A = pi R squared] E --> F[Main Shaft
Low-Speed Rotational Shaft] F --> G[Gearbox
Speed Multiplier 1 colon 50 to 1 colon 100] G --> H[High-Speed Shaft
1500 rpm Typical] H --> I[Generator
Doubly Fed Induction or PMG] I --> J[Power Converter
AC to DC to AC] J --> K[Transformer
Step Up to Grid Voltage] K --> L[Grid Interconnection
Point of Common Coupling] M[Yaw Drive System] --> C N[Pitch Control System] --> D O[Anemometer and Wind Vane] --> P[SCADA Controller] P --> M P --> N Q[Brake System
Mechanical and Aerodynamic] --> G R[Tower Structure
Tubular Steel 60 to 100 m] --> C ``` ### 4.2 Betz Actuator-Disk Flow Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Undisturbed Upstream Flow
Velocity V1
Pressure p infinity
Cross-section A1] -->|Stream Tube Contract| B[Rotor Disk
Actuator Model
Velocity V2 = V1 1 minus a
Velocity V3 = V1 1 minus 2a] B -->|Wake Expansion| C[Downstream Wake
Velocity V3
Pressure p infinity
Cross-section A3] D[Thrust Force F on Disk] --> B E[Power Extracted P = F times V2] --> B F[Betz Limit 16 by 27] --> E G[Axial Induction Factor a = 1 by 3] --> F ``` ### 4.3 Classification of WECS — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD WECS[Wind Energy Conversion Systems] --> Axis[Axis of Rotation] Axis --> HAWT[Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine] Axis --> VAWT[Vertical Axis Wind Turbine] HAWT --> HAWT1[Upwind Rotor] HAWT --> HAWT2[Downwind Rotor] HAWT1 --> HAWT1a[Three Blade Utility HAWT] HAWT1 --> HAWT1b[Two Blade Smaller Scale] HAWT1 --> HAWT1c[Multiblade Farm Water Pumping] VAWT --> VAWT1[Savonius Drag Type] VAWT --> VAWT2[Darrieus Egg Beater Lift Type] VAWT --> VAWT3[Giromill H Rotor] VAWT --> VAWT4[Twisted Savonius Hybrid] WECS --> Power[Power Output Type] Power --> Power1[Standalone Off Grid] Power --> Power2[Grid Connected Utility] Power --> Power3[Hybrid Wind Diesel Solar] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of a Grid-Connected WECS ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wind Kinetic Energy Source] --> B[Blade Rotor Assembly
Mechanical Power Output] B --> C[Gearbox
Speed Step Up] C --> D[Generator Unit
Induction or PMG] D --> E[Rectifier and Inverter
Power Electronics] E --> F[Filter and Protection] F --> G[Step Up Transformer] G --> H[Utility Grid 11 kV to 400 kV] I[Pitch Controller] --> B J[Yaw Controller] --> B K[SCADA Monitoring] --> D K --> E L[Load Demand Signal] --> H ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEMET614 Module 2]** State **Betz's Law** and explain its significance in the design of wind turbines. **Model Answer:** Betz's Law, formulated by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919, states that **no wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈59.26%) of the kinetic energy in the wind**. Mathematically, the maximum power coefficient is: $$ C_{P,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 $$ This maximum is achieved when the **axial induction factor** $a = 1/3$, meaning the wake velocity downstream is exactly two-thirds of the upstream velocity. **Significance in Design:** - Provides the **theoretical upper bound** on turbine efficiency. - Real turbines fall short due to **aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses** (typically achieving $C_P = 0.35$–$0.45$). - Acts as a **design benchmark** — engineers attempt to approach but never exceed Betz's limit. - Crucial for **capacity estimation and feasibility studies** in wind farm projects. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEMET614 Module 2]** Define **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)**. What is its typical range for modern utility-scale three-blade horizontal axis wind turbines (HAWTs)? **Model Answer:** The Tip Speed Ratio is the dimensionless ratio of the blade tip linear velocity to the free-stream wind velocity: $$ \lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R n}{30 V} $$ where $\omega$ is angular velocity, $R$ is rotor radius, $V$ is wind speed, and $n$ is rotational speed in rpm. For **modern utility-scale 3-blade HAWTs**, the optimum TSR is typically in the range **$\lambda = 7$ to $9$**, which balances high aerodynamic efficiency with acceptable noise and structural loads. Multi-blade pumps operate at $\lambda \approx 0.5$–$1$, while Savonius VAWTs operate at $\lambda \approx 0.7$–$1$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEMET614 Module 2]** **(a)** Derive the expression for **power available in the wind** passing through a swept area $A$. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the **Betz limit** using the actuator disk theory and state the corresponding optimal axial induction factor. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Power Available in the Wind (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Consider an elemental mass of air passing through a cross-sectional area $A$ with wind velocity $V$ normal to the area. **Step 2:** In time interval $\Delta t$, the air column of length $V \Delta t$ crosses area $A$. The mass of this column is: $$ m = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \Delta t $$ **Step 3:** Mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{m}{\Delta t} = \rho A V $$ **Step 4:** Kinetic energy of this mass: $$ KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m V^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} (\rho A V \Delta t) V^2 $$ **Step 5:** Power (energy per unit time): $$ P_{\text{wind}} = \dfrac{KE}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \quad [\text{Watts}] $$ --- **(b) Betz Limit Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Model rotor as actuator disk. Let $V_1$, $V_2$, $V_3$ be velocities upstream, at disk, and downstream (wake), with $V_1 > V_2 > V_3$. **Step 2:** Continuity: $\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A V_2 = \rho A_3 V_3$ **Step 3:** Momentum (thrust): $$ F = \rho A V_2 (V_1 - V_3) $$ **Step 4:** Bernoulli (upstream + downstream, with $p_1 = p_3 = p_\infty$): $$ F = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A (V_1^2 - V_3^2) $$ **Step 5:** Equating forces and solving: $$ V_2 = \dfrac{V_1 + V_3}{2} $$ **Step 6:** Define axial induction factor $a$: $V_2 = V_1 (1 - a)$, so $V_3 = V_1 (1 - 2a)$. **Step 7:** Power extracted: $$ P_{\text{ext}} = F \cdot V_2 = \rho A V_2^2 (V_1 - V_3) = 2\rho A V_1^3 a (1 - a)^2 $$ **Step 8:** Power coefficient: $$ C_P = \dfrac{P_{\text{ext}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \dfrac{2\rho A V_1^3 a(1-a)^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2 $$ **Step 9:** Maximize: $\dfrac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$ **Step 10:** Optimal $a = 1/3$, giving: $$ \boxed{C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926} $$ The optimal axial induction factor is $a = 1/3$, with downstream wake velocity $V_3 = V_1/3$. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEMET614 Module 2]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classification of Wind Energy Conversion Systems** based on axis of rotation. Compare Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT). (7 marks) **(b)** A horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 50 m and operates at a wind speed of 10 m/s. The air density is 1.20 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) The total wind power available. (ii) Maximum power extractable using Betz's law. (iii) The rotor rotational speed in rpm if the tip speed ratio is 7. Assume the turbine operates at Betz-optimal condition. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Classification of WECS (7 marks):** WECS are classified into two broad categories based on axis of rotation: **1. Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs):** - Axis of rotation is **parallel to the wind direction** (horizontal). - Requires **yaw mechanism** to keep the rotor facing the wind. - Types: Upwind (most common), Downwind. - Sub-types: 3-blade utility HAWTs (large scale), 2-blade HAWTs, Multiblade farm HAWTs (water pumping). - **Higher efficiency** (40–45% practical $C_P$). - **Self-starting** in most designs. **2. Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs):** - Axis of rotation is **perpendicular to the wind direction** (vertical). - **No yaw system** required — wind-omnidirectional. - Types: **Savonius** (drag-based, low speed, high torque), **Darrieus** ("egg beater," lift-based), **Giromill** (H-rotor with variable pitch). - **Lower efficiency** (20–30% practical $C_P$). - **Lower tip speed**, safer maintenance at ground level. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | HAWT | VAWT | |---|---|---| | Axis orientation | Horizontal | Vertical | | Yaw required | Yes | No | | Efficiency | Higher | Lower | | Maintenance | At hub height | At ground | | Self-starting | Yes (most) | Drag-types yes; Darrieus no | | Wind direction sensitivity | High | Low | | Typical TSR | 7–9 | 0.5–2 | | Dominant scale | Utility/massive | Small/distributed | --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $D = 50 \; \text{m}$, $V = 10 \; \text{m/s}$, $\rho = 1.20 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\lambda = 7$. **Step 1 — Compute rotor radius and swept area:** $$ R = \dfrac{D}{2} = 25 \; \text{m}, \quad A = \pi R^2 = \pi (25)^2 = 1963.5 \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Total wind power (i):** $$ P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot (10)^3 $$ $$ P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot 1000 = 1{,}178{,}100 \; \text{W} \approx 1.178 \; \text{MW} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum extractable power (Betz-limited) (ii):** $$ P_{\text{Betz}} = C_{P,\max} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5926 \cdot 1{,}178{,}100 $$ $$ P_{\text{Betz}} \approx 698{,}100 \; \text{W} \approx 698.1 \; \text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Rotational speed (iii):** $$ n = \dfrac{30 \lambda V}{\pi R} = \dfrac{30 \cdot 7 \cdot 10}{\pi \cdot 25} = \dfrac{2100}{78.54} $$ $$ n \approx 26.74 \; \text{rpm} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > - **Forgetting the cubed term** — students often write $V^2$ instead of $V^3$ in the wind power formula. This is the **#1 source of marks lost** on this topic. > - **Not stating assumptions** of the actuator disk model (frictionless, no swirl, incompressible flow) — examiners expect these to be listed before the Betz derivation. > - **Unit conversion errors** — using cm instead of m, or omitting density units. Always verify $\rho$ in kg/m³ and $V$ in m/s. > - **Forgetting $C_{P,\max}$ is dimensionless** — leaving units in the final answer indicates confusion. > - **Confusing $C_P$ and $C_T$** — $C_T$ is the thrust coefficient (max ≈ 8/9), $C_P$ is the power coefficient (max ≈ 16/27). Do not interchange them. > - **Skipping the maximization step** — examiners specifically award marks for showing $\dfrac{dC_P}{da} = 0$ and solving for $a = 1/3$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ⭐ **Power in Wind:** $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ — Cubic dependence on velocity is the key fact. - ⭐ **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$, achieved at axial induction factor $a = 1/3$. - ⭐ **Turbine Power:** $P_{\text{turbine}} = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$. - ⭐ **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi R n / (30V)$; optimum ≈ 7–9 for HAWTs. - ⭐ **Swept Area:** $A = \pi R^2$ — Doubling rotor diameter gives **4×** the area and **4×** the power. - ⭐ **Wind Shear:** $V/V_0 = (h/h_0)^\alpha$, with $\alpha \in [0.10, 0.40]$ depending on terrain. - ⭐ **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at 15 °C, sea level. - ⭐ **Classification:** HAWT (horizontal axis, high efficiency) vs. VAWT (vertical axis, omnidirectional). - ⭐ **Mass Flow:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V$. - ⭐ **Thrust:** $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^2 C_T$, with $C_T \le 8/9$. - ⭐ **Power Scales with $V^3$:** 10% speed error → 33% power error. - ⭐ **Actuator Disk Model:** Assumes frictionless, incompressible, swirl-free flow. - ⭐ **Practical $C_P$:** Real turbines achieve 35–45%, well below Betz's 59.26%. - ⭐ **Annual Energy:** $E = P_{\text{rated}} \times \text{Capacity Factor} \times 8760 \; \text{hr/yr}$. - ⭐ **Generator Speed:** $n = 60f/p$ — ensures grid synchronization (e.g., 1500 rpm for 50 Hz, 4-pole induction).

topics.title: Classification of WECS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of WECS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated electromechanical apparatus that captures the kinetic energy present in moving air masses and transforms it into useful forms of energy—primarily **mechanical shaft work** and subsequently **electrical energy**. The classification of WECS refers to the systematic categorization of wind turbines based on their **architectural geometry**, **operational dynamics**, and **installation topology**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Equivalent):**
> *"Wind Energy Conversion Systems are classified based on the orientation of the axis of rotation, the number of blades, the type of aerodynamic loading, the speed control mechanism, and the deployment site. The two principal categories are Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs)."*

### 1.2 Primary Classification Axes

The KTU 2024 Scheme module emphasizes **five primary classification parameters**:

1. **Axis of Rotation** → HAWT vs. VAWT
2. **Aerodynamic Force Exploited** → Lift-type vs. Drag-type
3. **Speed Control Strategy** → Constant Speed (CSWT) vs. Variable Speed (VSWT)
4. **Rotor Blade Count** → Single, Two, Three, Multi-blade
5. **Deployment Site** → Onshore vs. Offshore

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Kite and the Merry-Go-Round":**
> 
> Imagine a **kite** flying in the wind. The kite's string is attached to a fixed point on the ground, and as the wind pushes the kite, the **string is pulled horizontally** — this is analogous to a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)**, where the wind's force is captured perpendicular to the rotation axis, just like the kite's string tension.
> 
> Now imagine a **merry-go-round** spinning on a vertical pole in a strong wind. The wind pushes against the curved panels mounted on the rotating platform, causing it to spin about a **vertical shaft** — this perfectly mirrors a **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)**, where the rotation axis is parallel to the ground (and to the wind direction), and the blades sweep through the wind like a vertical carousel.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

The fundamental quantity used to characterize wind resource and turbine output is the **power available in the wind**, given by:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density (typically **1.225 kg/m³** at standard sea-level conditions)
- $A$ = swept area of the rotor in m²
- $V$ = undisturbed free-stream wind velocity in m/s

> [!TIP]
> **Why $V^3$ matters:** A mere **2× increase in wind speed yields 8× more power**. This cubic dependency is the single most important reason why wind farm site selection prioritizes locations with consistently high mean wind velocities. The **Betz Limit** of **16/27 ≈ 59.3%** caps the maximum extractable fraction of this power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Axis of Rotation

#### 2.1.1 Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs)

The rotor shaft is **parallel to the ground** and **perpendicular to the wind direction**. A yaw mechanism (or self-yawing tail) orients the rotor into the wind.

**Sub-classifications of HAWTs:**

| Sub-type | Characteristics | Typical Application |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Dutch Windmill** | 4 wooden lattice blades, slow rotation, low Tip Speed Ratio (TSR ≈ 1–2) | Mechanical grain pumping, historical drainage |
| **Multi-blade (American)** | 20–24 metal blades, very low TSR ≈ 0.5–1, high starting torque | Water pumping on farms |
| **Propeller-type (Modern)** | 2 or 3 aerodynamic blades, TSR = 5–8, high efficiency | **Utility-scale electricity generation (dominant)** |
| **Turbo / High-speed** | 2-bladed, slender airfoils, TSR > 8 | Specialized high-wind regimes |

#### 2.1.2 Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs)

The rotor shaft is **vertical** (perpendicular to the ground, parallel to wind). These machines accept wind from **any horizontal direction** without yawing.

**Sub-classifications of VAWTs:**

| Sub-type | Aerodynamic Principle | TSR Range | Self-Starting? |
|----------|----------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Savonius** | Pure **drag** differential | 0.3 – 0.5 | ✅ Yes |
| **Darrieus (Egg-beater)** | Pure **lift** (troposkein shape) | 4 – 6 | ❌ No (requires boost) |
| **Giromill / H-Rotor** | Lift with straight blades | 3.5 – 5 | ❌ No |
| **H-Darrieus (Cycloturbine)** | Lift, two-bladed H-frame | 3 – 4 | ❌ No |

### 2.2 Classification Based on Aerodynamic Force

- **Drag-type machines:** The wind exerts a **form drag** force on a flat or cupped surface. The power coefficient $C_P$ is fundamentally limited by the Betz limit, but practical efficiency is further constrained by the fact that a drag device cannot move faster than the wind. **Example:** Savonius rotor, traditional Persian windmills.
- **Lift-type machines:** The wind flows over an **airfoil-shaped blade**, generating a pressure differential that produces a **lift force perpendicular to the relative wind**. These can rotate faster than the free-stream wind speed. **Example:** Modern propeller turbines, Darrieus rotors.

### 2.3 Classification Based on Speed Control

- **Constant Speed Wind Turbine (CSWT):** The generator is directly grid-coupled (often via an induction generator with limited slip). The rotor speed is essentially fixed at synchronous speed (with ~1–2% slip). Simpler, cheaper, but suffers from high mechanical stress and poor energy capture in fluctuating winds.
- **Variable Speed Wind Turbine (VSWT):** Uses **power electronics (back-to-back converters)** to decouple rotor speed from grid frequency. The rotor turns at an **optimal TSR** that maximizes $C_P$ for each wind speed. Higher energy yield (~10–15% more annual energy), reduced structural loads, but increased converter cost and complexity.

**Two principal VSWT control strategies:**

1. **Pitch Control (Aerodynamic):** The blade pitch angle $\beta$ is actively rotated about its long axis to shed aerodynamic lift during high winds. Above rated wind speed, pitch is increased to limit power.
2. **Stall Control (Passive Aerodynamic):** The blade is deliberately designed so that at high wind speeds, the airflow **separates from the blade surface** (stalls), causing lift collapse and natural power limitation. No moving parts, but harder to fine-tune.

> [!NOTE]
> **Active Stall (Combined):** Modern turbines use a hybrid — blades can pitch *forward* into reverse-pitch to induce deep stall. This provides smooth power limiting without the abruptness of passive stall.

### 2.4 Classification Based on Rotor Configuration & Site

- **Upwind vs. Downwind:** In an upwind turbine, the rotor faces the wind and the tower sits *behind* the rotor disk. In a downwind turbine, the wind passes the tower first, creating a **tower-shadow effect** (cyclic load variation), but the rotor naturally conies into the wind (passive yaw).
- **Onshore vs. Offshore:** Offshore sites benefit from higher, steadier mean wind speeds (due to low surface roughness over water), but face higher capital expenditure (CAPEX), corrosion protection, and foundation engineering challenges (monopile, jacket, or floating substructures for deep water).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Power available in undisturbed wind | W |
| 2 | $C_P = \frac{P_{rotor}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2 P_{rotor}}{\rho A V^{3}}$ | Power coefficient (efficiency of extraction) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_P \leq C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | **Betz Limit** (maximum theoretical $C_P$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_T = \frac{F_{thrust}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{2}}$ | Thrust coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{rotor} = C_P(\lambda, \beta) \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Rotor power with $C_P$ dependence on TSR and pitch | W |
| 7 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Rotor swept area | m² |
| 8 | $E_{annual} = \sum_{i} P_{i} \cdot \Delta t$ | Annual energy (sum of power × time intervals) | Wh or kWh |
| 9 | $C_{FOM} = \frac{Annualized\ Fixed\ O\&M\ Cost}{Rated\ Power}$ | Fixed Operations & Maintenance cost coefficient | $/kW·yr |
| 10 | $LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelized Cost of Energy | $/kWh |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the daily workhorses of wind farm design. The $C_P$-$\lambda$-$\beta$ surface is the single most important performance map for any HAWT — it dictates the optimal operating point, the cut-in/cut-out strategy, and the pitch-control law. The **LCOE** formula is what financial analysts and utility planners use to compare wind energy against solar PV, fossil, and nuclear alternatives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Available in the Wind

This is the foundational derivation for the entire classification of WECS, because it sets the upper bound on what any wind turbine — regardless of its axis orientation, blade count, or aerodynamic principle — can ever extract.

**Starting Concept:** Consider a stationary, cylindrical control volume of air with cross-sectional area $A$ (equal to the rotor's swept area) and an undisturbed free-stream velocity $V$ approaching the turbine from the left.

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy Flux (Power) in the Wind Stream**

The mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ of air passing through area $A$ per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$$

where $\rho$ is the air density in kg/m³. This equation arises from the fundamental mass-flow definition $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$, with the volumetric flow rate $\dot{V} = A \cdot V$.

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy Per Unit Mass of Air**

The kinetic energy stored in 1 kg of air moving at velocity $V$ is:

$$KE_{per\ kg} = \frac{1}{2} V^{2}$$

This is the standard Newtonian kinetic energy relation for a particle of mass $m$ in translation.

**Step 3 — Power = Energy Flux**

Multiplying the per-unit-mass energy by the mass flow rate gives the total kinetic energy flux through area $A$, which is the power available in the wind:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \cdot KE_{per\ kg} = (\rho A V) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} V^{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$

This is the power of the undisturbed wind that impinges on the turbine's swept area. It is the **upstream**, unobstructed power, and therefore the absolute upper bound on what any WECS (HAWT, VAWT, drag, or lift) can ever extract.

**Step 4 — Introducing the Power Coefficient**

The rotor cannot capture 100% of this power. Define the power coefficient $C_P$ as the ratio of mechanical power extracted by the rotor to the upstream wind power:

$$C_P = \frac{P_{rotor}}{P_{wind}}$$

Therefore, the actual mechanical power extracted by the rotor is:

$$P_{rotor} = C_P \cdot P_{wind} = C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$

**Step 5 — The Betz Limit (Justification)**

A complete derivation from conservation of mass, momentum, and Bernoulli's equation shows that for an **idealized actuator disk** extracting maximum power with no rotational wake (no swirl), the maximum $C_P$ is:

$$C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%$$

This is the **Betz Limit**. No WECS — regardless of classification — can exceed this. The remaining 40.7% of the kinetic energy must remain in the wake to keep the air moving away from the turbine (otherwise it would pile up indefinitely, violating conservation of mass).

**Step 6 — Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) Connection to Classification**

The tip speed ratio is defined as the ratio of the blade tip linear speed to the free-stream wind speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$$

where $\omega$ is the angular speed of the rotor in rad/s and $R$ is the rotor radius. The connection to classification is direct:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{High-speed (Propeller HAWT):} \quad & \lambda \approx 6\text{ to }8 \text{ (high } C_P \text{, low starting torque)} \\
\text{Multi-blade HAWT:} \quad & \lambda \approx 0.5\text{ to }1 \text{ (low } C_P \text{, very high starting torque)} \\
\text{Savonius VAWT (drag):} \quad & \lambda \leq 1 \text{ (limited by drag, cannot exceed free-stream speed)} \\
\text{Darrieus VAWT (lift):} \quad & \lambda \approx 4\text{ to }6 \text{ (high TSR possible because lift, not drag, drives rotation)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Why Classification Matters for $C_P$**

Each WECS classification corresponds to a *different region* of the $C_P$-$\lambda$ performance curve. The $C_P$ value is **not a constant**; it is a function of the TSR and (for pitch-controlled machines) the blade pitch angle $\beta$:

$$C_P = f(\lambda, \beta)$$

The peak $C_P$ of a Savonius rotor tops out around **0.15–0.20** (limited by drag). A well-designed 3-bladed HAWT can reach **0.45–0.50** in field conditions, with a BEM (Blade Element Momentum) theoretical maximum approaching 0.48–0.50 for clean, stall-free airfoils.

### 3.2 Derivation: Classification Justification via Force Analysis

**Lift-based WECS (e.g., Darrieus, HAWT):**

The relative wind velocity seen by a blade moving at linear speed $v_{blade} = \omega R$ is:

$$V_{rel} = \sqrt{V^{2} + (\omega R)^{2}} = V\sqrt{1 + \lambda^{2}}$$

The lift force $L$ on an airfoil section of chord $c$ is:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{rel}^{2} \cdot c \cdot C_L(\alpha)$$

where $C_L$ is the lift coefficient (function of angle of attack $\alpha$). The tangential component of $L$ (and its associated drag $D$ component) produces torque on the rotor. As $\omega$ increases, $V_{rel}$ increases, and so does the available lift — this is why lift-based machines can sustain high TSR.

**Drag-based WECS (e.g., Savonius):**

The driving force is pure drag:

$$F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{rel}^{2} \cdot A_{cup} \cdot C_D$$

The fundamental constraint is that a drag device cannot move faster than the wind (otherwise the relative velocity would reverse). Therefore:

$$v_{blade} \leq V \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} \leq 1$$

This single inequality is the mathematical reason drag-based VAWTs are inherently low-speed, low-$C_P$ machines — a defining characteristic of their classification.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix for WECS Classification

| Classification Parameter | HAWT (Propeller) | VAWT (Darrieus) | VAWT (Savonius) | Multi-blade HAWT |
|--------------------------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Axis orientation** | Horizontal | Vertical | Vertical | Horizontal |
| **Aerodynamic principle** | Lift | Lift | Drag | Lift + Drag |
| **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** | 6 – 8 | 4 – 6 | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 1 |
| **Peak $C_P$ (field)** | 0.40 – 0.50 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.12 – 0.20 | 0.15 – 0.25 |
| **Self-starting** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (needs boost) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Yaw mechanism required** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (omnidirectional) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Tower shadow effect** | Minimal (upwind design) | N/A | N/A | Moderate |
| **Capacity range** | 1 kW – 15 MW+ | 1 kW – 5 MW | 0.1 kW – 100 kW | 0.5 kW – 50 kW |
| **Dominant application** | Utility-scale grid | Building-integrated, urban | Residential, off-grid | Water pumping, rural |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Classification Tree of WECS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WECS"]:::root
    A --> B["Axis of Rotation"]:::cat
    A --> C["Aerodynamic Force"]:::cat
    A --> D["Speed Control"]:::cat
    A --> E["Blade Count"]:::cat
    A --> F["Deployment Site"]:::cat

    B --> B1["HAWT Horizontal Axis"]:::sub
    B --> B2["VAWT Vertical Axis"]:::sub

    B1 --> B1a["Dutch Windmill"]
    B1 --> B1b["Multi-blade American"]
    B1 --> B1c["Modern Propeller 2 or 3 blade"]
    B1 --> B1d["High speed Turbo"]

    B2 --> B2a["Savonius Drag type"]
    B2 --> B2b["Darrieus Lift type Egg beater"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Classify Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS) based on the **axis of rotation**. List any two subtypes of Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs) with their aerodynamic principles.

**Model Answer:**

WECS are primarily classified into two categories based on the axis of rotation:

1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs):** The rotor shaft is parallel to the ground and perpendicular to the wind direction. A yaw mechanism or self-yawing tail is required to orient the rotor. *Example:* Modern 3-bladed propeller turbine.

2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs):** The rotor shaft is vertical and parallel to the wind direction. These machines are **omnidirectional** and do not require a yaw system. Two important subtypes are:

   - **Savonius rotor** – a *drag-type* machine that uses two half-cylindrical cups mounted on a vertical shaft. The drag differential between the concave and convex sides produces torque. It is self-starting but has a low power coefficient ($C_P \approx 0.15$).
   - **Darrieus rotor** – a *lift-type* machine with two or three curved blades forming an "egg-beater" or troposkein shape. It achieves higher TSR ($\lambda \approx 4$ to $6$) and higher $C_P$ ($\approx 0.30$), but is **not self-starting** and requires an auxiliary motor or aerodynamic fins to begin rotation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **lift-type** and **drag-type** wind machines. State one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lift-Type | Drag-Type |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Operating principle** | Uses the **aerodynamic lift** generated by airflow over an airfoil-shaped blade. | Uses the **aerodynamic drag** (form drag) on a bluff body. |
| **Tip speed ratio (TSR)** | High ($\lambda = 4$ to $8$); blade tips can move faster than the free-stream wind. | Low ($\lambda \leq 1$); blade tips cannot exceed the free-stream wind speed. |
| **Power coefficient ($C_P$)** | Higher, up to 0.40–0.50 in good designs. | Lower, typically 0.10–0.20. |
| **Self-starting** | Often not self-starting (e.g., Darrieus) but propeller HAWTs are. | Usually self-starting. |
| **Example** | Modern 3-bladed HAWT (propeller); Darrieus VAWT. | Savonius VAWT; traditional Persian vertical-axis windmill. |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of WECS based on the axis of rotation**. Compare HAWTs and VAWTs in terms of construction, aerodynamics, applications, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **Savonius Vertical Axis Wind Turbine**. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Classification of WECS based on axis of rotation:**

WECS are classified into two broad categories:

**1. Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs)**

In HAWTs, the axis of rotation is **horizontal** (parallel to the ground) and **perpendicular to the wind direction**. The rotor must be yawed to face the wind.

- **Construction:** Rotor with 2 or 3 aerodynamic blades, mounted on a horizontal shaft housed inside a nacelle, atop a tall tower. The nacelle contains the gearbox, generator, yaw motor, and control electronics. A wind vane / anemometer provides feedback to the yaw and pitch controllers.
- **Aerodynamics:** Lift-dominated. The airfoil blade is set at a precise angle of attack $\alpha$ relative to the relative wind. Lift $\propto C_L \cdot V_{rel}^{2}$, producing tangential force and torque.
- **Applications:** Utility-scale grid-connected electricity generation; modern turbines reach 15 MW rated power (e.g., Vestas V236-15.0 MW, SG 14-222 DD).
- **Advantages:** Highest power coefficient (up to 0.50 in field); mature technology; tall tower accesses higher, less-turbulent wind; efficient aerodynamic airfoils.
- **Limitations:** Requires yaw mechanism and complex controls; tower shadow and cyclic loads on blades; visually intrusive; transport and installation challenges for blades longer than 80 m.

**2. Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs)**

In VAWTs, the axis of rotation is **vertical** (perpendicular to the ground, parallel to the wind direction). The rotor is omnidirectional.

- **Construction:** Rotor blades mounted on a vertical shaft; generator and gearbox can be placed at ground level (simplifying maintenance).
- **Aerodynamics:** Either drag-type (Savonius) or lift-type (Darrieus, Giromill).
- **Applications:** Building-integrated systems, urban micro-generation, off-grid remote power.
- **Advantages:** No yaw system needed; generator and gearbox at ground level; omnidirectional wind acceptance; lower noise signature at ground level; can accept turbulent, multi-directional winds.
- **Limitations:** Lower $C_P$ than HAWTs (peak ~0.30 for Darrieus, ~0.15 for Savonius); self-starting issues for lift-type VAWTs; lower tip speeds limit output.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Savonius VAWT — Construction and Working:**

The Savonius rotor was invented by Finnish engineer Sigurd Johannes Savonius in 1922. It consists of **two or more half-cylindrical "S-shaped" buckets** (typically made of sheet metal or fiberglass) mounted on a vertical shaft. The two halves are offset so that the concave side of one bucket faces the wind while the convex side of the other bucket presents to the wind.

**Working Principle:**

When wind strikes the rotor, the **drag force on the concave (cup) side is much larger** than the drag on the convex (back) side. This is because the concave surface creates a low-pressure "trap" and the airflow must travel further around the cup, increasing form drag. The differential drag produces a **net tangential force**, which spins the rotor about the vertical axis.

The fundamental limitation is that the **tip speed cannot exceed the free-stream wind speed**, so:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} \leq 1$$

The Savonius operates at $\lambda \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$, peaking at $C_P \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$ — well below the Betz limit of 0.593 and far below modern HAWTs.

**Merits:**
- **Self-starting** at very low wind speeds (~2–3 m/s)
- Simple, rugged, cheap construction
- No yaw mechanism required
- Performs well in turbulent, multi-directional winds (suitable for urban rooftops)
- Quiet operation

**Demerits:**
- Low $C_P$ and low power-to-size ratio
- Low tip speed and rotational speed (large, heavy generator required for electricity)
- Pulsating torque output (requires smoothing for electrical generation)
- Aerodynamic stall in very high winds
- Limited commercial scalability (mostly small-scale, < 100 kW)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of WECS based on aerodynamic force** (lift vs. drag). With neat diagrams, explain the working of a **Darrieus rotor** and bring out its salient features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify WECS based on **speed control mechanism**. Differentiate between **pitch control** and **stall control** strategies, with their respective $C_P$-$\lambda$ characteristics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Classification based on aerodynamic force:**

Wind machines are classified as **lift-type** or **drag-type** depending on the dominant aerodynamic force that produces torque.

**Lift-type WECS:** Use the pressure differential (Bernoulli's principle) across an airfoil-shaped blade. The blade moves through the air with a relative velocity $V_{rel}$, generating a lift force $L \perp V_{rel}$. The tangential component of lift creates torque. Because lift is not bounded by the wind speed, the blade can travel faster than the free-stream wind, allowing high TSR ($\lambda = 4$ to $8$) and high $C_P$ (up to 0.50). *Examples:* HAWT propeller turbines, Darrieus VAWT, Giromill.

**Drag-type WECS:** Rely on the form drag of a bluff body. The wind pushes directly on a flat or cupped surface. Because drag force can never propel a body faster than the fluid (the so-called "windmilling constraint"), $\lambda \leq 1$. *Examples:* Savonius rotor, cup anemometer, traditional Persian windmills.

**Darrieus Rotor — Construction and Working:**

Invented by French engineer Georges Jean Marie Darrieus in 1931, the Darrieus rotor is a **lift-type VAWT** with two or three thin, curved blades (often called a "troposkein" or "egg-beater" shape) attached to a vertical shaft. The blades bow outward at their mid-span, following a catenary-like curve so that the bending stress due to centrifugal force is purely tensile — this is the elegant structural beauty of the Darrieus design.

When the wind blows, each blade experiences a **relative wind velocity** that is the vector sum of the free-stream wind $V$ and the tangential blade motion $v_{blade} = \omega R$:

$$V_{rel} = \sqrt{V^{2} + (\omega R)^{2}}$$

The angle of attack $\alpha$ between the chord line and $V_{rel}$ produces a lift force $L$ perpendicular to $V_{rel}$. The tangential component of $L$ drives the rotor.

A critical feature of the Darrieus rotor is that the **angle of attack varies cyclically** as the blade rotates through the wind:

- **Upstream half of rotation** (windward side): blade moves *with* the wind, $V_{rel}$ is reduced, $\alpha$ is large → strong positive torque.
- **Downstream half of rotation** (leeward side): blade moves *against* the wind, $V_{rel}$ is increased, $\alpha$ becomes negative (or small) → drag-dominated, weak or negative torque.

**Net torque is positive on average**, but the **torque varies cyclically during each rotation**, producing mechanical fatigue on the shaft and foundation.

**Salient features:**
- $C_P$ up to **0.30–0.35** (higher than Savonius)
- TSR = 4 to 6 (high-speed operation possible because lift, not drag, is the driver)
- Elegant pure-tension structure due to troposkein shape
- **NOT self-starting** — at $\omega = 0$, both blades experience the same drag and no net torque. Requires a small auxiliary motor or aerodynamic starting flaps.
- Generator can be placed at ground level → easy maintenance.
- Omnidirectional; no yaw required.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Classification of WECS based on speed control mechanism:**

**1. Constant Speed Wind Turbine (CSWT):**

In CSWTs, the generator is directly coupled (or via a fixed-ratio gearbox) to the grid. The rotor speed is essentially fixed at synchronous speed (with a small slip of 1–2% for induction generators). Simpler topology but poor energy capture in fluctuating winds.

**2. Variable Speed Wind Turbine (VSWT):**

In VSWTs, power electronics decouple the rotor speed from grid frequency. The rotor can turn at an **optimal TSR** ($\lambda_{opt}$) that maximizes $C_P$ for the prevailing wind speed. This yields 10–15% higher annual energy production. Two principal VSWT sub-classifications are:

**(i) Pitch-Controlled WECS:**

The blade pitch angle $\beta$ is **actively rotated** about its long axis by a hydraulic or electric pitch motor (one motor per blade, typically). 

- **Below rated wind speed** ($V < V_{rated}$): pitch is set to fine-tune $C_P$ for maximum capture.
- **Above rated wind speed** ($V > V_{rated}$): pitch is rotated to **feather** the blade (high $\beta$), reducing the angle of attack, shedding lift, and thereby limiting the power to the rated value of the generator.
- $C_P(\lambda, \beta)$ is a 2D function; the controller navigates this surface in real time.

**Advantages:** Smooth power regulation; precise control; good for high-power, large turbines.
**Disadvantages:** Complex pitch mechanism, prone to failure, increased cost.

**(ii) Stall-Controlled WECS:**

The blade is deliberately designed with an airfoil shape and twist distribution so that at wind speeds above the rated value, the airflow **separates from the blade surface** (aerodynamic stall occurs). The lift collapses, the drag rises, and the power is naturally limited to the rated value. No active mechanism is required.

**Advantages:** Simple, robust, no moving parts, low maintenance.
**Disadvantages:** Power is not precisely controlled; stall is an abrupt aerodynamic phenomenon; cannot be adjusted for varying air density.

**Active Stall-Controlled WECS:** A hybrid where blades can pitch *forward* (negative $\beta$) to induce controlled stall. This combines the simplicity of stall with the controllability of pitch — used in many modern megawatt-class turbines (e.g., NEG Micon, Bonus).

**$C_P$-$\lambda$ characteristic comparison:**

For each pitch angle $\beta$, the curve $C_P(\lambda)$ has a single peak at $\lambda_{opt}(\beta)$. As $\beta$ increases (blade feathered), the entire $C_P(\lambda)$ curve drops in amplitude. The pitch controller reduces $\beta$ to follow the $C_P,\max$ curve below rated wind speed, then increases $\beta$ to slide down the $C_P(\lambda, \beta)$ surface to hold power constant above rated wind speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing Savonius with Darrieus** (drag vs. lift) — many students incorrectly describe Savonius as a lift-type machine. This alone costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that Darrieus is NOT self-starting** — a classic conceptual trap. Examiners frequently award 1 mark for this specific point.
> 3. **Omitting the $\lambda \leq 1$ constraint** for drag-type machines. State it explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing pitch control with yaw control.** Pitch rotates the blade about its long axis; yaw rotates the entire nacelle about the tower's vertical axis. Examiners deduct 1 mark for mixing these up.
> 5. **Using outdated turbine ratings** in numerical examples. If you use a numerical illustration (e.g., a 100 kW Savonius), use realistic current values — outdated numbers suggest you haven't read current literature.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the Betz limit** when comparing $C_P$ values of different WECS classifications. The Betz limit is the universal ceiling — always state it.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **WECS** = Wind Energy Conversion System; converts kinetic energy of wind to mechanical then electrical energy.
- [x] **Primary classification axes:** axis of rotation, aerodynamic force, speed control, blade count, deployment site.
- [x] **HAWT** = Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine; modern 3-bladed propeller type is the dominant utility-scale design.
- [x] **VAWT** = Vertical Axis Wind Turbine; omnidirectional, no yaw needed, lower $C_P$ than HAWTs.
- [x] **Savonius** = drag-type VAWT, self-starting, low $C_P \approx 0.15$, low TSR $\leq 1$.
- [x] **Darrieus** = lift-type VAWT, troposkein / egg-beater shape, NOT self-starting, $C_P \approx 0.30$, TSR $\approx 4$–$6$.
- [x] **Lift vs. Drag:** Lift-based can have TSR > 1; drag-based is constrained to $\lambda \leq 1$.
- [x] **CSWT** = constant speed, grid-locked induction generator, simple but inefficient in fluctuating winds.
- [x] **VSWT** = variable speed, power electronics, tracks $\lambda_{opt}$ for $C_P,\max$, 10–15% more energy.
- [x] **Pitch control** = active blade rotation; smooth, precise; used in modern megawatt turbines.
- [x] **Stall control** = passive aerodynamic separation; no moving parts; abrupt.
- [x] **Active stall** = hybrid; pitch forward to induce deep stall.
- [x] **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — the universal upper bound.
- [x] **Power in wind:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ (cubic dependence on wind speed).
- [x] **Rotor power:** $P_{rotor} = C_P(\lambda, \beta) \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$.
- [x] **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$; classification-defining parameter.
- [x] **Onshore vs. Offshore:** Offshore has higher, steadier wind; higher CAPEX; monopile or floating foundations.
- [x] **Upwind vs. Downwind:** Upwind avoids tower shadow; downwind has passive yaw (self-coning).
- [x] **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level, 15°C.
- [x] **Key relationship:** $C_P$ is a *function* of $\lambda$ and $\beta$, not a constant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parts of WECS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parts of WECS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of WECS and Its Parts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS):** A WECS is an integrated electromechanical apparatus that captures the kinetic energy of moving air masses and converts it sequentially into rotational mechanical energy and finally into usable electrical energy through electromagnetic induction. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes its constituent components into **aerodynamic, mechanical, electrical, and control subsystems**, each performing a distinct stage of the energy conversion chain.

The "Parts of WECS" essentially refers to the **architectural anatomy** of a horizontal-axis grid-tied wind turbine — the dominant topology studied in KTU Module 2. These parts can be broadly classified under three macro-assemblies:

| Macro-Assembly | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Rotor Assembly** | Captures wind kinetic energy and converts it to low-speed rotation |
| **Nacelle Assembly** | Houses the power transmission, generation, and control hardware |
| **Support Assembly** | Provides structural elevation and orientation to the wind stream |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 places special emphasis on the *constituent parts, their constructional features, and the role each plays in maximizing the Coefficient of Performance (Cp)*. A typical utility-scale WECS is designed to operate within a **cut-in wind speed of 3 m/s** and a **cut-out wind speed of 25 m/s**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Modern Wind-Powered Flour Mill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a giant, high-tech water wheel — but instead of a flowing river, the "fluid" is invisible air, and instead of grinding wheat, the axle is bolted to an electrical generator. Every part of the WECS is essentially doing one of four jobs: **catch the wind, speed it up, generate electricity, or protect everything when the wind gets too violent.** The tower is simply the bridge pillar that lifts the wheel into the faster, less-turbulent air streams above our heads.

A rural Kerala paddy field gives a perfect local analogy: just as a farmer adjusts the *direction* of his threshing machine by hand to face the wind direction, a WECS does the same automatically through a **Yaw Drive**. Similarly, the *gears* on a bicycle (which a Keralite student rides to college) are exactly what the **gearbox** in a WECS does — multiply the slow, powerful rotation of the blades into a fast rotation suitable for the generator.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used in WECS Design

The following constants and metrics are standard throughout KTU Module 2 problems:

- **Air Density (ρ):** 1.225 kg/m³ (at Standard Sea Level, 15°C, ISA conditions)
- **Swept Area (A):** π R² (in m²), where R is the rotor radius
- **Power Coefficient (Cp):** Theoretical maximum = **0.593 (Betz Limit)**
- **Tip-Speed Ratio (λ):** ωR / V (dimensionless)
- **Gear Ratio (GR):** Ratio of generator RPM to rotor RPM
- **Tower Height Rule:** Typically ≥ 1.5 × rotor diameter to access laminar flow

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "RNTGYPBCT":** **R**otor, **N**acelle, **T**ower, **G**earbox, **Y**aw, **P**itch, **B**rake, **C**ontroller, **T**ransformer. Memorize this sequence for rapid 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Aerodynamic Subsystem (Rotor Side)

The rotor is the **single most critical aerodynamic component** of any WECS. It is a wind-energy collector that uses the principle of **lift** (not drag) to extract momentum from the incoming wind.

### 2.1.1 Rotor Blades
- Modern blades are **airfoil-shaped (typically S809, NREL S822, or NACA 44xx series profiles)**, modeled after aircraft wings.
- Each blade is a composite sandwich structure: a **PVC / PET foam core** sandwiched between **fiberglass or carbon-fiber reinforced epoxy laminates**.
- Blade lengths range from **40 m to 115 m** for offshore utility-scale turbines, with the **GE Haliade-X reaching 107 m** (as of recent engineering benchmarks).
- Each blade is mounted on the hub via a **pitch bearing**, allowing the blade to rotate about its own longitudinal axis to regulate the angle of attack.

### 2.1.2 Hub
- The hub is the **cast-iron or forged-steel central casting** that holds the three blades together.
- It transfers the aerodynamic torque from the blades to the **main shaft (low-speed shaft)**.
- The hub contains **pitch actuators** (hydraulic or electric servo motors) that collectively pitch all three blades — a process called **collective pitch control (CPC)**.

### 2.1.3 Spinner / Nose Cone
- The aerodynamic fairing attached to the front of the hub.
- Reduces **drag losses** and protects the pitch mechanism from rain, dust, and salt spray (critical for coastal Kerala installations).

## 2.2 The Nacelle Subsystem (Power Train)

The nacelle sits on top of the tower and is the **mechanical-electrical heart** of the turbine. It typically weighs between **50 to 200 tonnes** for a 2–5 MW turbine.

### 2.2.1 Main (Low-Speed) Shaft
- Connects the rotor hub to the gearbox.
- Operates at the rotor speed (typically **10–20 RPM** for a large utility turbine).
- Mounted on **two main bearings** (one cylindrical roller, one spherical roller) to handle radial and axial thrust loads.

### 2.2.2 Gearbox
- A planetary + helical compound gear train that steps up rotor RPM to generator RPM.
- Output ratio is typically **1:90 to 1:120** (e.g., rotor at 14 RPM → generator at 1500 RPM for 50 Hz grid).
- **Type:** 3-stage planetary (1st stage) + 2-stage helical (subsequent) is the industry standard.
- **KTU Insight:** Gearboxes incur **3–5% energy losses** and are the most maintenance-intensive component, leading to modern **direct-drive (gearless) PMSG turbines**.

### 2.2.3 Mechanical Brake (High-Speed Shaft Brake)
- A disc brake mounted on the high-speed shaft (between gearbox and generator).
- Used only as a **secondary / parking brake** during maintenance or emergencies.
- The primary aerodynamic braking is done by **full feathering of the blades** (pitch to 90°).

### 2.2.4 Generator
- Converts the mechanical rotation into electrical energy.
- **Common types studied in KTU Module 2:**
  - **SCIG** — Squirrel Cage Induction Generator (used in Danish concept, fixed speed)
  - **DFIG** — Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (used in 80%+ of modern turbines, variable speed ±30%)
  - **PMSG** — Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (used in direct-drive offshore turbines, e.g., Enercon, Siemens-Gamesa SG 8.0-167)

### 2.2.5 Yaw System
- Rotates the entire nacelle about the vertical tower axis to align the rotor with the prevailing wind direction.
- Consists of a **yaw bearing** (large slewing ring) and **yaw motors** (typically 4–6 geared motors).
- Controlled by the **wind vane (anemoscope)** mounted on top of the nacelle.
- Modern turbines use **active yaw** with a dead-band of ±5° to avoid continuous oscillation.

### 2.2.6 Controller (SCADA / PLC)
- The "brain" of the turbine. A Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) executes all real-time control loops.
- Manages: start-up, shut-down, yaw alignment, pitch control, grid synchronization, and fault diagnostics.
- Communicates remotely via **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** for remote monitoring from the plant control room.

## 2.3 The Support & Auxiliary Subsystem

### 2.3.1 Tower
- **Tubular steel tapered tower** (most common) or **concrete-steel hybrid** (for > 120 m hub heights).
- Tapered design (wider at base, narrower at top) provides optimal bending stiffness and material economy.
- **Hub heights** of 80–150 m are now standard, reaching **260 m for the tallest operational turbine** (Vestas V236-15.0 MW).

### 2.3.2 Foundation
- Reinforced concrete gravity foundation (most common onshore) or monopile / jacket (offshore).
- Designed to withstand **thrust loads** of 1500+ kN and **tower overturning moments** of 100,000+ kN·m for a 5 MW turbine.

### 2.3.3 Transformer & Grid Connection
- A **pad-mounted step-up transformer** (typically 690 V → 33 kV or 66 kV) is placed at the tower base.
- Connects to the substation via underground cables in modern wind farms.

### 2.3.4 Anemometer & Wind Vane
- **Anemometer:** Cup-type sensor for measuring wind speed.
- **Wind vane:** Measures wind direction and drives the yaw control loop.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams — Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Available wind power in the swept area | W |
| 2 | $P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind}$ | Mechanical power extracted by rotor | W |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V}$ | Tip-speed ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{p_{max}} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz limit (theoretical max $C_{p}$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N_{gen} = N_{rotor} \times GR$ | Generator speed via gearbox ratio | RPM |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Synchronous generator frequency relation | Hz |
| 7 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Rotor swept area | m² |
| 8 | $P_{electrical} = \eta_{gb} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{mech}$ | Net electrical output | W |
| 9 | $F_{thrust} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{2} C_{T}$ | Rotor thrust load on tower | N |
| 10 | $D = 2R$ | Rotor diameter | m |

Where:
- $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at standard conditions)
- $A$ = swept area
- $V$ = upstream wind speed
- $C_{p}$ = power coefficient
- $\omega$ = rotor angular velocity (rad/s)
- $R$ = rotor radius
- $C_{T}$ = thrust coefficient
- $N$ = rotational speed (RPM)
- $P$ = number of generator poles
- $\eta$ = component efficiency

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The parts of a WECS find direct application in:

1. **Wind Resource Assessment:** Swept area and hub-height wind speed are the two most decision-critical parameters for any Kerala wind farm site (e.g., Kanjikode, Muppandal, Ramakkalmedu).
2. **Predictive Maintenance:** Modern SCADA systems monitor gearbox oil temperature, generator winding temperature, and pitch motor currents for condition-based maintenance.
3. **Grid Stability Studies:** The DFIG and PMSG configurations determine the turbine's contribution to grid inertia and reactive power support.
4. **Hybrid Renewable Systems:** WECS components (especially the generator and controller) are directly integrated with solar PV in hybrid plants like the **Karnataka Pavagada Solar-Wind Hybrid Park**.
5. **Offshore Engineering:** The monopile foundation, tower, and nacelle must be designed for marine corrosion, wave loads, and 25-year design life.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Available Wind Power in the Rotor Swept Area

The most fundamental derivation in WECS analysis is for the **power available in the wind** crossing the swept area of the rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Premise:** Air is an incompressible fluid at typical wind turbine operating speeds (Mach < 0.3). The kinetic energy of an air mass *m* moving at velocity *V* is given by classical Newtonian mechanics.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Mass of air crossing the rotor per second:**

The mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ of air through the rotor swept area *A* is the density of air multiplied by the volumetric flow rate.

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V
$$

where:
- $\rho$ is the air density (kg/m³)
- $A$ is the swept area (m²)
- $V$ is the undisturbed upstream wind velocity (m/s)

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy of this air mass per second:**

By definition, kinetic energy is $E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$. Applied per unit time (i.e., power):

$$
P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the mass flow rate:**

We substitute the expression from Step 1 directly into Step 2.

$$
P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \rho A V \right) V^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Final simplification:**

The two $V$ terms are combined using the laws of exponents.

$$
P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}
$$

**Conclusion:** The available wind power scales **linearly with swept area, linearly with air density, and with the cube of wind speed**. This cubic dependency is the single most important fact in WECS economics — doubling the wind speed increases the power by a factor of 8.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mechanical Power Extracted (Power Coefficient Concept)

The rotor cannot extract 100% of the wind kinetic energy. The actual mechanical power is scaled by the **power coefficient** $C_{p}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the power coefficient:**

The power coefficient is defined as the ratio of mechanical power captured by the rotor to the total available wind power.

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{wind}}
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic rearrangement for $P_{mech}$:**

Multiplying both sides by $P_{wind}$ isolates the mechanical power.

$$
P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $P_{wind}$:**

We substitute the result from Section 3.1 (Step 4) into this equation.

$$
P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Final consolidated form:**

The coefficients are grouped together to yield the standard textbook form.

$$
P_{mech} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_{p}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the maximum theoretical value of $C_{p}$ is **0.593 (Betz limit)**, the maximum possible mechanical power from any wind turbine — even a perfect one — is **59.3%** of the available wind power. Real turbines achieve $C_{p}$ values in the range **0.35–0.45**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Power via the Betz Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Albert Betz published this derivation in 1919 in his book *Windenergie und ihre Ausnutzung durch Windmühlen* (Wind Energy and its Utilization through Windmills). It remains the foundational proof of WECS theory.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the velocity triangles:**

Let:
- $V_1$ = upstream wind velocity (m/s)
- $V_2$ = velocity at the rotor plane (m/s)
- $V_3$ = downstream wake velocity (m/s)
- $A_1 = A_3$ = cross-section far upstream and downstream
- $A_2 = A$ = rotor swept area

**Step 2 — Continuity equation (mass conservation):**

Since the air is incompressible and mass is conserved, the mass flow rate at the three stations must be equal.

$$
\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2 = \rho A_3 V_3
$$

**Step 3 — Momentum equation (force on the rotor):**

By Newton's second law, the force *F* on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream.

$$
F = \dot{m} \left( V_1 - V_3 \right) = \rho A_2 V_2 \left( V_1 - V_3 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Power extracted by the rotor:**

Power is force multiplied by the velocity at which the force acts (the velocity at the rotor plane, $V_2$).

$$
P = F \cdot V_2 = \rho A_2 V_2^{2} \left( V_1 - V_3 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Express $V_2$ as the arithmetic mean of $V_1$ and $V_3$:**

From actuator disk theory, the velocity at the rotor plane is the average of the upstream and downstream velocities.

$$
V_2 = \dfrac{V_1 + V_3}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V_2$ into the power equation:**

Replacing $V_2$ in Step 4 with the arithmetic mean gives a closed-form expression in terms of $V_1$ and $V_3$.

$$
P = \rho A_2 \left( \dfrac{V_1 + V_3}{2} \right)^{2} \left( V_1 - V_3 \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the squared term:**

The square of the arithmetic mean is expanded explicitly.

$$
P = \rho A_2 \left( \dfrac{V_1^{2} + 2 V_1 V_3 + V_3^{2}}{4} \right) \left( V_1 - V_3 \right)
$$

**Step 8 — Express as a fraction of the upstream power:**

We divide the extracted power by the available upstream power $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_1 V_1^{3}$. Recognizing $A_1 = A_2 = A$ in the far field:

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{P}{P_{wind}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( 1 + \dfrac{V_3}{V_1} \right)^{2} \left( 1 - \dfrac{V_3}{V_1} \right)
$$

**Step 9 — Define the induction factor:**

Let $a = \tfrac{V_1 - V_2}{V_1}$ (axial induction factor). It can be shown through the actuator disk theory that $V_3 = V_1 (1 - 2a)$ and $V_2 = V_1 (1 - a)$. Substituting these:

$$
C_{p} = 4 a (1 - a)^{2}
$$

**Step 10 — Maximize $C_{p}$ by differentiating with respect to $a$:**

Apply $\dfrac{d C_{p}}{d a} = 0$ to find the optimum induction factor.

$$
\dfrac{d C_{p}}{d a} = 4 \left[ (1 - a)^{2} - 2a(1 - a) \right] = 0
$$

**Step 11 — Solve for the optimum *a*:**

Factor out the common term $(1 - a)$ from the bracket.

$$
4 (1 - a) \left[ (1 - a) - 2a \right] = 0
$$

This gives two solutions: $a = 1$ (trivial, all energy absorbed) and $1 - 3a = 0$.

$$
a_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 12 — Compute the maximum $C_{p}$:**

Substitute $a_{opt} = 1/3$ back into the $C_{p}$ expression from Step 9.

$$
C_{p_{max}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{3} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \left( \dfrac{2}{3} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4}{9} = \dfrac{16}{27}
$$

**Step 13 — Numerical evaluation:**

The exact fraction is converted into a decimal value.

$$
C_{p_{max}} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \approx 0.593
$$

**Conclusion:** No wind turbine — no matter how perfectly designed — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy of the wind. This is the celebrated **Betz Limit**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**, operates at a wind speed of **12 m/s**, has a $C_{p}$ of **0.40**, a gearbox efficiency of **0.95**, and a generator efficiency of **0.92**. Calculate: (a) the swept area, (b) the available wind power, (c) the mechanical power captured, and (d) the net electrical output. Assume standard air density of **1.225 kg/m³**.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Rotor diameter $D = 80$ m
- Wind speed $V = 12$ m/s
- Power coefficient $C_{p} = 0.40$
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.95$
- Generator efficiency $\eta_{gen} = 0.92$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Part (a) — Swept Area:**

The radius is half the diameter, and the area of a circle is $\pi R^{2}$.

$$
R = \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times (40)^{2} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Part (b) — Available Wind Power:**

We apply the fundamental power-in-the-wind formula.

$$
P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^{3}
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \approx 5{,}318{,}163 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}
$$

**Part (c) — Mechanical Power Captured:**

The power coefficient scales the available wind power down to the mechanical output.

$$
P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5{,}318{,}163 \approx 2{,}127{,}265 \text{ W} \approx 2.13 \text{ MW}
$$

**Part (d) — Net Electrical Output:**

The mechanical power is multiplied by the two component efficiencies in cascade.

$$
P_{elec} = \eta_{gb} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{mech} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 2{,}127{,}265
$$

$$
P_{elec} = 0.874 \times 2{,}127{,}265 \approx 1{,}859{,}230 \text{ W} \approx 1.86 \text{ MW}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $A \approx 5026.55$ m²
- $P_{wind} \approx 5.32$ MW
- $P_{mech} \approx 2.13$ MW
- $P_{elec} \approx 1.86$ MW

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Generator Type

| Criterion | SCIG | DFIG | PMSG (Direct Drive) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Speed Range** | Fixed (≈±1%) | Variable (±30%) | Full Variable |
| **Gearbox Required?** | Yes | Yes | No (direct drive) |
| **Grid Code Compliance** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Reactive Power Control** | Requires capacitors | Yes (via rotor) | Yes (full converter) |
| **Maintenance Burden** | Moderate | High (gearbox + brushes) | Lowest |
| **Best Suited For** | Small turbines | Modern utility onshore | Offshore, large turbines |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | "Danish concept" | "Most common today" | "Future of offshore" |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question asks "which generator is used in modern grid-connected WECS?" — the answer is **DFIG** for variable-speed-with-gearbox configuration, and **PMSG** for direct-drive configuration. Don't confuse the two.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Horizontal-Axis WECS

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The following Mermaid block maps the *interactions and signal/power flow* between each part of a WECS. It follows the KTU Module 2 block-diagram convention rather than attempting a physical engineering drawing, which is impractical in text format.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Stream<br/>Kinetic Energy Source] --> B[Anemometer and Wind Vane<br/>Measurement Sensors]
    B --> C[Controller and SCADA<br/>Supervisory Logic]
    C --> D[Yaw Drive and Motor<br/>Nacelle Orientation]
    D --> E[Rotor Hub and Blades<br/>Aerodynamic Capture]
    E --> F[Pitch System<br/>Blade Angle Control]
    E --> G[Main Low Speed Shaft<br/>Torque Transmission]
    G --> H[Gearbox<br/>Speed Step Up]
    H --> I[High Speed Shaft with Brake<br/>Mechanical Safety]
    I --> J[Generator<br/>Electromechanical Conversion]
    J --> K[Power Converter and Transformer<br/>Grid Conditioning]
    K --> L[Electrical Grid<br/>Utility Network]
    C --> F
    C --> D
    C --> H
    C --> K
    M[Tower and Foundation<br/>Structural Support] --> E
    M --> D
```

### Architecture Flow Explanation (Step-by-Step):

1. **Input Stage — Wind Stream (Node A):** The atmospheric boundary layer air mass carries kinetic energy and is the primary energy source.
2. **Sensing Stage (Node B):** The **anemometer** measures wind speed and the **wind vane** measures wind direction. These analog signals are digitized and sent to the controller.
3. **Control Stage (Node C):** The **SCADA/PLC controller** is the master decision-maker. It compares measured values against setpoints and issues commands to multiple subsystems.
4. **Orientation Stage (Node D):** The **yaw drive** rotates the nacelle horizontally on the tower top to align the rotor plane perpendicular to the wind.
5. **Capture Stage (Nodes E and F):** The **blades and hub** capture wind energy via lift. The **pitch system** simultaneously adjusts the blade angle of attack to limit power at high wind speeds.
6. **Transmission Stage (Nodes G and H):** The **main shaft** carries low-RPM torque to the **gearbox**, which steps up the speed to generator-compatible RPM.
7. **Safety Stage (Node I):** The **mechanical disc brake** clamps the high-speed shaft during shutdowns and maintenance lockouts.
8. **Conversion Stage (Node J):** The **generator** (SCIG, DFIG, or PMSG) converts mechanical rotation into electrical energy.
9. **Grid Stage (Nodes K and L):** The **power electronic converter** conditions the variable-frequency output, and the **step-up transformer** raises voltage to grid levels before injection.
10. **Structural Stage (Node M):** The **tower and foundation** elevate the rotor into laminar wind and resist all thrust, weight, and overturning loads.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Subsystem Categorization of WECS Parts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AERO[Aerodynamic Subsystem]
        B1[Blades] --> B2[Hub]
        B2 --> B3[Spinner or Nose Cone]
    end

    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Subsystem]
        C1[Main Shaft] --> C2[Gearbox]
        C2 --> C3[High Speed Shaft]
        C3 --> C4[Mechanical Brake]
    end

    subgraph ELEC[Electrical Subsystem]
        D1[Generator] --> D2[Power Converter]
        D2 --> D3[Transformer]
        D3 --> D4[Grid]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control and Aux Subsystem]
        E1[Anemometer] --> E2[Wind Vane]
        E2 --> E3[Controller SCADA]
        E3 --> E4[Yaw Motor]
        E3 --> E5[Pitch Actuator]
    end

    subgraph SUPP[Support Subsystem]
        F1[Tower] --> F2[Foundation]
    end

    B2 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
    E4 --> AERO
    E5 --> AERO
    F1 --> E4
```

### Subsystem Summary Table:

| Subsystem | Components | Primary Engineering Discipline |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aerodynamic** | Blades, Hub, Spinner | Aerospace / Aerodynamics |
| **Mechanical** | Main Shaft, Gearbox, Brake | Mechanical Engineering |
| **Electrical** | Generator, Converter, Transformer, Grid | Electrical Engineering |
| **Control & Aux** | Anemometer, Wind Vane, SCADA, Yaw, Pitch | Instrumentation / Control |
| **Support** | Tower, Foundation | Civil / Structural Engineering |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Project Tip:** A multi-disciplinary WECS design project should ideally have a team of 5 students — one each from Aerospace (blades), Mechanical (gearbox), Electrical (generator), Control (SCADA), and Civil (tower). This mirrors actual industry turbine design teams at Suzlon, Vestas, and Siemens Gamesa.
