# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: List the major parts of a horizontal-axis wind energy conversion system and state the function of any three.**

#### Model Answer:

The major parts of a horizontal-axis WECS are: **rotor blades, hub, main shaft, gearbox, mechanical brake, generator, yaw system, pitch system, controller (SCADA), tower, foundation, anemometer, wind vane, and transformer**.

**Functions of three key parts:**

1. **Rotor Blades:** Aerofoil-shaped composite structures that capture the kinetic energy of the wind by generating lift. They convert linear wind energy into rotational mechanical energy.

2. **Gearbox:** A multi-stage planetary-helical gear train that steps up the low rotor RPM (typically 10–20 RPM) to the high RPM required by the generator (typically 1500 RPM for 50 Hz grid operation). The typical gear ratio is in the range of **1:90 to 1:120**.

3. **Yaw System:** An electromechanical drive system that rotates the entire nacelle about the vertical tower axis to keep the rotor plane perpendicular to the prevailing wind direction, thereby maximizing aerodynamic capture efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **yaw system** (rotates the *nacelle* about the vertical axis) with the **pitch system** (rotates each *blade* about its own longitudinal axis). Losing 1 mark here is common.

---

### Question 2 (Module 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between pitch control and yaw control in a WECS. Why is the gearbox considered the most maintenance-intensive component?**

#### Model Answer:

| Feature | Pitch Control | Yaw Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Axis of Rotation** | Each blade's longitudinal axis | The vertical tower axis |
| **Component Actuated** | Individual blade (via pitch bearing) | Entire nacelle |
| **Primary Purpose** | Regulate aerodynamic power capture; feather blades in storm | Align rotor with wind direction |
| **Actuator Type** | Hydraulic servo or electric motor per blade | Geared motor with slewing ring |
| **Response Speed** | Fast (seconds) | Slow (degrees per second) |

**Why the Gearbox is Maintenance-Intensive:**

The gearbox is the most maintenance-intensive component of a WECS due to: (1) **High torque transmission at variable loads** leading to gear-tooth wear and pitting, (2) **Lubricant degradation** under continuous high-temperature operation, (3) **Bearing failures** in the planetary stage due to combined radial and thrust loads from the rotor, (4) **Difficult accessibility** — being inside the nacelle at 80–150 m height, and (5) **Cumulative fatigue** from millions of load cycles per year. Modern offshore turbines therefore increasingly adopt **direct-drive PMSG** topologies to eliminate the gearbox entirely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do not write "gearbox is maintenance-intensive because it has gears" — that is a non-answer. Quantify the *why*: mention high torque, lubrication issues, and nacelle accessibility.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the parts of a horizontal-axis wind energy conversion system and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **60 m**, a hub-height wind speed of **10 m/s**, a power coefficient of **0.38**, a gearbox efficiency of **0.96**, and a generator efficiency of **0.93**. Calculate the **annual energy output (AEO)** in kWh. Assume capacity factor of 0.32, standard air density, and 8760 hours per year. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q (a):

The major parts of a horizontal-axis WECS and their functions are tabulated below (refer also to Section 4 architecture):

| Part | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Rotor Blades** | Capture wind kinetic energy via aerodynamic lift; composite construction for strength and low weight |
| **Hub** | Mechanical junction connecting three blades to the main shaft; houses pitch bearings |
| **Spinner** | Aerodynamic fairing reducing drag; protects pitch mechanism from environment |
| **Main Shaft (LSS)** | Transmits low-RPM torque from rotor to gearbox; mounted on two main bearings |
| **Gearbox** | Steps up rotor RPM (10–20 RPM) to generator RPM (1500 RPM) via planetary-helical gear train |
| **Mechanical Brake** | Secondary parking brake on the high-speed shaft; primary braking via blade feathering |
| **Generator** | Converts mechanical rotation into electrical energy (SCIG/DFIG/PMSG) |
| **Yaw System** | Rotates nacelle to align rotor with wind; includes slewing ring and yaw motors |
| **Pitch System** | Adjusts blade angle of attack; controls power and provides aerodynamic braking |
| **Controller (SCADA)** | Master PLC-based controller for start-up, shutdown, fault handling, and grid synchronization |
| **Tower** | Tubular tapered steel structure elevating the rotor to hub height; resists thrust and gravity loads |
| **Foundation** | Reinforced concrete gravity base transferring all loads to the ground |
| **Anemometer & Wind Vane** | Sensors for measuring wind speed and direction to drive the yaw and pitch control loops |
| **Transformer** | Steps up generator voltage (690 V) to medium voltage (33 kV) for grid connection |

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4 Mermaid block diagram — Aerodynamic → Mechanical → Electrical → Grid, with Control feedback loop.)

#### Model Solution to Q (b):

**Given Data:**
- Rotor diameter $D = 60$ m
- Wind speed $V = 10$ m/s
- Power coefficient $C_{p} = 0.38$
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.96$
- Generator efficiency $\eta_{gen} = 0.93$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Capacity factor $C_{f} = 0.32$
- Hours per year $H = 8760$ h

**Step 1 — Rotor Swept Area:**

The radius is computed, and the area formula is applied.

$$
R = \dfrac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times 30^{2} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Available Wind Power:**

The cubic dependence on wind speed is applied directly.

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 10^{3} \approx 1{,}731{,}806 \text{ W} \approx 1.732 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Power Captured:**

The power coefficient scales down the available power.

$$
P_{mech} = 0.38 \times 1{,}731{,}806 \approx 658{,}086 \text{ W} \approx 0.658 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4 — Net Electrical (Rated) Power Output:**

The two component efficiencies multiply to give the overall conversion efficiency.

$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.96 \times 0.93 = 0.8928
$$

$$
P_{rated} = 0.8928 \times 658{,}086 \approx 587{,}483 \text{ W} \approx 0.587 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Energy Output (AEO):**

The rated power is multiplied by the capacity factor and the total hours in a year.

$$
AEO = P_{rated} \times C_{f} \times H = 0.5875 \times 0.32 \times 8760
$$

$$
AEO \approx 1646.92 \text{ MWh/year} \approx 1.647 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Final Answer:** $AEO \approx 1.65 \times 10^{6}$ kWh/year (or 1.65 GWh/year).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q (a)** Explain in detail the **construction and working of the nacelle assembly** of a modern horizontal-axis WECS, with emphasis on the power transmission train. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Derive the **Betz limit** and explain its significance in wind turbine design. Show that the maximum power coefficient is **16/27**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q (a):

The **nacelle** is the housing mounted atop the tower that contains the entire power transmission train and electrical generation equipment of a horizontal-axis WECS. A modern 2–5 MW nacelle weighs between **50 and 200 tonnes** and is approximately 10–15 m long.

**Construction of the Nacelle:**

1. **Main Frame:** A welded steel box-section chassis that mounts on the yaw bearing atop the tower. All internal components are anchored to this frame.

2. **Main Shaft (LSS):** A forged steel shaft, typically 0.5–1 m in diameter, supported on **two main bearings** (one cylindrical, one spherical roller). One end connects to the hub; the other feeds into the gearbox input.

3. **Gearbox:** A compound **planetary (1st stage) + helical (2nd/3rd stage)** gearbox with a total ratio of **1:90 to 1:120**. Modern offshore turbines use **medium-speed (semi-direct) gearboxes** with PMSG generators to reduce failures.

4. **High-Speed Shaft (HSS) and Coupling:** Connects the gearbox output to the generator rotor via a flexible diaphragm coupling that absorbs misalignment.

5. **Mechanical Brake:** A hydraulically-actuated **caliper disc brake** mounted on the HSS, used only as a parking brake.

6. **Generator:** Slip-ring **DFIG** is the industry default. Direct-drive **PMSG** is used in modern offshore turbines like Siemens-Gamesa SG-14-222 DD.

7. **Power Converter:** Back-to-back **AC-DC-AC Voltage Source Converter (VSC)** with IGBT switches for variable-speed operation and grid synchronization.

8. **Yaw System:** Includes the **slewing ring bearing** (a large diameter gear) and **4–6 yaw drive motors** with planetary gear reduction.

9. **Controller Cabinet:** Houses the **PLC**, **SCADA terminals**, **circuit breakers**, and **pitch control electronics**.

10. **Cooling and Lubrication Systems:** Oil coolers, radiator fans, and gear oil pumps ensure thermal regulation of the gearbox and generator.

**Working of the Power Transmission Train:**

Wind → Rotor (slow rotation, high torque) → Main Shaft (torque transmission) → Gearbox (speed multiplication) → HSS (fast rotation, lower torque) → Generator (mechanical-to-electrical conversion) → Power Converter (frequency and voltage conditioning) → Transformer → Grid.

**Significance in WECS Design:** The nacelle is the most failure-prone assembly, contributing to **>60% of total turbine downtime**. Modern condition-monitoring systems use vibration sensors on the gearbox and temperature sensors on the generator bearings for predictive maintenance.

#### Model Solution to Q (b):

(Refer to the full step-by-step derivation of the Betz Limit in Section 3.3 above. The 13-step derivation starting from the continuity equation and concluding with $C_{p_{max}} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ should be reproduced in the answer script. The expected writing structure is:)

1. State the assumptions: incompressible flow, steady-state, infinite stream tube, ideal rotor (actuator disk).
2. Apply the **continuity equation** at the three stations (upstream, rotor plane, downstream).
3. Apply the **momentum equation** to find the thrust force on the rotor.
4. Express the **power extracted** as the product of force and rotor-plane velocity.
5. Use the **actuator disk identity** $V_2 = (V_1 + V_3)/2$.
6. Express $C_p$ as a function of the induction factor $a$.
7. Differentiate $C_p$ with respect to $a$ and equate to zero.
8. Solve for $a_{opt} = 1/3$.
9. Substitute back to obtain $C_{p_{max}} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$.

**Significance of the Betz Limit:**

- It establishes the **theoretical upper bound** on the fraction of wind kinetic energy that any turbine can extract.
- It explains why **CFD and blade-design efforts** are fundamentally bounded — you can never exceed 59.3% no matter how good the engineering.
- It guides the **optimum tip-speed ratio** design — for a 3-bladed turbine, $C_{p_{max}}$ occurs near $\lambda \approx 8$.
- It sets a **realistic target** for commercial turbines (currently 0.45–0.50) and motivates R&D in vertical-axis and diffuser-augmented designs to *circumvent* the limit by adding energy to the airstream.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall on Betz Derivation:** Many students write the answer as "Betz limit is 0.593" without showing the derivation. This is worth only 1–2 marks. The 7-mark question **demands the full algebraic chain** including the differentiation step. Also, do not confuse the **Betz limit (0.593)** with the **efficiency of a single blade element** (which can exceed 0.593 in certain local conditions — but the *rotor-averaged* $C_p$ cannot).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Parts of WECS**

### **Core Components to Memorize:**

- **Aerodynamic:** Blades, Hub, Spinner
- **Mechanical:** Main shaft, Gearbox, Mechanical brake
- **Electrical:** Generator (SCIG/DFIG/PMSG), Power converter, Transformer
- **Control:** SCADA/PLC, Anemometer, Wind vane, Yaw drive, Pitch actuator
- **Support:** Tower (tubular tapered steel), Foundation (gravity type)

### **Key Numerical Values to Remember:**

- **Betz Limit:** $C_{p_{max}} = 16/27 = 0.593$
- **Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- **Power Equation:** $P = 0.5 \rho A V^{3} C_{p}$
- **TSR:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$
- **Hours per year:** 8760 h
- **Standard grid frequencies:** 50 Hz (India) → generator RPM = 1500 (4-pole) or 3000 (2-pole)
- **Capacity factor range (onshore):** 0.25–0.40
- **Capacity factor range (offshore):** 0.40–0.60

### **High-Yield Distinctions (Frequently Asked):**

- **Yaw** vs **Pitch** (vertical axis vs blade axis)
- **SCIG** vs **DFIG** vs **PMSG** (fixed speed vs variable speed with gearbox vs direct drive)
- **Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out** wind speeds (typical: 3 / 12 / 25 m/s)
- **Aerodynamic brake** (feathering) vs **Mechanical brake** (disc brake)
- **Main shaft (LSS)** vs **High-speed shaft (HSS)** (10–20 RPM vs 1500 RPM)

### **Favourite KTU Question Patterns:**

1. "List and explain the parts of a horizontal-axis WECS" — straight from the syllabus. **(14 marks)**
2. "Derive Betz limit and state its significance" — guaranteed Module 2 question. **(7–14 marks)**
3. "Differentiate between SCIG, DFIG, and PMSG configurations." **(3–7 marks)**
4. "A wind turbine with given D, V, Cp, and efficiencies produces…?" — numerical. **(7 marks)**
5. "Explain the construction and working of the nacelle assembly." **(7–14 marks)**

### **One-Line Memory Anchors:**

- **"Betz is 16/27, perfect is 0.593, real is 0.4-0.5."**
- **"Yaw turns the house, Pitch turns the blade."**
- **"Cube law: double V, octuple P."**
- **"DFIG is king of onshore, PMSG is king of offshore."**
- **"Gearbox is the achilles heel of any turbine."**

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Memorize the block diagram in Section 4.1. In a 14-mark question, always start with the block diagram (1 mark), then list 8–10 parts with one-line functions (8 marks), and conclude with one numerical example (5 marks). This scoring pattern is what the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation key explicitly rewards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of OTEC System, Methods of OTEC Power Generation - Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) and Hybrid Cycle (Block Diagram Description of OTEC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of OTEC System, Methods of OTEC Power Generation - Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) and Hybrid Cycle (Block Diagram Description of OTEC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of OTEC

**Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a renewable energy technology that exploits the **thermal gradient** (temperature difference) between the **warm surface water** of tropical oceans and the **cold deep ocean water** to drive a heat engine and produce useful work — usually electrical power.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (per the OEMET614 syllabus):

> **OTEC** is a *low-temperature, low-pressure* thermal power conversion system in which a working fluid (either seawater itself or a secondary volatile fluid) is vaporized using **warm surface seawater (≈ 25–28 °C)** acting as the heat source, expanded through a turbine to generate mechanical/electrical power, and then condensed using **cold deep seawater (≈ 5–8 °C pumped from ≈ 800–1000 m depth)** acting as the heat sink, thereby completing a thermodynamic Rankine/Carnot-like cycle using solar energy stored in the ocean as the primary input.

The minimum **temperature differential (ΔT)** required between the surface (warm) and the deep (cold) reservoir for any practical OTEC operation is approximately **ΔT ≥ 20 °C (293 K)**. This restricts OTEC viability strictly to **tropical and sub-tropical latitudes between roughly 20° N and 20° S**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The OEMET614 Module 3 expects students to (a) state the principle of OTEC, (b) classify OTEC cycles into Open, Closed, and Hybrid, and (c) draw and explain the **Block Diagram** of each cycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine you have two buckets of water — one **lukewarm (25 °C)** on a kitchen table and one **chilled (5 °C)** inside a refrigerator. On their own, neither bucket is particularly useful for producing work. But if you could pipe heat *from* the warm bucket *into* a small enclosed volume of a very volatile fluid (such as ammonia), that fluid would boil, push a small paddle (a turbine), and the cold bucket would re-condense it. That is exactly what an OTEC plant does — except the warm bucket is the **sun-heated ocean surface** and the cold bucket is the **dark, deep ocean**.

A more refined analogy:

| Real-World Component | OTEC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A boiling kettle producing steam that drives a toy turbine | OTEC evaporator + warm surface seawater |
| The cold side of the kettle (cold water jacket) condensing steam back to water | OTEC condenser + cold deep seawater |
| Sun recharging the warm water each day | Solar input acting as the **prime mover of the cycle** |

So, in plain English: **OTEC is a heat engine whose "fire" is the sun-warmed tropical ocean and whose "cooling tower" is the cold ocean depth.** The sun continuously recharges the warm surface, making OTEC a *baseload-capable* renewable source (it can run 24×7, unlike solar PV).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Reference Values for OTEC Calculations (Tropical Pacific / Atlantic):**
> - Warm surface water temperature, **T_H ≈ 25 °C to 28 °C (298 K – 301 K)**
> - Cold deep water temperature (≈ 1000 m depth), **T_C ≈ 5 °C to 8 °C (278 K – 281 K)**
> - Minimum usable temperature difference, **ΔT_min ≈ 20 K**
> - Maximum (Carnot) thermal efficiency achievable, **η_Carnot ≈ 6 % to 7.5 %**
> - Typical usable temperature differential after heat-exchanger losses, **ΔT_useful ≈ 18 K – 22 K**
> - Solar energy stored in the upper ocean layer (mixed layer): **≈ 4 × 10¹⁴ MJ / day globally**
> - Typical net plant efficiency (real, after irreversibilities): **η_actual ≈ 2.5 % – 3 %**
> - Standard tropical OTEC capacity per module: **1 MW to 100 MW** (with floating plants up to **5–10 MW** demonstrated; ≥ 100 MW proposed for shore plants)

The fundamental driving equation (which we will derive fully in Section 3) is:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

The "cold" fact is that this efficiency is **small** — but the heat reservoir is enormous and continuously recharged by the sun, making the **gross available power** very large.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The OTEC principle rests on **Clausius's statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics**: heat will spontaneously flow from a hot body to a cold body. OTEC harnesses this natural flow by inserting a *heat engine* in between the two reservoirs.

**Step-by-step operational sequence:**

- **Step 1 — Solar Charging of Mixed Layer:**  
  Solar radiation (short-wave) penetrates the upper 50–100 m of clear tropical ocean water and is absorbed. This raises the upper mixed layer to **25–28 °C**.

- **Step 2 — Thermocline Establishment:**  
  A sharp temperature gradient (the **thermocline**) develops between the warm surface and the cold deep ocean (below ≈ 800 m). The cold deep water, originating from polar sinking regions, sits at **≈ 4–8 °C** year-round.

- **Step 3 — Warm Water Intake:**  
  Surface seawater is drawn through a large intake pipe (typically **20 m² to 60 m²** cross-section) at a rate of several **m³/s**.

- **Step 4 — Heat Transfer to Working Fluid (Evaporator):**  
  The warm seawater transfers its latent heat through a **plate-type heat exchanger (evaporator)** to a working fluid.  
  - In **Open Cycle** → the working fluid *is* seawater (which is flashed at low pressure).  
  - In **Closed Cycle** → the working fluid is ammonia (NH₃), propane, or R-134a (boiling point ≈ −33 °C at atmospheric pressure).

- **Step 5 — Vapor Expansion (Turbine):**  
  The high-pressure vapor expands through a **low-pressure axial-flow turbine** coupled to a generator, producing electricity.

- **Step 6 — Heat Rejection (Condenser):**  
  The low-pressure exhaust vapor is condensed back to liquid using the **cold deep seawater (≈ 5 °C)** in another plate heat exchanger (the condenser).

- **Step 7 — Working Fluid Recirculation (Pump):**  
  A **feed pump** returns the liquid working fluid to the evaporator pressure, closing the loop.

### 2.2 The Three OTEC Cycle Variants — Conceptual Comparison

| Feature | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Hybrid Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventor / Year** | Georges Claude (1930) | Joseph Anderson (1960s) | Combination (1970s) |
| **Working fluid** | Warm seawater itself (water/steam) | Ammonia (NH₃) or propane | Seawater (evaporator) + NH₃ (turbine loop) |
| **Turbine fluid** | Low-pressure steam | High-pressure NH₃ vapor | NH₃ vapor (driven by seawater flash steam) |
| **Surface hardware** | Large vacuum flash evaporator | Compact plate heat exchangers | Flash evaporator + secondary NH₃ loop |
| **By-product** | Desalinated fresh water (condensate) | None (closed loop) | Desalinated fresh water (like Open) |
| **Turbine size** | Very large (low ΔP, low ρ) | Compact (high ΔP, high ρ of NH₃) | Moderate |
| **Vacuum required** | Yes — evaporator operates at **≈ 0.03 – 0.05 bar** | No vacuum needed (NH₃ loop is pressurized) | Yes — for the seawater flash side |
| **Demineralization needed?** | No (using seawater directly) | Yes — seawater must be deaerated & cleaned before entering evaporator | Yes (NH₃ loop side) |
| **KTU Exam weightage** | **High (most frequent)** | **High (most frequent)** | Moderate |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas below are **KTU board-essential** and appear repeatedly in Module-3 questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Maximum possible (ideal) thermal efficiency of OTEC | $T$ in **Kelvin (K)**; result is dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{2nd Law}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Same as Carnot; OTEC is heat-engine analogue | $T$ in K |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \times \eta_{\text{relative}}$ | Real OTEC efficiency (2.5–3 % typically) | $\eta_{\text{relative}} \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$ |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_H \cdot c_p \cdot (T_H - T_{evap})$ | Heat absorbed from warm seawater (evaporator side) | W (kW), where $c_p \approx 4.186\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ for water |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_C = \dot{m}_C \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{cond} - T_C)$ | Heat rejected to cold seawater (condenser side) | W (kW) |
| 6 | $\dot{W}_{\text{net}} = \eta_{\text{actual}} \cdot \dot{Q}_H$ | Net electrical power output of OTEC plant | W (kW or MW) |
| 7 | $\Delta T = T_H - T_C$ | Useful temperature differential | K or °C (numerically equal) |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{\text{warm}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_H}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_w}$ | Warm seawater mass flow required | kg/s |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{\text{cold}} \approx 1.3 \text{ to } 2 \times \dot{m}_{\text{warm}}$ | Cold seawater flow (larger due to lower $c_p$ head available) | kg/s |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{working fluid}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_H}{h_{fg}}$ | Working fluid (NH₃) mass flow in closed cycle | kg/s, $h_{fg,\text{NH}_3} \approx 1370\,\text{kJ/kg}$ |
| 11 | $\text{FOM} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{\text{net}}}{A_{\text{plant}}}$ | Figure of Merit — power per unit plant footprint | W/m² |
| 12 | $P_{\text{gross}} = \eta_{\text{turbine}} \cdot \eta_{\text{generator}} \cdot \dot{Q}_H \cdot \eta_{\text{cycle}}$ | Gross plant power with sub-efficiencies | W |
| 13 | $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C + \Delta T_{\text{cond}}}{T_H - \Delta T_{\text{evap}}}$ | Modified Carnot with real heat-exchanger pinch | $\Delta T_{\text{evap}}$, $\Delta T_{\text{cond}}$ are pinch ≈ 2–4 K |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Examination Note:** When writing formula (1) for KTU valuation, always explicitly write *“T_H and T_C must be in Kelvin (K); convert from °C by adding 273.”* Failing to convert from °C to K is the most common **1-mark deduction** in KTU paper correction.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

OTEC, despite its low cycle efficiency, has **three distinct engineering products**:

1. **Electrical power** — net efficiency 2.5–3 % but **continuous 24×7 baseload** (unlike solar/wind).  
2. **Fresh water (desalination)** — open-cycle OTEC produces ≈ 1 L of fresh water per kWh of electricity as a byproduct.  
3. **Cold-water air-conditioning / aquaculture** — cold deep seawater can chill buildings or grow cold-water species (lobster, salmon) in tropical regions.

Major reference plants: **OTEC-1 (Natural Energy Laboratory of Hawaii, 1980s, 50 kW)**, **Kumejima Plant (Japan, 100 kW, 2013)**, and proposed **1 MW plant in Martinique (DCNS)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Carnot / Maximum Thermal Efficiency for OTEC

**Starting Statement (2nd Law, Kelvin–Planck form):**  
No heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same two reservoirs.

For a reversible cycle, the entropy change of the universe is zero:

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} \;=\; \Delta S_{\text{hot}} \;+\; \Delta S_{\text{cold}} \;+\; \Delta S_{\text{engine}} \;=\; 0$$

The hot reservoir loses heat $\dot{Q}_H$ at constant temperature $T_H$:

$$\Delta S_{\text{hot}} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{\dot{Q}_H}{T_H}$$

The cold reservoir gains heat $\dot{Q}_C$ at constant temperature $T_C$:

$$\Delta S_{\text{cold}} \;=\; +\,\dfrac{\dot{Q}_C}{T_C}$$

The working fluid undergoes a complete cycle, returning to its initial state (ΔS = 0):

$$\Delta S_{\text{engine}} \;=\; 0$$

Substituting into the entropy-balance equation:

$$-\,\dfrac{\dot{Q}_H}{T_H} \;+\; \dfrac{\dot{Q}_C}{T_C} \;+\; 0 \;=\; 0$$

Solving for $\dot{Q}_C$ in terms of $\dot{Q}_H$:

$$\dfrac{\dot{Q}_C}{T_C} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{Q}_H}{T_H}$$

$$\dot{Q}_C \;=\; \dot{Q}_H \cdot \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$$

By the **First Law (steady-flow, single-loop heat engine, negligible KE/PE)**:

$$\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \;=\; \dot{Q}_H \;-\; \dot{Q}_C$$

Substituting the expression for $\dot{Q}_C$:

$$\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \;=\; \dot{Q}_H \;-\; \dot{Q}_H \cdot \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$$

$$\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \;=\; \dot{Q}_H \cdot \left(1 \;-\; \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}\right)$$

Thermal efficiency is defined as useful work out divided by heat in:

$$\eta_{\text{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{W}_{\text{net}}}{\dot{Q}_H} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{Q}_H \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}\right)}{\dot{Q}_H}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}\;}$$

This is the **famous Carnot efficiency**, and it sets the **absolute upper ceiling** on any OTEC plant.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A tropical OTEC plant operates with warm surface seawater at $T_H = 28\,^\circ\text{C}$ and cold deep seawater at $T_C = 8\,^\circ\text{C}$. The warm seawater mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_H = 200\,\text{kg/s}$. The actual cycle efficiency (after heat-exchanger and turbine losses) is $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 0.025$ (i.e., 2.5 %).

Find:
- (a) The maximum (Carnot) efficiency of the plant.
- (b) The net electrical power output (in kW).
- (c) The required cold-seawater mass flow rate, if it absorbs heat at $c_p = 4.18\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $\Delta T_C = 8\,\text{K}$.
- (d) The thermodynamic *availability* (exergy) per kg of warm seawater drawn.

---

**Solution Step (a): Maximum Carnot Efficiency**

*Conversion logic:* $T$ in °C must be converted to Kelvin because the Carnot formula is **temperature-ratio based and only valid in absolute units**.

Convert warm-side temperature:

$$T_H \;=\; 28\,^\circ\text{C} \;+\; 273 \;=\; 301\,\text{K}$$

Convert cold-side temperature:

$$T_C \;=\; 8\,^\circ\text{C} \;+\; 273 \;=\; 281\,\text{K}$$

Apply the Carnot efficiency formula:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$$

Substitute the values:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{281\,\text{K}}{301\,\text{K}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; 0.9336$$

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 0.0664$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;\approx\; 6.64\,\%\;}$$

**Interpretation:** This means that, *theoretically*, at most 6.64 % of the solar heat stored in warm seawater can ever be converted to useful mechanical work. The remaining 93.36 % is inevitably rejected to the cold reservoir.

---

**Solution Step (b): Net Electrical Power Output**

Compute the heat input rate from the warm seawater. The warm water cools from 28 °C down to about 26 °C as it passes through the evaporator (so $\Delta T_H = 2\,$K):

$$\dot{Q}_H \;=\; \dot{m}_H \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_H$$

$$\dot{Q}_H \;=\; 200\,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \cdot 4.186\,\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \cdot 2\,\text{K}$$

$$\dot{Q}_H \;=\; 200 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 2 \;\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{s}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_H \;=\; 1674.4\,\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{s}} \;=\; 1674.4\,\text{kW}$$

Apply the actual efficiency:

$$\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \;=\; \eta_{\text{actual}} \cdot \dot{Q}_H$$

$$\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \;=\; 0.025 \cdot 1674.4\,\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \;\approx\; 41.86\,\text{kW}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** Even a 2.5 %-efficient plant using only 200 kg/s of warm water produces enough power for ~40 homes continuously.

---

**Solution Step (c): Required Cold-Seawater Mass Flow Rate**

By the First Law in the cycle (neglecting pump work):

$$\dot{Q}_C \;=\; \dot{Q}_H \;-\; \dot{W}_{\text{net}}$$

Compute $\dot{Q}_C$:

$$\dot{Q}_C \;=\; 1674.4\,\text{kW} \;-\; 41.86\,\text{kW}$$

$$\dot{Q}_C \;=\; 1632.54\,\text{kW}$$

Apply the heat-transfer relation on the cold side:

$$\dot{Q}_C \;=\; \dot{m}_C \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_C$$

Solve for $\dot{m}_C$:

$$\dot{m}_C \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{Q}_C}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_C}$$

$$\dot{m}_C \;=\; \dfrac{1632.54\,\text{kW}}{4.186\,\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \cdot 8\,\text{K}}$$

$$\dot{m}_C \;=\; \dfrac{1632.54}{33.488}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_C \;\approx\; 48.75\,\text{kg/s}\;}$$

(Note: in real OTEC, the cold water mass flow is *larger* than the warm water flow because cold water is typically only 5–8 K warmer than its intake, requiring a larger flow to absorb the same heat.)

---

**Solution Step (d): Specific Exergy of the Warm Seawater Stream**

The specific flow exergy (availability) of a thermal stream relative to the dead-state temperature $T_0$ is:

$$\psi \;=\; c_p \cdot (T \;-\; T_0) \;-\; T_0 \cdot c_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

With $T = 301\,$K, $T_0 = 281\,$K (taking cold seawater as the dead state):

Compute $(T - T_0)$:

$$\Delta T \;=\; 301 \;-\; 281 \;=\; 20\,\text{K}$$

Compute $c_p \cdot \Delta T$:

$$c_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 4.186 \cdot 20 \;=\; 83.72\,\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}$$

Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{301}{281}\right) \;=\; \ln(1.0712) \;\approx\; 0.0688$$

Compute $T_0 \cdot c_p \cdot \ln(T/T_0)$:

$$281 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 0.0688 \;\approx\; 80.91\,\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}$$

Subtract:

$$\psi \;=\; 83.72 \;-\; 80.91$$

$$\boxed{\;\psi \;\approx\; 2.81\,\tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** Each kilogram of warm seawater carries only about **2.81 kJ of useful exergy** — a tiny fraction of its total thermal energy. This *small exergy density* is the fundamental reason OTEC plants are physically large (intakes of tens of m²) for any reasonable power output.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Three Cycles Compared Numerically

Using the standard tropical temperatures $T_H = 301\,$K and $T_C = 281\,$K (ΔT = 20 K):

| Parameter | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Hybrid Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Seawater (vapor) | NH₃ | NH₃ (driven by seawater flash) |
| Evaporator pressure | **0.034 bar** (≈ 25 mmHg absolute) | **≈ 4–7 bar** (NH₃ at 25 °C) | **0.034 bar** (flash side) + ≈ 5 bar NH₃ |
| Turbine inlet T | ≈ 24 °C (slightly cooled) | ≈ 25 °C | ≈ 25 °C |
| Working fluid $h_{fg}$ | ≈ 2458 kJ/kg (water at 24 °C) | ≈ 1370 kJ/kg (NH₃) | NH₃ side dominates |
| Specific volume at turbine inlet | ≈ 47 m³/kg (huge!) | ≈ 0.13 m³/kg (small) | moderate |
| Turbine diameter for 1 MW | **≈ 6–10 m** (huge) | **≈ 1–2 m** (compact) | moderate |
| Cycle η_actual | ≈ 2.0–2.5 % | ≈ 3.0–3.5 % | ≈ 2.5–3.0 % |
| Fresh-water byproduct | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The diagrams below follow the **KTU board-exam convention** for OTEC block diagrams. Use the same labeling style (numbered blocks with arrows) when drawing in your answer scripts for full marks.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Open Cycle OTEC (Claude Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1 WARM SEAWATER INTAKE Pipe near surface"] --> B["2 FLASH EVAPORATOR Vacuum chamber at 0.034 bar"]
    B -->|"Low-pressure steam"| C["3 TURBINE Coupled to generator"]
    C --> D["4 GENERATOR Electrical output"]
    C -->|"Exhaust steam"| E["5 SURFACE CONDENSER At cold deep water side"]
    E -->|"Liquid water freshwater"| F["6 FRESH WATER COLLECTION"]
    E --> G["7 COLD SEAWATER INTAKE Pipe to 1000 m depth"]
    G --> E
    F --> H["8 VACUUM PUMP Maintains low pressure"]
    H --> B
    D --> I["9 POWER OUTPUT to grid"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
```

**Functional narrative:** Warm seawater enters block 1, gets flashed to steam in the vacuum evaporator (block 2), drives the turbine (block 3), which spins the generator (block 4) producing power (block 9). The low-pressure steam is condensed in block 5 by cold deep seawater from block 7, producing freshwater as a byproduct in block 6. A vacuum pump (block 8) maintains the low pressure in the evaporator.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed Cycle OTEC (Anderson Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1 WARM SEAWATER PUMP Surface intake"] --> B["2 EVAPORATOR Plate heat exchanger"]
    B -->|"High-pressure NH3 vapor"| C["3 TURBINE Drives generator"]
    C --> D["4 GENERATOR Electrical output"]
    C -->|"Low-pressure NH3 vapor"| E["5 CONDENSER Plate heat exchanger"]
    E -->|"Liquid NH3"| F["6 FEED PUMP Re-pressurizes NH3"]
    F --> B
    G["7 COLD SEAWATER PUMP From 1000 m depth"] --> E
    D --> H["8 POWER OUTPUT to grid"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
```

**Functional narrative:** Warm surface seawater (block 1) supplies heat through the evaporator (block 2) to a *closed loop* of liquid ammonia. The ammonia vaporizes, expands through the turbine (block 3), and the turbine drives the generator (block 4 → power to grid at block 8). The low-pressure ammonia vapor then enters the condenser (block 5) where cold deep seawater (block 7) recondenses it back to liquid. The feed pump (block 6) raises the ammonia pressure and returns it to the evaporator, closing the loop. No seawater ever mixes with the working fluid.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Hybrid Cycle OTEC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1 WARM SEAWATER INTAKE Surface"] --> B["2 FLASH EVAPORATOR Vacuum 0.034 bar"]
    B -->|"Low-pressure steam"| C["3 HEAT EXCHANGER Secondary NH3 loop evaporator"]
    C -->|"High-pressure NH3 vapor"| D["4 TURBINE NH3 expands"]
    D --> E["5 GENERATOR Electricity"]
    D -->|"Low-pressure NH3 vapor"| F["6 CONDENSER Cold side"]
    F -->|"Liquid NH3"| G["7 NH3 FEED PUMP"]
    G --> C
    B --> H["8 STEAM CONDENSER Desalinated freshwater out"]
    H -->|"Waste water"| I["9 DEEP COLD SEAWATER INTAKE 1000 m"]
    I --> F
    I --> H
    E --> J["10 POWER OUTPUT"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
```

**Functional narrative:** The hybrid cycle (block 2) uses seawater flash evaporation *exactly like the open cycle*, but instead of routing the low-pressure steam directly to a (huge) turbine, it uses the flashed steam to boil ammonia in a secondary heat exchanger (block 3). The ammonia vapor then drives a compact high-pressure turbine (block 4) as in the closed cycle. The freshwater condensate from the flashed steam (block 8) provides desalinated water as a byproduct. The combined system *retains the freshwater advantage of the open cycle* while *avoiding the gigantic turbine* of the open cycle by using ammonia on the turbine side.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Heat-Flow Architecture of All Three

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q_H["HEAT SOURCE Warm surface seawater 25 to 28 C"]
        Q_H_NODE["T_H = 301 K"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["OTEC CORE Heat Engine"]
        direction TB
        EVAP["EVAPORATOR Heat input"]
        TURB["TURBINE Work output"]
        COND["CONDENSER Heat rejection"]
        PUMP["PUMP Closes the loop"]
        EVAP --> TURB --> COND --> PUMP --> EVAP
    end
    subgraph Q_C["HEAT SINK Cold deep seawater 5 to 8 C"]
        Q_C_NODE["T_C = 281 K"]
    end
    subgraph CYCLES["CYCLE VARIANT"]
        C_OPEN["OPEN: Working fluid is seawater"]
        C_CLOSED["CLOSED: Working fluid is NH3"]
        C_HYB["HYBRID: Seawater flash heats NH3 loop"]
    end

    Q_H_NODE --> EVAP
    COND --> Q_C_NODE
    TURB -->|"W_net Electricity"| OUT["POWER GRID"]
    CYCLES --> CORE

    style Q_H fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style Q_C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style CORE fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style CYCLES fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

**Reading this diagram:** Regardless of which cycle variant is used, the *fundamental architecture* is identical — a heat engine sandwiched between a hot reservoir (sun-warmed surface) and a cold reservoir (deep ocean). The **only difference between Open, Closed, and Hybrid is which physical fluid plays the role of the working fluid in the central loop.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> *State the principle of Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). Why is OTEC feasible only in tropical regions?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** OTEC is a heat-engine based renewable energy conversion system that exploits the **temperature difference (ΔT)** between the warm surface seawater (≈ 25–28 °C) and the cold deep seawater (≈ 5–8 °C, pumped from ≈ 1000 m depth) of tropical oceans. The warm water acts as the heat source (boiler/evaporator), the cold water acts as the heat sink (condenser), and a working fluid (seawater in Open Cycle, ammonia in Closed Cycle) is vaporized and expanded through a turbine to generate electricity.

**Tropical feasibility (1 mark):** A minimum ΔT of **≈ 20 K** is required for practical OTEC. This large temperature differential is naturally available only in **tropical and sub-tropical latitudes (between ≈ 20° N and 20° S)**, where solar insolation maintains the surface layer warm year-round and polar-origin deep water remains cold.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
> *List any three differences between the Open Cycle (Claude) and Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each for any three of the following):**

| # | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Working fluid is **warm seawater itself**, flashed into low-pressure steam | Working fluid is a **secondary fluid like ammonia (NH₃)** in a closed loop |
| 2 | Evaporator operates in **vacuum (≈ 0.034 bar)** | Evaporator operates at **moderate pressure (≈ 4–7 bar)** for NH₃ |
| 3 | Turbine is **very large** (low steam density) | Turbine is **compact** (high NH₃ vapor density) |
| 4 | Produces **fresh water (desalinated)** as a byproduct | **No fresh-water** byproduct |
| 5 | Lower cycle efficiency (≈ 2.0–2.5 %) | Higher cycle efficiency (≈ 3.0–3.5 %) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, students must answer **one full 14-mark question** from this module. Internal choice is provided.

---

#### ▶ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**  
> **(a)** Explain the principle of OTEC with a neat block diagram of the **Open Cycle (Claude)** system. List its advantages and disadvantages. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** A tropical OTEC plant operates with $T_H = 28\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_C = 6\,^\circ\text{C}$. The warm-water mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_H = 250\,$kg/s, and the temperature drop in the warm stream is 3 K. The actual plant efficiency is 2.5 %. Determine:  
>     (i) the Carnot efficiency,  
>     (ii) the net power output,  
>     (iii) the cold-water mass flow rate, given that cold water warms by 6 K. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

The principle of OTEC rests on the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**: a heat engine can produce work from heat flowing between two reservoirs at different temperatures. In tropical oceans, the upper ≈ 100 m layer is heated by solar radiation to ≈ 25–28 °C, while the deep ocean (below ≈ 800 m) remains cold at ≈ 5–8 °C, producing a temperature differential of ≈ 20 K year-round.

In the **Open Cycle (Claude Cycle)**:
- **Block 1 (Warm water intake):** Warm surface seawater is drawn through a large-diameter pipe.
- **Block 2 (Flash evaporator):** The warm water enters a vacuum chamber at ≈ 0.034 bar, where it **flash-evaporates** (≈ 0.5 % by mass) into low-pressure steam.
- **Block 3 (Turbine):** The low-pressure steam expands through a large axial-flow turbine coupled to a generator.
- **Block 4 (Condenser):** The exhaust steam is condensed by direct contact with cold deep seawater, producing **fresh water** as a byproduct.
- **Block 5 (Vacuum pump):** Maintains the low evaporator pressure; non-condensable gases are removed.

**Advantages (any 3, 1 mark each):**
1. Produces desalinated freshwater as a byproduct — useful in water-scarce tropical islands.
2. Uses seawater directly as working fluid — no need to procure ammonia or other working fluids.
3. Environmentally benign — no fuel combustion, no greenhouse-gas emissions during operation.
4. Continuous baseload generation — runs 24×7 (unlike solar PV or wind).

**Disadvantages (any 3):**
1. Very low thermal efficiency (2–2.5 % actual) — large hardware per MW.
2. Turbine must be enormous due to the very low density of low-pressure steam.
3. Requires strict vacuum maintenance and deaeration.
4. Limited geographically — only viable within ≈ 20° of equator.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**(i) Carnot Efficiency (3 marks):**

Convert to Kelvin:

$$T_H = 28 + 273 = 301\,\text{K}; \quad T_C = 6 + 273 = 279\,\text{K}$$

Apply Carnot formula:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{279}{301} = 1 - 0.9269 = 0.0731$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 7.31\,\%}$$

**(ii) Net Power Output (2 marks):**

Heat input from warm stream:

$$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_H \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_H = 250 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 3 = 3139.5\,\text{kW}$$

Net power:

$$\dot{W}_{\text{net}} = \eta_{\text{actual}} \cdot \dot{Q}_H = 0.025 \cdot 3139.5 = 78.49\,\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_{\text{net}} \approx 78.5\,\text{kW}}$$

**(iii) Cold-Water Mass Flow Rate (2 marks):**

Heat rejected (using energy balance):

$$\dot{Q}_C = \dot{Q}_H - \dot{W}_{\text{net}} = 3139.5 - 78.49 = 3061.0\,\text{kW}$$

Solve for $\dot{m}_C$:

$$\dot{m}_C = \frac{\dot{Q}_C}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_C} = \frac{3061.0}{4.186 \cdot 6} = \frac{3061.0}{25.116} \approx 121.9\,\text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_C \approx 122\,\text{kg/s}}$$

---

#### ▶ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**  
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC** system. Discuss why ammonia is the preferred working fluid. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Explain the principle and block diagram of the **Hybrid OTEC cycle**. Compare its advantages over the open and closed cycles. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

The **Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC** uses a *secondary, low-boiling-point working fluid* — almost always **ammonia (NH₃)** — confined in a sealed loop. The seawater never enters the working-fluid loop; it only provides or absorbs heat through plate-type heat exchangers.

**Working of the cycle (refer to block diagram from Section 4.2):**
1. Warm surface seawater is pumped through the **evaporator** (block 2), transferring heat to liquid ammonia.
2. Liquid NH₃ vaporizes at ≈ 25 °C and ≈ 4–7 bar pressure.
3. High-pressure NH₃ vapor enters the **turbine** (block 3), expands, and drives the generator (block 4).
4. Low-pressure NH₃ vapor enters the **condenser** (block 5), where it is re-condensed by cold deep seawater (block 7).
5. Liquid NH₃ is pressurized by the **feed pump** (block 6) and returned to the evaporator — the loop is closed.

**Why ammonia is preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Very low boiling point** (−33 °C at 1 atm) — easily vaporized at 25 °C warm-side temperature.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg} \approx 1370\,$kJ/kg) — high energy density per kg.
3. **High vapor density** at moderate pressure → **compact turbine** (unlike huge steam turbines of Open Cycle).
4. **Chemical stability, commercial availability, easy handling** (with safety protocols).
5. **Low cost** compared to refrigerants like R-134a.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Hybrid Cycle Principle:** The hybrid cycle combines the **freshwater byproduct** of the Open Cycle with the **compact turbine** of the Closed Cycle. Warm seawater is first **flash-evaporated** (just like Open Cycle) at vacuum ≈ 0.034 bar to produce low-pressure steam. This steam does **not** go directly to a turbine. Instead, it enters a secondary heat exchanger where it vaporizes **ammonia** in a closed loop. The ammonia vapor then drives a compact turbine. The leftover steam on the seawater side is condensed by cold deep seawater, producing **fresh water** as a byproduct.

**Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 above.

**Advantages over the Open Cycle (any 2):**
1. Compact NH₃ turbine — much smaller and cheaper than the huge steam turbine of the Open Cycle.
2. Higher cycle efficiency (≈ 2.5–3 % vs ≈ 2 %).
3. Seawater intake screens are less prone to biofouling (because seawater is not the working fluid).

**Advantages over the Closed Cycle (any 2):**
1. Produces **fresh water** as a byproduct (Closed Cycle does not).
2. Lower-pressure NH₃ loop is more compact due to the extra preheating by flash steam.
3. Lower pumping power on warm side (no need to pump seawater through the entire evaporator).

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. More complex system — two coupled loops → higher capital cost and maintenance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on OTEC Questions:**
> 
> **Pitfall #1 — Wrong temperature units in Carnot formula (–1 to –2 marks):**  
> Writing $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$ with °C values directly is the **single most common** error. The exam key **requires** Kelvin. Always write the explicit conversion step: $T_K = T_{°C} + 273$.
> 
> **Pitfall #2 — Confusing Open vs Closed Cycle working fluid (–1 mark):**  
> Many students write "Open Cycle uses ammonia" or "Closed Cycle uses seawater." The correct statement is: **Open Cycle uses seawater (flashed); Closed Cycle uses ammonia (NH₃); Hybrid uses both.**
> 
> **Pitfall #3 — Missing the block-diagram arrows / unlabelled blocks (–2 marks):**  
> In the 14-mark answer, the block diagram must show **(i) numbered or named blocks** (evaporator, turbine, condenser, pump), **(ii) labeled arrows** (warm water in, cold water in, power out, working fluid direction), and **(iii) pressure/temperature values** for top marks. A diagram without labels will lose at least 2 marks.
> 
> **Pitfall #4 — Forgetting the cold-water intake depth (–1 mark):**  
> Always mention that cold seawater is drawn from ≈ **800–1000 m depth** for full clarity on the source of the cold reservoir.
> 
> **Pitfall #5 — Confusing Hybrid Cycle description (–1 mark):**  
> The Hybrid cycle uses **seawater to flash-evaporate, then uses the flashed steam to drive NH₃ vaporization in a secondary loop** — not the reverse. Memorize this direction correctly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Top 15 things you MUST remember for OTEC (Module 3):**
> 
> 1. **Full form:** Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion.
> 2. **Principle:** Heat engine using warm surface (≈ 25–28 °C) and cold deep seawater (≈ 5–8 °C).
> 3. **Min ΔT required:** ≈ 20 K; viable only between latitudes 20° N and 20° S.
> 4. **Carnot efficiency formula:** $\eta_C = 1 - T_C/T_H$ (T in Kelvin).
> 5. **Typical Carnot efficiency:** 6–7 %; typical actual efficiency: 2.5–3 %.
> 6. **Open Cycle (Claude):** Uses seawater itself as working fluid; needs **vacuum (≈ 0.034 bar)** evaporator; produces **fresh water**; very large turbine.
> 7. **Closed Cycle (Anderson):** Uses **ammonia (NH₃)** in a sealed loop; **plate heat exchangers**; compact turbine; **no freshwater** byproduct.
> 8. **Hybrid Cycle:** Combines Open Cycle flash evaporation with Closed Cycle NH₃ turbine; produces freshwater; compact turbine.
> 9. **Working fluid properties that matter:** low boiling point, high $h_{fg}$, high vapor density.
> 10. **Three by-products of OTEC:** electricity + (in Open/Hybrid) fresh water + cold water for AC/aquaculture.
> 11. **Solar input:** OTEC is an *indirect* solar technology — sun heats the surface water, which is the energy reservoir.
> 12. **Cold water intake depth:** ≈ 800–1000 m below sea surface.
> 13. **Cold water flow rate:** typically 1.3–2 × warm water flow rate.
> 14. **Pinch losses:** Real plants lose ≈ 2–4 K at each heat exchanger due to finite ΔT; this reduces real efficiency.
> 15. **Block diagram must contain:** warm intake → evaporator → turbine → generator → condenser → cold intake → (and either vacuum pump for Open or feed pump for Closed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geothermal Energy: Introduction, Hot Dry Rock Resources, Magma Resources, Vapour and Liquid Dominated Systems, Binary Cycle, Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geothermal Energy: Introduction, Hot Dry Rock Resources, Magma Resources, Vapour and Liquid Dominated Systems, Binary Cycle, Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Geothermal Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Geothermal Energy** is the thermal energy generated and stored within the Earth's crust. It is a form of renewable energy derived from the natural heat of the Earth, originating from the original planetary accretion (primordial heat) and the continuous radioactive decay of unstable isotopes (such as Uranium-238, Thorium-232, and Potassium-40) present in the core and mantle.

The accessible portion of this energy for human exploitation is concentrated in the upper **10–15 km** of the Earth's crust, where the average **geothermal gradient** is approximately **25 °C per kilometre**, although localised values can be 5–10 times higher in tectonically active regions.

The standard academic classification (per Dickson and Fanelli, used in KTU syllabus) categorises geothermal systems by their reservoir temperature:

| Resource Type | Reservoir Temperature (°C) | Typical Utilisation |
|---------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Low-Enthalpy  | < 90 °C                    | Direct use, heat pumps |
| Medium-Enthalpy | 90 – 150 °C               | Binary cycle power |
| High-Enthalpy | > 150 °C                   | Flash, dry steam power |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Earth as a Pressure Cooker

Imagine the Earth as a giant, very slow pressure cooker that has been "switched on" for **4.5 billion years**. The crust acts as the thick metallic lid, the mantle as the boiling water below, and the radioactive isotopes as the heating element buried inside.

- **At the edges (plate boundaries)**, the lid is cracked or thin — this is where steam naturally escapes (think of Iceland, Japan, New Zealand, the Larderello fields in Italy, and The Geysers in California). These "leaks" are the **conventional** geothermal resources.
- **Away from the edges**, the lid is thick and intact. The heat is still there — locked inside **hot, dry rocks** (HDR) or molten **magma** — but we must drill down and engineer our own "leak" to harvest it. These are the **unconventional / enhanced** resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition Question (3 marks):** A student is expected to write the formal definition, mention the three origins of heat (primordial, radiogenic, and frictional), and state the average geothermal gradient value (**≈ 25–30 °C/km**).

### 1.3 Global Heat Flow & Physical Constants

- **Earth's core temperature:** ≈ **5000 – 6000 °C** (comparable to the surface of the Sun).
- **Average surface heat flow:** ≈ **60 mW/m²** (Global mean).
- **Continental heat flow:** ≈ **65 mW/m²**.
- **Oceanic heat flow:** ≈ **100 mW/m²** (thinner crust).
- **Estimated global thermal energy in the upper 10 km:** > **10²⁶ J** — roughly two to three orders of magnitude larger than the energy content of all fossil fuel reserves combined.
- **Installed global geothermal capacity (2024):** ≈ **15.4 GW_e** (electrical) plus ≈ **28 GW_th** (direct use).

> [!TIP]
> **Unit conversion memory aid:** $1 \, \text{TWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J} = 3.6 \, \text{TJ}$. The global geothermal resource in the upper 10 km is therefore equivalent to **> 10¹⁴ TWh** — practically inexhaustible on a human time scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Geothermal Resources

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a clear understanding of the resource taxonomy:

#### A. Hydrothermal Resources (Conventional)
These systems contain both **heat, permeable rock, and a natural fluid (water or steam)** in the same place — the classic "natural boiler" configuration. They are subdivided into:

1. **Vapour-Dominated (Dry Steam) Systems**
   - Reservoir fluid is **predominantly steam** (vapour quality ≈ 1).
   - Temperature: **230 – 250 °C**, Pressure: **30 – 40 bar**.
   - Prime examples: **The Geysers (California, USA)** and **Larderello (Tuscany, Italy)**.
   - Steam is piped **directly to the turbine** — the most efficient and oldest exploitation method.
   - These are rare: only **< 1%** of identified geothermal resources worldwide.

2. **Liquid-Dominated (Wet Steam / Hot Water) Systems**
   - Reservoir fluid is **predominantly liquid water** at high temperature and pressure.
   - The fluid reaches the surface as a **two-phase mixture** of hot water and steam (a "geyser" effect).
   - Sub-categorised by enthalpy:
     - **High-temperature (> 200 °C):** Used in **flash steam plants**.
     - **Low-to-medium temperature (100 – 200 °C):** Used in **binary cycle plants**.
   - Examples: **Wairakei (New Zealand)**, **Cerro Prieto (Mexico)**, **Most Philippine fields**.

#### B. Hot Dry Rock (HDR) / Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS)
- These systems have **heat and rock, but little or no natural fluid or permeability**.
- Heat is extracted by **engineering a closed loop**: drilling two wells (injection + production), creating a fracture network (hydraulic or chemical stimulation), and circulating water through the artificial reservoir.
- Typical depths: **3 – 10 km**; Temperatures: **150 – 300 °C**.
- Pioneering project: **Fenton Hill, New Mexico (USA)** — Los Alamos National Laboratory, 1970s.
- Current flagship: **The DEEP project (UK)**, Soultz-sous-Forêts (France), Cooper Basin (Australia), and the Iceland Deep Drilling Project (IDDP).

#### C. Magma Resources
- Direct utilisation of **molten or partially molten rock** at temperatures of **700 – 1300 °C**, found in **active volcanic regions**.
- Enormous energy density (≈ 100× higher than conventional hydrothermal resources per unit volume), but technologically extreme.
- The only well-documented attempt is the **Magma Energy Program at Krafla, Iceland (IDDP-1)**, where a well accidentally drilled into magma at 2.1 km depth struck **427 °C superheated steam**.

#### D. Geopressured Resources
- Deep sedimentary basins containing **hot water (150 – 200 °C) under very high pressure (up to 1000 bar)** dissolved with **natural gas (methane)** and **brine**.
- Triple-value resource: **thermal energy, hydraulic pressure, and methane**.
- Most prevalent along the **US Gulf Coast**.

### 2.2 Operating Principles of Key Systems

#### 2.2.1 Flash Steam Plant (for high-enthalpy liquid-dominated systems)

**Operating sequence:**
1. High-pressure hot brine (≥ 200 °C, > 15 bar) is pumped from the production well.
2. The fluid is throttled through a **flash tank** at reduced pressure (typically 4–7 bar) — a fraction of the water **flashes to steam** instantly.
3. Steam is separated, dried, and sent to the turbine.
4. Remaining hot brine is either reinjected (in modern closed-loop designs) or cascaded into a **double-flash** stage for improved efficiency.
5. Condenser condenses the exhaust steam; condensate is cooled in a cooling tower and reinjected.

**Thermal efficiency range:** 10 – 20 % (single flash) and 20 – 25 % (double flash).

#### 2.2.2 Dry Steam Plant (for vapour-dominated systems)

The simplest possible cycle:
1. **Natural dry steam** (≈ 240 °C) is piped from the wellhead.
2. A **centrifugal separator (cyclone)** removes rock dust and entrained solids.
3. Steam drives the **turbine** directly.
4. Exhaust steam is **condensed** in a surface condenser.
5. The condensate is either reinjected (best practice) or discharged.

**Thermal efficiency range:** 15 – 25 %.

#### 2.2.3 Binary Cycle Plant (for low-to-medium enthalpy and HDR resources)

This is the **KTU 2024 syllabus focus** and the most rapidly growing technology worldwide.

**Operating sequence:**
1. **Hot geothermal brine** (typically 120 – 180 °C) is pumped through a **pre-heater (evaporator)**.
2. A **secondary working fluid** with a low boiling point (e.g., **isobutane, isopentane, R-134a, R-245fa, or n-pentane**) is vaporised in a closed loop.
3. The vapour expands through an **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)** turbine.
4. The vapour is condensed in a **standard air-cooled or water-cooled condenser**.
5. The working fluid is pressurised by a **feed pump** and returns to the evaporator — completing a **fully closed, zero-emission loop**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a binary cycle?** The geothermal fluid is never in contact with the atmosphere or the turbine. This means: (i) no release of H₂S, no scaling in the turbine, (ii) exploitation of low-enthalpy resources (< 150 °C) that are useless for flash plants, and (iii) modular construction is easy.

**Key working fluids and their saturation temperatures at 1 atm:**

| Working Fluid | Boiling Point (°C) | Typical Reservoir Temp | Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Water (H₂O)   | 100                | > 200 °C (Flash only)    | 0                               |
| Isobutane     | -11.7              | 120 – 180 °C             | 0                               |
| Isopentane    | 28                 | 120 – 160 °C             | 0                               |
| R-134a        | -26                | 100 – 150 °C             | 0                               |
| R-245fa       | 15                 | 100 – 160 °C             | 0                               |
| n-Pentane     | 36                 | 130 – 180 °C             | 0                               |

**Thermal efficiency of binary plant:** 8 – 14 % (lower than flash, but applicable to much larger resource base).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU values the ability to **state the formula, identify inputs, substitute values, and report units with the correct significant figures**. Memorise the table below.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $q_d = \dfrac{Q_d}{A}$ | Surface heat flow density (heat flux) | $\text{mW/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dT}{dz} = \dfrac{T_d - T_s}{z}$ | Geothermal gradient | $°\text{C/km}$ |
| 3 | $Q = \rho \, c_p \, V \, (T_r - T_{ref})$ | Total heat stored in a rock volume (volumetric method) | J |
| 4 | $Q_R = R_f \times Q$ | Recoverable heat ($R_f$ = recovery factor, typically 0.1 – 0.25) | J |
| 5 | $P_e = \eta_{th} \times \dfrac{Q_R}{t \times 3.6 \times 10^{12}}$ | Electrical power output (if $Q_R$ in J, $t$ in hours) | $\text{MWe}$ |
| 6 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Maximum theoretical (Carnot) thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Actual thermal efficiency of cycle | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_g = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{c_{p,g} \, (T_r - T_{rej})}$ | Required geothermal mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 9 | $\Delta P = \rho \, g \, \Delta z$ | Hydrostatic head (well design) | Pa |
| 10 | $E_{stored} = \rho c V \Delta T$ | Stored thermal energy per unit volume | J/m³ |
| 11 | $\eta_{binary} \approx 0.1 \, (T_h - T_c) \, / \, 100$ | Approximate binary plant efficiency (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $C_{f} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{rej}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = 1 - \eta_{th}$ | Capacity rejection factor | dimensionless |

**Standard constants to commit to memory:**

- $\rho_{rock} \approx \mathbf{2700 \, kg/m^3}$
- $c_{p,rock} \approx \mathbf{920 \, J/(kg \cdot K)}$
- $\rho_{water} \approx \mathbf{1000 \, kg/m^3}$
- $c_{p,water} \approx \mathbf{4186 \, J/(kg \cdot K)}$
- Volumetric specific heat of rock: $(\rho c_p)_{rock} \approx \mathbf{2.5 \times 10^6 \, J/(m^3 \cdot K)}$

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real-World Engineering

- **Baseload Renewable Power:** Unlike solar and wind, geothermal provides **24/7, dispatchable power** with a typical capacity factor of **> 90 %** (vs. ≈ 25 % for solar and ≈ 40 % for onshore wind). This is a critical enabler for grid stability.
- **Small Land Footprint:** A 50 MWe flash plant occupies about **1 – 2 km²** (including wellfield), compared to **> 20 km²** for an equivalent solar farm.
- **Independence from Atmospheric Weather:** Operates in deserts, polar regions, and underground — uniquely suited to off-grid applications.
- **Direct Use Applications:** District heating, greenhouse cultivation, aquaculture, balneology (spas), industrial process heat, snow melting, and ground-source heat pumps (GSHPs) are all part of the geothermal portfolio.

## SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Volumetric Heat-in-Place Method (the most common KTU numerical)

**Statement:** A cylindrical geothermal reservoir has a surface area $A = 5 \, \text{km}^2$ and an average vertical thickness $H = 300 \, \text{m}$. The average reservoir temperature is $T_r = 220 \, °\text{C}$ and the reference (mean surface) temperature is $T_{ref} = 25 \, °\text{C}$. Calculate the total thermal energy stored in the rock volume, the recoverable heat (assume $R_f = 0.20$), and the maximum theoretical electrical power available if the plant operates for $t = 30$ years continuously at a thermal-to-electrical conversion efficiency of $\eta_{th} = 0.18$.

**Step 1 — Compute the reservoir volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A \times H \\[2pt]
  &= (5 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^2) \times (300 \, \text{m}) \\[2pt]
  &= 1.5 \times 10^9 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The reservoir is treated as a flat slab of area $A$ and thickness $H$. Unit conversion: $5 \, \text{km}^2 = 5 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the volumetric specific heat of the rock matrix.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\rho c_p)_{rock} &= \rho_{rock} \times c_{p,rock} \\[2pt]
                 &= (2700 \, \text{kg/m}^3) \times (920 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}) \\[2pt]
                 &= 2.484 \times 10^6 \, \text{J/(m}^3 \cdot \text{K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** This is the heat capacity of 1 m³ of rock per 1 K of temperature rise — it is the fundamental rock property used in all volumetric heat-in-place estimates.

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature difference.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_r - T_{ref} \\[2pt]
         &= 220 - 25 \\[2pt]
         &= 195 \, °\text{C} \equiv 195 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** A temperature *difference* in °C equals the same in K — this is a frequently tested KTU concept.

**Step 4 — Compute the total thermal energy stored in place ($Q$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= (\rho c_p)_{rock} \times V \times \Delta T \\[2pt]
  &= (2.484 \times 10^6) \times (1.5 \times 10^9) \times 195 \\[2pt]
  &= 7.265 \times 10^{17} \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the recoverable heat ($Q_R$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_R &= R_f \times Q \\[2pt]
    &= 0.20 \times (7.265 \times 10^{17}) \\[2pt]
    &= 1.453 \times 10^{17} \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Only a fraction $R_f$ of the in-place heat can be extracted, because the temperature of the rock cannot be reduced to $T_{ref}$ — pumping losses, fluid residence time, and rock-mechanical limits constrain the recovery to typically 5–25 %.

**Step 6 — Compute the operating time in seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 30 \, \text{years} \times 365.25 \, \text{d/yr} \times 24 \, \text{h/d} \times 3600 \, \text{s/h} \\[2pt]
  &= 30 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \\[2pt]
  &= 9.461 \times 10^8 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the average available thermal power ($\dot{Q}_{avail}$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{avail} &= \dfrac{Q_R}{t} \\[2pt]
                &= \dfrac{1.453 \times 10^{17}}{9.461 \times 10^8} \\[2pt]
                &= 1.536 \times 10^8 \, \text{W} = 153.6 \, \text{MW}_{th}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the electrical power output ($P_e$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= \eta_{th} \times \dot{Q}_{avail} \\[2pt]
    &= 0.18 \times 153.6 \, \text{MW} \\[2pt]
    &= 27.65 \, \text{MW}_e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed style for KTU):**
> The reservoir stores $\approx 7.27 \times 10^{17} \, \text{J}$, of which $\approx 1.45 \times 10^{17} \, \text{J}$ is recoverable, sufficient to support a **27.6 MWe** plant for 30 years.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Geothermal Mass Flow Rate for a Given Binary Plant

**Statement:** A binary cycle plant is to deliver $P_e = 5 \, \text{MW}_e$ using a geothermal brine at $T_{in} = 165 \, °\text{C}$ that is cooled to $T_{out} = 80 \, °\text{C}$ in the pre-heater. The brine has $c_{p,brine} = 4200 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$. The cycle efficiency is $\eta_{binary} = 0.11$. Determine the required brine mass flow rate $\dot{m}_g$.

**Step 1 — Relate electrical power to thermal input.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= \eta_{binary} \times \dot{Q}_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{P_e}{\eta_{binary}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot{Q}_{in}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^6 \, \text{W}}{0.11} \\[2pt]
             &= 4.545 \times 10^7 \, \text{W} = 45.45 \, \text{MW}_{th}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature drop of the brine.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{brine} &= 165 - 80 = 85 \, \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the sensible-heat equation for the brine.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} &= \dot{m}_g \, c_{p,brine} \, \Delta T_{brine} \\[2pt]
\dot{m}_g &= \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{c_{p,brine} \, \Delta T_{brine}} \\[2pt]
         &= \dfrac{4.545 \times 10^7}{(4200) \times (85)} \\[2pt]
         &= 127.3 \, \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The plant requires a brine flow of **≈ 127.3 kg/s** (or ≈ **460 m³/h**).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Carnot Upper Bound for a Binary Cycle (for comparative analysis)

**Statement:** A binary cycle operates between a hot reservoir at $T_h = 160 \, °\text{C} = 433.15 \, \text{K}$ and a cold sink at $T_c = 25 \, °\text{C} = 298.15 \, \text{K}$. Compute the **Carnot efficiency** and compare it to the actual binary cycle efficiency of 11 %.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Carnot} &= 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h} \\[2pt]
              &= 1 - \dfrac{298.15}{433.15} \\[2pt]
              &= 1 - 0.6884 \\[2pt]
              &= 0.3116 = 31.16 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The actual cycle achieves only **11 / 31.16 = 35.3 %** of the theoretical Carnot efficiency. This is a realistic number for an ORC binary plant (typical: 30 – 50 % of Carnot).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Resource Type vs. Plant Type (KTU Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Dry Steam Plant | Flash Steam Plant | Binary Cycle Plant | HDR / EGS Plant |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Reservoir state | Vapour | Liquid | Liquid | Solid (dry) |
| Reservoir T (°C) | > 200 | > 180 | 120 – 180 | 150 – 300 |
| Working fluid | Steam (H₂O) | Steam (H₂O) | Isobutane / R-134a | Water (closed loop) |
| Plant η (%) | 15 – 25 | 10 – 20 | 8 – 14 | 5 – 12 |
| Emission to air | Some H₂S, CO₂ | Some H₂S, CO₂ | Zero (closed loop) | Zero (closed loop) |
| Reinjection | Partial | Required | Required | Mandatory |
| Reservoir fluid | Natural | Natural | Natural | Engineered |
| Plant complexity | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Example | The Geysers, USA | Wairakei, NZ | Heber, USA; Chena, Alaska | Fenton Hill, USA |
| Investment $/kWe | 4000 – 6000 | 3500 – 5000 | 3000 – 4500 | 6000 – 10000 |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: HDR Reservoir Heat Extraction Rate (simplified closed-loop model)

**Statement:** An HDR reservoir at depth $z = 4 \, \text{km}$ has rock temperature $T_r = 250 \, °\text{C}$ and the mean surface is $T_{ref} = 15 \, °\text{C}$. If a closed-loop system injects water at $T_{inj} = 70 \, °\text{C}$ and recovers it at $T_{prod} = 200 \, °\text{C}$, compute (a) the geothermal gradient, (b) the heat extracted per kg of circulating water.

**Part (a): Geothermal gradient.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dT}{dz} &= \dfrac{T_r - T_{ref}}{z} \\[2pt]
               &= \dfrac{250 - 15}{4} \\[2pt]
               &= 58.75 \, °\text{C/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b): Heat extracted per kg.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= c_{p,w} \times (T_{prod} - T_{inj}) \\[2pt]
  &= 4186 \times (200 - 70) \\[2pt]
  &= 4186 \times 130 \\[2pt]
  &= 544{,}180 \, \text{J/kg} = 544.18 \, \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For each kg of water circulated, ~544 kJ of heat is recovered — equivalent to the calorific value of ≈ 12 mL of gasoline per kg of water.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define geothermal energy. State the three sources of heat within the Earth and the average geothermal gradient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Geothermal energy is the thermal energy stored in the Earth's crust, originating from (i) **primordial heat** from planetary accretion, (ii) **radiogenic heat** from the decay of isotopes (²³⁸U, ²³²Th, ⁴⁰K) in the crust and mantle, and (iii) **frictional heat** from tectonic movement. The average geothermal gradient is **25 – 30 °C per km depth** of the Earth's crust.

> [!NOTE]
> Award **1 mark** for a clear definition, **1 mark** for the three heat sources, **1 mark** for the numerical gradient value.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between vapour-dominated and liquid-dominated geothermal systems with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vapour-dominated (dry steam) system contains primarily **steam** in the reservoir, with temperatures of 230 – 250 °C. The steam is piped directly to the turbine; an example is **The Geysers, California**. A liquid-dominated (wet steam/hot water) system contains primarily **hot pressurized water**, which flashes to steam only at the surface; an example is **Wairakei, New Zealand**. The vapour system is **rarer but more efficient**.

---

### 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Choice 1]`**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **Binary Cycle geothermal power plant** with a neat block diagram. List **two advantages** of using an organic working fluid (e.g., isobutane) over water. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A geothermal reservoir occupies an area of **4 km²** and has an average thickness of **250 m**. The rock density is 2700 kg/m³, the specific heat is 920 J/(kg·K), the reservoir temperature is 200 °C, and the surface reference temperature is 30 °C. If the recovery factor is **0.18** and the plant operates continuously for **25 years** at a thermal conversion efficiency of **15%**, calculate:
  - (i) Total thermal energy in place.
  - (ii) Recoverable thermal energy.
  - (iii) Average electrical power output of the plant. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Principle of Operation (5 marks):**

A binary cycle plant has **two separate fluid loops** — the geothermal loop and the organic Rankine cycle (ORC) loop — hence the name "binary".

1. **Hot geothermal brine** (typically 120 – 180 °C) is pumped from the production well through a **preheater/evaporator**.
2. The brine's heat is transferred across the evaporator tubes to a **secondary working fluid** with a low boiling point (isobutane, isopentane, R-134a, n-pentane).
3. The working fluid **vaporises**, expands through an **ORC turbine**, and drives a generator.
4. The expanded vapour enters a **condenser** (water-cooled or air-cooled), liquefies, and is pressurised by a **feed pump** back to the evaporator — closing the ORC loop.
5. The cooled geothermal brine is **reinjected** into the reservoir — closing the geothermal loop.
6. Since the working fluid is in a **fully closed loop**, there are no atmospheric emissions, no scaling in the turbine, and the cycle is **modular & compact**.

**Two advantages of organic working fluid over water (2 marks):**

1. **Low boiling point** (e.g., isobutane boils at -11.7 °C) allows efficient power generation from **low-enthalpy resources** (as low as 100 °C), where flashing water to steam is impossible.
2. **Closed-loop operation** ensures **zero direct emissions** of H₂S, CO₂, and mercury — making it **environmentally benign** and acceptable in ecologically sensitive areas.

---

**Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given data:**
$A = 4 \, \text{km}^2 = 4 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^2$; $H = 250 \, \text{m}$; $\rho = 2700 \, \text{kg/m}^3$; $c_p = 920 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$; $T_r = 200 \, °\text{C}$; $T_{ref} = 30 \, °\text{C}$; $R_f = 0.18$; $t = 25 \, \text{yr}$; $\eta_{th} = 0.15$.

**Step 1 — Reservoir volume $V$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A \times H = (4 \times 10^6) \times 250 = 1.0 \times 10^9 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total thermal energy in place $Q$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \rho \, c_p \, V \, (T_r - T_{ref}) \\
  &= 2700 \times 920 \times (1.0 \times 10^9) \times (200 - 30) \\
  &= 2700 \times 920 \times 10^9 \times 170 \\
  &= 4.2228 \times 10^{17} \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recoverable heat $Q_R$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_R &= R_f \times Q = 0.18 \times 4.2228 \times 10^{17} = 7.601 \times 10^{16} \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Operating time $t$ in seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 25 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 7.884 \times 10^8 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Average electrical power $P_e$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= \eta_{th} \times \dfrac{Q_R}{t} \\
    &= 0.15 \times \dfrac{7.601 \times 10^{16}}{7.884 \times 10^8} \\
    &= 0.15 \times 9.642 \times 10^7 \, \text{W} \\
    &= 1.446 \times 10^7 \, \text{W} = 14.46 \, \text{MW}_e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final boxed results:**
- (i) $Q = 4.22 \times 10^{17} \, \text{J}$
- (ii) $Q_R = 7.60 \times 10^{16} \, \text{J}$
- (iii) $P_e = 14.46 \, \text{MW}_e$

---

#### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice 2]`**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of **Hot Dry Rock (HDR) geothermal energy extraction**. Mention **two technical challenges** of HDR systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four advantages and three disadvantages** of geothermal energy. Compare the thermal efficiency of **dry steam, flash steam, and binary cycle** plants. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Principle of HDR (5 marks):**

Hot Dry Rock (HDR) systems tap heat from **impermeable hot rock formations** (typically granite) at depths of **3 – 10 km** that contain **insufficient natural fluid or permeability**. The extraction method is:

1. **Drill an injection well** into the hot rock zone (target rock temperature 150 – 300 °C).
2. **Create an artificial fracture network** in the rock by high-pressure hydraulic stimulation (water at 200 – 400 bar) — this is called the **reservoir** or "heat exchanger".
3. **Drill a second (production) well** that intersects the fracture network at a point offset from the injection well.
4. **Inject cold water** (≈ 70 °C) down the injection well; it flows through the fracture network and is **heated by conduction** to ≈ 150 – 250 °C.
5. **Pump the hot water** up the production well to the surface, where it drives a **binary cycle ORC plant** (HDR water is too mineral-laden for direct flash).
6. **Cooled water is reinjected** — completing the closed loop.

**Two Technical Challenges of HDR (2 marks):**

1. **Induced seismicity:** Hydraulic fracturing can trigger small earthquakes (e.g., the 2006 Basel, Switzerland EGS project was abandoned after a magnitude 3.4 event).
