2. **Reservoir sustainability & water loss:** Maintaining fracture permeability over decades is difficult; micro-seismic monitoring and tracer tests are required to track fluid pathways and prevent short-circuiting.

---

**Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Four Advantages of Geothermal Energy (4 marks):**

1. **Baseload & dispatchable:** Capacity factor > 90 % (vs. 25 % solar, 40 % wind); output is independent of weather and time of day.
2. **Small land footprint:** A 50 MWe plant needs ≈ 1 km² vs. ≈ 20 km² for solar PV of equal capacity.
3. **Low operating cost & long life:** Plant lifetime 25 – 30 years; LCOE for new geothermal is becoming competitive ($0.05 – 0.10 / kWh).
4. **Clean & low-emission:** 50 g CO₂/kWh vs. ≈ 1000 g CO₂/kWh for coal; no SOx, NOx, or particulate matter emissions at the site.

**Three Disadvantages of Geothermal Energy (3 marks):**

1. **Location-specific:** Economic-grade resources are limited to tectonically active zones (Ring of Fire, Iceland, Rift Valley, Indonesia, Philippines).
2. **High upfront capital cost:** Drilling exploration wells costs $5 – 15 M each; deep EGS wells can exceed $20 M.
3. **Risk of induced seismicity & H₂S release:** Even with closed-loop systems, fluid reinjection pressures must be carefully managed; sour gas (H₂S) is a corrosive hazard in some high-enthalpy fields.

**Comparative Thermal Efficiency Table (additional, 1 mark):**

| Plant Type | Typical Thermal Efficiency | Reservoir T (°C) |
|------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| Dry Steam | 15 – 25 % | > 200 |
| Flash Steam (single) | 10 – 20 % | > 180 |
| Flash Steam (double) | 20 – 25 % | > 200 |
| Binary Cycle | 8 – 14 % | 120 – 180 |
| HDR / EGS | 5 – 12 % | 150 – 300 |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Geothermal Numerical Questions:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error in $A$ (km² → m²):** Many students forget to multiply $A$ by $10^6$. Worth **0.5 to 1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Time conversion (years → seconds):** Use $1 \, \text{year} = 3.1536 \times 10^7 \, \text{s}$. A wrong conversion can swing the answer by an order of magnitude.
> 3. **Confusing °C with K in temperature difference:** Always remember that a *difference* in °C is numerically equal to K (ΔT = Δ°C), but an *absolute* temperature **must** be in Kelvin for the Carnot formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the volumetric specific heat formula $E = \rho c V \Delta T$ and writing only $E = m c \Delta T$ without the $V$ step** — you may lose **2 marks** for skipping a logical step.
> 5. **Writing "geothermal" as "geothermal" is correct, but students often incorrectly write "geo-thermal" as two words** — KTU examiners may deduct **0.5 mark** for spelling.
> 6. **In binary-cycle theory questions, students forget to mention that the working fluid is in a CLOSED loop** — this is a **mandatory** concept worth **2 marks** in the KTU marking scheme.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Geothermal energy = heat from Earth's interior (primordial + radiogenic + frictional).
- **Average gradient:** **25 – 30 °C/km**; Heat flow density: **60 mW/m²** (continental).
- **Three origins of heat:** Accretion, radioactive decay, tidal friction.
- **Resource classes:** Hydrothermal (conventional) → Vapour-dominated & Liquid-dominated; HDR/EGS, Geopressured, Magma (unconventional).
- **Dry steam plants:** Use natural steam directly → only 2 major sites globally (Geysers, Larderello) → highest efficiency (15 – 25 %).
- **Flash steam plants:** Hot water throttled to lower pressure → steam separated → efficiency 10 – 25 %; standard for > 180 °C.
- **Binary cycle plants:** Geothermal fluid heats an **organic working fluid** in a closed loop → enables use of low-enthalpy resources (120 – 180 °C); efficiency 8 – 14 %; **zero emissions**; **modular**.
- **Common working fluids:** Isobutane, isopentane, R-134a, R-245fa, n-pentane (all with low boiling point & zero ODP).
- **HDR/EGS:** Heat in dry rock (3 – 10 km, 150 – 300 °C); requires **hydraulic fracturing**; closed loop; **2 wells minimum**.
- **Magma resources:** Molten rock at 700 – 1300 °C; extremely high energy density but technically extreme; IDDP-1 at Krafla, Iceland is the only real attempt.
- **Volumetric Heat-in-Place:** $Q = \rho c_p V (T_r - T_{ref})$; Recoverable = $R_f \times Q$ with $R_f = 0.1 - 0.25$.
- **Electrical power:** $P_e = \eta_{th} \times Q_R / t$.
- **Binary cycle mass-flow:** $\dot{m}_g = P_e / [\eta \cdot c_{p,brine} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})]$.
- **Carnot upper bound:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_c/T_h$ (in Kelvin) — actual binary ≈ 30 – 50 % of Carnot.
- **Key advantages:** Baseload, low emissions, small footprint, high capacity factor (> 90 %), long life, weather-independent.
- **Key disadvantages:** Location-specific, high upfront cost, induced seismicity risk, H₂S corrosion, exploration risk.
- **Standard constants to memorise:** $\rho_{rock} = 2700 \, \text{kg/m}^3$; $c_{p,rock} = 920 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$; $c_{p,water} = 4186 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$; $(\rho c_p)_{rock} \approx 2.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{J/(m}^3 \cdot \text{K)}$.
- **KTU favourite comparison tables:** Vapour vs Liquid dominated; Dry Steam vs Flash vs Binary; HDR vs Hydrothermal; Advantages vs Disadvantages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Mass Energy - Biomass Conversion Technologies - Bio Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Mass Energy - Biomass Conversion Technologies - Bio Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Biomass Gasification (KTU Definition)**
> **Biomass gasification** is a *thermo-chemical conversion process* in which solid carbonaceous biomass feedstock (wood, agricultural residues, municipal solid waste, bagasse, coconut shell, rice husk, etc.) is partially oxidised at **elevated temperatures (typically 800 °C – 1200 °C)** in a *controlled, sub-stoichiometric* supply of an oxidising agent (air, pure oxygen, steam, or a mixture) to produce a combustible mixture of gases known as **producer gas** or **synthesis gas (syngas)**.
> 
> The primary combustible constituents of the producer gas are **Carbon Monoxide (CO), Hydrogen (H₂), and Methane (CH₄)**, accompanied by inert species such as **N₂, CO₂**, and traces of **H₂O** and **tars**.

The governing chemical principle is *partial oxidation*, governed by the **Equivalence Ratio (ER)**:

$$ER = \frac{\text{Actual Air–Fuel Ratio}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio}}$$

For gasification, **ER is deliberately maintained between 0.20 and 0.40**, ensuring incomplete combustion and maximum conversion of solid carbon into gaseous fuel.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Campfire"

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine lighting a large bonfire with wet logs. The smoke that escapes unburnt represents *incomplete combustion* — energy leaving as unburnt volatiles. 
> 
> Now, imagine placing those logs inside a sealed metal drum with only **a few small air holes at the bottom**. The air is *limited* — not enough to produce a roaring flame, but just enough to keep the fuel glowing red-hot. The wood does not *burn* in the conventional sense; instead, it **transforms** into a hot, combustible gas that escapes through a pipe at the top.
> 
> This hot gas (producer gas) can be piped to a boiler, an engine, or a turbine to do useful mechanical/electrical work. **That sealed-drum-with-restricted-air setup is, in principle, a biomass gasifier.**

| Everyday Object | Gasification Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Smouldering charcoal in a BBQ | Pyrolysis + Reduction Zone |
| Restricted air holes | Sub-stoichiometric oxidant (ER ≈ 0.25) |
| Fumes leaving the BBQ | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄) |
| Pipe carrying fumes to a stove | Gas cleaning & engine supply line |

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Favoured Terms)

The following standard metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU university examinations:

> [!IMPORTANT] **Five Golden Metrics of Biomass Gasification**
> 1. **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** — dimensionless, optimum 0.20 – 0.40 for air gasification
> 2. **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** — typically **70 % – 80 %** for downdraft gasifiers
> 3. **Gas Yield (GY)** — **2.0 – 2.5 Nm³ of producer gas per kg of dry biomass**
> 4. **Calorific Value (CV) of Producer Gas** — **4.5 – 6.0 MJ/Nm³** (air gasification)
> 5. **Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE)** — **> 95 %** in well-designed systems

The **stoichiometric air required** for complete combustion of carbon is **11.5 kg air per kg of carbon** or **9.55 kg air per kg of biomass** (approximately, depending on ultimate analysis).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Reaction Zones of a Gasifier

A conventional *downdraft* gasifier operates with **four distinct thermochemical zones** stacked vertically. Mastering these zones is a KTU-favourite examination question (typically 7 marks).

### Zone 1 — Drying Zone (100 °C – 200 °C)

- **Purpose:** Evaporate *moisture* from incoming biomass.
- **Process:** Sensible + latent heat transfer from upper combustion/reduction zones.
- **Critical Threshold:** Biomass feed must have **moisture content < 15 % (wet basis)** for stable gasifier operation; otherwise temperature drops and tar generation surges.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *drying* with *devolatilisation*. Drying removes only water; pyrolysis releases volatile hydrocarbons.

### Zone 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilisation Zone (200 °C – 600 °C)

- **Purpose:** Thermal decomposition of biomass in the *absence of oxidant*.
- **Outputs:**
  - **Char** (solid carbonaceous residue)
  - **Volatiles:** CO, H₂, CO₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, tars, water vapour
- **Characteristic Reaction:**
$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Char (C)} + \text{Volatiles (CO, CO}_2, \text{H}_2, \text{CH}_4, \text{tars)} $$

### Zone 3 — Combustion / Oxidation Zone (800 °C – 1200 °C)

- **Purpose:** Supply process heat by exothermic oxidation of char and volatiles.
- **Dominant Reactions:**

$$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

- **Temperature Peak:** Highest in gasifier; supplies heat to drying and reduction zones by counter-current gas flow.

### Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (600 °C – 1000 °C)

- **Purpose:** Endothermic reduction of combustion products to combustible gases.
- **Three Critical Heterogeneous Reactions:**

$$ \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{(Boudouard Reaction, } \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol)} $$

$$ \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water–Gas Reaction, } \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol)} $$

$$ \text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad \text{(Methanation, } \Delta H = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol)} $$

Plus the catalytic **Water–Gas Shift Reaction** in the gas phase:

$$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longleftrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Reduction Zone Matters Most:** It is here that the *low-grade combustion products (CO₂, H₂O)* are converted into *high-grade combustible gases (CO, H₂, CH₄)*. The longer the residence time in this zone, the **higher the calorific value of producer gas** but the **lower the throughput**.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Gasifiers (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Gasifier Type | Air Flow Direction | Biomass Flow | Tar Content | CV of Gas (MJ/Nm³) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Updraft (Counter-Current) Fixed-Bed** | Upward | Downward | **Very High** (10–100 g/Nm³) | 5.0 – 6.0 | Direct firing, drying |
| **Downdraft (Co-Current) Fixed-Bed** | Downward | Downward | **Low** (< 1 g/Nm³) | 4.5 – 5.5 | IC engines (most popular) |
| **Crossdraft Fixed-Bed** | Horizontal | Downward | Moderate | 4.0 – 5.0 | Small-scale, quick start-up |
| **Bubbling Fluidised Bed (BFB)** | Upward | Mixed | Low–Moderate | 4.0 – 7.0 | Medium-large scale, > 1 MW |
| **Circulating Fluidised Bed (CFB)** | Upward (with recirculation) | Mixed | Low | 5.0 – 8.0 | Large-scale, > 5 MW |
| **Entrained Flow** | Co-current | Co-current | Very Low | 8.0 – 15.0 (with O₂) | Slurry-fed, large scale |

> [!NOTE] **Why downdraft dominates KTU numericals:** It produces *clean gas* with *minimal tar*, making it directly suitable for *internal combustion (IC) engines* without elaborate cleaning — hence the most commonly asked design in KTU university exams.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equivalence Ratio** | $ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for carbon** | $O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{32}{12} \times m_C$ | kg O₂ / kg C | ★★★★ |
| 3 | **Stoichiometric air for carbon** | $A_{\text{stoich}} = 11.5 \times m_C$ | kg air / kg C | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | **Producer gas calorific value** | $CV_g = \sum y_i \cdot CV_i$ | MJ/Nm³ | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | **Gas Yield** | $GY = \dfrac{V_g}{m_{\text{biomass}}}$ | Nm³/kg | ★★★★ |
| 6 | **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** | $\eta_{CGE} = \dfrac{V_g \cdot CV_g}{m_{\text{biomass}} \cdot CV_{\text{biomass}}} \times 100$ | % | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | **Hot Gas Efficiency (HGE)** | $\eta_{HGE} = \eta_{CGE} + \dfrac{\text{Sensible heat of gas}}{m_{\text{biomass}} \cdot CV_{\text{biomass}}}$ | % | ★★★★ |
| 8 | **Carbon Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta_{CC} = \dfrac{m_{C,\text{gas}}}{m_{C,\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | ★★★ |
| 9 | **Mass balance (overall)** | $m_{\text{biomass}} + m_{\text{air}} = m_{\text{gas}} + m_{\text{char}} + m_{\text{tar}} + m_{\text{ash}}$ | kg | ★★★★ |
| 10 | **Energy balance** | $E_{\text{biomass}} + E_{\text{air}} = E_{\text{gas}} + E_{\text{losses}}$ | MJ | ★★★★ |
| 11 | **Air–Fuel ratio (mass basis)** | $A/F = \dfrac{2.67 \cdot (32\text{C}/12 + 8\text{H}/2 - \text{O}/32)}{1} \times \dfrac{100}{\text{CV}_{biomass}}$ | kg/kg | ★★★ |
| 12 | **Air requirement per kg biomass** | $A = 11.5\text{C} + 34.5(\text{H} - \text{O}/8) + 4.3\text{S}$ | kg/kg | ★★★★★ |

(Where C, H, O, S are the mass fractions of elements from ultimate analysis, expressed as decimals.)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Biomass gasification is the **bridge technology** between low-density solid biofuels and modern high-efficiency energy conversion devices:

| Application | Energy Vector | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Gasifier + Spark-Ignition Engine | Mechanical / Electrical | 20 % – 25 % overall |
| Gasifier + Diesel Engine (dual-fuel) | Mechanical / Electrical | 30 % – 38 % overall |
| Gasifier + Gas Turbine (BIG/CCGT) | Electrical | 35 % – 45 % overall |
| Gasifier + Solid Oxide Fuel Cell | Electrical | 45 % – 55 % overall |
| Producer gas for direct drying | Thermal | 60 % – 75 % |
| Syngas for Fischer–Tropsch diesel | Liquid biofuel | 35 % – 45 % |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Industry Insight:** India has over **60,000 installed small-scale biomass gasifier systems** (mainly downdraft, 10 kW – 500 kW range) deployed for rice-mills, tea-estate drying, brick-kilns, and rural electrification under the *MNRE Decentralised Distributed Generation* programme. Kerala alone has notable installations in **coir, cashew, and rubber processing industries**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Biomass Feedstock

### Given Ultimate Analysis (mass basis, dry basis):

- Carbon: $\text{C} = 47 \%$
- Hydrogen: $\text{H} = 6 \%$
- Oxygen: $\text{O} = 43 \%$
- Sulphur: $\text{S} = 0.5 \%$
- Nitrogen: $\text{N} = 0.5 \%$
- Ash: $\text{Ash} = 3.0 \%$
- Higher Heating Value: $\text{HHV} = 19.2 \text{ MJ/kg}$

The complete combustion reaction is:

$$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 $$

$$ \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{SO}_2 $$

### Step 1 — Oxygen required per kg of each element (kg O₂ per kg element)

| Element | Stoichiometric Coefficient | O₂ Required (kg/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| C | $\tfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ | 2.667 |
| H | $\tfrac{16}{2} = 8.000$ | 8.000 |
| S | $\tfrac{32}{32} = 1.000$ | 1.000 |
| O | already bound | -1.000 (released when fuel-O combusts) |

### Step 2 — Total O₂ requirement (kg O₂ per kg dry fuel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2^{\text{stoich}} &= 2.667 \, m_C + 8.000 \, m_H + 1.000 \, m_S - 1.000 \, m_O \\
&= 2.667 \cdot 0.47 + 8.000 \cdot 0.06 + 1.000 \cdot 0.005 - 1.000 \cdot 0.43 \\
&= 1.2535 + 0.4800 + 0.0050 - 0.4300 \\
&= 1.3085 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg dry biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to stoichiometric air (mass basis, air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{\text{O}_2^{\text{stoich}}}{0.232} \\
&= \frac{1.3085}{0.232} \\
&= 5.640 \text{ kg air per kg dry biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Actual air supplied (gasification at ER = 0.30):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{actual}} &= ER \times A_{\text{stoich}} \\
&= 0.30 \times 5.640 \\
&= 1.692 \text{ kg air per kg dry biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to **1.402 Nm³ of air per kg of biomass** (taking air density ≈ 1.207 kg/Nm³).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Cold Gas Efficiency from Gas Composition

### Given: Producer gas composition from downdraft gasifier (vol %, dry basis):

| Component | Volume % | Heating Value (MJ/Nm³) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 20.0 | 12.64 |
| H₂ | 18.0 | 10.79 |
| CH₄ | 2.0 | 35.88 |
| CO₂ | 11.0 | 0 |
| N₂ | 49.0 | 0 |

### Step 1 — Calculate calorific value of producer gas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_g &= \sum y_i \cdot CV_i \\
&= 0.20 \cdot 12.64 + 0.18 \cdot 10.79 + 0.02 \cdot 35.88 + 0.11 \cdot 0 + 0.49 \cdot 0 \\
&= 2.528 + 1.9422 + 0.7176 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 5.188 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate gas yield using mass balance (assume all C converts to gas):

Using the air supplied = 1.692 kg/kg biomass and ultimate analysis from §3.1:

- Mass of C in 1 kg biomass = 0.47 kg
- Moles of C = $\frac{0.47}{12} = 0.03917$ kmol
- These carbon atoms distribute between CO and CO₂ in gas:
  - From volume %: $\frac{n_{CO}}{n_{CO} + n_{CO_2}} = \frac{20}{20+11} = 0.6452$
  - So moles in CO = $0.6452 \times 0.03917 = 0.02527$ kmol
  - Moles in CO₂ = $0.03917 - 0.02527 = 0.01390$ kmol

- Moles of H₂ in gas (assume all fuel-H goes to H₂):
  - Fuel H = 0.06 kg → $0.06/2 = 0.030$ kmol H₂
  - But gas shows only 18 % H₂ — let me compute total dry gas moles via N₂ balance:
  - Air supplied = 1.692 kg, N₂ mass = $1.692 \times 0.768 = 1.300$ kg
  - N₂ moles = $1.300/28 = 0.04643$ kmol
  - Total dry gas moles = $\frac{0.04643}{0.49} = 0.09475$ kmol
  - Therefore:
    - CO moles = $0.20 \times 0.09475 = 0.01895$ kmol
    - H₂ moles = $0.18 \times 0.09475 = 0.01705$ kmol
    - CH₄ moles = $0.02 \times 0.09475 = 0.00190$ kmol
    - CO₂ moles = $0.11 \times 0.09475 = 0.01042$ kmol

- Carbon balance check: $(0.01895 + 0.01042) \times 12 = 0.3528$ kg C in gas
  - So carbon conversion = $0.3528/0.47 = 75.1$ % (the rest is in tar/char — typical of gasifier analysis)

### Step 3 — Total volume of dry producer gas (at NTP: 22.414 Nm³/kmol):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_g &= n_{\text{total}} \times 22.414 \\
&= 0.09475 \times 22.414 \\
&= 2.124 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg dry biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Cold Gas Efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{CGE} &= \frac{V_g \cdot CV_g}{m_{\text{biomass}} \cdot CV_{\text{biomass}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.124 \times 5.188}{1 \times 19.2} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{11.020}{19.20} \times 100 \\
&= 57.4 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** A CGE of ~57 % is reasonable for a *basic downdraft gasifier without air pre-heat or gas cleaning heat-recovery*. Well-optimised systems with heat recovery can reach **70 % – 80 %**.

---

## 3.3 Energy Balance Around the Gasifier

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{biomass, in}} + E_{\text{air, in}} &= E_{\text{gas, out}} + E_{\text{tar, out}} + E_{\text{loss,sensible}} + E_{\text{loss,wall}} \\
m_b \cdot CV_b + m_a \cdot h_a &= V_g \cdot CV_g + m_t \cdot CV_t + Q_{\text{stack}} + Q_{\text{wall}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a quick "back-of-envelope" first-law check:

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \frac{\dot{m}_g \cdot CV_g}{\dot{m}_b \cdot CV_b + \dot{W}_{\text{input}}}
$$

where $\dot{W}_{\text{input}}$ accounts for any auxiliaries (blowers, screw feeders).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation (Board-Exam Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Updraft | Downdraft | Crossdraft | BFB | CFB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max gas temp (°C) | 200 – 400 | 700 – 900 | 800 – 1000 | 750 – 900 | 800 – 950 |
| Exit gas temp (°C) | Low | High | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| Tar loading (g/Nm³) | 30 – 150 | 0.5 – 5 | 5 – 20 | 1 – 10 | 1 – 8 |
| CV of producer gas (MJ/Nm³) | 5.0 – 6.0 | 4.5 – 5.5 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 4.5 – 7.0 | 5.5 – 8.0 |
| Carbon conversion (%) | 85 – 95 | 90 – 97 | 80 – 90 | 92 – 98 | 95 – 99 |
| Cold gas efficiency (%) | 50 – 65 | 65 – 80 | 50 – 65 | 65 – 80 | 70 – 85 |
| Feed size tolerance | Wide (up to 100 mm) | Narrow (10 – 80 mm) | Narrow | Very fine | Fine |
| Moisture tolerance | Up to 60 % | < 20 % | < 12 % | < 15 % | < 15 % |
| Scale (MWth) | < 5 | < 2 | < 0.5 | 1 – 50 | 10 – 200 |
| Load turndown ratio | 3 : 1 | 2 : 1 | 2 : 1 | 4 : 1 | 5 : 1 |
| Best suited for | Drying, boilers | IC engines | Small engines | Power, CHP | Large power |

> [!TIP] **KTU Exam Mnemonic:** "**U**pdraft = **U**se for drying; **D**owndraft = **D**rive engines" — memorise the application mapping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Zone Downdraft Gasifier — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock Hopper] --> B[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 600 C]
    C --> D[Combustion Oxidation Zone 800 to 1200 C]
    D --> E[Reduction Zone 600 to 1000 C]
    E --> F[Producer Gas Outlet]
    G[Air Inlet via Nozzles] --> D
    D --> H[Ash and Char Discharge Grate]
    F --> I[Cyclone Particulate Removal]
    I --> J[Heat Exchanger Gas Cooling]
    J --> K[Scrubber Tar Removal]
    K --> L[Clean Producer Gas to Engine or Boiler]
    B -.-> M[Moisture Vapour Escape]
    D -.-> N[Exothermic Heat Recirculation]
    N -.-> B
    N -.-> C
```

---

## 4.2 Comparative Gasifier Classification — Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BIO GASIFIERS] --> B[Fixed Bed Type]
    A --> F[Fluidised Bed Type]
    A --> J[Entrained Flow Type]
    B --> C[Updraft Counter Current]
    B --> D[Downdraft Co Current]
    B --> E[Crossdraft Horizontal]
    F --> G[Bubbling Fluidised Bed BFB]
    F --> H[Circulating Fluidised Bed CFB]
    C --> C1[High Tar High CV Suitable for Direct Firing]
    D --> D1[Low Tar Suitable for IC Engine]
    E --> E1[Fast Response Suitable for Small Loads]
    G --> G1[Medium Scale Uniform Temperature]
    H --> H1[Large Scale High Carbon Conversion]
    J --> K1[Syngas Production Highest CV]
```

---

## 4.3 Reaction Pathway Schematic — From Solid Carbon to Producer Gas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid Biomass CHON] --> B[Drying Zone H2O out]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis 200 to 600 C]
    C --> D[Char Solid Carbon]
    C --> E[Volatiles CO CO2 H2 CH4 Tars]
    D --> F[Combustion Zone C plus O2]
    F --> G[CO2 and Heat]
    G --> H[Reduction Zone C plus CO2]
    H --> I[2 CO Producer Gas]
    G --> J[Reduction Zone C plus H2O]
    J --> K[CO plus H2 Producer Gas]
    E --> L[Water Gas Shift CO plus H2O]
    L --> M[CO2 plus H2]
    I --> N[Final Producer Gas Mixture]
    K --> N
    M --> N
```

---

## 4.4 Producer Gas Conditioning Train (Post-Gasifier Treatment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Hot Producer Gas 700 C] --> B[Cyclone Separator Particulates]
    B --> C[Heat Exchanger Cool to 200 C]
    C --> D[Venturi Scrubber Tar Removal]
    D --> E[Packed Tower Wet Scrubbing]
    E --> F[Demister Droplet Removal]
    F --> G[Activated Carbon Filter]
    G --> H[Clean Cool Producer Gas 40 C]
    H --> I[Storage Gas Holder]
    I --> J[IC Engine or Boiler End Use]
```

> [!NOTE] **KTU Interpretation:** The cyclone–cooler–scrubber–filter train is collectively called the **"gas cleaning train"** and is essential when producer gas is fed to *internal combustion engines* (to avoid valve fouling, knocking, and engine wear). Boards often award 3–5 marks for a labelled diagram of this train.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Define biomass gasification. List any four endothermic reactions occurring in the reduction zone of a downdraft gasifier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Biomass gasification is a thermochemical conversion process in which solid biomass is converted into a combustible gaseous fuel (producer gas) by partial oxidation at elevated temperatures (800 °C – 1200 °C) using a sub-stoichiometric amount of air, oxygen, or steam (Equivalence Ratio ≈ 0.20 – 0.40).

**Four endothermic reactions (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i) Boudouard reaction:} \quad \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&\text{(ii) Water-gas reaction:} \quad \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&\text{(iii) Steam methane reforming:} \quad \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&\text{(iv) Reverse water-gas shift:} \quad \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = +41.2 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Explain the significance of "Equivalence Ratio" (ER) in biomass gasification. What is its typical range for air gasification?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Equivalence Ratio is the ratio of the actual air–fuel ratio supplied to the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio required for complete combustion:

$$ER = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- ER < 1 implies *incomplete combustion*, which is the condition for gasification.
- At very low ER (< 0.20), the process shifts toward *pyrolysis*, yielding high tar and low gas quality.
- At ER ≈ 0.25 – 0.35, optimum gas composition is achieved with good carbon conversion and manageable tar levels.
- At ER > 0.40, the gasifier tends toward combustion, gas temperature rises sharply, and calorific value of producer gas drops (excess N₂ dilution).
- **Typical range for air gasification: ER = 0.20 – 0.40**, with industrial downdraft systems commonly operating at **ER ≈ 0.25 – 0.30**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **A biomass gasifier processes 500 kg/h of dry rice husk with the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): C = 38 %, H = 5 %, O = 36 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 20 %. The higher heating value (HHV) of the biomass is 14.8 MJ/kg. The producer gas composition (volume %, dry basis) is: CO = 18 %, H₂ = 15 %, CH₄ = 2 %, CO₂ = 12 %, N₂ = 53 %.**

> **(a) Calculate the stoichiometric air requirement and the actual air supplied at an Equivalence Ratio of 0.30. (7 marks)**
> 
> **(b) Determine the calorific value of the producer gas, the gas yield, and the cold gas efficiency. Take CV of CO = 12.64 MJ/Nm³, H₂ = 10.79 MJ/Nm³, CH₄ = 35.88 MJ/Nm³. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Stoichiometric and Actual Air Supply (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert mass % to decimals:** C = 0.38, H = 0.05, O = 0.36, S = 0.005 (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute O₂ requirement per kg dry biomass (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2^{\text{stoich}} &= 2.667 \, m_C + 8.000 \, m_H + 1.000 \, m_S - 1.000 \, m_O \\
&= 2.667 \cdot 0.38 + 8.000 \cdot 0.05 + 1.000 \cdot 0.005 - 1.000 \cdot 0.36 \\
&= 1.0135 + 0.4000 + 0.0050 - 0.3600 \\
&= 1.0585 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ to stoichiometric air (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{\text{O}_2^{\text{stoich}}}{0.232} = \frac{1.0585}{0.232} = 4.562 \text{ kg air / kg biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual air at ER = 0.30 (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{actual}} &= ER \times A_{\text{stoich}} = 0.30 \times 4.562 = 1.369 \text{ kg air / kg biomass} \\
\text{Total air (500 kg/h basis)} &= 1.369 \times 500 = 684.5 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Calorific Value, Gas Yield, CGE (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calorific value of producer gas (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_g &= \sum y_i \cdot CV_i \\
&= 0.18 \cdot 12.64 + 0.15 \cdot 10.79 + 0.02 \cdot 35.88 + 0.12 \cdot 0 + 0.53 \cdot 0 \\
&= 2.2752 + 1.6185 + 0.7176 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 4.611 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Gas yield via N₂ mass balance (3 marks):**

- Mass of N₂ in actual air = $0.768 \times 1.369 = 1.0514$ kg air-N₂ per kg biomass
- Moles of N₂ in gas = $\frac{1.0514}{28} = 0.03755$ kmol per kg biomass
- N₂ is 53 % of dry gas volume → total dry gas moles per kg biomass:
$$ n_{\text{gas}} = \frac{0.03755}{0.53} = 0.07085 \text{ kmol/kg biomass} $$
- Volume at NTP (22.414 Nm³/kmol):
$$ GY = 0.07085 \times 22.414 = 1.588 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per kg biomass} $$

**Step 3 — Cold Gas Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{CGE} &= \frac{GY \cdot CV_g}{CV_{\text{biomass}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.588 \times 4.611}{14.8} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.323}{14.80} \times 100 = 49.5 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on Question A:**
> 1. Students frequently forget the *sign convention* in the O₂ formula and write $\text{O}_2 = 2.667 \, m_C + 8 m_H + m_S + m_O$ (instead of *minus* $m_O$). This inflates O₂ demand by ~30 % and cascades into wrong ER. **Deduct 1 mark.**
> 2. Using **air density 1.29 kg/m³** instead of NTP **1.207 kg/Nm³** in intermediate conversions. **Deduct 0.5 mark.**
> 3. Forgetting to convert CO₂ (12 %) and N₂ (53 %) which have *zero calorific value*; some students erroneously assign CV to N₂. **Deduct 0.5 mark.**
> 4. Reporting Gas Yield in m³ instead of Nm³. **Deduct 0.5 mark.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

> **(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a downdraft biomass gasifier. Label the various zones and state the operating temperature range of each. (7 marks)**
> 
> **(b) Compare updraft and downdraft gasifiers on the basis of (i) gas quality / tar content, (ii) calorific value of producer gas, (iii) suitability for IC engines, (iv) moisture tolerance of feed, and (v) cold gas efficiency. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Downdraft Gasifier Description (7 marks)

**Sketch (3 marks):** Draw a vertical cylindrical reactor with:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Hopper Top] --> B[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 600 C]
    C --> D[Combustion Zone 800 to 1200 C Air Inlet Below]
    D --> E[Reduction Zone 600 to 1000 C]
    E --> F[Producer Gas Outlet Side Near Bottom]
    E --> G[Grate Ash Discharge Bottom]
    H[Air Blower] --> I[Air Nozzles Below Combustion Zone]
    I --> D
```

**Working (3 marks):**

| Zone | Temp (°C) | Function | Key Reactions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drying | 100 – 200 | Evaporates moisture | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{liq}} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{vap}}$ |
| Pyrolysis | 200 – 600 | Thermal decomposition | Biomass $\rightarrow$ Char + Volatiles |
| Combustion | 800 – 1200 | Heat generation | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ |
| Reduction | 600 – 1000 | Producer gas formation | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ |

**Operating features (1 mark):** Air enters *below* the combustion zone through tuyeres; producer gas exits *near the bottom* — co-current flow passes hot gas through char bed, which *cracks tars* and gives a *clean* low-tar gas ideal for IC engines.

---

#### Part (b) — Updraft vs Downdraft Comparison (7 marks)

| # | Parameter | Updraft Gasifier | Downdraft Gasifier |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Tar content** | Very high (30 – 150 g/Nm³) — tar-laden volatiles escape upward through cool biomass | **Very low (< 1 g/Nm³)** — volatiles pass through hot reduction zone and crack thermally |
| (ii) | **CV of producer gas** | **Higher** (5.0 – 6.0 MJ/Nm³) — methane and tars increase CV | **Lower** (4.5 – 5.5 MJ/Nm³) — tars cracked; gas diluted by N₂ |
| (iii) | **IC engine suitability** | **Poor** — extensive gas cleaning required | **Excellent** — clean gas, direct engine use |
| (iv) | **Feed moisture tolerance** | **High** — can accept biomass up to ~50 – 60 % moisture | **Low** — biomass must be < 15 – 20 % moisture for stable operation |
| (v) | **Cold gas efficiency** | 50 – 65 % | **65 – 80 %** (superior due to tar cracking) |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on Question B:**
> 1. Students often draw an *updraft* gasifier but *label* it as downdraft (or vice versa) — losing all 3 sketch marks.
> 2. Failing to mention *why* downdraft produces low tar (the hot reduction zone acting as a *thermal tar cracker* is the key).
> 3. Reversing the air-flow direction in the sketch.
> 4. Missing one or more of the five comparison points — each row carries ~1.4 marks; missing *feed moisture tolerance* is a frequent omission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision — Print and Pin to Your Wall**

- ✅ Gasification = *partial oxidation* at ER = 0.20 – 0.40 → producer gas.
- ✅ Four zones: **Drying (100–200 °C) → Pyrolysis (200–600 °C) → Combustion (800–1200 °C) → Reduction (600–1000 °C)**.
- ✅ Three reduction-zone reactions to *memorise*:
   - $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ (Boudouard, +172.5 kJ/mol)
   - $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ (Water-gas, +131.3 kJ/mol)
   - $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$ (Methanation, −74.8 kJ/mol)
- ✅ **Downdraft = engines** (low tar); **Updraft = drying/boilers** (high tar).
- ✅ Air gasification CV ≈ 4.5 – 6.0 MJ/Nm³; oxygen gasification CV ≈ 10 – 18 MJ/Nm³.
- ✅ Stoichiometric O₂ for C: $32/12 = 2.667$ kg/kg; Air: $11.5$ kg/kg C.
- ✅ Cold Gas Efficiency: $\eta_{CGE} = (V_g \cdot CV_g)/(m_b \cdot CV_b) \times 100$.
- ✅ Gas Yield: $GY = V_g/m_b$ in Nm³/kg (typical 2.0 – 2.5 for downdraft).
- ✅ Stoichiometric air formula: $A_{\text{stoich}} = 11.5\text{C} + 34.5(\text{H} - \text{O}/8) + 4.3\text{S}$ kg/kg.
- ✅ Carbon Conversion Efficiency $\geq$ 95 % in well-designed gasifiers.
- ✅ **Always quote producer gas calorific value with units MJ/Nm³**, not MJ/m³.
- ✅ Typical KTU numerical "traps": wrong sign for O, wrong air composition (use 23.2 % O₂ by mass, 21 % by volume), forgetting N₂ mass-balance for gas yield.
- ✅ Industrial memory aid: *"Boudouard-Burning" → produces CO (combustible); "Water-gas-Winning" → produces CO + H₂ (combustible).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bioethanol** is a **biofuel** defined as **ethyl alcohol (ethanol, chemical formula C₂H₅OH)** produced through the **fermentation of sugars, starches, or lignocellulosic biomass** derived from renewable biological feedstocks. It is chemically identical to petroleum-derived ethanol but is distinguished by its **renewable origin** and **net carbon-neutral lifecycle** when sourced sustainably. Bioethanol is primarily used as a **transportation fuel** either in its pure form (**E100**) or as a **blending component with gasoline** (e.g., **E10, E20, E85**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Bioethanol is classified as a **first-generation biofuel** when produced from food crops (sugarcane, corn) and a **second-generation biofuel** when produced from lignocellulosic biomass (agricultural residues, wood waste, grasses).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "Brewing Beer, but for Engines"**

Imagine the process of making an alcoholic beverage — yeast consumes sugar from fruits/grains and produces alcohol. Bioethanol production is essentially the **industrial-scale, highly purified version of this biochemical reaction**. The key differences are:

1. **Feedstock** → From dedicated energy crops (sugarcane, corn, switchgrass) rather than beverage grains.
2. **Purity** → Distilled and dehydrated to fuel-grade standards (**99.5%+ purity**).
3. **Purpose** → Combusted in internal combustion engines instead of being consumed.

**The "Oxygenated Fuel" Intuition:**
Gasoline is essentially a hydrocarbon soup (C and H only). Ethanol carries **oxygen atoms inside its molecular structure** (C₂H₅-OH). When burned, this built-in oxygen helps fuel burn **more completely**, reducing harmful carbon monoxide (CO) emissions — much like how adding a little water to a campfire reduces smoke.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these values — they appear in 70% of numerical problems:**

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical Formula** | C₂H₅OH | Differentiates from methanol (CH₃OH) |
| **Molecular Weight** | **46.07 g/mol** | Used in stoichiometry |
| **Density** | **789 kg/m³** | Lower than gasoline (≈ 750 kg/m³) |
| **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** | **26.9 MJ/kg** | ~33% lower than gasoline |
| **Octane Number (RON)** | **108–110** | Higher than gasoline (90–95) |
| **Heat of Vaporization** | **842 kJ/kg** | 2.5× gasoline — causes cold-start issues |
| **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio** | **9.0 : 1** | Lower than gasoline (14.6:1) |
| **Flash Point** | **12.8 °C** | Highly flammable — safety concern |
| **Auto-ignition Temperature** | **363 °C** | Higher than gasoline (246 °C) |

---

### 1.4 Ethanol vs. Gasoline — Quick Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why blend ethanol with gasoline at all?**
> 1. **Octane booster** — replaces toxic tetraethyl lead
> 2. **Oxygenate** — replaces MTBE (a groundwater pollutant)
> 3. **Renewable** — reduces dependence on crude oil
> 4. **Cleaner combustion** — reduces CO and unburned hydrocarbons

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bioethanol Production Pathways (Three Generational Routes)

Bioethanol can be produced via **three distinct feedstock-dependent routes**, each with increasing complexity and decreasing competition with food supply.

#### **Route 1: Sugar Route (Simplest)**
- **Feedstocks:** Sugarcane juice, sugar beet, molasses, sweet sorghum
- **Process:** Direct fermentation (sugar is already in fermentable form)
- **Reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂
- **Example Country:** **Brazil** (world's largest producer, uses sugarcane)

#### **Route 2: Starch Route**
- **Feedstocks:** Corn, wheat, cassava, potatoes
- **Process:** Requires **enzymatic hydrolysis (liquefaction + saccharification)** using α-amylase and glucoamylase to break starch (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ into glucose monomers
- **Reaction:** (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ + n H₂O → n C₆H₁₂O₆
- **Example Country:** **USA** (uses corn as primary feedstock)

#### **Route 3: Lignocellulosic Route (Second Generation)**
- **Feedstocks:** Agricultural residues (rice straw, wheat straw, bagasse), wood waste, energy crops (switchgrass, miscanthus)
- **Process:** Most complex — requires **pretreatment** (acid/steam explosion) to break down **lignin**, then **enzymatic hydrolysis** of cellulose and hemicellulose
- **Challenge:** Lignin removal and pentose (C5) sugar fermentation by genetically modified yeast

---

### 2.2 The Five-Stage Bioethanol Production Process

| Stage | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Feedstock Preparation** | Milling, grinding, washing | Increase surface area for reaction |
| **2. Hydrolysis (Saccharification)** | Acid or enzymatic treatment | Convert starch/cellulose to fermentable sugars |
| **3. Fermentation** | Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) at 25–35 °C | Convert sugars to ethanol + CO₂ (takes 48–72 hours) |
| **4. Distillation** | Multi-column distillation at ~78.4 °C | Separate ethanol from water (produces ~95% azeotrope) |
| **5. Dehydration** | Molecular sieves (zeolite 3Å) or azeotropic distillation | Remove residual water to reach fuel-grade ≥99.5% purity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Azeotrope Problem:** Ethanol and water form a constant-boiling mixture at **95.6% ethanol + 4.4% water (by mass)** at 78.2 °C. Simple distillation cannot exceed this concentration. **Molecular sieves (zeolites with 3 Å pores)** are used to selectively adsorb water molecules, breaking the azeotrope.

---

### 2.3 Combustion Chemistry of Bioethanol

**Stoichiometric Combustion Reaction:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**With Air (accounting for 79% N₂):**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 11.28\,\text{N}_2$$

**Molecular Oxygen Required (theoretical):**
- 1 mole ethanol requires 3 moles O₂
- Mass basis: 46 g ethanol requires 96 g O₂
- **Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass):** 96/46 = **9.0 : 1**

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they cover ~80% of numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m_{ethanol} \times LHV_{ethanol}}{m_{sugar} \times LHV_{sugar}}$ | Energy conversion efficiency from feedstock | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Y_{mass} = \dfrac{2 \times M_{ethanol}}{M_{glucose}} = \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.5111$ | Theoretical mass yield of ethanol from glucose | kg ethanol / kg glucose |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{n_{O_2} \times M_{O_2} \times (1 + 3.76 \times M_{N_2}/M_{O_2})}{M_{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{st}}$ | Equivalence ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_{released} = m_{fuel} \times LHV$ | Heat released during combustion | kJ or MJ |
| 6 | $CO_2\text{ reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_E \times LHV_E}{\rho_G \times LHV_G}\right) \times 100$ | CO₂ reduction per km vs. gasoline | % |
| 7 | $E_{blend} = x \cdot E_{eth} + (1-x) \cdot E_{gas}$ | Energy content of E-x blend (linear mixing) | MJ/L |
| 8 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{(A/F)_{st}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | Combustion efficiency (lean burn) | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical: When writing tables in markdown, never use `\|` for absolute value notation.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ as `$\vert x \vert$`.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Transportation Sector** — Brazil's **flex-fuel vehicles** run on any blend from E20 to E100; USA mandates **E10** nationwide.
2. **Octane Enhancement** — **10% ethanol addition raises gasoline RON by ~2.5–3 points** (replaces lead-based anti-knock additives).
3. **Rural Electrification** — Decentralized bioethanol plants enable local energy independence for agricultural economies.
4. **Oxygenated Fuel Additive** — Required in **reformulated gasoline (RFG)** under the US Clean Air Act to reduce urban CO and ozone.
5. **Cellulosic Ethanol (2G)** — Uses **agricultural waste**, solving the **food-vs-fuel debate** that plagues 1G ethanol.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Ethanol

**Given:** Combustion reaction: C₂H₅OH + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O
**Find:** Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio (mass basis)

**Step 1:** Write molecular weights
- M(C₂H₅OH) = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = **46 g/mol**
- M(O₂) = 2(16) = **32 g/mol**

**Step 2:** Calculate mass of O₂ required per mole of fuel

$$
m_{O_2} = 3 \text{ moles} \times 32 \text{ g/mol} = 96 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass of air (air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass)

$$
m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \dfrac{96}{0.232} = 413.79 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Air-Fuel ratio

$$
(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{413.79}{46} = 8.996 \approx 9.0
$$

**Result:** $(A/F)_{st} \approx 9.0$ — confirmed experimentally.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

**Given:** Fermentation reaction: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂

**Step 1:** Molecular weights
- M(C₆H₁₂O₆) = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = **180 g/mol**
- M(C₂H₅OH) = **46 g/mol**

**Step 2:** Mass of ethanol produced per mole of glucose

$$
m_{ethanol} = 2 \text{ moles} \times 46 \text{ g/mol} = 92 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3:** Mass yield (kg ethanol per kg glucose)

$$
Y_{mass} = \dfrac{92 \text{ g ethanol}}{180 \text{ g glucose}} = 0.5111 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Theoretical maximum = **51.11% by mass**

**Practical yield (industrial):** 90–95% of theoretical = **~46–48%** (due to yeast metabolism, side products like glycerol, and distillation losses).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Density of E10 Blend (Gasoline with 10% Ethanol)

**Given:** 
- LHV of pure gasoline = 44 MJ/kg
- LHV of pure ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg
- Density of gasoline = 750 kg/m³
- Density of ethanol = 789 kg/m³
- Blend: 10% ethanol by volume (E10)

**Step 1:** Mass of each component in 1 L of E10

$$
m_{eth} = 0.10 \times 1\text{ L} \times 789 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.0789 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{gas} = 0.90 \times 1\text{ L} \times 750 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.675 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Energy from each component

$$
E_{eth} = 0.0789 \times 26.9 = 2.122 \text{ MJ}
$$

$$
E_{gas} = 0.675 \times 44 = 29.7 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 3:** Total energy per liter of E10

$$
E_{E10} = 2.122 + 29.7 = 31.82 \text{ MJ/L}
$$

**Step 4:** Energy content reduction compared to pure gasoline (44 × 0.75 = 33 MJ/L)

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{33 - 31.82}{33} \times 100 = 3.58\%
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** E10 sacrifices only ~3.6% energy density but provides significant octane boost and emissions reduction — a worthwhile trade-off for most spark-ignition engines.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Fermentation Reaction Mass Balance (Numerical Example)

**Problem:** 1000 kg of glucose is fermented with a 90% conversion efficiency. How much ethanol and CO₂ are produced?

**Step 1:** Moles of glucose

$$
n_{glucose} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{1,000,000 \text{ g}}{180} = 5555.56 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Actual moles reacted (90% efficiency)

$$
n_{reacted} = 0.90 \times 5555.56 = 5000 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Moles of ethanol produced (1:2 stoichiometry)

$$
n_{ethanol} = 2 \times 5000 = 10,000 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass of ethanol

$$
m_{ethanol} = 10,000 \times 46 \text{ g/mol} = 460,000 \text{ g} = 460 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5:** Mass of CO₂ produced

$$
m_{CO_2} = 2 \times 5000 \times 44 = 440,000 \text{ g} = 440 \text{ kg}
$$

**Mass balance check:** 1000 kg glucose → 460 kg ethanol + 440 kg CO₂ + (100 kg unreacted glucose) = **1000 kg** ✓

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Ethanol Blending on Engine Performance

| Parameter | Pure Gasoline | E10 (10% Ethanol) | E85 (85% Ethanol) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Octane Number (RON) | 90–95 | 92–95 | 100–105 | Higher compression ratio possible |
| Energy Content (MJ/L) | ~33.0 | ~31.8 | ~25.6 | Higher fuel consumption |
| Cold Start (°C) | Good | Slightly affected | **Poor** (needs heated fuel rail) | Heater/secondary fuel system required |
| Material Compatibility | OK | OK (modern cars) | **Corrosive to older rubbers/plastics** | Stainless fuel lines, Viton seals |
| CO Emissions | Baseline | -20% | -30% | Cleaner urban air quality |
| NOx Emissions | Baseline | +5% | +10% | Trade-off — slight increase |
| Water Tolerance | Low | Low | **High** (phase separation risk) | Storage and handling care |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bioethanol Production Process Flow Diagram (Lignocellulosic Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock: Bagasse, Straw, Wood Chips] --> B[Step 1: Size Reduction and Milling]
    B --> C[Step 2: Pretreatment - Acid or Steam Explosion at 160 to 220 degrees Celsius]
    C --> D[Step 3: Enzymatic Hydrolysis - Cellulase Enzyme Action for 48 to 72 Hours]
    D --> E[Step 4: Fermentation - Saccharomyces cerevisiae Yeast at 30 degrees Celsius]
    E --> F[Step 5: Distillation - Multi-Column at 78.4 degrees Celsius producing 95 percent Ethanol]
    F --> G[Step 6: Dehydration - Molecular Sieve Zeolite 3A Pore Size]
    G --> H[Final Product: Anhydrous Bioethanol 99.5 percent Purity]
    E --> I[Byproduct: Carbon Dioxide CO2]
    D --> J[Byproduct: Lignin Residue - Used as Boiler Fuel]
    H --> K[Application: E10 Gasoline Blend for Transportation]
    H --> L[Application: E85 Flex Fuel Vehicles]
    H --> M[Application: Pure E100 for Modified Engines]
```

**Diagram Interpretation Notes:**

- **Milling** increases biomass surface area for efficient chemical attack
- **Pretreatment** breaks the lignin seal that protects cellulose — without this step, enzyme access is blocked
- **Hydrolysis** converts cellulose to glucose (C6 sugars) and hemicellulose to xylose (C5 sugars)
- **Fermentation** is anaerobic — yeast converts sugars to ethanol + CO₂
- **Distillation** exploits the 78.4 °C boiling point of ethanol (vs. 100 °C for water)
- **Molecular sieves** adsorb water selectively (3 Å pores exclude larger ethanol molecules)
- **Lignin residue** is combusted in a boiler to provide process heat — improving the **net energy balance** of 2G ethanol

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Ethanol-Blended Fuel Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Petroleum Crude Oil] --> B[Refining Process]
    B --> C[Gasoline: Pure Hydrocarbon Fuel]
    D[Sugarcane or Corn or Lignocellulose] --> E[Fermentation and Distillation]
    E --> F[Anhydrous Bioethanol: 99.5 percent C2H5OH]
    F --> G[E10 Blend: 10 percent Ethanol plus 90 percent Gasoline]
    F --> H[E20 Blend: 20 percent Ethanol plus 80 percent Gasoline]
    F --> I[E85 Blend: 85 percent Ethanol plus 15 percent Gasoline]
    F --> J[E100: Pure Bioethanol]
    G --> K[Standard Spark Ignition Engines]
    H --> L[Flexible Fuel Vehicles FFV]
    I --> L
    J --> M[Modified Engines - Brazil and Specialized Markets]
    K --> N[Emission Reduction: 20 percent less CO]
    L --> O[Octane Boost: RON above 100]
    M --> P[Renewable Transport Fuel]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The diagram distinguishes **blend ratios** (E10, E20, E85, E100) by ethanol content
- Each blend corresponds to a specific **engine compatibility class**
- E10 is mandated in many countries (USA, India, Brazil — partial) as the simplest entry point
- E85/E100 requires **flex-fuel vehicles (FFVs)** with modified fuel systems

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**
**Define bioethanol. List any four important properties that make it suitable for use as a transportation fuel.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Bioethanol is ethyl alcohol (C₂H₅OH) produced from the fermentation of biomass-derived sugars, starches, or lignocellulosic materials. It is chemically identical to synthetic ethanol but is distinguished by its renewable biological origin and lower net carbon footprint over its lifecycle.

**Four Important Properties:**

1. **High Octane Number (RON = 108–110):** Excellent anti-knock quality, allows higher compression ratios and replaces toxic lead additives.
2. **Oxygenated Molecule (~35% oxygen by mass):** Promotes complete combustion, reducing CO and unburned hydrocarbon emissions.
3. **Renewable and Biodegradable:** Produced from biomass, theoretically carbon-neutral since the CO₂ released during combustion was previously absorbed by the feedstock during growth.
4. **High Heat of Vaporization (842 kJ/kg):** Provides significant **volumetric efficiency** benefit in hot conditions, increasing air charge density and reducing knock tendency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the term "azeotrope" in the context of bioethanol distillation. How is the azeotrope broken to produce fuel-grade ethanol?**

**Model Answer:**

**Azeotrope Definition:** An azeotrope is a liquid mixture of two or more components that boils at a constant temperature and has a constant composition in both liquid and vapor phases. **Ethanol and water form a minimum-boiling azeotrope at 95.6% ethanol and 4.4% water (by mass), boiling at 78.2 °C** — which is *lower* than the boiling point of pure water (100 °C) and slightly lower than pure ethanol (78.4 °C).

**Why it's a problem:** Simple fractional distillation cannot produce ethanol purer than 95.6% because the vapor produced has the same composition as the liquid. This 95.6% purity is insufficient for fuel use because:
- Water causes **phase separation** when blended with gasoline
- Water causes **corrosion** in engine fuel systems

**How the Azeotrope is Broken (Dehydration Methods):**

1. **Molecular Sieve Adsorption (Most Common):** Zeolite 3Å beads selectively adsorb water molecules (small diameter ≈ 2.6 Å) while excluding larger ethanol molecules (≈ 4.4 Å). The water-loaded sieves are regenerated by applying vacuum and heat (pressure-swing adsorption).

2. **Azeotropic Distillation:** Adding a third component (e.g., benzene, cyclohexane, or diethyl ether) that forms a *new* ternary azeotrope with water, which can be easily separated, leaving anhydrous ethanol in the column.

3. **Pressure-Swing Distillation:** Operates two columns at different pressures to exploit the pressure sensitivity of the ethanol-water azeotrope composition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to state the **specific azeotrope composition (95.6% ethanol / 4.4% water)** and the **boiling point (78.2 °C)**. Vague answers like "water and ethanol don't separate easily" will lose 1 mark. Always mention the exact percentage and the molecular sieve pore size (3 Å) for full credit.

---

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **production of bioethanol from sugarcane** (sugar route). Include a neat process flow diagram, the chemistry of fermentation, and the energy balance considerations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **theoretical yield of bioethanol** from 5000 kg of glucose feedstock, given 92% fermentation efficiency and 95% distillation efficiency. Also compute the **CO₂ generated** and the **overall mass yield**.

---

**Model Solution to Part A(a):**

**Production of Bioethanol from Sugarcane (Sugar Route):**

Sugarcane is the simplest bioethanol feedstock because it contains **fermentable sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁)** in its juice. Brazil uses this route for ~60% of its bioethanol.

**Process Flow:**

1. **Sugarcane Harvesting & Washing:** Fresh cane is harvested and washed to remove soil and debris.

2. **Juice Extraction:** Cane is passed through roller mills, crushing it to extract the sugary juice (≈ 65% of cane mass). The remaining fibrous residue is called **bagasse**.

3. **Juice Purification:** Lime (CaO/Ca(OH)₂) is added to remove impurities, raise pH, and prevent bacterial contamination. The juice is heated to ~105 °C and clarified.

4. **Concentration (Optional):** Juice may be concentrated by evaporation to a syrup to increase sugar content.

5. **Fermentation:** The clarified juice (or diluted molasses) is cooled to 28–32 °C and inoculated with yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*). The biochemical reaction proceeds:

$$
\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6
$$

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

The reaction is **exothermic** (heat must be removed) and takes 6–12 hours. The fermented "wine" contains 6–10% ethanol.

6. **Distillation:** The wine is heated; ethanol vaporizes at 78.4 °C and is captured in distillation columns, producing **hydrated ethanol (~95.6% purity — the azeotrope)**.

7. **Dehydration:** Molecular sieves remove residual water → **anhydrous ethanol (≥99.5% purity)**.

8. **Bagasse Utilization:** The fibrous bagasse is combusted in boilers to provide process steam and electricity — a key factor in making Brazilian sugarcane ethanol **energy-positive** (output energy / input energy ≈ 8–10).

**Energy Balance Considerations:**
- **Energy Input:** Solar energy (photosynthesis), fertilizers, transportation, process heat.
- **Energy Output:** Ethanol LHV (26.9 MJ/kg) + electricity from bagasse.
- **Net Energy Ratio (NER):** For Brazilian sugarcane ethanol, NER ≈ **8–10** — meaning 8–10 units of energy are produced per unit of fossil energy consumed. This is significantly better than corn ethanol (NER ≈ 1.3–1.6).

---

**Model Solution to Part A(b):**

**Step 1: Theoretical mass yield of ethanol from glucose**

From stoichiometry: 180 kg glucose → 92 kg ethanol
For 5000 kg glucose (theoretical):
$$
m_{ethanol,\,theory} = 5000 \times \dfrac{92}{180} = 2555.56 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Apply fermentation efficiency (92%)**

$$
m_{ethanol,\,fermented} = 2555.56 \times 0.92 = 2351.11 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Apply distillation efficiency (95%)**

$$
m_{ethanol,\,final} = 2351.11 \times 0.95 = 2233.56 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate CO₂ generated (based on fermented glucose only)**

From stoichiometry: 180 kg glucose → 88 kg CO₂ (1:1 by moles, but 44/180 × 2 = 88/180)

Actually, more precisely: 1 mole glucose → 2 moles CO₂ = 88 g CO₂
For 5000 kg glucose (100% conversion): CO₂ = 5000 × 88/180 = **2444.44 kg**
With 92% conversion: CO₂ = 2444.44 × 0.92 = **2248.89 kg**

**Step 5: Overall mass yield**

$$
\text{Overall yield} = \dfrac{2233.56}{5000} \times 100 = 44.67\%
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Theoretical ethanol | 2555.56 kg |
| Ethanol after fermentation (92%) | 2351.11 kg |
| Final ethanol (after 95% distillation) | **2233.56 kg** |
| CO₂ generated | **2248.89 kg** |
| Overall mass yield | **44.67%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):** Many students make two common errors: (1) **forgetting to apply BOTH efficiencies sequentially** — fermentation and distillation losses are multiplicative, not additive. (2) **Confusing molar masses** — CO₂ has molar mass 44 g/mol, not 88. Remember, 2 moles of CO₂ are produced per mole of glucose, so the total CO₂ mass is (2 × 44)/180 = 88/180 of glucose mass. Get the sequence right to earn full 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **combustion characteristics of bioethanol in a spark-ignition (SI) engine**. Compare E10 and E85 with pure gasoline in terms of octane rating, energy content, and emissions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2.0 L displacement, 4-cylinder SI engine operates on **E20 fuel (20% ethanol + 80% gasoline by volume)**. The engine runs at 3000 RPM with a **volumetric efficiency of 85%** and a **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) of 320 g/kWh**. Calculate:
- (i) The air flow rate (kg/hr) entering the engine
- (ii) The fuel consumption rate (kg/hr)
- (iii) The brake power output (kW)

Take: 
- LHV of E20 blend = 32.5 MJ/kg
- Air density = 1.18 kg/m³
- Stoichiometric A/F ratio for E20 ≈ 11.0:1

---

**Model Solution to Part B(a):**

**Combustion Characteristics of Bioethanol in SI Engines:**

[Refer to SECTION 4, Block Diagram 4.2 for the visual representation]

**Key Combustion Behavior:**

1. **Flame Speed:** Ethanol has a **laminar flame speed of ~41 cm/s** (vs. ~37 cm/s for gasoline) → more rapid and complete combustion → higher thermal efficiency.

2. **Auto-ignition Temperature:** 363 °C (vs. 246 °C for gasoline) → higher resistance to auto-ignition → enables higher compression ratios (up to 14:1) without knock.

3. **Heat of Vaporization (HoV):** 842 kJ/kg (vs. 290 kJ/kg for gasoline) → cools the intake charge → increases air density → improves volumetric efficiency. Also causes **cold-start difficulty** in cold climates.

4. **Flame Luminosity:** Ethanol burns with a **near-invisible (blue) flame** due to absence of carbon particles, which is a **safety hazard** in fire scenarios.

**Comparison Table: Pure Gasoline vs. E10 vs. E85**

| Property | Pure Gasoline | E10 | E85 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Octane Number (RON) | 90–95 | 92–95 | 100–105 |
| Energy Content (MJ/L) | ~33.0 | ~31.8 | ~25.6 |
| Volumetric Fuel Economy | 1.0× (baseline) | ~0.97× | ~0.78× |
| CO Emissions (g/km) | Baseline | -20% | -30% |
| NOx Emissions (g/km) | Baseline | +5% | +10% |
| Cold Start Capability | Excellent | Good | **Requires assisted starting** |
| Material Compatibility | OK | OK | **Requires stainless/Viton components** |

---

**Model Solution to Part B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Displacement volume: V_d = 2.0 L = 0.002 m³
- Number of cylinders: 4
- Speed: N = 3000 RPM
- Volumetric efficiency: η_v = 0.85
- BSFC = 320 g/kWh = 0.32 kg/kWh
- LHV of E20 = 32.5 MJ/kg
- Air density: ρ_air = 1.18 kg/m³
- Stoichiometric A/F = 11.0

---

**(i) Air Flow Rate Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the **theoretical volume of air** per minute (4-stroke engine → 1 power stroke per 2 revolutions per cylinder)

$$
V_{theoretical} = \dfrac{V_d \times N}{2} = \dfrac{0.002 \times 3000}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m}^3\text{/min}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply volumetric efficiency

$$
V_{actual} = V_{theoretical} \times \eta_v = 3.0 \times 0.85 = 2.55 \text{ m}^3\text{/min}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = 2.55 \text{ m}^3\text{/min} \times 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 3.009 \text{ kg/min}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to kg/hr

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = 3.009 \times 60 = 180.54 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Result: Air flow rate ≈ 180.5 kg/hr**

---

**(ii) Fuel Consumption Rate:**

**Step 1:** Calculate stoichiometric fuel flow

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{st}} = \dfrac{180.54}{11.0} = 16.41 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Result: Fuel consumption rate ≈ 16.41 kg/hr**

*(Note: This is the theoretical stoichiometric rate. In practice, the actual fuel rate must be determined from the BSFC and brake power.)*

---

**(iii) Brake Power Output:**

**Step 1:** Using the BSFC relationship: BSFC = m_fuel / P_brake

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{BSFC} = \dfrac{16.41 \text{ kg/hr}}{0.32 \text{ kg/kWh}}
$$

$$
P_{brake} = 51.28 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Verification using energy balance

$$
P_{thermal} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times LHV = \dfrac{16.41}{3600} \times 32,500 = 148.13 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{brake} = \dfrac{51.28}{148.13} = 34.6\%
$$

This is realistic for a naturally aspirated SI engine (typical brake thermal efficiency: 25–35%).

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Air flow rate | **180.54 kg/hr** |
| Fuel consumption rate | **16.41 kg/hr** |
| Brake power output | **51.28 kW** |
| Brake thermal efficiency | 34.6% (verification) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):** (1) **Engine cycle factor:** For a 4-stroke engine, the number of power strokes per minute is N/2, not N. Many students forget this and double the air flow — losing 2 marks. (2) **BSFC units:** BSFC is given in **g/kWh** or **kg/kWh** — convert to consistent units. (3) **Stoichiometric A/F for blends:** E20 has an A/F of ~11.0, not 9.0 (pure ethanol) and not 14.6 (pure gasoline) — linear interpolation is acceptable.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Chemical formula:** C₂H₅OH; **Molecular weight:** 46 g/mol
- ✅ **LHV of ethanol:** 26.9 MJ/kg; **Density:** 789 kg/m³
- ✅ **Octane number (RON):** 108–110 (much higher than gasoline)
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F (mass):** 9.0 : 1 (lower than gasoline's 14.6:1)
- ✅ **Heat of vaporization:** 842 kJ/kg (causes cold-start problems)
- ✅ **Azeotrope composition:** 95.6% ethanol + 4.4% water at 78.2 °C
- ✅ **Azeotrope-breaking method:** Molecular sieves (zeolite 3Å)
- ✅ **Fermentation reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ (anaerobic, by yeast)
- ✅ **Theoretical mass yield from glucose:** 92/180 = **0.5111 or 51.11%**
- ✅ **Combustion reaction:** C₂H₅OH + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O
- ✅ **Brazil = sugarcane route; USA = corn route; future = lignocellulosic (2G)**
- ✅ **E10 = 10% ethanol; E85 = 85% ethanol; E100 = pure ethanol**
- ✅ **E85 requires flex-fuel vehicles (FFVs)** with modified fuel systems
- ✅ **Major advantage:** Octane booster + oxygenate (replaces lead & MTBE)
- ✅ **Major disadvantage:** Lower energy density → ~30% higher fuel consumption (E85)
- ✅ **Net Energy Ratio (NER):** Sugarcane ethanol ≈ 8–10; Corn ethanol ≈ 1.3–1.6
- ✅ **Key feedstocks:** 1G (sugarcane, corn), 2G (bagasse, straw, wood), 3G (algae — under research)
- ✅ **Process stages:** Milling → Hydrolysis → Fermentation → Distillation → Dehydration
- ✅ **Byproducts:** CO₂ (from fermentation), bagasse/lignin (combusted for process heat)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy Tip:** When asked to "explain bioethanol production," always structure your answer in the **5 sequential stages** (Section 2.2) and **mention the azeotrope problem explicitly** — these two elements appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Diesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bio Diesel ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biodiesel** is defined as a **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids** derived from a renewable lipid feedstock, such as vegetable oil, animal fat, or used cooking oil. It is produced via a chemical reaction called **transesterification**, in which the triglyceride molecules of the parent oil/fat react with a short-chain alcohol (typically **methanol** or **ethanol**) in the presence of a catalyst, yielding **fatty acid methyl esters (FAME)** — which constitute biodiesel — and **glycerol** as a by-product. According to the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM)**, biodiesel is officially designated as **B100** and is defined under the standard specification **ASTM D6751**. In Europe, the equivalent standard is **EN 14214**. The chemical shorthand for biodiesel is typically **FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters)** when methanol is used. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Restaurant Grease to Engine Fuel" Analogy:** > Imagine the oil leftover after frying samosas or pakoras at a restaurant. It is thick, sticky, and would completely clog a car's engine if poured directly into the fuel tank. Now, picture a chemical "translator" — a process called **transesterification** — that breaks down this thick oil into three thin, clean-burning molecules of **FAME** (biodiesel), releasing a sweet, viscous liquid called **glycerol** as a side product. > > The result? A fuel so clean that you can practically wash your hands with the exhaust emissions. Just like translating a thick book into three thin, easy-to-read comic strips, transesterification translates one heavy triglyceride molecule into three light, fuel-ready FAME molecules. --- ### Key Physical Metrics at a Glance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metrics for Biodiesel (B100):** > - **Density (at 15 °C):** $\rho \approx \mathbf{880 \ kg/m^3}$ > - **Kinematic Viscosity (at 40 °C):** $\nu \approx \mathbf{3.5 - 5.0 \ mm^2/s}$ (cSt) > - **Flash Point:** $\approx \mathbf{130 - 170 \ °C}$ > - **Cetane Number:** $\geq \mathbf{47}$ (ASTM minimum) > - **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** $\approx \mathbf{37 \ MJ/kg}$ > - **Cloud Point:** $\approx \mathbf{-3 \ to \ +12 \ °C}$ (feedstock dependent) These properties are central to KTU exam problems and must be memorized. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Chemistry of Biodiesel — Transesterification The parent feedstock (vegetable oil / animal fat) is composed primarily of **triglycerides**. Each triglyceride is an ester of **glycerol** with three long-chain **fatty acids**. Because of their large molecular size and partial unsaturation, triglycerides are too viscous for direct use in compression-ignition engines. To overcome this, **transesterification** is employed. The reaction proceeds as follows: **Step 1 — Conceptual Logic:** - The triglyceride is a bulky molecule that does not vaporize easily inside a diesel engine cylinder. - We need to "shatter" it into smaller ester molecules. - A short-chain alcohol (methanol: **CH₃OH**) is used as the "attacker." - A catalyst (typically **NaOH** or **KOH** for base catalysis) accelerates this bond-breaking. **Step 2 — Reaction Overview:** The triglyceride reacts with **three moles of alcohol** to produce **three moles of fatty acid alkyl ester (biodiesel)** and **one mole of glycerol**. **Step 3 — Why a Base Catalyst?** - Base catalysts (e.g., NaOH, KOH) are preferred because they operate at **moderate temperatures (50–65 °C)** and provide **fast reaction kinetics** (reaction completes in ~1 hour). - Acid catalysts (e.g., H₂SO₄) are slower but are used when the feedstock has **high free fatty acid (FFA)** content (>2%). - An **acid-catalyzed pre-treatment esterification** is often performed first to convert FFAs into esters, followed by base-catalyzed transesterification. **Step 4 — Separation & Purification:** - After the reaction, the mixture separates into two phases due to density difference: - **Top phase:** Biodiesel (FAME), density ≈ 880 kg/m³ - **Bottom phase:** Glycerol, density ≈ 1260 kg/m³ - Biodiesel is washed with water to remove residual catalyst and soap, then dried. --- ### 2.2 Feedstock Classification Biodiesel feedstocks are broadly classified into four generations: | Generation | Examples | FFA Content | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1st Gen** | Soybean oil, Rapeseed oil, Palm oil, Sunflower oil | Low | Edible; "food vs fuel" debate | | **2nd Gen** | Jatropha, Karanjia, Mahua, Used Cooking Oil (UCO) | Medium to High | Non-edible; preferred in India | | **3rd Gen** | Microalgae (Chlorella, Spirulina) | Variable | Highest oil yield per hectare | | **4th Gen** | Engineered algal / microbial oils | Variable | Research stage | > [!NOTE] > For Indian context, **Jatropha curcas** is the most relevant non-edible feedstock because it grows on marginal lands and has oil content of **30–40%** by weight of seed. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every critical formula required for solving KTU numerical problems on biodiesel. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value-like notation to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Transesterification Reaction** | $TG + 3 \cdot ROH \rightarrow 3 \cdot R'COOR + GL$ | TG = triglyceride, R'COOR = FAME, GL = glycerol | | 2 | **Stoichiometric Molar Ratio** | $n_{alcohol} : n_{oil} = 3 : 1$ | Practically, **6 : 1** is used to drive equilibrium forward | | 3 | **Biodiesel Mass Yield** | $\eta_{yield} = \dfrac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in percent (%) | | 4 | **Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta_{conv} = \dfrac{n_{FAME,produced}}{3 \cdot n_{TG,initial}} \times 100\%$ | Stoichiometric max = 3 mol FAME per mol TG | | 5 | **Molar Mass of Biodiesel (Average FAME)** | $M_{FAME} \approx \mathbf{292 \ g/mol}$ | Approx. for C16:0 methyl palmitate | | 6 | **Molar Mass of Glycerol** | $M_{GL} = \mathbf{92.09 \ g/mol}$ | By-product | | 7 | **Density-based Purity Check** | $\rho_{mixture} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{V_{total}}$ | Pure biodiesel $\rho \approx 880$ kg/m³ at 15 °C | | 8 | **Energy Density Ratio (vs Diesel)** | $R_{energy} = \dfrac{LHV_{biodiesel}}{LHV_{diesel}} \approx \dfrac{37}{43} \approx \mathbf{0.86}$ | Biodiesel has ~14% less energy per kg | | 9 | **Cetane Number (CN) Correlation** | $CN \uparrow \ as \ chain \ length \uparrow \ and \ unsaturation \downarrow$ | Saturated fats → higher CN | | 10 | **Acid Value (AV)** | $AV = \dfrac{V_{KOH} \cdot N_{KOH} \cdot 56.1}{m_{sample}}$ | Units: mg KOH/g; AV < 0.5 required by EN 14214 | | 11 | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N_{KOH} \cdot 56.1}{m_{sample}}$ | Indicates average molecular weight of oil | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transportation Sector:** Biodiesel blends such as **B5 (5% biodiesel + 95% diesel)** and **B20 (20% biodiesel)** are deployed in commercial fleets worldwide without engine modification. Pure biodiesel (B100) requires minor fuel-system upgrades for older vehicles. - **Power Generation:** Biodiesel can run diesel generators in **off-grid rural electrification** schemes, particularly in regions with abundant Jatropha cultivation. - **Aviation & Marine:** Research-grade **HEFA (Hydroprocessed Esters and Fatty Acids)** — derived from the same fatty acids — is certified for aviation turbine fuel (ASTM D7566). - **Sustainability:** Biodiesel is **carbon-neutral** because the CO₂ released during combustion was originally absorbed by the plant during photosynthesis, closing the carbon cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU Module 4, the **transesterification reaction**, **feedstock classification**, **property comparison vs petroleum diesel**, and **standards (ASTM/EN)** are the most frequently tested topics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Biodiesel Yield from Transesterification **Problem Context (typical KTU-style numerical):** *1000 kg of Jatropha oil (triglyceride, average molar mass = 884 g/mol) is transesterified with methanol (molar mass = 32 g/mol) in a 6:1 molar ratio using NaOH catalyst. The conversion efficiency is 98%. Calculate: (i) the mass of methanol required, (ii) the theoretical mass of FAME produced, and (iii) the actual mass of FAME obtained.* --- **Given Data:** - Mass of oil: $m_{oil} = 1000$ kg - Molar mass of oil (triglyceride): $M_{TG} = 884$ g/mol - Molar mass of methanol: $M_{MeOH} = 32$ g/mol - Molar mass of FAME (average C16-C18 methyl ester): $M_{FAME} = 292$ g/mol - Molar mass of glycerol: $M_{GL} = 92$ g/mol - Alcohol : Oil molar ratio: $R = 6 : 1$ - Conversion efficiency: $\eta_{conv} = 98\% = 0.98$ --- **Step (i) — Mass of methanol required:** First, calculate the number of moles of triglyceride (oil): $$ n_{TG} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{M_{TG}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1000 \ g}{884 \ g/mol} = \dfrac{10^6}{884} \ mol $$ $$ n_{TG} = 1131.22 \ mol $$ Now apply the molar ratio of 6 : 1 (methanol : oil): $$ n_{MeOH} = R \cdot n_{TG} = 6 \times 1131.22 = 6787.33 \ mol $$ Convert moles of methanol to mass: $$ m_{MeOH} = n_{MeOH} \cdot M_{MeOH} = 6787.33 \times 32 \ g = 217194.57 \ g $$ $$ \boxed{m_{MeOH} \approx 217.2 \ kg} $$ > **Logic Note:** The extra methanol beyond the 3:1 stoichiometric ratio pushes the reversible reaction forward (Le Chatelier's Principle), improving FAME yield. --- **Step (ii) — Theoretical mass of FAME produced:** From the balanced reaction, **3 moles of FAME** are produced per mole of triglyceride. Hence: $$ n_{FAME, theoretical} = 3 \cdot n_{TG} = 3 \times 1131.22 = 3393.66 \ mol $$ Convert to mass: $$ m_{FAME, theoretical} = n_{FAME, theoretical} \cdot M_{FAME} = 3393.66 \times 292 \ g $$ $$ m_{FAME, theoretical} = 990948.42 \ g \approx 991.0 \ kg $$ $$ \boxed{m_{FAME, theoretical} \approx 991.0 \ kg} $$ > **Logic Note:** This corresponds to ~99% theoretical mass yield — very close to but never exceeding the input oil mass because three H atoms are lost to glycerol. --- **Step (iii) — Actual mass of FAME obtained:** Apply the conversion efficiency: $$ m_{FAME, actual} = \eta_{conv} \cdot m_{FAME, theoretical} = 0.98 \times 991.0 \ kg $$ $$ \boxed{m_{FAME, actual} \approx 971.2 \ kg} $$ --- **Step (iv) — Bonus: Mass of glycerol by-product (analytical completeness):** $$ n_{GL, theoretical} = 1 \cdot n_{TG} = 1131.22 \ mol $$ $$ m_{GL, theoretical} = 1131.22 \times 92.09 \ g = 104174.5 \ g \approx 104.2 \ kg $$ With 98% conversion: $$ m_{GL, actual} = 0.98 \times 104.2 = 102.1 \ kg $$ > **Mass Balance Check:** $m_{oil} + m_{MeOH} \approx m_{FAME} + m_{GL} + m_{loss}$ > $1000 + 217.2 \approx 971.2 + 102.1 + 143.9$ → Mass is conserved (within rounding). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity Adjustment Using Blending When biodiesel is blended with petroleum diesel, the resulting viscosity can be predicted using a **linear blending rule** (acceptable for first approximation): $$ \nu_{blend} = x_{BD} \cdot \nu_{BD} + x_{PD} \cdot \nu_{PD} $$ Where: - $x_{BD}, x_{PD}$ are the volume fractions of biodiesel and petroleum diesel. - $\nu_{BD}, \nu_{PD}$ are the kinematic viscosities in mm²/s. **Example:** Determine the viscosity of **B20 blend** (20% biodiesel, 80% diesel). Given: $\nu_{BD} = 4.5$ mm²/s, $\nu_{PD} = 2.8$ mm²/s. $$ \nu_{B20} = (0.20 \times 4.5) + (0.80 \times 2.8) = 0.90 + 2.24 = 3.14 \ mm^2/s $$ $$ \boxed{\nu_{B20} \approx 3.14 \ mm^2/s} $$ This value lies within the **ASTM D975 diesel fuel range** (1.9 – 4.1 mm²/s), confirming that B20 is a drop-in fuel. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Biodiesel vs Petroleum Diesel > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision block**.
