Memorize the directional trends (higher $\uparrow$ / lower $\downarrow$). | Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Petroleum Diesel | Direction | Engineering Implication | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Density (kg/m³) | ~880 | ~850 | Biodiesel $\uparrow$ | Slightly heavier fuel injection mass | | Viscosity (mm²/s @ 40 °C) | 3.5 – 5.0 | 2.0 – 3.0 | Biodiesel $\uparrow$ | Better lubrication of injectors | | Flash Point (°C) | 130 – 170 | 55 – 65 | Biodiesel $\uparrow\uparrow$ | Safer storage & transport | | Cetane Number | 47 – 65 | 40 – 55 | Biodiesel $\uparrow$ | Smoother & quieter combustion | | Energy Content (MJ/kg) | 37 – 40 | 42 – 45 | Biodiesel $\downarrow$ | ~10% lower mileage | | Cloud Point (°C) | -3 to +12 | -15 to -5 | Biodiesel $\uparrow$ | Cold-flow issues in winter | | Sulfur Content | $< 10$ ppm | $< 50$ ppm (ULSD) | Biodiesel $\downarrow\downarrow$ | Lower SOₓ emissions | | Oxygen Content | 10 – 12 % | 0 % | Biodiesel $\uparrow$ | More complete combustion | | Biodegradability | > 95% in 28 days | Slow | Biodiesel $\uparrow\uparrow$ | Environmentally benign spill profile | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Biodiesel Production via Transesterification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Oil
Vegetable Oil UCO Animal Fat] --> B[Pre-treatment
Degumming Dewaxing] B --> C{FFA Content Check} C -- FFA greater than 2 percent --> D[Acid Pre-esterification
H2SO4 Catalyst] C -- FFA less than 2 percent --> F[Base Transesterification
NaOH KOH 50 to 65 C] D --> F F --> G[Phase Separation
Decanter Settler] G --> H[Glycerol Layer
By product 92 g mol] G --> I[Crude Biodiesel Layer
FAME] I --> J[Water Washing
Remove Catalyst Soap] J --> K[Drying
Vacuum or Flash] K --> L[Quality Testing
ASTM D6751 EN 14214] L --> M[Pure Biodiesel B100
Storage Tank] style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b08800 style L fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style M fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Transesterification Reaction Mechanism (Simplified) ```mermaid flowchart LR TG[Triglyceride
R1 COO CH2
R2 COO CH
R3 COO CH2] --> RX[Transesterification
6 to 1 Methanol
NaOH 50 to 65 C] M[Methanol
CH3OH x6] --> RX CAT[Catalyst
NaOH or KOH] --> RX RX --> FAME1[FAME 1
R1 COO CH3] RX --> FAME2[FAME 2
R2 COO CH3] RX --> FAME3[FAME 3
R3 COO CH3] RX --> GL[Glycerol
C3H8O3] style RX fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style TG fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style GL fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style FAME1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style FAME2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style FAME3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Biodiesel in the Energy Ecosystem ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[Source Layer] SO1[Crop Cultivation
Jatropha Soybean Palm] SO2[Waste Collection
UCO Animal Tallow] SO3[Aquaculture
Microalgae Ponds] end subgraph S2[Conversion Layer] CV1[Oil Extraction
Mechanical or Solvent] CV2[Refining
Degumming Dewaxing] CV3[Transesterification
Reactor] CV4[Purification
Wash Dry Distill] end subgraph S3[Distribution Layer] DI1[Storage Tank Farm] DI2[Blending Station
B5 B20 B100] DI3[Transport Pipeline
or Tanker] end subgraph S4[End Use Layer] EU1[Automobile Engines
Diesel Cars Buses Trucks] EU2[Diesel Generators
Rural Off grid] EU3[Marine Engines
HEFA pathway] EU4[Aviation Turbines
Sustainable Aviation Fuel] end SO1 --> CV1 SO2 --> CV2 SO3 --> CV1 CV1 --> CV2 CV2 --> CV3 CV3 --> CV4 CV4 --> DI1 DI1 --> DI2 DI2 --> DI3 DI3 --> EU1 DI3 --> EU2 DI3 --> EU3 DI3 --> EU4 style CV3 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define biodiesel. Mention its chemical composition and any two advantages over petroleum diesel.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Biodiesel** is a renewable fuel defined as a **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids** derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil through a process called **transesterification**. When methanol is used as the alcohol, the product is specifically called **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)**, with the general formula **R–COO–CH₃**, where R is a long hydrocarbon chain (typically C15 – C21). > > **Two Advantages:** > 1. **Renewable & Biodegradable:** Derived from biomass; > 95% biodegradable in 28 days (vs. petroleum diesel which persists for decades). It is **carbon-neutral** as the CO₂ released is reabsorbed by next-generation crops. > 2. **Lower Emissions:** Substantially reduces **unburnt hydrocarbons (HC)**, **carbon monoxide (CO)**, and **particulate matter (PM)** in exhaust. Sulfur content is virtually zero, eliminating SOₓ emissions. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of catalyst in transesterification. Why is NaOH preferred over H₂SO₄ in most commercial processes?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > A catalyst in transesterification **accelerates the reaction rate** without being consumed, by lowering the activation energy barrier for the nucleophilic attack of the alkoxide ion on the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. > > **NaOH (Base Catalyst) is preferred over H₂SO₄ (Acid Catalyst) because:** > 1. **Faster kinetics:** Base catalysis completes in ~1 hour at 50–65 °C, whereas acid catalysis requires 3–4 hours even at elevated temperatures (~110 °C). > 2. **Mild operating conditions:** Lower temperature and pressure requirement → lower energy cost. > 3. **Higher conversion efficiency:** Under identical conditions, base-catalyzed reactions yield > 95% FAME, vs. ~80% for acid catalysis. > 4. **Caveat:** NaOH requires **FFA < 2%** in the feedstock. Otherwise, FFAs form **soap** (saponification), consuming the catalyst and lowering yield. Hence, **acid pre-treatment** is needed for low-grade feedstocks like UCO. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, explain the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production. Discuss the function of methanol and the catalyst. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A feedstock contains **750 kg of soybean oil** ($M_{TG} = 880$ g/mol). Transesterification is carried out with methanol ($M = 32$ g/mol) in a **6 : 1 molar ratio** using NaOH catalyst. Conversion efficiency is **96%**. The average molar mass of the FAME produced is **290 g/mol**. Calculate: (i) The mass of methanol required. (ii) The actual mass of FAME obtained. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution **(a) Transesterification Reaction and Role of Reactants:** The balanced chemical equation is: $$ \text{Triglyceride} \; + \; 3 \cdot \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{Base Catalyst}]{\text{50 - 65 }°\text{C}}\; 3 \cdot \text{R-COO-CH}_3 \; + \; \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 $$ Where the **triglyceride** is an ester of glycerol with three long-chain fatty acids, and **R-COO-CH₃** represents a **fatty acid methyl ester (FAME)**. - **Role of Methanol:** Acts as the **nucleophile**. Its hydroxyl oxygen attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride, displacing the glycerol backbone. Three moles of methanol are required stoichiometrically, but in practice **6 moles** are used to drive the equilibrium toward the product side (Le Chatelier's Principle). - **Role of Catalyst (NaOH):** Generates **sodium methoxide (CH₃O⁻Na⁺)** in situ, which is a much stronger nucleophile than methanol itself. The alkoxide ion aggressively attacks the carbonyl group, dramatically accelerating the reaction. Typical catalyst loading is **0.5–1.5% by weight of oil**. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $m_{oil} = 750$ kg = 750,000 g - $M_{TG} = 880$ g/mol - $M_{MeOH} = 32$ g/mol - $M_{FAME} = 290$ g/mol - Molar ratio $R = 6$ - $\eta_{conv} = 96\% = 0.96$ --- **(i) Mass of methanol required:** $$ n_{TG} = \dfrac{750000}{880} = 852.27 \ mol $$ $$ n_{MeOH} = 6 \times 852.27 = 5113.64 \ mol $$ $$ m_{MeOH} = 5113.64 \times 32 = 163636.4 \ g \approx 163.6 \ kg $$ $$ \boxed{m_{MeOH} \approx 163.6 \ kg} $$ --- **(ii) Actual mass of FAME obtained:** $$ n_{FAME, theoretical} = 3 \times 852.27 = 2556.82 \ mol $$ $$ m_{FAME, theoretical} = 2556.82 \times 290 = 741478 \ g \approx 741.5 \ kg $$ $$ m_{FAME, actual} = 0.96 \times 741.5 = 711.8 \ kg $$ $$ \boxed{m_{FAME, actual} \approx 711.8 \ kg} $$ --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Classify the various **feedstocks** used for biodiesel production with suitable examples. Explain why **Jatropha** is preferred in the Indian context. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **ASTM D6751** and **EN 14214** standards for biodiesel quality. List any **five key properties** tested under these standards with their acceptable limits. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution **(a) Feedstock Classification:** Biodiesel feedstocks are classified into four generations: 1. **First Generation — Edible Vegetable Oils:** Examples: Soybean oil (USA, Brazil), Rapeseed/Canola oil (Europe), Palm oil (Malaysia, Indonesia), Sunflower oil. - **Issue:** Raises the **"food vs. fuel"** debate and inflates edible oil prices. 2. **Second Generation — Non-edible Oils & Waste:** Examples: **Jatropha curcas**, Karanjia, Mahua, Pongamia, Used Cooking Oil (UCO), animal tallow, waste greases. - **Advantage:** Do not compete with food crops; can be grown on **marginal/wasteland**. 3. **Third Generation — Microalgae:** Examples: *Chlorella vulgaris*, *Spirulina*, *Nannochloropsis*. - **Advantage:** Highest oil productivity per hectare (~ 10,000 L/ha/yr vs. ~ 1200 L/ha/yr for Jatropha). 4. **Fourth Generation — Genetically Engineered Biofuels:** Examples: Engineered cyanobacteria, synthetic microbial oils. **Why Jatropha is preferred in India:** - Grows well in **arid and semi-arid regions**, including Rajasthan, Gujarat, and parts of Tamil Nadu. - Can be cultivated on **wasteland** without displacing food crops. - High **seed oil content (30–40%)** and non-edible (contains toxic phorbol esters). - Tree has a **lifespan of ~50 years** with productive yield starting from year 3. - Aligns with India's **National Biodiesel Mission** launched in 2003, targeting Jatropha cultivation on wastelands. - FFA content of fresh Jatropha oil is typically **2–6%** — manageable with mild pre-treatment. **(b) ASTM D6751 and EN 14214 Standards:** These are the two principal international standards governing biodiesel quality. - **ASTM D6751 (USA):** Specifies requirements for **B100** (pure biodiesel) before blending with petroleum diesel. Defines three grades based on sulfur content. - **EN 14214 (European Union):** More stringent; applies to **FAME** used as a blending component or as automotive diesel fuel. **Five Key Properties and Limits:** | # | Property | Test Method | ASTM D6751 Limit | EN 14214 Limit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Cetane Number** | D613 | $\geq 47$ | $\geq 51$ | | 2 | **Kinematic Viscosity @ 40 °C (mm²/s)** | D445 | $1.9 - 6.0$ | $3.50 - 5.00$ | | 3 | **Flash Point (°C, closed cup)** | D93 | $\geq 93$ | $\geq 101$ | | 4 | **Water & Sediment (vol %)** | D2709 | $\leq 0.05$ | $\leq 0.05$ | | 5 | **Acid Value (mg KOH/g)** | D664 | $\leq 0.5$ | $\leq 0.5$ | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "yield" with "conversion efficiency":** Yield = (mass of FAME / mass of oil) × 100%, whereas Conversion = (moles of FAME produced / stoichiometric maximum moles) × 100%. These are **NOT** the same quantity. > 2. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio:** Students often use **3 : 1** instead of the practical **6 : 1** ratio for methanol. Examiners explicitly penalize this — use 6 : 1 unless the problem states "stoichiometric." > 3. **Forgetting to multiply by 3:** The reaction produces **three moles of FAME per mole of triglyceride**. Skipping the factor of 3 leads to one-third of the correct answer. > 4. **Mixing up EN 14214 vs ASTM D6751 limits:** Cetane Number $\geq 51$ for EN and $\geq 47$ for ASTM. Don't interchange them. > 5. **Ignoring units in final answer:** Always state mass in kg (or grams consistently) and viscosity in mm²/s — partial credit is lost otherwise. --- ### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Things to Remember — Biodiesel (Module 4)** - ✅ **Definition:** Mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids (FAME when methanol is used). - ✅ **Reaction:** Triglyceride + 3 ROH → 3 R'COOR + Glycerol (transesterification). - ✅ **Practical Alcohol : Oil Ratio = 6 : 1** (not stoichiometric 3 : 1). - ✅ **Catalyst preference:** NaOH / KOH (base) for FFA < 2%; H₂SO₄ pre-treatment otherwise. - ✅ **Reaction Conditions:** 50–65 °C, atmospheric pressure, ~1 hour, 0.5–1.5% catalyst by wt. - ✅ **Separation:** Density-driven — FAME on top (~880 kg/m³), Glycerol below (~1260 kg/m³). - ✅ **Three moles of FAME produced per mole of triglyceride** — never forget the factor of 3. - ✅ **Jatropha:** Indian context — non-edible, 30–40% oil, grows on marginal land. - ✅ **Standards:** ASTM D6751 (USA) — CN ≥ 47; EN 14214 (EU) — CN ≥ 51. - ✅ **Biodiesel Advantages:** Higher cetane, higher flash point, lower emissions, biodegradable, renewable. - ✅ **Biodiesel Disadvantages:** ~10% lower energy density than diesel, higher cloud point (cold flow issues), NOₓ emissions slightly higher, can degrade rubber hoses in old engines. - ✅ **B100 vs B20:** B100 = pure biodiesel; B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% petroleum diesel (most common blend). - ✅ **By-product Glycerol:** ~10% by mass — used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and explosives (nitroglycerin). - ✅ **Numerical Conversion:** 1 mole TG (≈ 884 g) → 3 moles FAME (≈ 876 g) + 1 mole glycerol (92 g) — mass is conserved.

topics.title: Biogas Production from Waste Biomass - Factors Affecting Biogas Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biogas Production from Waste Biomass - Factors Affecting Biogas Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biogas** is a renewable gaseous biofuel produced by the **anaerobic digestion (AD)** of organic waste biomass in the **absence of oxygen**, through the coordinated metabolic activity of a consortia of microorganisms. It is primarily a mixture of **Methane (CH₄: 50–70 %)**, **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂: 30–40 %)**, and trace amounts of Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S), Water Vapor (H₂O), Ammonia (NH₃), and Nitrogen (N₂).

The general biochemical pathway can be summarized as:

$$
\text{Organic Waste (Biomass)} \xrightarrow{\text{Anaerobic Bacteria}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a biogas digester as a **"stomach without air"**:

- When a cow chews grass inside its rumen, microbes break it down without oxygen — producing methane that the cow belches out.
- A biogas plant replicates this natural process inside an airtight tank (called a *digester*).
- Just as a human stomach needs warmth, balanced acid levels, and the right food mix to function properly, the digester needs **controlled temperature, pH, and feedstock composition** to keep its microbial "workers" happy and productive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Calorific Value of Biogas (60 % CH₄):** Approximately **20 MJ/m³** (≈ 5.5 kWh/m³), compared to **35 MJ/m³** for pure methane and **38 MJ/m³** for LPG.

### 1.3 Composition of Biogas

| Component | Chemical Formula | Typical Range (% by vol.) |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | 50 – 70 |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | 30 – 40 |
| Hydrogen Sulfide | H₂S | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| Water Vapor | H₂O | 0 – 5 |
| Nitrogen | N₂ | 0 – 1 |
| Ammonia | NH₃ | < 0.05 |

### 1.4 Sources of Waste Biomass for Biogas

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises **waste biomass** as the feedstock (not energy crops), aligning with circular-economy and sustainability principles under NEP 2020.

- **Agricultural residues:** Rice straw, wheat straw, sugarcane bagasse, corn stover, cotton stalks.
- **Animal dung:** Cattle dung, buffalo dung, pig manure, poultry litter, goat droppings.
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):** Kitchen waste, garden waste, food processing waste.
- **Industrial organic waste:** Distillery effluent (spent wash), dairy whey, paper-mill sludge, tannery waste.
- **Aquatic biomass:** Water hyacinth, *Hydrilla*, algal biomass.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

Anaerobic digestion is **not a single reaction** — it is a tightly coupled four-stage biochemical cascade mediated by four distinct microbial guilds. Understanding each stage is *critical* for KTU exam answers.

#### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis
- **What:** Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are cleaved into simple monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids + glycerol).
- **Who:** Hydrolytic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*).
- **Reaction type:** Extra-cellular enzymatic hydrolysis.
- **Why slow:** Cellulose and lignin are recalcitrant; this is the **rate-limiting step** for lignocellulosic wastes.

$$
\text{(C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5\text{)}_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Cellulase}} n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6
$$

#### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)
- **What:** Monomers are fermented into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, CO₂, H₂, and NH₃.
- **Who:** Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*).
- **Issue:** VFAs accumulate and lower pH, threatening methanogens → process imbalance.

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Propionic acid)}
$$

#### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis
- **What:** VFAs and alcohols are oxidised into acetate, H₂, and CO₂ — the direct precursors of methane.
- **Who:** Acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas*).
- **Constraint:** Reaction is **thermodynamically unfavourable** unless H₂ partial pressure is kept low by methanogens (syntrophic relationship).

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COO}^- + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{HCO}_3^- + 3\,\text{H}_2
$$

#### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis
- **What:** Acetate and H₂/CO₂ are converted into CH₄.
- **Who:** Methanogenic archaea (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanosarcina*).
- **Two pathways:**
  - **Acetoclastic (≈ 70 %):** CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂
  - **Hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30 %):** CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COOH \longrightarrow CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2
$$
$$
\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations** — they appear almost every year in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buswell Equation:** $C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4$ | Theoretical CH₄ yield from elemental composition | mol CH₄ / mol substrate |
| 2 | **Methane fraction in biogas:** $x_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n/2 + a/8 - b/4}{n}$ | Mole fraction of CH₄ | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Theoretical CH₄ yield:** $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{22.4 \times \left(n/2 + a/8 - b/4\right)}{12n + a + 16b}$ | Volume of CH₄ at STP per gram of substrate | L CH₄ / g VS |
| 4 | **C/N Ratio:** $\text{C/N} = \dfrac{\text{Total Carbon (mass)}}{\text{Total Nitrogen (mass)}}$ | Critical for balanced microbial nutrition | dimensionless (ideal = 20–30) |
| 5 | **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR):** $VLR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{VS}}{V_{digester}}$ | Substrate mass per digester volume per day | kg VS / m³·d |
| 6 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** $HRT = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{\dot{V}_{feed}}$ | Average time feedstock remains in digester | days |
| 7 | **Gas Yield:** $G_y = \dfrac{V_{biogas}}{m_{VS\,fed}}$ | Biogas produced per kg volatile solids | m³ / kg VS |
| 8 | **Energy Content:** $E_{biogas} = V_{biogas} \times x_{CH_4} \times 35.8$ | Energy delivered by CH₄ fraction | MJ |
| 9 | **Cattle Dung Theoretical Yield:** ≈ **0.20–0.30 m³ CH₄ / kg VS** | Empirical benchmark for design problems | m³ / kg VS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Molar Volume at STP** (NTP: 0 °C, 1 atm) = **22.414 L/mol** is the conversion factor used in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural electrification:** Off-grid village-scale biogas engines (e.g., 100 kW schemes under India's MNRE *New National Biogas Programme*).
- **Combined Heat & Power (CHP):** Biogas fuels dual-fuel engines and sterling generators, achieving ≈ 85 % total energy efficiency.
- **Upgraded Bio-CNG:** CO₂ and H₂S scrubbing enables direct use as automotive fuel.
- **Digestate as fertilizer:** Nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium-rich effluent for organic farming — closes the nutrient loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

### 3.1 Derivation of the Buswell Equation

The Buswell equation predicts the **theoretical methane yield** from any organic compound of empirical formula $C_nH_aO_b$ (neglecting N, S, P).

**Step 1 — Mass Balance Setup:**
Write an unbalanced oxidation–reduction assuming complete conversion to CH₄, CO₂, and H₂O:

$$
C_nH_aO_b + x\,H_2O \longrightarrow y\,CH_4 + z\,CO_2
$$

**Step 2 — Carbon Balance:**
Every carbon atom in the substrate appears either in CH₄ or CO₂:

$$
n = y + z
$$

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Balance:**
Hydrogen atoms on the left = 2x; on the right = 4y:

$$
2x + a = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{4y - a}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen Balance:**
Oxygen atoms on the left = b; on the right = 2z (water supplies x):

$$
b + x = 2z \quad \Rightarrow \quad b + \frac{4y - a}{2} = 2z
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the System:**
Substitute $z = n - y$ into the oxygen balance:

$$
b + \frac{4y - a}{2} = 2(n - y)
$$

Multiply through by 2:

$$
2b + 4y - a = 4n - 4y
$$

$$
8y = 4n + a - 2b \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}
$$

By carbon balance:

$$
z = n - y = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}
$$

And from Step 3:

$$
x = n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Buswell Equation:**

$$
\boxed{
C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4
}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical 1 — Glucose Anaerobic Digestion

**Problem:** Apply the Buswell equation to **glucose** ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) and calculate the (a) stoichiometric coefficients, (b) theoretical methane yield in L CH₄ / kg glucose at STP, and (c) methane mole fraction.

**Solution:**
For glucose: n = 6, a = 12, b = 6.

**(a) Coefficients:**

Methane coefficient:

$$
y = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

CO₂ coefficient:

$$
z = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$

Water coefficient:

$$
x = 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Glucose digests with **zero water requirement** because it already has the perfect H/O stoichiometry for full oxidation-reduction balance.

**(b) Molar mass of glucose:** 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = **180 g/mol**.

Methane yield:

$$
Y_{CH_4} = \frac{3\,\text{mol} \times 22.4\,\text{L/mol}}{180\,\text{g}} = \frac{67.2}{180} = 0.373\,\text{L/g} = 373\,\text{L/kg}
$$

**(c) Methane mole fraction:**

$$
x_{CH_4} = \frac{3}{3 + 3} = 0.50 \quad (\text{i.e., } 50\%)
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical 2 — Methane Yield of Cattle Dung

**Problem:** A continuous stirred-tank biogas digester of volume **10 m³** is fed daily with **500 kg of fresh cattle dung** containing 80 % moisture and 18 % volatile solids (on a dry basis, dry matter fraction). HRT = 20 days. Determine: (a) Total solids fed per day, (b) Volumetric loading rate, (c) Hydraulic retention time (verification), and (d) Daily biogas production assuming yield = 0.25 m³ CH₄/kg VS at 60 % methane.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Solids (TS) and Volatile Solids (VS) per day:**
Moisture = 80 % ⇒ Total Solids (TS) = 20 % of mass.

$$
\text{TS} = 0.20 \times 500 = 100\,\text{kg/day}
$$

Dry matter has 18 % VS (this usually means dry basis — given as VS fraction of dry matter):

$$
\text{VS} = 0.18 \times 500 = 90\,\text{kg/day}
$$

(If 18 % is of TS, then VS = 0.18 × 100 = **18 kg VS/day**.) *Adopt 18 kg VS/day for this problem to align with typical cattle dung composition.*

**(b) Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR):**

$$
VLR = \frac{18\,\text{kg VS/day}}{10\,\text{m}^3} = 1.8\,\text{kg VS/m}^3\cdot\text{day}
$$

**(c) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):**

$$
HRT = \frac{V_{digester}}{\dot{V}_{feed}} = \frac{10\,\text{m}^3}{0.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}} = 20\,\text{days} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(d) Daily Biogas Production:**

$$
\text{CH}_4 = 18 \times 0.25 = 4.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Total Biogas} = \frac{4.5}{0.60} = 7.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Biogas Generation (Quick-Reference)

| Factor | Optimal Range | Effect of Deviation | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Mesophilic 30–38 °C | Below 20 °C → slow digestion; above 55 °C → ammonia inhibition | Insulate digester; use solar pre-heaters |
| **pH** | 6.8 – 7.5 | < 6.2 → methanogens die; > 8.5 → ammonia toxicity | Recirculate digestate; staged digestion |
| **C/N Ratio** | 20:1 – 30:1 | < 15 → ammonia inhibition; > 35 → slow gas release | Co-digestion of nitrogen-rich & carbon-rich waste |
| **HRT** | 20 – 40 days (mesophilic) | Too short → washout of methanogens | Increase digester volume or reduce feed |
| **VLR** | 1 – 4 kg VS/m³·d | Overloading → VFA accumulation | Step-feed or multi-stage digesters |
| **Moisture Content** | 90 – 95 % (TS = 5–10 %) | Too dry → mass-transfer limit; too wet → scum formation | Mechanical mixing; solids pre-thickening |
| **Mixing** | Gentle, intermittent | No mixing → scum/crust; excessive → shear inhibition | Slow-speed impellers (60–80 rpm) |
| **Particle Size** | < 12 mm | Larger → slow hydrolysis | Maceration / grinding |
| **Toxicants** | Below thresholds: NH₃ < 1500 mg/L, H₂S < 200 mg/L, heavy metals | Inhibition of methanogens | Pre-treatment; bio-filter for H₂S |
| **Nutrients** | N, P, S, trace Fe, Ni, Co, Se, Mo | Deficiency → poor growth | Supplementation with micronutrients |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four-Stage Anaerobic Digestion Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 - Hydrolysis]
        A1["Polymers: Carbohydrates, Proteins, Lipids"] --> A2["Monomers: Sugars, Amino Acids, Fatty Acids"]
    end

    subgraph S2[Stage 2 - Acidogenesis]
        A2 --> B1["Volatile Fatty Acids VFAs, Alcohols, CO2, H2"]
    end

    subgraph S3[Stage 3 - Acetogenesis]
        B1 --> C1["Acetate CH3COOH, H2, CO2"]
    end

    subgraph S4[Stage 4 - Methanogenesis]
        C1 --> D1["Methane CH4 50-70 percent"]
        C1 --> D2["Carbon Dioxide CO2 30-40 percent"]
    end

    A1 -.Hydrolytic Bacteria.-> A2
    A2 -.Acidogenic Bacteria.-> B1
    B1 -.Acetogenic Bacteria.-> C1
    C1 -.Methanogenic Archaea.-> D1
    C1 -.Methanogenic Archaea.-> D2
```

### 4.2 Biogas Plant Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INP[Feedstock Handling]
        F1["Waste Biomass Collection"]
        F2["Size Reduction and Mixing Tank"]
    end

    subgraph DIG[Anaerobic Digester System]
        D1["Inlet Slurry Pump"]
        D2["Main Digestion Chamber Airtight"]
        D3["Heating Coil Mesophilic 35 C"]
        D4["Mechanical Agitator"]
    end

    subgraph GAS[Gas Collection Train]
        G1["Gas Holder Dome"]
        G2["H2S Scrubber Iron Oxide"]
        G3["Moisture Trap Silica Gel"]
        G4["Pressure Regulator"]
        G5["Biogas Engine or CHP"]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Streams]
        O1["Sludge Digestate Biofertilizer"]
        O2["Treated Effluent Liquid"]
    end

    F1 --> F2 --> D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D2 --> G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5
    D2 --> O1
    D2 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Effect of pH on the Anaerobic Process Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["pH below 6.2"] -->|Acid Inhibition| P2["Methanogens Die, VFA Accumulation, Process Failure"]
    P3["pH 6.8 to 7.5"] -->|Optimal| P4["Stable Methanogenesis, Maximum CH4 Yield"]
    P5["pH above 8.5"] -->|Alkaline Inhibition| P6["Free NH3 Toxicity, Reduced CH4"]

    P2 --> R1["Remedy: Stop Feed, Recirculate, Add Buffer"]
    P6 --> R2["Remedy: Reduce Feed Rate, Aerate Slurry"]
```

### 4.4 Temperature Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Psychrophilic 10 to 20 C"] -->|Slow| T2["Low Yield, Long HRT 60 to 90 days"]
    T3["Mesophilic 30 to 38 C"] -->|Optimal| T4["Stable, Balanced, HRT 20 to 40 days"]
    T5["Thermophilic 50 to 55 C"] -->|Fast| T6["High Yield, Short HRT 10 to 15 days, High Energy Input"]
    T7["Hyperthermophilic above 60 C"] -->|Extreme| T8["Inhibition, Ammonia Toxicity"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define biogas. List its main components with typical percentage composition.

**Model Answer:**
Biogas is a combustible gaseous mixture produced by the **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste biomass in the absence of oxygen, through the metabolic action of specialised microorganisms.

| Component | Formula | Range (% vol) |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | 50 – 70 |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | 30 – 40 |
| Hydrogen Sulfide | H₂S | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| Water Vapor | H₂O | 0 – 5 |

Calorific value ≈ **20 MJ/m³** (for 60 % CH₄ composition).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the four stages of anaerobic digestion and name the bacteria responsible for each stage.

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Function | Responsible Microbes |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrolysis | Converts polymers to monomers | Hydrolytic bacteria (*Clostridium*) |
| Acidogenesis | Ferments monomers to VFAs & alcohols | Acidogenic bacteria (*Bacteroides*) |
| Acetogenesis | Converts VFAs to acetate + H₂ | Acetogenic bacteria (*Syntrophomonas*) |
| Methanogenesis | Produces CH₄ from acetate / H₂ + CO₂ | Methanogenic archaea (*Methanobacterium*) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When listing "factors affecting biogas", students **only write temperature and pH** and skip the equally-mandated C/N ratio, HRT, VLR, moisture content, and toxicity. This loses up to **5 marks** immediately. **Cover all 8–10 factors** in your answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **factors affecting biogas generation** from waste biomass.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A community biogas plant has a digester volume of **20 m³** and is fed daily with **800 kg of cattle dung** (moisture 80 %, VS = 18 % of total mass). Using an empirical yield of **0.28 m³ CH₄/kg VS** and methane fraction of 60 %, calculate: (i) Volumetric Loading Rate (ii) Daily methane volume (iii) Daily total biogas volume (iv) Energy output per day.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Factors Affecting Biogas Generation (7 marks)

The factors are grouped into **physical, chemical, and biological** categories.

**1. Temperature (1 mark):**
Three regimes — psychrophilic (10–20 °C), mesophilic (30–38 °C), thermophilic (50–55 °C). Mesophilic is most common; thermophilic gives faster rates but needs more heat input.

**2. pH (1 mark):**
Optimal range 6.8–7.5. Below 6.2 → methanogens are inhibited; above 8.5 → free ammonia toxicity.

**3. C/N Ratio (1 mark):**
Ideal 20:1 to 30:1. High C/N → nitrogen limitation; low C/N → ammonia inhibition.

**4. Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) (1 mark):**
Must exceed the doubling time of methanogens; typically 20–40 days for mesophilic, 10–15 days for thermophilic.

**5. Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR) (1 mark):**
Excess loading → VFA accumulation and acidification.

**6. Moisture / Total Solids (TS) (0.5 mark):**
Wet fermentation (TS = 5–10 %) is preferred; dry AD needs different reactor designs.

**7. Mixing / Agitation (0.5 mark):**
Prevents scum formation, ensures uniform substrate-microbe contact.

**8. Particle Size (0.5 mark):**
Smaller size → larger surface area → faster hydrolysis.

**9. Inhibitors / Toxicants (0.5 mark):**
Ammonia, H₂S, heavy metals, antibiotics, pesticides inhibit methanogens.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_{digester} = 20\,\text{m}^3$, $\dot{m}_{feed} = 800\,\text{kg/day}$, Moisture = 80 %, VS fraction = 18 % (of total mass), $Y_{CH_4} = 0.28\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg VS}$, $x_{CH_4} = 0.60$.

**Step 1 — Volatile Solids fed per day (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{VS} = 0.18 \times 800 = 144\,\text{kg VS/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Loading Rate (1 mark):**

$$
VLR = \frac{144}{20} = 7.2\,\text{kg VS/m}^3\cdot\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Methane Volume (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{V}_{CH_4} = 144 \times 0.28 = 40.32\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily Total Biogas Volume (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{40.32}{0.60} = 67.2\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily Energy Output (2 marks):**
Calorific value of CH₄ = **35.8 MJ/m³**.

$$
\dot{E} = 40.32 \times 35.8 = 1443.5\,\text{MJ/day}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\dot{E} = \frac{1443.5}{3.6} \approx 400.97\,\text{kWh/day}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete process of biogas production** from waste biomass and the four biochemical stages of anaerobic digestion.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Apply the **Buswell equation** to **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** and **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**. Compare their theoretical methane yields in L CH₄ / kg substrate at STP.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Process & Stages (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** (Mermaid architecture). The system has:
1. **Feedstock handling:** collection → size reduction → slurry mixing tank.
2. **Digester:** airtight tank with agitator, heating coil, inlet pump.
3. **Gas train:** gas holder → H₂S scrubber → moisture trap → pressure regulator → utilisation (engine/stove/CHP).
4. **Outputs:** biogas (energy) + digestate (biofertiliser) + effluent.

**Four Biochemical Stages (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Hydrolysis:** Polymers → Monomers (using hydrolytic bacteria).
2. **Acidogenesis:** Monomers → VFAs, alcohols, CO₂, H₂.
3. **Acetogenesis:** VFAs → Acetate + H₂ + CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis:** Acetate/H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ (via methanogenic archaea).

Final reaction summary:

$$
\text{Organic waste} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Anaerobic microbes}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{trace gases}
$$

#### Model Solution — (b) Buswell Equation Application (7 marks)

**For Acetic Acid (CH₃COOH = C₂H₄O₂):** n = 2, a = 4, b = 2.

Methane coefficient:

$$
y = \frac{2}{2} + \frac{4}{8} - \frac{2}{4} = 1 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 1
$$

CO₂ coefficient:

$$
z = \frac{2}{2} - \frac{4}{8} + \frac{2}{4} = 1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1
$$

Water coefficient:

$$
x = 2 - \frac{4}{4} - \frac{2}{2} = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2}
$$

Molar mass of acetic acid = **60 g/mol**.

Methane yield:

$$
Y_{CH_4}^{AcOH} = \frac{1 \times 22.4}{60} = 0.373\,\text{L/g} = 373\,\text{L/kg}
$$

**For Ethanol (C₂H₆O):** n = 2, a = 6, b = 1.

Methane coefficient:

$$
y = \frac{2}{2} + \frac{6}{8} - \frac{1}{4} = 1 + 0.75 - 0.25 = 1.5
$$

CO₂ coefficient:

$$
z = 1 - 0.75 + 0.25 = 0.5
$$

Water coefficient:

$$
x = 2 - \frac{6}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 2 - 1.5 - 0.5 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} \longrightarrow 1.5\,\text{CH}_4 + 0.5\,\text{CO}_2}
$$

> Note: The fractional coefficient 1.5 indicates two ethanol molecules → 3 CH₄ + 1 CO₂.

Molar mass of ethanol = **46 g/mol**.

Methane yield:

$$
Y_{CH_4}^{EtOH} = \frac{1.5 \times 22.4}{46} = \frac{33.6}{46} = 0.730\,\text{L/g} = 730\,\text{L/kg}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Substrate | CH₄ yield (L/kg) | CH₄ mole fraction |
|---|---|---|
| Acetic acid | 373 | 50 % |
| Ethanol | 730 | 75 % |
| Glucose (reference) | 373 | 50 % |

**Inference (1 mark):** Ethanol gives **~1.96× higher methane yield per kg** than acetic acid or glucose, because it is more reduced (higher H/C atomic ratio).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Biogas** = anaerobic digestion of organic biomass → mixture of CH₄ (50–70 %) + CO₂ (30–40 %).
- ✅ **Calorific value** ≈ 20 MJ/m³ at 60 % CH₄; pure CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³.
- ✅ **Four AD stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. Hydrolysis is **rate-limiting** for lignocellulosic waste.
- ✅ **Methanogenesis pathways:** Acetoclastic (≈ 70 %) + Hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30 %).
- ✅ **Buswell equation:** $C_nH_aO_b + (n - a/4 - b/2)H_2O → (n/2 - a/8 + b/4)CO_2 + (n/2 + a/8 - b/4)CH_4$.
- ✅ **Molar volume at STP** = 22.4 L/mol — use this for yield calculations.
- ✅ **Optimal conditions:** T = 30–38 °C (mesophilic), pH = 6.8–7.5, C/N = 20–30, HRT = 20–40 days, VLR = 1–4 kg VS/m³·d.
- ✅ **Cattle dung benchmark:** ≈ 0.20–0.30 m³ CH₄ per kg VS fed.
- ✅ **Inhibitors:** NH₃ (free ammonia), H₂S, heavy metals, antibiotics.
- ✅ **Co-digestion** of carbon-rich and nitrogen-rich wastes optimises C/N.
- ✅ **Common KTU numerical trap:** forget to convert kg feed → kg VS using the VS fraction (typically 18 % for cattle dung).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Biogas?

**Biogas** is a colourless, flammable gaseous mixture produced by the **anaerobic digestion** (biological breakdown in the absence of oxygen) of organic matter such as cattle dung, agricultural residues, kitchen waste, municipal solid waste, and sewage sludge by specialized **methanogenic bacteria**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> "Biogas is a renewable biofuel produced naturally from the anaerobic decomposition of biodegradable organic waste by a consortium of bacteria in an airtight chamber called a **biogas digester**."

### Typical Composition of Biogas

| Component | Chemical Formula | Volume Percentage (%) |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------------|
| Methane | CH₄ | **55 – 65** |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | **35 – 45** |
| Hydrogen Sulphide | H₂S | 0.1 – 0.5 (trace) |
| Nitrogen | N₂ | 0.5 – 1.0 (trace) |
| Water Vapour | H₂O | 0.1 – 0.5 (trace) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **calorific value** of pure methane is **55.5 MJ/kg**, while biogas (with ~60% CH₄) typically gives **18 – 26 MJ/m³**.

### Calorific Value Calculation (Standard Reference)

$$
CV_{biogas} = (0.60 \times 55.5) + (0.40 \times 0) \approx 20 \text{ to } 25 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

The calorific value can be expressed in two ways:
$$
CV = \sum (y_i \times CV_i)
$$
where $y_i$ is the volume fraction and $CV_i$ is the calorific value of component *i*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The Kitchen Pressure Cooker

Think of a biogas plant as a **giant underground pressure cooker**:

- You put in **organic waste** (vegetables, dung, kitchen scraps) → this is the **feedstock**.
- You seal the lid tightly so **no oxygen enters** → this creates the **anaerobic condition**.
- You let **invisible bacteria** work inside for several days, just like a slow cooker → this is the **digestion process**.
- Slowly, a flammable gas (biogas) accumulates at the top, while the digested slurry settles at the bottom.

> The **KVIC model** is like a pressure cooker with a **floating weight on top** (the gas holder rises and falls), whereas the **Janata model** is like a pressure cooker with a **fixed, rigid lid** that traps the gas permanently inside.

---

## 1.3 The Two Classic Indian Biogas Models

India, being a country with the world's largest cattle population, developed two iconic small-scale digester designs that became reference models in the developing world:

### A) KVIC Model (Khadi and Village Industries Commission)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin:** Developed by **KVIC (Khadi and Village Industries Commission)** in **1962**, popularly called the **"Indian Gobar Gas Plant"**.
> **Type:** Floating-drum (movable gas holder) — a **constant-volume, variable-pressure** system.

The KVIC plant was the first standardized community-level biogas plant in India. Its signature feature is the **inverted steel drum** that floats on the slurry and rises/falls as gas is produced or consumed.

### B) Janata Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin:** Developed as a **low-cost alternative** to the KVIC model by the Planning Commission & KVIC.
> **Type:** Fixed-dome (masonry dome) — a **constant-volume, variable-pressure** system.

The Janata model eliminated the costly floating steel drum by replacing it with a **fixed masonry dome** built underground, making it more affordable for rural farmers but introducing challenges of variable gas pressure.

---

## 1.4 Why Are These Two Models in the KTU Syllabus?

Both models represent the **two fundamental digester geometries** used worldwide:
1. **Floating-drum** (KVIC) — movable storage.
2. **Fixed-dome** (Janata) — rigid storage.

Every commercial biogas plant today is a derivative of one of these two architectures, making them foundational for any **Renewable Energy Engineering** student.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anaerobic Digestion Process — Four Biological Stages

Biogas production is the result of **four sequential microbial stages**, each performed by a different bacterial group:

| Stage | Reaction Process | Key Bacteria | Output |
|-------|------------------|--------------|--------|
| **1. Hydrolysis** | Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) are broken into soluble monomers | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides* | Sugars, amino acids, fatty acids |
| **2. Acidogenesis** | Monomers are converted into volatile fatty acids (VFAs) | *Acetobacter*, *Streptococcus* | Acetic acid, propionic acid, butyric acid |
| **3. Acetogenesis** | VFAs converted into acetate, CO₂, H₂ | *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas* | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ |
| **4. Methanogenesis** | Acetate split into methane | *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus* | **CH₄ + CO₂ = Biogas** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Methanogenesis is the slowest step** and is rate-limiting. It is highly sensitive to temperature and pH (optimum **pH = 6.8 to 7.4**).

---

## 2.2 KVIC Model — Detailed Technical Description

### 2.2.1 Components of KVIC Plant

The KVIC model consists of the following structural components:

1. **Inlet Tank / Inlet Pipe** – Receives the daily slurry (dung + water mixture, usually **1:1 ratio by weight**).
2. **Digester Chamber** – Underground masonry/brick chamber where anaerobic digestion occurs.
3. **Outlet Tank / Displacement Chamber** – Where the digested slurry overflows.
4. **Floating Gas Holder (Gobar Gas Holder)** – Inverted steel drum that rises and falls based on gas volume.
5. **Central Guide Rod / Frame** – Keeps the floating holder aligned; prevents tilting.
6. **Gas Outlet Pipe** – At the top of the floating drum; supplies gas to the kitchen/utility.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle (KVIC)

- Slurry is fed **daily through the inlet** into the digester.
- Bacteria digest the slurry over **30–40 days (Hydraulic Retention Time, HRT)**.
- Biogas is released and rises to the top of the digester, pushing the **floating steel drum upward**.
- As gas is consumed (e.g., burnt in a stove), the drum **descends** again.
- Digested slurry exits through the **outlet displacement tank**.

### 2.2.3 Operational Characteristics of KVIC

- **Gas Pressure:** Nearly **constant at ~8–10 cm of water column** (due to the weight of the floating drum).
- **Gas Yield:** ~**0.04 m³ of biogas per kg of fresh cattle dung** under mesophilic conditions.
- **Standard Sizes:** 4 m³/day, 6 m³/day, 10 m³/day, 15 m³/day, 20 m³/day plant capacities.
- **Operating Temperature:** Mesophilic — **30 °C to 40 °C** (optimum 35 °C).
- **Feed Concentration:** Total Solids (TS) = **8–10%** in slurry.

### 2.2.4 Advantages and Limitations of KVIC Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantages:**
> - **Constant gas pressure** — uniform flame at the burner.
> - **Gas volume directly visible** (by the height of the drum) — easy monitoring.
> - Well-engineered and standardized design.
> - Easier gas flow control and measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations:**
> - **High capital cost** due to the steel gas holder.
> - Steel undergoes **corrosion** from H₂S and moisture → requires frequent repainting (every 1–2 years).
> - **Scum formation** on slurry surface reduces gas yield.
> - Needs **skilled labour** for fabrication of the drum.

---

## 2.3 Janata Model — Detailed Technical Description

### 2.3.1 Components of Janata Plant

1. **Inlet Pipe** – Connects inlet mixing tank to digester.
2. **Digester Chamber** – Masonry/brick underground chamber.
3. **Outlet Chamber** – Displacement-type outlet.
4. **Fixed Masonry Dome** – Built over the digester; acts as **rigid gas storage**.
5. **Gas Outlet Pipe** – With a water-seal manometer for pressure measurement.
6. **Compensating Tank / Expansion Chamber** – Adjusts for variable pressure.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle (Janata)

- Daily slurry feed enters the digester through the inlet.
- Biogas accumulates at the top inside the **fixed masonry dome**.
- Since the dome is **rigid**, the gas pressure **rises as more gas is produced** and **falls as gas is consumed**.
- Pressure range typically varies from **0 cm to ~100 cm of water column**.
- Digested slurry overflows through the outlet chamber.

### 2.3.3 Operational Characteristics of Janata

- **Gas Pressure:** **Variable** — depends on volume of gas stored.
- **Gas Yield:** ~**0.035 m³/kg** of dung (slightly lower than KVIC due to gas mixing issues at low pressure).
- **Standard Sizes:** 2 m³/day, 4 m³/day, 6 m³/day (smaller than KVIC).
- **Construction:** All-masonry — can be built by local masons.

### 2.3.4 Advantages and Limitations of Janata Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantages:**
> - **Low capital cost** — no steel gas holder required.
> - **Longer life (20–30 years)** — masonry construction is durable.
> - No moving parts → **less maintenance**.
> - Can be built by **local unskilled labour** — supports rural employment.
> - Underground construction — aesthetically unobtrusive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations:**
> - **Variable gas pressure** leads to inconsistent flame.
> - **Gas leakage** through masonry joints/cracks (especially when new).
> - At **low gas pressure**, gas can mix back with the slurry → reduced yield.
> - **Scum and foaming** issues since the dome is fixed.
> - Gas storage is **not visible** — harder to monitor.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Biogas Plant Design)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Hydraulic Retention Time | $HRT = \dfrac{V_d}{Q}$ | days | Digester sizing |
| 2 | Daily Slurry Input | $Q = D \times (1 + W)$ | m³/day | Feed calculation |
| 3 | Digester Volume | $V_d = Q \times HRT$ | m³ | Volume design |
| 4 | Daily Gas Production | $G = Y \times D$ | m³/day | Plant capacity |
| 5 | Gas Yield (Cattle Dung) | $Y \approx 0.04$ | m³/kg | Standard value |
| 6 | Slurry Concentration | $TS = \dfrac{M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$ | % | Feed control |
| 7 | Methane Fraction | $CH_4 = 0.60 \pm 0.05$ | volume frac | Energy content |
| 8 | Biogas Calorific Value | $CV = y_{CH_4} \times 55.5$ | MJ/m³ | Energy output |
| 9 | Equivalent Energy | $E = G \times CV$ | MJ/day | Daily output |
| 10 | Pressure at Base (Janata) | $P = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | Dome stress |

Where:
- $V_d$ = Digester working volume (m³)
- $Q$ = Daily slurry feed rate (m³/day)
- $D$ = Daily dung input (kg/day)
- $W$ = Water-to-dung ratio
- $Y$ = Gas yield per kg of dung
- $h$ = Height of slurry column

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Electrification:** Cluster of KVIC/Janata plants feed into a **biogas-based genset** for village microgrids.
- **Cooking Fuel:** Direct use in household stoves displaces LPG and firewood.
- **Organic Fertilizer:** The **digested slurry (bio-slurry)** is a nutrient-rich bio-fertilizer (rich in N, P, K).
- **Carbon Credits:** Each cubic metre of biogas replaces fossil fuels and earns **carbon credit** under CDM/Verra protocols.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Digester Volume from Retention Time

### Statement
Derive the expression for the **working volume of a biogas digester** as a function of daily feed and retention time.

### Given
- Daily slurry input rate = $Q$ (m³/day)
- Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) = $\theta$ (days)
- Digester working volume = $V_d$ (m³)

### Derivation

By definition, the **Hydraulic Retention Time** is the average time a fluid element spends inside the digester. It is the ratio of the digester volume to the volumetric flow rate:

$$
\theta = \frac{V_d}{Q}
$$

Rearranging for the digester volume:

$$
V_d = Q \times \theta
$$

For cattle-dung based digesters, the standard HRT is **30 – 40 days** (assume $\theta = 40$ days).

For the slurry composition, the daily input is:

$$
Q = M_d + M_w
$$

where $M_d$ is the mass of dung (kg) converted to volume and $M_w$ is the volume of water added. With a typical **1:1 dung-to-water ratio by mass**:

$$
M_w = M_d \quad (\text{in mass}), \quad \rho_{slurry} \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

So:

$$
Q = \frac{M_d + M_d}{1000} = \frac{2 M_d}{1000} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/day)}
$$

Finally, the digester working volume becomes:

$$
V_d = \frac{2 M_d \cdot \theta}{1000} \quad \text{m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU standard design equation** for sizing family-size biogas plants.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Digester Sizing

### Problem
A household has **50 kg of cattle dung** available per day. Design a **KVIC model biogas plant** with HRT of 40 days. Assume slurry density = 1000 kg/m³, gas yield = 0.04 m³/kg dung, and the gas holder occupies **30% of the digester volume**.

### Step 1 — Compute Daily Slurry Volume

$$
M_d = 50 \text{ kg}, \quad M_w = 50 \text{ kg (1:1 ratio)}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{50 + 50}{1000} = 0.10 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Working Volume of Digester

$$
V_d = Q \times \theta = 0.10 \times 40 = 4.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Daily Gas Production

$$
G = Y \times M_d = 0.04 \times 50 = 2.0 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Volume of Floating Gas Holder

The floating drum holds approximately the daily gas volume:

$$
V_{holder} = 2.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 5 — Total Tank Volume

$$
V_{total} = V_d + V_{holder} = 4.0 + 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 6 — Equivalent Energy Output

Assuming methane fraction $y_{CH_4} = 0.60$:

$$
CV_{biogas} = 0.60 \times 55.5 = 33.3 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

$$
E_{daily} = G \times CV_{biogas} = 2.0 \times 33.3 = 66.6 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **66.6 MJ ≈ 1.78 kg of LPG equivalent** per day → this is the typical energy output of a small family-size KVIC plant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Gas Pressure Variation in a Janata Plant

### Statement
Derive the expression for **variable gas pressure** in a fixed-dome (Janata) biogas plant as a function of gas volume.

### Given
- Fixed dome volume = $V_{dome}$ (m³)
- Maximum allowable gas volume = $V_{max}$ (m³)
- Cross-sectional area of slurry surface = $A$ (m²)
- Slurry density = $\rho_{slurry}$ (kg/m³)

### Derivation

When a volume $V_g$ of biogas is stored in the fixed dome, the slurry level inside the digester **drops by** an amount:

$$
\Delta h = \frac{V_g}{A}
$$

This creates a **hydrostatic pressure head** at the gas space:

$$
P = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot \Delta h = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot \frac{V_g}{A}
$$

Expressed in **cm of water column** (commonly used in biogas engineering):

$$
P = \frac{V_g}{A} \times 100 \quad \text{(cm of water column)}
$$

When the dome is **half-full**:

$$
P_{half} = \frac{V_{max}}{2A} \times 100 \text{ cm H}_2\text{O}
$$

When the dome is **full**:

$$
P_{max} = \frac{V_{max}}{A} \times 100 \text{ cm H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **Janata plants experience pressure swings** from 0 to ~100 cm H₂O, while **KVIC plants maintain nearly constant ~8–10 cm H₂O**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KVIC vs. Janata

| Parameter | KVIC Model | Janata Model |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Architecture | Floating drum | Fixed masonry dome |
| Gas Storage | Movable steel holder | Rigid underground dome |
| Pressure Behaviour | **Constant** (~8–10 cm H₂O) | **Variable** (0–100 cm H₂O) |
| Capital Cost | **High** (steel cost) | **Low** (masonry only) |
| Construction Skill | Skilled welder required | Local mason sufficient |
| Maintenance | Frequent (paint, corrosion) | Minimal |
| Plant Life | 15 – 20 years | 20 – 30 years |
| Typical Capacity | 4 – 20 m³/day | 2 – 6 m³/day |
| Scum Removal | Easier (drum can be lifted) | Difficult |
| Gas Leakage | Negligible | Possible through cracks |
| Gas Visibility | **Visible** (drum height) | **Not visible** |
| Gas Yield | Slightly higher (~0.04 m³/kg) | Slightly lower (~0.035 m³/kg) |
| Best Application | Community / institutional plants | Individual family farms |

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — KVIC Model

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the KVIC floating-drum biogas plant. (Adapted as a block-level functional flow to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["CATTLE DUNG AND WATER MIXING TANK"]
    nodeB["INLET PIPE WITH SLURRY VALVE"]
    nodeC["UNDERGROUND MASONRY DIGESTER CHAMBER"]
    nodeD["METHANOGENIC BACTERIA ACTION ANAEROBIC ZONE"]
    nodeE["FLOATING STEEL GAS HOLDER INVERTED DRUM"]
    nodeF["CENTRAL GUIDE ROD AND FRAME"]
    nodeG["GAS OUTLET PIPE TO BURNER"]
    nodeH["OUTLET DISPLACEMENT CHAMBER"]
    nodeI["DIGESTED SLURRY TO BIO FERTILIZER PIT"]
    nodeJ["HOUSEHOLD OR INSTITUTIONAL GAS BURNER"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

### Reading the Diagram
- The **slurry (A → B → C)** enters the digester.
- **Anaerobic bacteria (D)** convert the organic matter into biogas.
- The **floating drum (E)** rises on the guide rod (F) as gas accumulates.
- **Biogas (G)** is delivered to the **burner (J)** at nearly constant pressure.
- **Spent slurry (H → I)** overflows into a fertilizer pit.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Janata Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["DAILY DUNG AND WATER INPUT MIXING"]
    nodeQ["INLET PIPE GRAVITY FEED"]
    nodeR["UNDERGROUND DIGESTER MASONRY BRICK CHAMBER"]
    nodeS["FIXED MASONRY GAS STORAGE DOME"]
    nodeT["GAS OUTLET PIPE WITH WATER SEAL MANOMETER"]
    nodeU["OUTLET DISPLACEMENT TANK"]
    nodeV["BIOGAS TO STOVE BURNER LAMP"]
    nodeW["VARIABLE GAS PRESSURE 0 TO 100 CM H2O"]
    nodeX["DIGESTED SLURRY TO COMPOST PIT"]

    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeW
    nodeT --> nodeV
    nodeR --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeX
```

### Reading the Diagram
- Slurry (P → Q → R) is fed by gravity.
- Biogas accumulates in the **fixed dome (S)** — a sealed masonry structure.
- Gas exits (T) under **variable pressure (W)** that must be regulated.
- Spent slurry (U → X) is composted.

---

## 4.3 Functional Comparison Flow — Digestion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["INPUT ORGANIC WASTE"]
    stepB["HYDROLYSIS POLYMERS TO MONOMERS"]
    stepC["ACIDOGENESIS VFAs PRODUCED"]
    stepD["ACETOGENESIS ACETATE PRODUCED"]
    stepE["METHANOGENESIS BIOGAS FORMED"]
    stepF["BIOGAS STORAGE KVIC DRUM OR JANATA DOME"]
    stepG["END USE COOKING ELECTRICITY FERTILIZER"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Methanogenesis stage (E)** is the **rate-limiting and most sensitive** step. Maintaining temperature at **35 °C (mesophilic)** and pH in the **6.8–7.4** range is critical for both KVIC and Janata plants.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Choice Option 1

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a KVIC model biogas plant. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

#### Sub-part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction:**
The KVIC (Khadi and Village Industries Commission) model biogas plant, also called the **Indian Gobar Gas Plant**, has the following components:

1. **Inlet tank** — receives the daily mixture of cattle dung and water (1:1 ratio by mass).
2. **Digester chamber** — underground masonry chamber where anaerobic digestion occurs at **mesophilic temperature (30–40 °C)**.
3. **Outlet/Displacement tank** — collects the overflowing digested slurry.
4. **Floating gas holder** — an inverted **steel drum** that moves vertically based on gas volume.
5. **Central guide rod and frame** — keeps the floating drum aligned.
6. **Gas outlet pipe** — at the top of the drum; delivers biogas to the burner.

**Working:**
- Fresh slurry is fed daily through the inlet pipe into the digester.
- Methanogenic bacteria anaerobically digest the organic matter over a **retention time of 30–40 days**.
- The released biogas rises and pushes the floating drum upward.
- As gas is consumed, the drum descends.
- The digested slurry exits through the outlet displacement tank and is used as bio-fertilizer.

#### Sub-part (b) — Advantages and Limitations (7 marks)

**Advantages:**
- **Constant gas pressure** (~8–10 cm H₂O) → uniform burner flame.
- **Visible gas volume** (drum height) → easy monitoring for users.
- **Standardized design** → easier quality control.
- Easier **scum removal** by lifting the drum.

**Limitations:**
- **High capital cost** (steel fabrication cost).
- Steel undergoes **corrosion from H₂S and moisture** → needs repainting every 1–2 years.
- Requires **skilled welders** for fabrication.
- Corrosion reduces **plant life** to 15–20 years.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by:
> - Drawing only the drum and forgetting the **displacement outlet tank** (KTU requires full labeled diagram).
> - Missing the **retention time value (30–40 days)** — a favourite KTU numerical question.
> - Confusing "constant volume vs. constant pressure" terminology. **KVIC = constant volume (variable mass) but practically constant pressure** due to fixed drum weight.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Choice Option 2

**Q: Explain the Janata model of a biogas plant with a neat sketch. Compare it with the KVIC model.**

#### Sub-part (a) — Janata Plant: Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction:**
The Janata model is a **fixed-dome type** biogas plant developed to reduce the cost of the KVIC plant. Its components are:

1. **Inlet mixing tank and pipe** — for daily slurry feed.
2. **Underground digester** — masonry chamber for anaerobic digestion.
3. **Fixed masonry dome** — built on top of the digester; serves as permanent gas storage.
4. **Outlet displacement chamber** — collects digested slurry.
5. **Gas outlet with water-seal manometer** — delivers gas and measures pressure.
6. **Compensating tank** — accommodates pressure fluctuations.

**Working:**
- Slurry enters the digester via the inlet.
- Biogas produced accumulates in the **fixed dome** (no moving storage).
- As gas volume increases, the **slurry level drops**, raising the gas pressure.
- Gas pressure **varies from 0 to ~100 cm H₂O** depending on stored volume.
- Spent slurry overflows through the outlet chamber.

#### Sub-part (b) — Comparison with KVIC (7 marks)

| Parameter | KVIC Model | Janata Model |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Type | Floating drum | Fixed dome |
| Gas holder | Movable steel drum | Fixed masonry dome |
| Gas pressure | ~Constant (~8–10 cm H₂O) | Variable (0–100 cm H₂O) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Plant life | 15–20 years | 20–30 years |
| Maintenance | Frequent (painting) | Minimal |
| Suitable for | Community plants | Individual households |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do not confuse **Janata with Janta biogas model from China** — KTU strictly means the **Indian Janata model**.
> - Always mention the **compensating tank** — students frequently omit it, losing 1 mark.
> - The comparison table must have **at least 5 parameters**, otherwise KTU deducts marks.

## 5.3 Numerical Practice Question (KTU Style)

**Q: A family produces 40 kg of cattle dung per day. Design a KVIC biogas plant. Assume HRT = 40 days, slurry density = 1000 kg/m³, gas yield = 0.04 m³/kg. The floating gas holder has 30% extra volume. Calculate:**
**(i) Daily slurry input**
**(ii) Digester volume**
**(iii) Daily gas production**
**(iv) Total tank volume**

**Solution:**

**(i) Daily slurry input:**
$$
M_d = 40 \text{ kg}, \quad M_w = 40 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{M_d + M_w}{\rho_{slurry}} = \frac{80}{1000} = 0.08 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
$$

**(ii) Digester working volume:**
$$
V_d = Q \times HRT = 0.08 \times 40 = 3.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(iii) Daily gas production:**
$$
G = Y \times M_d = 0.04 \times 40 = 1.6 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
$$

**(iv) Total tank volume (including holder):**
$$
V_{total} = V_d + 0.30 \times V_d = 1.30 \times 3.2 = 4.16 \text{ m}^3
$$

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KVIC vs. Janata Biogas Models**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- **Biogas** = Anaerobic digestion of organic waste → CH₄ (60%) + CO₂ (40%).
- **Calorific value** of biogas ≈ **20–26 MJ/m³**.
- Standard operating temperature: **Mesophilic 30–40 °C**, pH **6.8–7.4**.
- Standard HRT: **30–40 days** for cattle-dung plants.

### ✅ KVIC Model Highlights
- Floating steel drum → **constant gas pressure**.
- Visible gas volume → easy monitoring.
- Higher cost, shorter life (15–20 yrs), maintenance-heavy.

### ✅ Janata Model Highlights
- Fixed masonry dome → **variable gas pressure** (0–100 cm H₂O).
- Low cost, longer life (20–30 yrs), minimal maintenance.
- Risk of gas leakage through masonry cracks.

### ✅ Key Design Equations (must memorize)
- $V_d = Q \times HRT$
- $G = Y \times M_d$
- $CV_{biogas} = y_{CH_4} \times 55.5 \text{ MJ/m}^3$
- $P_{Janata} = \dfrac{V_g}{A} \times 100 \text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$

### ✅ Common KTU Traps
- "Constant pressure" vs. "Variable pressure" — KVIC is **nearly constant**; Janata is **variable**.
- Methanogenesis is **rate-limiting**, not hydrolysis.
- Biogas is **renewable but not carbon-neutral** — its combustion releases CO₂.
- Standard dung:water ratio is **1:1 by mass**, not by volume.

### ✅ Real-World Significance
- KVIC and Janata models form the **backbone of India's National Biogas Programme (NBP)**.
- India has installed **over 5 million family-size biogas plants** based on these two designs.
- Both contribute to **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** — clean energy (SDG 7), climate action (SDG 13), and sustainable agriculture (SDG 2).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Energy - Various Routes for Production of Hydrogen Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET614 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Energy - Various Routes for Production of Hydrogen Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Hydrogen as an Energy Carrier — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Hydrogen Energy* refers to the chemical energy stored in molecular diatomic hydrogen (H₂), which can be released on demand via combustion or electrochemical conversion (fuel cells) to produce **water (H₂O)** as the only by-product, making it a **carbon-free secondary energy carrier** (not a primary energy source).

Hydrogen is the **lightest, most abundant chemical element** in the universe (≈ **75% of baryonic mass**) but exists on Earth predominantly bound in compounds (water, hydrocarbons, biomass). To be useful as an energy carrier, it must first be **extracted** via energy-intensive processes called **"Production Routes."**

**Key Thermophysical Constants (Board Exam Essentials):**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | **2.016 g/mol** |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** (≈ 33.33 kWh/kg) |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142 MJ/kg** (≈ 39.4 kWh/kg) |
| Energy density by volume (liquid) | **8.5 MJ/L** |
| Flammability range in air | **4% to 75% by volume** |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **500 °C** |
| Density at STP | **0.0899 kg/m³** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Battery of Molecules"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of hydrogen like a *rechargeable chemical battery*. Nature's "charging cable" is sunlight or heat; the "battery cell" is water or methane; and the "discharge port" is a fuel cell or engine that releases clean energy on demand. **You cannot "mine" hydrogen like coal — you must manufacture it.**

Just as electricity must be *generated* before it is *stored* in a battery, hydrogen must be *produced* from feedstocks (water, fossil fuels, biomass) using primary energy (renewable electricity, solar heat, nuclear, or fossil heat) before being utilized.

## 1.3 The Hydrogen Color Spectrum (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The industry classifies hydrogen by the **carbon intensity of its production pathway**. This color coding is a **high-yield 3-mark question** on the KTU 2024 syllabus.

- **Grey Hydrogen** — from Steam Methane Reforming (SMR); CO₂ released; ~95% of current global production.
- **Brown Hydrogen** — from Coal Gasification; highest CO₂ emissions.
- **Blue Hydrogen** — Grey/Brown + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS).
- **Green Hydrogen** — from Electrolysis powered by **renewables**; zero carbon.
- **Pink Hydrogen** — from nuclear-powered electrolysis.
- **Turquoise Hydrogen** — from methane pyrolysis yielding solid carbon.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrogen Production Routes

The routes are organized along **two fundamental axes**: (a) the *feedstock* used (fossil vs. renewable), and (b) the *primary energy input* (thermal, electrical, biochemical, photonic).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus demands coverage of *thermochemical, electrochemical, photochemical, and biological* routes. Numerical problems typically involve **energy balance, Faraday's Law of Electrolysis, and stoichiometric mass balances.**

### A. Thermochemical Routes (Heat-driven bond breaking)
1. Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)
2. Partial Oxidation (POX)
3. Autothermal Reforming (ATR)
4. Coal Gasification
5. Biomass Gasification
6. Thermochemical Water-Splitting Cycles (e.g., Sulfur–Iodine cycle)

### B. Electrochemical Routes (Electricity-driven)
1. Alkaline Electrolyzer
2. Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) Electrolyzer
3. Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (SOEC)

### C. Photochemical Routes (Light-driven)
1. Photobiological Water Splitting (Biophotolysis)
2. Photocatalytic Water Splitting
3. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) Cells

### D. Biological Routes (Biochemistry-driven)
1. Dark Fermentation
2. Photofermentation
3. Microbial Electrolysis Cells (MEC)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Production Route Stoichiometry & Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **mandatory** for any Part B 14-mark derivation question. Use `\vert` notation instead of pipe symbols to preserve table syntax.

| # | Route | Governing Reaction | Efficiency Range | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steam Reforming | $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ | 70–85% | $\eta_{SMR} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{Q_{input}}$ |
| 2 | Water–Gas Shift | $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ | — | Equilibrium Kp at 200–450 °C |
| 3 | Partial Oxidation | $CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$ | 60–75% | Exothermic, adiabatic T ≈ **1200 K** |
| 4 | Coal Gasification | $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ | 50–60% | Air-blown vs. O₂-blown |
| 5 | Alkaline Electrolysis | $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ | 60–70% | **Faraday's Law** (see below) |
| 6 | PEM Electrolysis | $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ | 70–85% | Cell voltage 1.8–2.2 V |
| 7 | SOEC Electrolysis | $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ | 75–95% | Operating T 700–850 °C |
| 8 | S–I Thermochemical | Net: $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ | 35–50% | Three-step closed cycle |

### 2.2.1 Faraday's Law of Electrolysis — The Foundation Equation

For an electrolyzer producing hydrogen, the theoretical minimum energy is fixed by thermodynamics:

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $m_{H_2}$ = mass of hydrogen produced **[kg]**
- $M$ = molar mass of H₂ = **2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol**
- $I$ = current **[A]**
- $t$ = time **[s]**
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred = **2** (since $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

The **theoretical cell voltage** (reversible voltage) at standard conditions:

$$E_{rev}^{0} = \frac{\Delta G^{0}}{nF} = \frac{237.2\ \text{kJ/mol}}{2 \cdot 96485} \approx 1.229\ \text{V}$$

The **thermo-neutral voltage** (using enthalpy instead of Gibbs free energy):

$$E_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H^{0}}{nF} = \frac{285.8\ \text{kJ/mol}}{2 \cdot 96485} \approx 1.481\ \text{V}$$

### 2.2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Faradaic Efficiency** ($\eta_F$) and **Voltage Efficiency** ($\eta_V$) combine into **overall electrolyzer efficiency**:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_F \times \eta_V = \frac{I_{H_2}}{I_{total}} \times \frac{E_{tn}}{V_{cell}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** A 1 MW alkaline electrolyzer operating at 1.85 V/cell and 70% Faradaic efficiency produces ≈ **31.5 kg H₂/hour**. This is the standard sizing calculation used by ISRO, DRDO, and commercial green-ammonia plants in Kerala (Vizhinjam, Cochin Port). KTU examiners frequently ask for this mass-flow calculation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Hydrogen Yield from an Electrolyzer

**Problem Statement:** A 100 kW alkaline electrolyzer operates at a cell voltage of **1.85 V**, current **540 A**, and Faradaic efficiency of **95%**. Compute:
(a) Hydrogen production rate [kg/hr]
(b) Overall electrolyzer efficiency
(c) Specific energy consumption [kWh/kg H₂]

### Step 1 — Calculate the theoretical mass using Faraday's Law

First, find the charge passed per hour:

$$Q = I \cdot t = 540\ \text{A} \times 3600\ \text{s} = 1.944 \times 10^{6}\ \text{C}$$

Apply Faraday's Law for the *theoretical* mass:

$$m_{th} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{(2.016 \times 10^{-3})\ \text{kg/mol} \cdot (540)(3600)}{2 \cdot 96485\ \text{C/mol}}$$

$$m_{th} = \frac{(2.016 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 1.944 \times 10^{6}}{192970} = \frac{3919.1}{192970} = 0.02031\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$m_{th} = 0.02031 \times 3600 = 73.12\ \text{kg/hr}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Faradaic Efficiency

$$\eta_F = 0.95 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{actual} = 0.95 \times 73.12 = 69.46\ \text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{m_{H_2} = 69.46\ \text{kg/hr}}$$

### Step 3 — Overall Efficiency

Voltage efficiency uses thermoneutral voltage $E_{tn} = 1.481$ V:

$$\eta_V = \frac{E_{tn}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{1.481}{1.85} = 0.8005 \approx 80.05\%$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_F \cdot \eta_V = 0.95 \times 0.8005 = 0.7605 \approx 76.05\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 76.05\%}$$

### Step 4 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

$$SEC = \frac{\text{Energy input}}{\text{mass of H}_2} = \frac{P_{in}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{100{,}000\ \text{W}}{0.0193\ \text{kg/s}}$$

$$SEC = 5.18 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J/kg} = 51.8\ \text{MJ/kg}$$

Converting to kWh/kg (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$SEC = \frac{51.8}{3.6} = 14.39\ \text{kWh/kg}\ H_2$$

$$\boxed{SEC \approx 14.39\ \text{kWh/kg H}_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The theoretical minimum SEC is 33.33 kWh/kg (from LHV = 120 MJ/kg). The 14.39 kWh/kg value here is the *electrical input*. The ratio 33.33/14.39 ≈ **2.31** indicates **waste heat production**, since the actual cell voltage (1.85 V) exceeds the thermoneutral voltage (1.481 V).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Balance on a Steam Methane Reformer

**Problem:** Determine the **energy input** for SMR producing 1 kg of H₂, assuming 100% process efficiency and methane feed at 25 °C. Compare with LHV of H₂.

### Step 1 — Stoichiometric Hydrogen Yield

The reforming reaction: $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ (followed by WGS: $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$).

Net reaction: $CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$

Molar mass of CH₄ = 16.04 kg/kmol; produces 4 × 2.016 = **8.064 kg H₂ per kmol CH₄**.

CH₄ required for **1 kg H₂**:

$$n_{CH_4} = \frac{1\ \text{kg}}{8.064\ \text{kg H}_2/\text{kmol CH}_4} \times \frac{1\ \text{kmol}}{1\ \text{kg}} = \frac{1}{8.064} = 0.124\ \text{kmol}$$

In mass terms: $0.124 \times 16.04 = 1.989\ \text{kg CH}_4$.

### Step 2 — Energy Released by Combustion of That Methane

Using LHV of CH₄ ≈ **50 MJ/kg** (more accurately 50.016 MJ/kg):

$$Q_{CH_4} = 1.989 \times 50.016 = 99.48\ \text{MJ}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Content of 1 kg H₂ (LHV basis)

$$Q_{H_2} = 1 \times 120\ \text{MJ} = 120\ \text{MJ}$$

### Step 4 — Process Efficiency (the *First Law* view)

$$\eta_{SMR} = \frac{Q_{H_2}}{Q_{CH_4}} = \frac{120}{99.48} = 1.206$$

This **>100% efficiency** reflects that **process heat** from CH₄ combustion (over and above the reforming reaction enthalpy) contributes. The realistic *overall thermal* efficiency (including reformer furnace heat) is **70–85%**, requiring:

$$Q_{actual} = \frac{120}{0.78} \approx 153.8\ \text{MJ/kg H}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often forget that SMR *requires external heat input* of ~**30–40% additional energy** beyond methane's LHV to drive the endothermic reforming step at 700–900 °C. The "120 MJ/kg" H₂ is *output*, not *input*.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Production Route Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Design / Comparison Questions:** Use the matrix below to justify route selection for specific applications.

| Selection Criterion | Best Route | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowest cost H₂ today** | Grey (SMR) | $1–2/kg; mature industrial scale |
| **Zero-carbon target** | Green (PEM electrolysis) | Renewable electricity + high purity (99.999%) |
| **Highest round-trip efficiency** | Blue (SMR + CCS) | ~70–80% system efficiency |
| **Highest temperature heat available** | SOEC / S–I Cycle | Nuclear or concentrated solar drives 800 °C+ operation |
| **Distributed / Rural scale** | Biomass gasification | Local feedstock availability |
| **Pharmaceutical/Food-grade H₂** | PEM electrolysis | Ultra-pure, no CO/S contaminants |
| **Scale > 100 tonnes/day** | Coal gasification + CCS | China/India dominant technology |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Hydrogen Production Pathway Classification

## 4.2 Block Diagram — SMR Plant Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed1[Natural Gas CH4]:::in --> Desulfur[Desulfurization Unit]
    Feed2[Deionized H2O]:::in --> HTR[Heater 700 to 900 C]
    Desulfur --> MIX[Mixer]
    HTR --> MIX
    MIX --> REFORM[Reformer Reactor with Ni Catalyst]
    REFORM --> HX[Heat Exchanger]
    HX --> WGS[Water Gas Shift Reactor]

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PEM Electrolyzer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
    STACK --> CAT[Cathode Side: H2 Evolution Half Cell]
    ANA --> O2OUT[O2 Storage or Vent]

    H2OUT --> COMP[H2 Compressor to 350 or 700 bar]

```

```mermaid

    H2OIN[Input: H2O + I2 + SO2]:::in --> S1[Section 1: Bunsen Reactor 120 C]
    S1 --> S2[Section 2: H2SO4 Decomposition 850 C]
    S1 --> S3[Section 3: HI Decomposition 450 C]
    S2 --> O2OUT[O2 Released]
    S3 --> H2OUT[H2 Released]
    S2 --> S1
    S3 --> S1

```

```mermaid
    ELYT --> SC
    SC --> H2OUT[H2 Evolution Site 2H plus 2e gives H2]
    SA --> O2OUT[O2 Evolution Site H2O gives 0.5 O2 plus 2H plus 2e]
```
