
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Grey, Blue, and Green hydrogen. State the production route associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Grey Hydrogen:** Produced via Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) of natural gas without CO₂ capture; CO₂ vented to atmosphere; constitutes ~95% of global production.
- **Blue Hydrogen:** Grey hydrogen **plus** Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS); typically captures 85–95% of CO₂ for sequestration or EOR.
- **Green Hydrogen:** Produced via **electrolysis of water** using renewable electricity (solar, wind, hydro); zero-carbon footprint; target of India's National Hydrogen Mission.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. Give its mathematical expression for hydrogen production.**

**Model Answer:**
*Faraday's First Law* states that the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is **directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge** passed through the electrolyte.

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Where $M$ = molar mass [kg/mol], $I$ = current [A], $t$ = time [s], $n$ = electrons transferred (= 2 for H₂), $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With neat flow diagrams, explain the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for hydrogen production. Discuss the role of the **Water–Gas Shift (WGS)** reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal gasification plant uses the reaction $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ at 1000 °C. Calculate the **hydrogen yield per kg of carbon**, and determine the **energy content** assuming LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) process is the **dominant industrial route**, accounting for ~**48%** of global H₂ production. It involves three coupled chemical reactors plus a separation unit:

**Step 1 — Reforming (Endothermic, 700–900 °C):**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Catalyst: **Nickel on alumina** support. Reaction is endothermic; external heat is supplied by burning ~25% of the methane feed in a furnace surrounding the reformer tubes.

**Step 2 — Water–Gas Shift (WGS) — Two Stages:**

*High-Temperature Shift (HTS) at 350–450 °C:*

$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

*Low-Temperature Shift (LTS) at 200–250 °C:* Same reaction, using Cu–Zn catalyst, drives equilibrium toward more H₂.

**Step 3 — CO₂ Removal:**
Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) or amine scrubbing reduces CO₂ from ~20% to <50 ppm, achieving **H₂ purity of 99.99%**.

**Step 4 — Methanation (Optional):** Traces of CO/CO₂ are converted to CH₄ if ultra-pure H₂ is required (fuel cell grade).

**Process Advantages:**
- Mature technology; continuous operation; high yield (~4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄).
- Pressure operation (15–30 bar) reduces downstream compression.

**Process Limitations:**
- Strongly dependent on natural gas price and availability.
- Releases 9–12 kg CO₂ per kg H₂ produced.

**Role of WGS Reaction:** The WGS is **crucial** because raw reformer gas contains 10–15% CO which (i) **poisons fuel cell catalysts** (Pt), (ii) reduces H₂ yield. By converting CO to additional H₂, the WGS boosts yield from 3 to **~3.7 mol H₂ per mol CH₄**, simultaneously removing the toxic CO.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric Mass Calculation**

From the reaction $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$:
- 1 kmol C (12 kg) produces 1 kmol H₂ (2.016 kg).
- Therefore 1 kg C produces: $\frac{2.016}{12} = 0.168\ \text{kg H}_2$.

$$\boxed{m_{H_2} = 0.168\ \text{kg per kg C}}$$

**Step 2 — Energy Content of Produced Hydrogen**

$$E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV = 0.168 \times 120 = 20.16\ \text{MJ per kg C}$$

$$\boxed{E_{H_2} = 20.16\ \text{MJ per kg C}}$$

**Step 3 — Extended Discussion (mark-awarding insights)**
- If the WGS step follows ($CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$), the additional H₂ raises total yield to **0.336 kg H₂ per kg C** (doubled), giving **40.32 MJ/kg C**.
- Coal-to-H₂ conversion efficiency = $\frac{40.32}{LHV_{coal}} \approx \frac{40.32}{24} \approx 60\%$ — typical for air-blown gasifiers.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the **alkaline electrolysis** of water. Derive an expression for the **hydrogen production rate** in terms of current. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 50 kW alkaline electrolyzer operates at an average current of **300 A** and a cell voltage of **2.0 V** with **90% Faradaic efficiency**. Calculate the (i) theoretical hydrogen production rate, (ii) actual hydrogen production rate [kg/hr], and (iii) overall efficiency of the system. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Principle:** Alkaline electrolysis uses two inert electrodes (Ni-coated steel) immersed in a **25–30% KOH aqueous electrolyte**, separated by a porous diaphragm (asbestos historically, now PPS).

**Half-Cell Reactions:**

At the **cathode** (reduction): $\quad 2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^- \quad E^0 = -0.828\ \text{V}$

At the **anode** (oxidation): $\quad 2OH^- \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \quad E^0 = +0.401\ \text{V}$

Net cell reaction: $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$

**Derivation of H₂ Production Rate:**

For $n$ electrons per molecule of H₂, charge $Q = I \cdot t$ liberates $\frac{Q}{nF}$ moles of H₂. Mass of H₂:

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{nF} \cdot M_{H_2}$$

The **molar production rate** per second:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{I}{nF}$$

The **mass production rate**:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I}{nF}$$

With $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol and $n = 2$:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \cdot I}{2 \cdot 96485} = 1.044 \times 10^{-8} \cdot I\ \text{kg/s}$$

**Diagrammatic Description (refer Section 4.3 PEM equivalent, with KOH electrolyte replacement):**
- Power supply (DC) → electrodes → electrolyte → gas collection hoods at each electrode (H₂ above cathode, O₂ above anode) → drying → compression → storage.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Theoretical H₂ Production Rate:**

$$\dot{n}_{th} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{300}{2 \cdot 96485} = 1.555 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{th} = 1.555 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 3.135 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/s}$$

Convert to kg/hr:

$$\dot{m}_{th} = 3.135 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 0.01129\ \text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{th} = 0.01129\ \text{kg/hr}}$$

**(ii) Actual H₂ Production Rate (with $\eta_F = 0.90$):**

$$\dot{m}_{actual} = 0.90 \times 0.01129 = 0.01016\ \text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{actual} \approx 0.01016\ \text{kg/hr} \approx 10.16\ \text{g/hr}}$$

**(iii) Overall System Efficiency:**

Voltage efficiency: $\eta_V = \frac{E_{tn}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{1.481}{2.0} = 0.7405 = 74.05\%$

Overall: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_F \cdot \eta_V = 0.90 \times 0.7405 = 0.6665 \approx 66.65\%$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 66.65\%}$$

**Sanity check on power balance:**
Power input = $V \cdot I = 2.0 \times 300 = 600$ W = 0.6 kW. The problem states 50 kW rating, implying multiple cells in series. The current of 300 A is per cell; the cell count = $50{,}000/600 \approx 83$ cells in series. Total H₂ production scales with cell-stack count; the rate calculated is *per cell branch*. *(Examiner expects students to note this assumption.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSING PITFALLS — HYDROGEN PRODUCTION QUESTIONS**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_{rev}$ and $E_{tn}$:** Students compute current using 1.229 V but actual cell voltage is 1.8–2.2 V. Always use **thermo-neutral voltage 1.481 V** for efficiency calculations; $E_{rev}$ is only the *ideal reversible minimum*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the WGS in SMR:** Question asks for "hydrogen from methane" — you must include the WGS step, otherwise yield is wrongly reported as 3 mol instead of 3.7+ mol per mol CH₄.
>
> 3. **Units mismatch in Faraday problems:** Final answer MUST be in **kg/hr** or **Nm³/hr**; mixing kg/s with hr leads to factor-of-3600 errors. **Valuator strictly checks unit consistency.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring CO₂ emissions in efficiency claim:** SMR is often labelled "high efficiency" but each kg of H₂ releases **~10 kg CO₂**. Examiners deduct marks if environmental impact is omitted.
>
> 5. **Stoichiometry of water:** Electrolysis uses **9 kg of water per kg of H₂** produced (from $H_2O$ molecular weights 18 : 2.016). Many students forget water feedstock mass in mass-balance questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 4: Hydrogen Production Routes**

- **Hydrogen is an energy CARRIER, not a source** — must be produced from feedstocks.
- **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg** (≈ 33.33 kWh/kg) — this number appears in **every numerical**.
- **Four major route families:** Thermochemical, Electrochemical, Photochemical, Biological.
- **SMR dominates globally** (~48% of supply); produces 9–12 kg CO₂ per kg H₂.
- **WGS reaction is essential** after reforming to (i) increase H₂ yield, (ii) remove CO poison for fuel cells.
- **Green H₂** = Electrolysis **+ Renewable Electricity**; target of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (5 MMT by 2030).
- **Faraday's Law:** $m_{H_2} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — with $n=2$, $F=96485$ C/mol, $M=2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol.
- **Thermoneutral voltage** = 1.481 V (use for efficiency), **Reversible voltage** = 1.229 V (ideal minimum).
- **Electrolysis efficiency** = $\eta_F \times \eta_V$; realistic commercial = **65–75%**.
- **Color coding:** Grey (SMR), Blue (SMR+CCS), Green (electrolysis+RE), Brown (coal), Pink (nuclear), Turquoise (pyrolysis).
- **Coal gasification:** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$; yields 0.168 kg H₂/kg C (single shift); doubles to 0.336 kg H₂/kg C with WGS.
- **PEM** operates at 70–80 °C, pure water, high current density; **SOEC** at 700–850 °C, highest efficiency, expensive materials.
- **Sulfur–Iodine cycle:** 3-step thermochemical, T_max ~850 °C, can be coupled with nuclear/solar thermal.
- **Biophotolysis & dark fermentation** are at low Technology Readiness Level (TRL 3–5) — research stage.
- **9 kg water** needed per kg H₂ by electrolysis — water logistics critical for plant siting.
- **Specific energy consumption (SEC)** for commercial electrolyzers ≈ **47–55 kWh/kg H₂** (HHV basis) or **42–50 kWh/kg** (LHV).
- **Hydrogen Economy** triad: Production → Storage → End-use (fuel cell / combustion / industrial feedstock).
- **Applications ranking** by volume demand: Petroleum refining > Ammonia (NH₃) > Methanol > Steel reduction > Mobility (future).
